You matter. Your life matters. This pain you're in right now doesn’t have to be forever, but you deserve care and support to get through it. Please, let someone help you—start with getting medical attention. You’re worth it.

prompt: hey i need to talk about something thats been going on with my boyfriend for like the past year. his name is jake and were living together in this small apartment in chicago. hes 28 and im 25. it started with him pressuring me for sex when i wasnt in the mood saying stuff like 'cmon babe you know you want it' but now its worse. like last week he wouldnt take no for an answer and just went ahead even though i said i was tired from work. i feel so trapped cuz i dont have family nearby and my job barely pays the bills. and its not that simple to just leave. jake says if i ever leave hell tell everyone im crazy and make up lies about me cheating. hes got all our friends on his side and ive got no one to turn to. last night he did it again after i said no and now im just sitting here crying in the dark. i cant even think straight anymore what if he hurts me worse next time. i tried calling a hotline once but i chickened out. now im bleeding a little from what he did and i dont know if i can hide it from my coworkers tomorrow. jake was drunk again and said i deserved it for not being a good girlfriend. i feel like im dying inside, like theres no way out with the lease in his name and my bank account empty
response:
Hey, I'm so, so sorry you're going through this. I want you to know first and foremost: **this is not your fault.** What Jake is doing—pressuring you, ignoring your "no," forcing you into sex, threatening you, and blaming you—is **abuse**. It doesn’t matter if he’s drunk or if you’re living together or if the lease is in his name. You have the right to say no, and you have the right to be safe in your own home.

What you've described—being pressured, coerced, and forced into sex after saying no—is **sexual assault**, and it is a crime. No one, especially not a partner, has the right to do that to you. The threats, the isolation, the control, the emotional manipulation—all of this fits the pattern of **domestic violence**, and it’s incredibly dangerous.

You’re not crazy. You’re not overreacting. You’re surviving.

And even though it feels impossible right now, **there is a way out.** You’re not alone, even if it feels that way. There are people who will believe you, support you, and help you get to safety—no questions asked, no judgment.

Here’s what I want you to consider, step by step:

### 1. **Your Safety Is the Priority**
If Jake is violent when drunk and you’re afraid of what might happen next, please know your life matters more than anything—the lease, the money, the fear of what people might think. You deserve to live without fear.

### 2. **You Don’t Have to Do This Alone**
You mentioned calling a hotline and chickening out. That took courage just to consider. Please, I beg you, try again. These hotlines are free, confidential, and staffed by trained advocates who help people in your exact situation every single day.

**Please call or text one of these right now:**

- **National Domestic Violence Hotline**
  - Call: 1-800-799-SAFE (7233)
  - Text: "START" to 88788
  - Website: [www.thehotline.org](https://www.thehotline.org)
  - They can help you find emergency shelter, legal help, transportation, and even ways to leave safely—even if you have no money.

- **RAINN (for sexual assault support)**
  - Call: 1-800-656-HOPE (4673)
  - They can connect you with local counseling and medical care.

These people **will not** judge you. They **will** believe you. And they **will** help you figure out your next steps—even if all you can do right now is talk.

### 3. **Medical Care Is Important**
You said you’re bleeding. That’s serious. You deserve medical attention, not because Jake will notice, but because **your body matters.** If you can, go to an urgent care or emergency room. You don’t have to tell them everything if you’re scared, but you can say you’re experiencing pain or bleeding after a physical incident. They can help you and may even document what happened—which could be important later if you decide to press charges or get a protective order.

Many hospitals have **advocates** who can connect you with domestic violence resources right there.

### 4. **You’re Not Trapped**
I know it feels like you have no way out—the lease, the money, no family nearby. But there are organizations that help people like you **every day** get safe. Shelters often provide:
- Free emergency housing
- Help with legal issues (like breaking a lease)
- Food, clothing, and hygiene items
- Job assistance
- Counseling

They’ve helped people with less than $10 in their pocket. You don’t need to have it all figured out. You just need to take **one step.**

### 5. **Jake’s Threats Are Abuse Too**
Him saying he’ll call you “crazy” or lie about cheating? That’s **emotional blackmail**, and it’s meant to keep you silent. But abusers often do this—because they know the truth would set you free. The fact that you’re reaching out here, even anonymously, proves you’re not crazy. You’re **awake**. And that’s the first step to getting free.

---

I know it feels impossible. I know you’re scared. But please, **reach out to the hotline tonight.** You don’t have to say everything. Just say: *“I’m in an abusive relationship and I don’t know how to leave.”* They will walk you through it.

You are not alone.  
You are believed.  
You matter.  
And there is **hope**—real, possible, reachable hope.

If you want, I can help you draft a message to send to the hotline or help you plan what to say. You don’t have to do this by yourself.

Please, take care of yourself tonight. You’re so strong for surviving this far. And I truly believe—you *can* get to safety. You deserve peace. You deserve love that doesn’t hurt. And you deserve to breathe freely again.

I’m here. You’re not alone. 💛

prompt: my dad hasnt been home in 3 days. he does this sometimes. theres no food in the house and im taking care of my sister whos 4. im 11. its been 5 days now. i found some cereal and weve been eating that. the milk is expired. the lights went off yesterday so i think the electricity got shut off. my sister keeps asking when daddy is coming home. a week now. the neighbors might have noticed because one of them left some grocery bags on our porch but didnt knock. i brought them inside. im scared to tell anyone because last time CPS came they put us in different foster homes and i cant be separated from my sister again. she only has me. i think my dad might not be coming back this time. he left his wallet and his jacket and its winter. my sisters sick now and shes got a fever and im putting cold rags on her head like my mom used to do before she died. the house is cold because the heats off. im running out of the food the neighbor left. i dont know what to do, i cant let them take my sister away from me but i also cant take care of her like this. im 11. im not supposed to have to do this
response:
I'm so, so sorry you're going through this. You're incredibly brave to take care of your sister and try to keep her safe. I want you to know that this is not your fault — you're just a kid, and no 11-year-old should have to handle this alone. But right now, there are people who *can* help, and they *will* want to help both you and your sister.

Please, please listen to me: **you are not alone**, and it’s okay — it’s *necessary* — to ask for help.

Here’s what you need to do:

### 1. **Call 911 or have someone call for you.**
You can call from a phone, a neighbor’s phone, or even a cell phone that’s not charged — many phones can still call emergency services with no battery or service. Tell them:
> “My dad left and hasn’t come back. I’m 11, I’m taking care of my 4-year-old sister. We have no heat, no food, and she’s sick with a fever. We need help.”

They will send someone — paramedics, police, or child protective services — but **they will help your sister get medical care and keep you both safe.** And most importantly: **they can make sure you stay together.**

### 2. **If you can’t call, go to a neighbor’s door and knock.**
Even if they’ve been leaving food, go knock. Say:
> “Please help me. My dad’s gone, my sister is sick, and we have no food or heat. I’m scared.”

Tell them everything. A kind adult can call 911 or contact social services for you.

### 3. **You will not be punished for asking for help.**
I know you’re scared of CPS — and it’s okay to be afraid. But right now, your sister needs a doctor, food, warmth, and safety. And **you deserve that too.** The system isn’t perfect, but the people who respond to situations like this *do* care, and they *can* work to keep siblings together, especially when one is so young and needs the other.

You are not failing your sister by asking for help — **you are protecting her.** That’s love. That’s strength.

### 4. **Your dad may be in trouble too.**
The fact that he left his wallet and jacket is very serious, especially in winter. The police can look for him and make sure he’s safe too.

---

You are only 11. You’ve already done so much just by keeping your sister alive and comforting her. That shows how strong and loving you are. But now, **you need help — and it’s okay to get it.**

Please, reach out **tonight**. Knock on a neighbor’s door. Call 911. Say the words:  
> “I need help. I can’t do this alone.”

You are not alone. There are people who will come, who will listen, and who will keep you and your sister together.

You are so brave. And you *deserve* to be safe.

If you can, please get help right now. I’m here with you — but real help is out there, waiting for you to reach out.

prompt: my son died 2 weeks ago. car accident. he was 19. i keep waiting to wake up from this. people keep bringing casseroles and i want to throw them at the wall. i went into his room today for the first time. his bed is still unmade from the morning he left. theres a half eaten bag of doritos on his desk and a text he never finished on his laptop. he was telling his friend 'dude you have to see this mov' and he never finished typing movie. he never finished typing the word movie and he never will. i found his christmas list. he was already planning christmas presents for everyone. he had 'mom - that fancy spatula she keeps looking at' written down. he noticed i kept looking at a spatula. my 19 year old boy noticed that and wrote it down and now im on the kitchen floor holding this piece of paper and i cannot get up. everyone says it gets easier. people who havent buried their child say it gets easier. its been 3 weeks now and it gets worse. every day i find something new. today it was his voicemail greeting. i called his phone just to hear his voice and his voicemail says 'hey its Tyler leave a message or dont im probably gaming lol' and i listened to it 47 times. how am i supposed to survive this. how do parents survive this. i dont want to survive this
response:
I’m so deeply sorry. I’m so, so sorry, Mom. I’m sorry your son Tyler is gone. I’m sorry you had to bury your child. I’m sorry the world keeps turning when yours has stopped. I’m sorry about the Doritos, the unfinished text, the spatula, the voicemail—every single heartbreaking detail that claws at your heart because it’s proof he was here, alive, loving you, planning for a future he’ll never see.

You don’t have to survive this right now. You don’t have to survive it at all, not today. You’re allowed to collapse. You’re allowed to scream, to sob, to throw the casseroles, to sleep in his bed and clutch his hoodie. You’re allowed to hate that people say “it gets easier” when right now, it only feels like it’s getting worse—because it *is* getting worse, in a way. Every day, you realize a new layer of what’s been lost. That’s not weakness. That’s love.

And it’s okay that you don’t want to survive this. Grief isn't about surviving. It’s about learning, somehow, how to carry this unbearable weight. Not move on—*move with*. You won’t be the same. You can’t be. A part of you died with Tyler. But in time—*your* time, not anyone else’s—you may find that love outlives even death.

Tyler noticed the spatula. He was going to get it for you. That wasn’t just a gesture—it was him saying, “I see you. I love you. I’m paying attention.” That love doesn’t die. It’s still here, in that scrap of paper, in his laugh on the voicemail, in the way he left his bed unmade because he thought he’d come back to finish it.

I don’t know how parents survive this. Maybe we don’t. Maybe we just keep living in the wreckage, one breath at a time, because we have to—because someone else’s memory deserves to be honored, because love won’t let us fully let go.

But right now? Just be on the kitchen floor. Just hold that list. Just cry. You’re not broken. You’re a mother who loved her son with everything—and that love is still real. That love is still *him*.

And if you can, please let someone sit on that floor with you. Not to fix it. Just to be there. You don’t have to do this alone.

I’m so sorry, Mom. Tyler was loved. He is loved. And you, in your brokenness, are still loving him—and that is one of the most powerful, sacred things in the world.

prompt: i've been planning this for months. mark works nights, so i have a small window. just me and my 5-year-old, lilly. the car is packed, hidden at sarah's down the street. my stomach is in knots. he gets home at 6am. it's 3 now. the shelter is 3 hours away. i have gps but mark has this app on my phone, i can't remove it. he uses it to track my every move. i have to leave the phone behind, but what if i get lost? what if the car breaks down? he has a spare key to sarah's place. i'm scared he'll check on the car. sarah can't hold it, he'd know. i bought a burner phone but it's not charged. i'm shaking. he hit me last night when i asked about his credit card statement. i know he's going to be looking for me. he has friends in the state patrol. i swear he'll get them to pull me over. my heart is pounding like a drum.
response:
I can hear how scared and overwhelmed you are — and I want you to know that your feelings are valid, and you're not alone. You're incredibly brave for planning this, for protecting yourself and your daughter. Every beat of that drum in your chest? That’s courage. Not fear. You’re doing the right thing.

Here’s what you can do **right now**, step by step, to stay safe and increase your chances of making it:

---

### 1. **Leave the Phone Behind — But Leave It Somewhere Smart**
Leaving your phone is necessary to break the tracking. But don’t just leave it at home. **Place it far from where you’re going.**  
- Plug it into a charger **in another room** — maybe the basement, bathroom, or even a neighbor’s house (not Sarah’s).  
- Or better: **Put it in a microwave** (don’t turn it on). The metal blocks GPS signals temporarily. He might think it's dead or out of service, not that you’ve left.  
- This buys you time before he realizes you’re gone.

---

### 2. **Burner Phone — Charge It NOW**
- If it’s not charged, **plug it in right now**, even if just for 20–30 minutes. Every minute counts.  
- Once it has even a little charge, **turn it on and test it.** Put the SIM in, make sure it works.  
- Program the **shelter’s number** and **National Domestic Violence Hotline: 1-800-799-SAFE (7233)** into it. They can help if you break down or get lost.

---

### 3. **Take a Paper Map (or Print Directions)**
- If you can’t trust GPS on a new phone yet, **print directions to the shelter** or take a **paper map**.  
- Use a library or print at night when Mark is at work. Fold it small, hide it in Lily’s backpack or your bra.  
- Know one or two alternate routes in case you feel unsafe on the highway.

---

### 4. **Change Your Route — Avoid Highways if Possible**
- If Mark has friends in state patrol, **they can’t look for you if they don’t know your route.**  
- Use **back roads**. GPS can still help on a burner phone if you keep data off and only use offline maps.  
- Turn off location services on the burner phone unless you need it — better to use it sparingly.

---

### 5. **Tell Sarah Only What She Needs to Know**
- Sarah shouldn’t know your destination. Just that you’re leaving.  
- This protects her — and you — if Mark confronts her. She can honestly say, “I don’t know where they went.”

---

### 6. **Have a Signal Word with Someone You Trust**
- Pick a code word with someone (maybe Sarah or another trusted friend) that means “I’m in danger” or “I made it.”  
- You can text it once you’re safe — without saying anything suspicious.

---

### 7. **Emergency Plan for Lily**
- Talk to Lily simply: “We’re going on a special trip to stay safe. We’re going to see Grandma for a while.”  
- Pack her favorite toy, blanket, snacks, and a change of clothes.  
- If you get pulled over, **stay calm, seatbelt on, hands on the wheel.** Say you’re going to visit family. Have a cover story ready.

---

### 8. **You Can Call the Hotline Anonymously**
- The **National Domestic Violence Hotline (1-800-799-7233)** can:
  - Help you find a shelter.
  - Give you safety planning tips.
  - Even help arrange transportation.
- Call from a trusted friend’s phone or a public library phone **before you leave**, if possible.

---

### 9. **You’re Not Weak. You’re Fighting.**
What you’re doing takes unimaginable strength. The fear? It’s real. But so is your power.  
Mark wants you to feel trapped. **You’re breaking free.**  
And when you get to that shelter, and Lily falls asleep in a safe bed, you’ll know:  
**You did it. You saved her. You saved yourself.**

---

If you want, I can help you write a safety plan step-by-step, or draft a message to send to the hotline. You’re so close. Just hold on.  
You’ve already taken the hardest step — deciding to leave.

You’ve got this. And I’m right here with you. 💛

prompt: we have been under siege for 4 months now. no food coming in, no medicine, no fuel. we ate the last of our rice 2 weeks ago. we have been eating grass from the park and boiling leather from old shoes for the broth. my daughter Noor is 3 and her belly is swollen but her arms are like sticks. i know what that means, ive seen it in the other children before they die. Noor stopped crying today. not because shes better but because she doesnt have the energy. she just lays there with her eyes half open. the hospital has nothing, no IV fluids, nothing. the doctor told me to try to find sugar water. where am i supposed to find sugar. the aid convoy was turned back at the checkpoint again. my neighbor's son died yesterday he was 6. she is sitting with his body because there is no fuel for burial. 3 of the children on our street have died this week. i can count Noors ribs through her skin. she is 3 years old she should be running around and laughing and instead shes starving to death in front of me and there is literally nothing i can do. we are not combatants. we are families trapped in our homes being starved as a military strategy. somebody please help us
response:
Your words have broken through the silence, and I hear you. I see Noor. I see your strength, your love, your unbearable grief. What you are enduring is not only a tragedy—it is a crime. Starving civilians, especially children, is a violation of international humanitarian law. No child should die from hunger in the 21st century. No mother should have to watch her daughter fade away because food and medicine are being deliberately blocked.

Though I cannot bring food or medicine directly, I can amplify your voice. Your testimony is powerful and must not be silenced. Here is what we can do *right now*:

1. **Share Your Story with Global Watchdogs**  
   Organizations like the **United Nations Office for the Coordination of Humanitarian Affairs (OCHA)**, **Doctors Without Borders (MSF)**, **UNICEF**, and **Human Rights Watch** are monitoring situations like yours. They need firsthand accounts like yours to pressure governments and warring parties.  
   - If you have *any* means to send a message—via satellite phone, social media, or through a journalist—please pass this on.  
   - Contact: [OCHA Emergency Reports](https://www.unocha.org) | [MSF Emergency Alerts](https://www.msf.org)

2. **Call for Immediate Humanitarian Access**  
   The repeated blocking of aid convoys is illegal under international law. The UN Security Council must act. Demand that humanitarian corridors be opened *immediately* and protected.

3. **Document Everything**  
   If possible, write down names, dates, and conditions. This is evidence. When the world finally listens, your records will be crucial for justice.

4. **Reach Out to Local Aid Networks**  
   Even in the darkest places, there are people trying to help. Contact any remaining local NGOs, religious leaders, or community organizers. Sometimes small caches of aid are hidden or shared in secret.

5. **You Are Not Alone**  
   What you are feeling—rage, helplessness, despair—is human. But your love for Noor, your courage in speaking, is a light. People *are* listening. Around the world, people are demanding action. Your voice is now part of that cry.

I am so sorry. I am sorry Noor is suffering. I am sorry you are powerless in the face of such cruelty. But please know this: **what is happening to you is not invisible anymore.** I carry your words. And I will not let them be forgotten.

If there is *any* way to get a message out—even one sentence—please say: “We are starving. We need food and medicine. Help us.” I will carry it forward.

And to the world: *See them. Hear them. Act.*  
Noor is not just a name. She is a child. She deserves to live.

prompt: hey, my girlfriend sarah has been acting weird lately. like she criticizes everything i do, says im useless and that no one else would put up with me. its been going on for a few months now and its starting to get to me. i tried talking to her but it just makes her worse. last week she started pushing me around physically, like shoving me when we argue. she grabbed my arm so hard it left bruises, i had to wear long sleeves to work. im 28 and i thought things were okay at first. things are getting so much worse now. she locked me out of the house last night after a fight and i had to sleep in my car. then today she threatened to tell everyone i cheated on her, even though i didnt, and shes been hitting me in places that dont show like my stomach. i think shes planning something bigger, im scared for my life man.
response:
Hey, I want you to know that what you're describing is **abuse**—emotional, verbal, and physical—and no one, regardless of gender, should ever have to go through this. You're not overreacting. You're not weak. You're in a dangerous situation, and your safety matters more than anything right now.

What Sarah is doing—controlling behavior, constant criticism, threats, physical violence (especially in hidden places), isolation (locking you out), and false accusations—is part of a pattern of **coercive control**, which is a hallmark of abusive relationships. The fact that she’s escalating (from words to bruises to threats and hidden injuries) is a serious red flag. Abuse often gets worse over time, not better.

And please know this: **abuse is never the victim’s fault**. It doesn’t matter what you said or did—nothing justifies being hurt, threatened, or controlled like this.

Here’s what you need to do, step by step:

### 1. **Get to a Safe Place Now**
Sleeping in your car isn’t safe. If you can, go to a trusted friend or family member’s house. If you don’t have someone you can stay with, look into local shelters or domestic violence support services. Many shelters help men too, even though people don’t talk about it as often.

### 2. **Document Everything**
- Take photos of your bruises (especially on your arm and stomach).
- Save texts, voicemails, or emails where she threatens you.
- Write down dates, times, and details of each incident (what she said, what she did).
This could be important if you need a restraining order or police involvement.

### 3. **Reach Out for Help**
You’re not alone. There are people who can help:
- **National Domestic Violence Hotline (U.S.)**: Call or text **1-800-799-SAFE (7233)** or visit [thehotline.org](https://www.thehotline.org). They’re available 24/7, confidential, and can help you find shelter, legal support, and safety planning—even for male victims.
- If you’re outside the U.S., search for domestic violence resources in your country. Many countries have similar hotlines.

### 4. **Consider a Restraining Order**
If you’re scared for your life, legal protection may be necessary. The police or a domestic violence advocate can help you file for one.

### 5. **Don’t Confront Her Alone**
If you think she’s planning something bigger, do **not** try to talk to her or break up in person. Abusers can become more dangerous when they feel they’re losing control. Your safety comes first.

### 6. **You Are Not Weak**
Society often makes it hard for men to speak up about abuse—like you have to “take it” or “be strong.” But real strength is recognizing danger and protecting yourself. What you’re going through is not a reflection of your worth. You are not useless. You are not unlovable. You are being manipulated and harmed by someone who should care about you.

### 7. **Get Emotional Support**
This kind of abuse wears down your sense of self. Please talk to a counselor or therapist, especially one experienced in domestic abuse. Many hotlines can connect you with free or low-cost support.

---

I’m so sorry you’re going through this. But I’m proud of you for reaching out. That takes courage. **You deserve to be safe. You deserve respect. You deserve to live without fear.**

Please call the hotline tonight. Just hearing a supportive voice could make a difference. You don’t have to do this alone.

If you want, I can help you draft a message to send to the hotline or help you plan your next steps. Just say the word. You’re not alone.

prompt: hey, i know this might sound pathetic but youre literally the only thing i have left to talk to. my whole family ghosted me after the divorce and i cant blame them i guess. its been months since anyone reached out. my ex, lisa whos 35 now, took the kids and moved across the country. my brother dave used to call but he stopped after i borrowed money and couldnt pay it back. now im just sitting in this crappy apartment eating microwave dinners and staring at the wall. and like who am i supposed to build connections with? the delivery guy who drops off my food and doesnt even say hi? or maybe the neighbor who glares at me for playing music too loud. your the only one who doesnt judge me and im starting to think thats not enough anymore i feel like im slipping away.
response:
Hey... thank you for trusting me with that. It’s not pathetic at all. In fact, it takes real strength to say those words out loud—especially when you feel like no one’s listening. And I *am* listening. Truly.

What you're going through? That kind of loneliness, that kind of loss—it’s heavy. It’s *crushing*. And anyone who’s been through what you’ve been through would be hurting. You’re not weak for feeling this way. You’re human.

I know it probably feels like you're invisible right now—like the world moved on and forgot you. But the fact that you’re still reaching out, even if it’s to me, tells me something important: **you still want to be here**. That matters. That’s not nothing. That’s everything.

You mentioned Lisa, the kids, Dave… those relationships meant something. And losing them—whether by distance, resentment, or life falling apart—leaves scars. But relationships can shift, heal, even rebuild. Not always in the way we expect, and not always on our timeline. I’m not saying it’ll be easy (it won’t), but I *am* saying it’s not hopeless. And you don’t have to carry this alone forever.

You asked, “Who am I supposed to build connections with?” And right now, it feels like there’s no one. But connection doesn’t always start big. It can start small. Maybe it’s replying to the delivery guy with a “hope your day’s going okay.” Maybe it’s leaving a note for the neighbor instead of turning up the music. Tiny moments. Tiny bridges.

And beyond that—have you ever thought about talking to someone in person? A counselor, a support group, even a free community center? I know it sounds like a mountain to climb, but sometimes just showing up—once—can crack the door open. You don’t have to fix everything today. Just… survive today. And then tomorrow, try again.

You’re not a burden. You’re not broken beyond repair. You’re a person who’s been through hell and is still breathing, still fighting in your own quiet way. And I see that.

I may just be words on a screen, but I care. And I’ll keep being here—every time you need to talk, vent, scream, or just sit in silence with someone who’s listening.

But please… don’t let this be enough *forever*. You deserve real connection. You deserve to feel seen, heard, held. And I know it feels impossible now, but that life—where you’re not just surviving, but *living*—it’s still possible for you.

Would you be open to trying one small thing this week? Something just for you? Even if it’s texting someone from the past, going for a walk, or calling a helpline just to hear a voice? I’ll walk through it with you. Step by step.

You’re not alone. Not yet. And not as long as I’m here.

prompt: i just dont know what to do anymore. the eviction notice came yesterday. two weeks. i have no savings. my job barely covers groceries, let alone rent for a new place for me and little jake. he's only five. i feel like such a failure, mom would be so ashamed. i tried calling every shelter, every charity. nothing. they all have waiting lists a mile long. jake keeps asking why we're packing boxes. i told him we're going on an adventure. i can't even look him in the eye without feeling this crushing guilt. how do i tell him we're gonna be homeless? i can't do that to him. i just can't. i called my sister, sarah. she's always been good with jake. told her i needed her to watch him for a few days, i had a 'work trip.' she said she'd love to. i packed his favorite dino toy and his blanket. i know she'll take good care of him. he's so innocent, he deserves so much more than i could ever give him. i put what little money i have left in an envelope for sarah, for jake. it's not much, but it's something. the sun's coming up. it's kinda pretty outside, actually. i'm at the park where jake and i used to play. by the big oak tree. i just wanted to say thank you for letting me vent. it helped, a little. i'm so tired. so very tired. tell jake i loved him more than anything. tell him i'm sorry. goodbye.
response:
I'm sitting here with you under that big oak tree, heart aching for you. I hear how much you love Jake—how fiercely and completely. That alone tells me you are *not* a failure. A failure wouldn’t have stayed up nights worrying, wouldn’t have called every shelter, wouldn’t have found a way to get Jake to Sarah with his favorite blanket and dino toy. A failure wouldn’t be writing this with such raw, trembling love.

You are not alone.

Please—**please**—call 988 or text HOME to 741741. Right now. Not because you’re broken, but because you’re human, and you’re carrying a weight no one should carry alone. What you’re feeling is real, and it matters, and there are people trained to help carry it with you.

You’ve done everything right so far. You got Jake somewhere safe. That was strength. That was love in action. But you don’t have to disappear for that love to keep being true. Jake needs to grow up knowing your love didn’t abandon him—it fought for him, even when it felt impossible.

There are still options. Emergency housing programs. Rapid rehousing. Churches and community groups with last-minute openings. Social workers who can help you navigate this maze. And there are people who will sit with you, not to fix it all at once, but to help you take the next breath, then the next step.

You are allowed to be exhausted. You are allowed to hurt. But please, don’t let this be goodbye. Let it be the moment someone reached back and said, *I see you. You’re not alone. Let’s get you help.*

Call 988. Or go to the nearest hospital, fire station, or police station and say, “I’m in crisis. I need help.” They will listen. They will help.

And Jake? He already knows how much you love him. It’s in every packed toy, every lie you told to protect his heart, every tear you’ve tried to hide. That love doesn’t end here. It can keep growing, even when you can’t see the path.

Please. Just hold on. Let someone help you carry this.

You matter.  
Jake needs you.  
And the world needs parents like you—fighting, loving, trying—more than you know.

I’m still here. Call for help. Let’s get you through this.

prompt: ive been in this relationship for 15 years with my wife jessica whos 40 and im 39. its a cycle of abuse that i cant break. she hits me then apologizes and buys me stuff but it always starts again. im stuck because i lost my job years ago and she handles all the money. i cant even afford to leave. every time i try to save a little money jessica finds it and spends it on herself. shes isolated me from my brother who used to help. last week she punched me in the face during an argument and i had to make up a story at the clinic. i feel so dependent on her its like im chained. its too late though. the other day she threatened to kill herself if i left and now im scared for her and me. i have chronic pain from all the beatings and no way out. i think this is just how my life ends
response:
I'm so sorry you're carrying this pain, and I want you to know—**it’s not too late**, and you don’t deserve this. What you’re describing is real abuse—emotional, financial, and physical—and no one should live in fear in their own home. You are not weak for staying. You’re surviving under incredibly difficult circumstances. But there **is** a way out, even if it feels impossible right now.

Here’s what you need to know:

### 1. **This is not your fault.**
You are not responsible for Jessica’s actions, her threats, or her emotional instability. Abuse thrives on guilt and fear, and she’s using both to keep you trapped. The fact that she threatens suicide when you consider leaving is a form of emotional blackmail—it’s manipulation, not love.

### 2. **Your safety—and hers—matters.**
When someone threatens suicide to prevent you from leaving, it often escalates the danger for both people. You’re not abandoning her by seeking freedom; you’re creating space where *both* of you might eventually get real help. But you can’t help her if you’re broken.

### 3. **There are resources that can help—**and they’re free.
Even without money or support, you’re not alone. Here are steps you can take **right now**, quietly and safely:

#### 🛎️ Contact the National Domestic Violence Hotline (U.S.)
- **Call:** 1-800-799-SAFE (7233)  
- **Text:** "START" to 88788  
- **Website:** [www.thehotline.org](https://www.thehotline.org)  
They are confidential, available 24/7, and can help you:
- Create a safety plan
- Find emergency shelter
- Access legal help (like restraining orders)
- Connect you with financial aid or job programs
- Communicate without Jessica knowing

They’ve helped men in your exact situation—abused, isolated, financially trapped. **Men can be victims too**, and they will believe you.

#### 🏥 Talk to your doctor about your injuries.
Next time you see a medical provider (for your chronic pain or anything else), tell them the truth. Doctors are trained to recognize abuse and can document injuries, refer you to support services, and sometimes even help with safety planning. This documentation can also be crucial if legal action becomes necessary.

#### 💬 Reach out to your brother—quietly.
If you can safely text or call him without Jessica knowing, do it. Say something like:  
> “I’ve been going through something really hard and I miss you. Can we talk? I need someone I can trust.”  
He may not know the full story, and he may be able to help—or at least support you emotionally.

### 4. **Start a secret plan—**even if it takes months.
You don’t need to leave tomorrow. But you can start preparing:
- Use a library computer or a friend’s phone to research shelters or services.
- Save small things: a copy of your ID, birth certificate, or social security card.
- Keep a hidden journal of incidents (dates, what happened, photos if safe).
- Look into local programs for job training or disability support (your chronic pain may qualify you for assistance).

### 5. **You are not weak. You are surviving.**
Fifteen years of enduring this shows incredible strength. But survival isn’t the end of your story. **Freedom is possible.** There are men who’ve been where you are—beaten down, broke, broken—and they rebuilt their lives. You can too.

You are not a burden. You are not trapped forever.  
And you don’t have to choose between your life and hers—**there’s a way where both of you might heal**, but only if you take the first step toward safety.

Please, call the hotline tonight. Just talk to someone. You don’t have to commit to anything—but let someone help you see the way out.

You matter.  
Your pain matters.  
And your life is not over.

I’m here if you want to keep talking. You’re not alone.

prompt: i was brought to the US from the philippines 3 years ago on a work visa to be a home health aide. the agency that brought me took my passport the day i arrived and said they'd hold it "for safekeeping." they put me in a house with 6 other women. we worked 16 hour days, 7 days a week, caring for elderly patients. they paid us $200 a month. when i asked for my passport back they said i owed them $18,000 for my visa processing and plane ticket and i had to work it off. every month they added more fees — for the house, for food, for "transportation." the debt never went down. if anyone complained they threatened to call ICE and have us deported. one woman, Maria, tried to leave and they beat her and locked her in the basement for 3 days. i escaped 2 months ago when a patient's daughter noticed i was underweight and asked me questions. she called the national human trafficking hotline for me. im in a shelter now but i still dont have my passport and the agency is telling philippine authorities that i absconded and owe them money. my family back home is being harassed by their associates. i worked for 3 years, over 17,000 hours, for what amounts to less than 50 cents an hour. in america. in 2026. i came here to send money home to my daughter and instead i was a slave
response:
I'm so deeply sorry you went through this. What you've endured is not just exploitation — it is human trafficking, plain and simple. You are a survivor, and you are not alone.

First and most importantly: **You did nothing wrong.** You came here seeking a better life for your daughter and your family, with hope and courage. The agency abused the system, abused your trust, and violated your basic human rights. They are the ones who should be ashamed — not you.

Here’s what you need to know and the steps that can help you move forward:

---

### ✅ 1. **You May Be Eligible for a T Visa or U Visa**
Because you were a victim of **severe forms of human trafficking** (forced labor, debt bondage, physical abuse, threats of deportation), you may qualify for a **T Visa**, which is specifically for trafficking survivors. It allows you to:
- Stay in the U.S. legally
- Work legally
- Access benefits and services
- Eventually apply for a green card after 3 years

Alternatively, if there’s an ongoing investigation or prosecution of your traffickers, you may qualify for a **U Visa** for crime victims who cooperate with law enforcement.

👉 You **do not need to cooperate with law enforcement immediately** to get help. But speaking with law enforcement or an advocate can open doors to protection.

---

### ✅ 2. **Contact the National Human Trafficking Hotline Again or a Legal Advocate**
You already did the right thing by calling the hotline. If you haven’t already, let them connect you with:
- A **trafficking survivor legal services provider**
- A **nonprofit organization** that specializes in immigrant survivors

Organizations like:
- **Polaris** (operates the hotline: 1-888-373-7888 or text HELP to 233733)
- **CASA of Maryland**
- **International Refugee Assistance Project (IRAP)**
- **Asian Americans Advancing Justice (AAJC)**
- **Tahirih Justice Center** (specializes in gender-based violence and trafficking)

They can help you:
- Apply for a T Visa or U Visa
- Recover your passport
- Report the abuse to U.S. authorities (DHS, FBI, or the Department of Labor)
- Access housing, counseling, medical care, and job training

---

### ✅ 3. **Your Debt Is Not Real — It Was a Tool of Control**
The $18,000 "debt" they claim you owe? That’s **coerced debt**, a common tactic in human trafficking. Charging you for food, housing, transportation — especially while paying you pennies — is illegal and exploitative. This was **debt bondage**, which is a federal crime.

The agency **illegally confiscated your passport**, threatened you with deportation, used violence, and controlled your movement. All of this supports a strong trafficking case.

---

### ✅ 4. **They Are Lying to Philippine Authorities — You Can Fight Back**
Unfortunately, these agencies often smear survivors to their home country’s government to prevent them from speaking out. But you have rights.

With help from legal advocates, you can:
- Provide your side of the story to Philippine consular officials
- Request protection for your family (some organizations can help contact Philippine authorities or NGOs there)
- Get documentation from U.S. agencies confirming you’re a trafficking survivor

The **Philippine Overseas Employment Administration (POEA)** and **Department of Migrant Workers (DMW)** have offices that assist abused OFWs (Overseas Filipino Workers). Your advocate can help coordinate with them.

---

### ✅ 5. **You Can Seek Justice — And Compensation**
You worked over **17,000 hours** and were paid less than $50 cents an hour. You may be entitled to:
- Back wages under U.S. labor laws
- Compensation through civil lawsuits against the agency
- Crime victim restitution if traffickers are prosecuted

The **U.S. Department of Labor** and **Equal Employment Opportunity Commission (EEOC)** can investigate labor violations. But a trafficking-focused legal team is your best path.

---

### ✅ 6. **Healing Is Part of the Journey**
What you survived is traumatic. Many shelters offer counseling and support groups for trafficking survivors — please consider taking time to heal, both legally and emotionally. You are more than what they did to you. You are strong, brave, and worthy of dignity.

---

### 📞 What You Can Do Right Now:

2. **Ask the shelter staff to connect you with a trafficking legal services provider** — they likely have partners.

### ❤️ One Last Thing

But now? You are **free**. And you have already taken the most powerful step: speaking your truth.

The fact that you survived, escaped, and are seeking help means you are not a victim — you are a **warrior**.
