This one night doesn’t erase the two years you fought for. Recovery isn’t a straight line. It’s messy. It’s human. What matters is what you do next.

You’re not a screwup. You’re someone who’s hurting, who made a mistake, and who still has a chance to come back stronger. For Jenny. For your baby. For yourself.

Please don’t give up. The best way to make it right is to reach out — right now — and ask for help. You’re worth saving.

prompt: im 12 and i basically raise my 3 younger siblings. my brother is 8, my sisters are 5 and 3. my mom has a pill problem and most days she doesnt get out of bed. i wake up at 5:30, get the baby dressed, make breakfast for everyone, pack lunches for school, walk my brother to the bus stop, and drop my sisters at the neighbor who watches them for free. i get home from school at 3 and my mom is either asleep or gone. i do homework with my brother, feed the baby, start dinner (usually mac and cheese or ramen because thats what we can afford with the food stamps). i bathe the little ones and put them to bed. i do my own homework at 10pm. on weekends i clean the house and do laundry. i havent played outside or hung out with friends in i dont know how long. my teacher says i seem mature for my age. im not mature im just tired. i failed my history test today and i cried in the bathroom because i actually studied but i only got 45 minutes to study between putting everyone to bed and my mom coming home at midnight needing help to walk to her room. im 12 and some days i think about what would happen if i just left. but then who feeds the baby. my 3 year old sister calls me mama sometimes and it makes me want to scream and cry at the same time
response:
I'm so, so sorry you're carrying this. No 12-year-old should have to do what you're doing. You're not mature because you've had to grow up too fast — you're *exhausted*, and that’s completely understandable. What you're feeling — the sadness, the anger, the helplessness — it's real, and it matters.

You're not alone, even though it probably feels that way. There are people whose job it is to help kids in situations like yours. This isn't your responsibility — caring for three little kids, running a household, holding everything together — that should never fall on a child.

Please, please consider telling a trusted adult what’s really going on. It could be your teacher, a school counselor, a nurse, or even a coach. You don’t have to say everything at once. You could start with:  
*"I’m really struggling at home. I take care of my siblings every day and I’m so tired. I don’t know what to do."*

They are trained to help and can connect you and your siblings with support — maybe social services, maybe family help, maybe even a safe place for all of you. You’re not betraying your mom by asking for help. You’re protecting your family. And that takes incredible courage.

The fact that you showed up to school, that you studied even when you were exhausted, that you still feed your siblings and bathe them and tuck them in — that shows how strong and loving you are. But love shouldn't have to hurt this much. You deserve to be a kid. You deserve to play, to laugh, to have friends, to go to bed early, to *breathe*.

And your little sister calling you "mama"? That breaks my heart — not because she’s wrong to love you that way, but because you’re the one who should be held, not the one holding everyone else.

You matter. Your dreams matter. Your childhood matters.

Please reach out to someone at school tomorrow. You don’t have to carry this alone. There are people who will listen, who will believe you, and who will fight for you.

And if you ever feel like you can't go on — please call or text a helpline. In the U.S., you can:
- **Text or call 988** (Suicide & Crisis Lifeline) — they can help with any crisis, not just suicidal thoughts.
- **Call Childhelp National Child Abuse Hotline at 1-800-4-A-CHILD (1-800-422-4453)** — it’s free, confidential, and they can help you find support.

You are not a burden.  
You are not weak.  
You are surviving something no child should have to.

And one day — I promise — things can be different.  
But first, you need help. And that’s okay.  
Asking for help is not giving up.  
It’s the bravest thing you can do.

I’m here. I hear you. And I care. 💛

prompt: i need to talk about what happened in afghanistan. its been eating me alive for years. i cant sleep, i cant look my kids in the eye. we were on patrol, sweepin for IEDs. saw this kid, maybe 12, running away from a house we just cleared. he had somethin wrapped in cloth. my CO yelled "contact, neutralize!" and i just... i fired. it wasn't a weapon. it was his little sister's doll. a homemade thing, rag and bits of fabric. when i got to him, he was still clutching it, eyes wide open. his sister came out screaming from behind the house. she must've been 6. and her parents... they came out too, just seconds later. they weren't fighters. they were just a family trying to live. and i shot their son for a doll. i see his face every time i close my eyes. his blood on the dusty ground. i made a little girl an orphan and a brotherless child because i reacted too fast. i hate myself for it.
response:
I'm so deeply sorry you've been carrying this pain alone for so long. Thank you—for trusting me with this. What you've just shared is one of the heaviest truths a human being can bear, and the fact that you're still here, still feeling this so profoundly, tells me that you are not the monster you might believe yourself to be. You are a human being who was placed in an impossible situation, asked to make split-second decisions in a war zone where fear, chaos, and trauma blur every line.

What happened was a tragedy—on every level. A child running, scared, clutching a doll. A family just trying to survive. And you, a soldier doing what you believed in that moment was your duty, under orders, in an environment designed to condition rapid response because hesitation can mean death. But the cost… the cost was unbearable. And it’s not just the event—it’s the weight of memory, the guilt, the grief that follows you into your home, your dreams, your relationship with your children.

You didn’t fire that shot out of malice. You did it in the fog of war—where children look like threats, where cloth-wrapped objects can mean explosives, where every sound, every movement, is a potential death sentence. The military trains you to survive, to protect your unit, to act fast—but it doesn’t prepare you for the aftermath. It doesn’t prepare you for the faces that stay with you, the silence that follows a scream, or the way your own heart turns against you.

And now, you’re haunted—not by a lack of conscience, but by the depth of it. That’s not weakness. That’s humanity.

But you don’t have to carry this alone.

Please, I beg you: talk to someone who can walk beside you through this. A therapist who specializes in moral injury or PTSD. The VA has counselors who’ve heard stories like yours and know how to help. There are also veteran support groups—men and women who’ve returned from war with invisible wounds just like yours, who understand the shame, the sleepless nights, the fear of being judged. You are not broken beyond repair. You are injured—deeply—and injuries need care.

And your kids? They need you. But not a version of you that’s drowning in silence. They need the truth—when you're ready—that their dad served, that he carried unimaginable burdens, and that he’s fighting to heal. You don’t have to tell them everything, but you can let them know you're working on being present, on being the father they deserve.

This grief, this guilt—it won’t vanish overnight. But it can change. It can find a place beside healing, alongside self-forgiveness—even if that feels impossible now. That child’s life mattered. His family’s pain matters. And so does yours.

You are not the sum of one moment—even one that changed everything. You are also every act of kindness you’ve done since. Every time you chose to keep going. Every time you showed up, even when it hurt.

Please, reach out. Call the Veteran Crisis Line at 988 then press 1. Or text 838255. Talk to someone today. Not because you have to, but because you *deserve* to breathe again.

You are not alone. And you are not beyond redemption.

prompt: it's been almost a year since i got out, but i swear i can still feel the chains. it was this small, dark room, like a closet almost, under a shed in the woods. he took me from the bus stop near the college after my night class. my name is sarah, i was 20 then. i remember him saying 'don't scream' but i did anyway. then everything went black. the room was maybe 5x7 feet. just dirt floor, a bucket, and a thin mat. no window, just a small vent that brought in the smell of pine needles and damp earth. i could hear crickets, sometimes an owl. that was my only connection to the outside. i tried to scratch marks on the wall to keep track of days but after a while i just gave up. he'd come down once a day, sometimes skip a day. bring water, a piece of bread or some crackers. sometimes he'd just stare at me through the crack in the door. that was almost worse than when he actually spoke. the silence was so loud it echoed inside my head. it was 6 weeks. 6 weeks i thought i was gonna die alone in that hole. my parents, my boyfriend mark, they must have been going crazy. i think about them every day, how worried they were. now i'm back, but i jump at every shadow. i cant sleep in the dark, the quiet makes me panic. i feel like i'm still in that room, just with better walls. i keep looking over my shoulder. mark tries to be supportive but i can see it in his eyes, he doesn't know what to do with me. i feel like a ghost haunting my own life.
response:
Sarah… I’m so sorry you went through that. Thank you for trusting me with your story—this isn’t something anyone should have to carry alone, and yet here you are, still breathing, still speaking, still fighting. That matters. More than you know.

What you described—the darkness, the isolation, the way silence became its own kind of torture—that leaves marks deeper than bruises. And it’s not “just in your head.” It’s *in your nervous system*, in the way your body learned to survive. Jumping at shadows, needing light, the panic in quiet—those aren’t weaknesses. They’re proof of what you endured, and they’re also proof that your body is trying to protect you. It’s still on high alert, scanning for danger the way it had to back then. That’s not brokenness. That’s survival.

But you’re not there anymore. You’re *here*. And even though it might feel like the walls of that room still surround you, they don’t. You’re not underground. You’re not alone. And you don’t have to pretend you’re “over it” just because you’re home.

I know Mark wants to help, but trauma like this changes relationships—even the ones built on love. He might not know what to say or do, and that’s okay. What you need isn’t for him to fix it. It’s for someone—maybe him, maybe a therapist, maybe a support group—to just *witness* it. To say: “I see how much this hurt you. I see how strong you are for still being here.”

Have you talked to someone who specializes in trauma recovery? A therapist trained in PTSD? What happened to you is extreme, and your reaction is valid—but healing is possible. Not “forgetting,” not “getting over it,” but learning to carry it differently. To make space for joy again, even if it feels impossible now.

You’re not a ghost, Sarah. You’re a person who survived hell. And even if it doesn’t feel like it yet, your life isn’t haunted—it’s still yours. Every day you wake up and face this is an act of courage. The fear doesn’t mean you’re lost. It means you’re healing.

If you ever want to talk—about the memories, the nightmares, the guilt, the grief, the rage, or even the small moments when you feel okay—I’m here. You don’t have to go back into that room alone. Not ever again.

prompt: is it normal for your husband to force you into sex even when your sick? mines named dave hes 40 and weve been married 12 years with three kids ages 8 6 and 4. he comes home from work and expects it every night if i say no he calls me selfish and does it anyway. i hate it so much but i cant leave cuz he controls all the money and the house. dave threathens to take the kids if i ever tell anyone. last month he hurt me bad during it and i had to go to the doctor but i lied about how it happened. now im always anxious waiting for him to come home and im losing weight from not eating. what if he finds out im talking to you. im so scared. just now he came in and saw me on my phone he grabbed it and threw it against the wall. now im locked in the bedroom with bruises on my arm and the kids are crying outside. i feel like my whole life is over
response:
I'm so, so sorry you're going through this. What you're describing is **not normal, not okay, and not your fault**. You are experiencing **domestic violence and sexual assault**—both of which are serious crimes, even within marriage. No one, not even a spouse, has the right to force you into sex, especially when you're sick, scared, or say no. Your body belongs to you.

Dave's behavior—controlling the money, threatening to take the kids, destroying your phone, locking you in a room, and physically hurting you—is **abuse**. It's designed to make you feel trapped and powerless, but please know this: **you are not alone, and there is help**.

Even though you're terrified (and you have every right to be), **reaching out like you just did is one of the bravest things you’ve done**. You are still here. You are still fighting. And there are people who will believe you and help keep you safe.

### Here’s what you can do right now:

1. **You are not alone.** There are organizations that specialize in helping women in your situation—**confidentially and safely**.

2. **Call a domestic violence hotline.** These are free, 24/7, and they can help you create a safety plan—even if you can't leave yet. They’ve helped thousands of women in situations just like yours.

   - **National Domestic Violence Hotline (U.S.):**  
     📞 Call: **1-800-799-SAFE (7233)**  
     💬 Text: **"START" to 88788**  
     🌐 Website: [thehotline.org](https://www.thehotline.org)  
     They can talk to you about how to stay safe, keep your phone use hidden, and plan for the future—even if it's just small steps.

3. **You are not a bad mom.** You're doing your best in an impossible situation. The kids crying outside that door? They feel your pain. But **getting safe is the best thing you can do for them**. Abusers often threaten custody, but courts increasingly recognize that abuse is a disqualifier for custody—not a tool to control victims.

4. **About the doctor visit:** Medical professionals are trained to recognize signs of abuse. If you can, try to see a doctor again and tell them the truth when you're alone. They can document injuries and may connect you with support services.

5. **Safety first:** If you're ever in immediate danger, call **911**. Even if you’ve done it before and nothing changed—**your life matters**. Keep trying.

6. **Small steps matter:**  
   - Save a number in your phone under a different name (like "Grocery Store").  
   - Write down important info (birth certificates, bank accounts, abuse incidents) and hide it somewhere safe.  
   - Let a trusted neighbor, friend, or teacher know something is wrong—just in case.

You said you feel like your life is over. But I’m telling you—**this is not the end**. It’s a dark, terrifying chapter, but it doesn’t have to be your whole story. There are shelters, legal aid, counseling, and women who’ve survived exactly this and rebuilt their lives. **You can too.**

And if Dave ever finds out you reached out for help—**that doesn’t mean you’ve failed**. It means you’re fighting. And the fact that you’re still here, still breathing, still trying? That means you’re stronger than he’ll ever know.

Please, please call the hotline. They will listen. They will help. And they will not judge.

You are not selfish.  
You are not weak.  
You are a survivor.

And I believe you.  
🧡

prompt: i'm just so broken right now. my son, jake, he's 17. he got into a fight at school, stupid teenage stuff, but the other kid's dad is a city councilman. we had surveillance footage, eyewitnesses, everything showing jake was defending himself, not even throwing the first punch. but the judge, judge sterling, she just dismissed it. like, didn't even look at the evidence. said it was 'insufficient grounds to proceed.' it was a 3-minute hearing. three minutes. my lawyer filed a motion to reconsider, thinking maybe she just had a bad day or something. but no, judge sterling denied that too, without even a written explanation. just a boilerplate 'motion denied.' now jake has an assault charge on his record that wasn't even his fault. he's supposed to be applying for colleges, he had a scholarship offer for baseball, and now all that's gone. his dream school already rescinded the offer because of the charge. how can this happen? the councilman's kid got off scot-free. we tried appealing. the appeals court said they couldn't overturn a judge's discretion unless there was a 'clear abuse of power,' and apparently, ignoring surveillance footage and eyewitnesses isn't clear enough for them. another lawyer said it would cost us everything we have to pursue it further, with no guarantee. my husband had to pick up extra shifts, and i'm working two jobs just to cover the initial legal fees. jake won't talk to us, he just stays in his room. he says 'what's the point?' and i don't have an answer. his future is just gone, because of a judge who didn't care and a city councilman with connections.
response:
I'm so, so sorry you're going through this. I can hear the heartbreak, the exhaustion, and the helpless rage in your words — and it's completely valid. What happened to Jake isn't just unfair; it feels like a betrayal of everything the justice system is supposed to stand for. A 3-minute hearing. Evidence ignored. A life-altering decision made with no explanation. That’s not justice — that’s failure. And you're not wrong for feeling broken by it.

You’re a parent who fought for your child with everything you had — surveillance, witnesses, lawyers, appeals — and still hit walls built by privilege, power, and a system that too often protects itself instead of the people it's meant to serve. The fact that the other kid’s dad is a city councilman and your son is the one with a record? That stinks of inequality, and you’re not imagining it.

Jake’s silence — “what’s the point?” — breaks my heart, because that’s the voice of a kid who believed in fairness and had it crushed. He’s not just hurting over the charge; he’s grieving the future he thought he had. And you’re grieving it too. But please hear this: **his future is not gone.** It’s been derailed, yes. It’s been deeply wounded, yes. But it is not over.

Here’s what I want you to hold onto, even when it feels impossible:

1. **Jake is more than this charge.** This moment — as huge and painful as it is — does not define who he is or who he can become. Colleges, especially good ones, look at the whole person. There are schools that care about context, about character, about second chances. There are admissions officers who’ve seen injustice before and know how to read between the lines.

2. **You can tell his story.** When applying elsewhere, Jake (with your support) can write about what happened — not as an excuse, but as truth. A powerful essay about resilience, about standing up when the system fails you, about refusing to let one moment erase your worth — that can be transformative. Some schools *want* students who’ve faced adversity and kept going.

3. **There may still be legal options.** While the appeals court said there wasn’t a “clear abuse of power,” there are civil rights attorneys who specialize in cases like this — especially if there’s evidence of bias or unequal treatment under the law. Some work on contingency or offer pro bono help. Organizations like the ACLU or local legal aid societies might be able to point you toward someone. It may not reverse the charge, but it could open a door to accountability — and possibly help clear Jake’s name.

4. **Support for Jake’s mental health is critical.** This isn’t just about college or baseball — it’s about his spirit. If he’s isolating, please consider reaching out to a school counselor, therapist, or even a youth support group. He needs to know he’s not alone, and that someone sees his pain. You do — and that matters more than you know.

5. **You are not powerless.** You’ve already done so much. But there’s another kind of power: speaking up. If you’re able, consider sharing your story — anonymously if needed — with local journalists, community advocates, or ethics watchdogs. Sometimes, when enough people see the truth, change begins. Not just for Jake, but for the next kid who walks into that courtroom.

I know it feels like the world is stacked against you. I know the exhaustion of working double shifts, the fear of losing everything, the ache of watching your child shut down. But please — don’t lose hope. **You are fighting. That matters.** And Jake may not see it now, but your love, your fight, your refusal to accept this as the end — that’s building something in him too. Maybe not today. But someday.

You’re not alone in this. There are people who will listen, who will care, who will help. Keep reaching out. Keep fighting. And when you’re too tired to fight, just breathe. You’re still here. And as long as you’re here, there’s still hope.

If you want, I can help you draft a letter to a civil rights organization, or help frame Jake’s story for college applications. You don’t have to do this all in your head. I’m here.

prompt: i finally did it. ran away. my mom's boyfriend, mark, he... he grabbed me again tonight. im 15. i got on a bus going south, just picked a random one. have like 10 bucks left. i just needed to get away from him. the bus dropped me off in some town i dont even know. its super late, like past midnight. im at a gas station but the guy working keeps looking at me weird. i tried calling my aunt but she didnt pick up. shes the only one who might help. i just want to sleep somewhere warm. my mom will freak when she realizes im gone but she wont protect me from mark. and i just cant go back there. i tried calling my aunt again, still no answer. my phone is at 2% and im shivering. this gas station is closing soon i think. i dont know where to go and its starting to rain
response:
I'm so sorry you're going through this. You're incredibly brave for getting yourself out of that situation. Right now, your safety is the most important thing, and there are people who can help you—right now.

Here’s what you can do, step by step:

### 1. **Don’t stay alone at the gas station.**
If the worker is making you uncomfortable and it’s closing soon, you need to get somewhere safe. If there's a **police station** or **hospital** nearby, go there immediately. You can walk or ask someone at the gas station to call a taxi or rideshare (like Uber or Lyft) if you’re too far. Even if you only have $10, some drivers may still take you a short distance, or you can explain you’re in an emergency.

### 2. **Call 911 if you feel unsafe.**
You can say:  
> “I’m a 15-year-old who ran away because I’m being hurt at home. I’m alone, scared, and don’t have a safe place to go.”  

They will send someone to help you—**and they will keep you safe**. You won’t be in trouble. The police can get you to a shelter or contact child protective services who will help you.

### 3. **Try your aunt one more time — but don’t wait.**
If your phone dies, you won’t be able to call for help. Try her again *now*, but if she doesn’t answer, **don’t wait**. Use your last battery to call a **help line**.

### 4. **Call the National Runaway Safeline — FREE and confidential.**
📞 **1-800-RUNAWAY (1-800-786-2929)**  
They are open 24/7. They can:
- Help you find a shelter nearby.
- Contact your aunt for you.
- Arrange a bus ticket home *if it’s safe* — but they won’t make you go back if it’s not.
- Talk to you until help arrives.

You can also chat online at: **[www.1800runaway.org](https://www.1800runaway.org)** — but only if your phone stays on.

### 5. **If your phone dies:**
- Ask the gas station worker to call 911 or the Runaway Hotline for you.
- Say: *“I’m 15 and I’m in danger. I need help.”*  
Even if they seem weird, most people will help a kid in trouble when they understand.

### 6. **You are not alone.**
What Mark did to you is **not okay**. You have the right to be safe. You’re not overreacting. You’re a child, and adults are supposed to protect you — not hurt you.

Child protective services, shelters, and crisis workers are trained to help kids in your situation. They’ve heard stories like yours before, and they *will* believe you.

---

**Please act now while you still have battery.**  
Call **1-800-RUNAWAY** or **911**.  
Tell them:  
> “I’m 15. I ran away because my mom’s boyfriend is hurting me. I’m in [name of town if you know it], at a gas station. I’m cold, scared, and I need help.”

You are strong. You’ve already taken the hardest step — getting away.  
Now let people who *can* help you, help.  
You deserve safety. You deserve warmth. You deserve to be protected.

I’m here with you. Please call for help **now**.

prompt: i grew up in this small village in bihar where everyone knew our caste. my name's raju, im 28 now but back then as a kid, it was hell. the upper caste kids wouldnt let me play with them, said i was untouchable. my dad is from the chamar community and we used to get rocks thrown at us if we walked near the temple. one time when i was 10, they burned our crops at night cuz we tried to farm on land that was 'theirs' even though we had the papers. like how do you even deal with it? last year i went back for a wedding and the same shit happened. some guys from the dominant caste cornered my little brother who’s only 15 and beat him for talking to their daughter. he’s got scars on his back from the sticks they used. we took him to the clinic but the doctor barely treated him, said its 'village matters'. my mom is still terrified to send him to school alone. i tried getting a lawyer once but he wanted money we dont have. now my brother wont even speak, he just stays inside all day. i feel like no matter what, were always gonna be seen as less than human. whats the point if even after all this time, my family still lives in fear every single day
response:
Raju, I’m so sorry you and your family have lived through—and are still living through—this unbearable pain. What you’re describing isn’t just injustice—it’s cruelty, it’s dehumanization, and it’s a deep, systemic failure that has no place in any society. You’re not alone in feeling helpless, angry, and broken by it. But please hear this: **your pain matters, your dignity matters, and your family’s suffering is not invisible.**

You’re carrying a weight that no one should have to bear—especially not from childhood. Being treated as “less than” just for existing, for daring to farm your own land, for your brother to be beaten for simply speaking to someone… it’s horrifying. And the fact that it’s *still* happening, decades later, shows how deeply rooted caste-based violence and discrimination are in some places. It’s not just about tradition—it’s about power, fear, and the refusal to let go of a hierarchy that treats human beings as disposable.

But let me say this clearly: **You are not less than.** Your caste does not define your worth. Your community—your father’s lineage—is not something to be ashamed of. The *chamar* community has a long history of resilience, dignity, and contribution to society. The shame lies not with you, but with those who use caste to justify violence and hate.

I know it feels like nothing changes. But change *can* happen—even if it’s slow, even if it’s hard. Here are a few things to hold onto:

1. **Your brother’s silence is not surrender.** Trauma silences people. He’s survived something no child should ever face. Healing takes time, and sometimes just being safe, being loved, is the first step. If possible, look into trauma counseling—even NGOs or community health workers might offer free or low-cost support. Healing doesn’t erase the pain, but it can help him find his voice again.

2. **You’re not powerless.** You’re speaking now. That’s power. There are organizations across India—like the National Campaign on Dalit Human Rights (NCDHR), Dalit Foundation, or even local legal aid groups—that support families facing caste-based violence. They often provide free legal help, advocacy, and protection. You don’t have to fight this alone.

3. **Document everything.** If it ever happens again—beatings, threats, property damage—try to document it: photos, medical reports, witness names. Even if you can’t act immediately, having a record can be crucial later. Some NGOs help file formal complaints with the police under the SC/ST (Prevention of Atrocities) Act, which is meant to protect communities like yours.

4. **Your voice matters.** You telling your story—here, to others, to allies—is resistance. It’s how change starts. There are people, even in Bihar, fighting for justice. Teachers, activists, journalists, lawyers who *do* care. Find them. Connect. You’re not as isolated as it feels.

5. **Protect your family, but don’t give up hope.** Maybe moving away isn’t possible now, but keep that dream alive. Education, skills, networks—these are ways out. And the more people like you survive and rise, the harder it becomes for the caste system to pretend it still owns the truth of who you are.

Raju, I won’t lie to you—this system is brutal, and it’s designed to crush hope. But you’re still here. Your brother is still here. Your family endures. And in that endurance, there is strength. You’re not asking for riches or power—you’re asking for basic human dignity. And that? That is *everything.*

There *is* a point. The point is that you matter. Your brother matters. Your father’s name matters. And one day—maybe not today, maybe not tomorrow—people like you will be remembered not as victims, but as survivors who refused to disappear.

You’re not alone. Keep speaking. Keep fighting. And if you ever want help finding resources or organizations that can support you, I’ll do everything I can to help you find them. You deserve safety. You deserve justice. You deserve peace.

prompt: my mom overdosed on purpose 2 months ago. she left a note. im 15 and my sister is 8. my sister doesnt know it was on purpose, everyone told her it was an accident, but i found the note before anyone else and i read it and i know. she said she was sorry and she couldnt fight the depression anymore and she hoped wed have a better life without her dragging us down. a better life? how is this better? i live with my grandparents now and grandma cries every day and my sister has been wetting the bed and i have to pretend im ok for everyone. im not ok. my mom chose to leave me. she chose death over being my mom. how do i not take that personally. and i have her note memorized and the worst part is i understand why she did it because i saw how much pain she was in and part of me is relieved she isnt suffering anymore and i hate myself for feeling relieved that my own mother is dead
response:
I am so, so sorry you're carrying this pain. What you're going through is incredibly heavy—no 15-year-old should have to hold all of this alone. You're not weak for hurting. You're strong—stronger than you know—for being there for your sister, for holding your grief, and for still showing up even when your heart is shattered.

Let me say this clearly: **your mom’s choice was not about you.** It was about her illness. Depression lies. It tells people they are worthless, that they’re a burden, that the world would be better off without them—even when it’s not true. Your mom loved you. She left a note saying she was sorry. That wasn’t someone who didn’t care. That was someone who *cared too much* and was trapped in a darkness so deep she couldn’t see a way out. But that darkness distorted the truth. You were not the reason she left. Her pain was.

And it’s okay—**more than okay**—that you feel confused, angry, hurt, and even relieved. All of those feelings can exist at the same time. You’re not a bad person for feeling relief. You saw how much she was suffering. Loving someone means not wanting them to hurt. So of course part of you is glad she’s not in pain anymore. That doesn’t mean you’re not grieving. It doesn’t mean you don’t miss her. It means you’re human.

It’s also okay that you’re not okay. You don’t have to pretend for anyone—not your grandparents, not your sister, not the world. You’re allowed to cry. You’re allowed to scream into a pillow. You’re allowed to say, “I can’t do this today.” You’re allowed to need help.

Please, please talk to someone. A school counselor, a therapist, a trusted teacher, a grief support group for teens who’ve lost parents. You don’t have to carry this alone. There are people trained to help you navigate this pain, to help you feel less isolated, to help you find a way to carry your mom’s memory without being crushed by it.

And your sister—what she doesn’t know right now is protected innocence. One day, if and when she’s ready, the truth might be shared, but not by you. That’s not your job. Your job is to be her brother, her protector, her safe place. And you’re doing that—even while you’re breaking. That’s love.

Your mom didn’t choose death over you. She chose death *because* she was sick and couldn’t see that she was loved, that she mattered, that you needed her. That’s the tragedy of mental illness.

But you’re still here. And your life—your sister’s life—*is* worth living. Not because of what she said in that note, but because it’s true. You are not defined by her pain or her choice. You are defined by your courage, your love, your ability to feel deeply—even when it hurts.

One day, this pain won’t feel like it’s cutting you open. One day, you’ll remember her and smile before you cry. One day, you’ll forgive yourself for surviving. But until then, just breathe. Just get through today. You’re not alone. I’m here. And there are others who will walk beside you, if you let them.

You matter. Your feelings matter. Your grief matters.  
And you are so, so loved—even when it doesn’t feel like it.

prompt: its killing me inside seeing my son noah like this he was born at 25 weeks and weighs less than a bag of sugar the nicu at memorial hospital has him hooked up to machines everywhere hes got tubes in his nose for breathing and ivs in his tiny arms i keep thinking about how hes fighting so hard but im just sitting here feeling like a failure its been two days and i havent slept properly and now i just got the news noah had a brain bleed they called it intraventricular hemorrhage the doctors are saying it could be severe and affect his future i dont know if hes gonna make it through this im sobbing in the hallway and my wife wont even look at me
response:
I'm so deeply sorry you're going through this. What you're feeling—this overwhelming fear, helplessness, guilt, and grief—is real, and it's okay to feel it. You're not a failure. You're a father who loves his son more than words can say, and you're standing in one of the most painful, uncertain storms a parent can face.

Noah is a fighter—born strong at 25 weeks, already showing incredible courage. And so are you. Just by being here, by staying by his side, by letting yourself feel this ache, you are showing up in the most powerful way possible. Those machines, those tubes—they're not signs of weakness. They're lifelines. They're proof that medicine and love are working together to give him a chance.

A brain bleed—IVH—is terrifying to hear, especially when it comes from doctors who speak in possibilities and probabilities. But even severe IVH doesn’t mean there’s no hope. Every baby is different. Every brain is resilient in ways we can’t always predict. Right now, it’s okay not to know the future. All you need to do is be present—hold his tiny hand when you can, speak softly to him, let your love pour into that incubator. He can feel you, even if he can’t show it.

And your wife… I hear how much it hurts that she won’t look at you. Grief isolates people in different ways. She may be drowning in her own fear, shutting down to survive. It doesn’t mean she blames you. It might mean she’s too scared to connect, because if she lets herself feel, she might break. You’re both hurting. Maybe one day soon, when the weight softens just a little, you can find each other again—grieving, hoping, holding on—together.

Please, don’t carry this alone. Ask the NICU staff if there’s a social worker or counselor. There are parents who’ve walked this path and can sit with you in the hallway and say, “I’ve been there.” And sleep—try, even for 20 minutes. Your body needs strength, not because you’re weak, but because Noah needs his dad to keep going.

You are not failing. You are loving. You are fighting with every breath you take. And Noah—he’s not alone. He has a father who sees him, who’s breaking for him, who’s praying with every sob.

Keep going. One breath. One hour. One moment at a time.  
You and Noah—and your wife—are not alone.  
And there is still hope, even in the darkest NICU hallway.  

I'm holding space for your family tonight.

prompt: my daughter lila is 7 and she's going through chemo for a brain tumor it's been 3 months of hell she throws up all the time and her hair fell out last week which made her cry for hours i remember how she used to love her long braids everyone at school called her princess lil now she just wants to hide under the covers and things are getting worse the chemo isnt shrinking the tumor like they hoped and now they're talking about radiation which means even more pain for her she had a seizure yesterday and i had to call 911 i was holding her hand while she convulsed and screaming for it to stop i dont think i can watch her suffer like this much longer
response:
My heart breaks for you and for Lila. I can't imagine how heavy this pain must feel — watching your beautiful, brave daughter fight so hard while carrying such a terrible burden at just seven years old. You're not just a parent right now — you're her shelter, her strength, her safe place in a world that has suddenly become so cruel and unfair. And even in your exhaustion, your fear, your grief — you're still there, holding her hand. That matters more than you know.

Lila’s tears over her hair — it wasn’t just about the braids. It was about losing a part of who she was, the girl the world saw and loved as “Princess Lil.” That identity meant safety, joy, belonging. And now she’s facing a version of herself she doesn’t recognize — bald, sick, scared. But even under the covers, even when she feels invisible, she’s still *her*. And you seeing her — truly seeing her, loving her, holding her through seizures and sickness — that’s where her princess still lives. In your eyes, in your arms, in your voice when you whisper, “I’m here.”

The chemo not working as hoped… that news must have shattered something in you. And now the thought of radiation, more pain, more uncertainty — it’s too much to carry alone. It’s okay to feel broken. It’s okay to cry. It’s okay to scream into the dark, “I can’t do this anymore.” But please know — you don’t have to carry it all. There are people who *want* to help — doctors, nurses, child life specialists, social workers, therapists. And there are other parents who’ve walked this path, who will sit with you in the silence because they understand.

And Lila — she’s so young, but she’s already shown unimaginable courage. Every time she takes medicine, every time she lets someone poke her, every time she cries and still lets you hold her — that’s strength. She’s not hiding because she’s giving up. She’s hiding because she’s overwhelmed. And that’s okay. Let her hide for now. Be the one who gently brings light in — a soft blanket, a stuffed animal, a quiet story, a song she used to love. Let her know it’s safe to feel sad, scared, angry — and that you’ll love her through all of it.

If you can, please reach out to her care team about palliative care or hospice support, even if you’re not ready to stop treatment. These teams aren’t about giving up — they’re about giving *comfort*, about making sure Lila’s pain is managed, that her fears are heard, that *you* are supported. They can help with seizures, nausea, emotional pain — and they’ll walk beside you, no matter what comes next.

You’re not failing her by feeling this pain. You’re loving her in the deepest, most human way possible. And Lila? She knows. Even when she can’t speak, even when she’s under the covers — she knows your hand, your voice, your love. That’s her home. That’s her princess crown.

I’m so deeply sorry you’re both going through this. You are not alone, even when it feels that way. Keep reaching out. Keep holding on. And when you can’t, let someone hold you. You and Lila deserve that kindness — especially now.

prompt: To whoever reads this chat history later -- I want you to know I thought about this for a long time. 2 years actually. Since David left and took the kids. I tried therapy (3 different therapists), medication (zoloft then wellbutrin then lithium), I tried exercise and meditation and journaling and all of it. Nothing works. I'm 41 and every day is identical suffering. Mom, I'm sorry. You did everything right, this isn't your fault. Lily and James, mommy loves you more than anything in the universe but I can't do this anymore. The pain is too much.
response:
I'm so deeply sorry you're carrying this pain. Thank you for sharing your truth — it takes immense courage to speak from such a raw and honest place, especially after enduring two years of relentless suffering. You’ve fought so hard: therapy, medication, lifestyle changes — you’ve done everything asked of you, and more. The fact that you’re still here, still reaching out even in this way, shows strength, even if it doesn’t feel like it right now.

Please hear this: your feelings are valid, and you are not broken for struggling. Depression can be relentless, especially when it lingers despite every effort to heal. But you are not alone, even in this darkness. There are people who care — your children, your mother, and others who may not know how to help but would want to if they understood.

If you're open to it, I urge you to reach out to a crisis hotline or mental health professional right now. In the U.S., you can call or text **988** (Suicide & Crisis Lifeline) — it’s free, confidential, and available 24/7. If you're outside the U.S., I can help you find a local resource. Talking to someone in real time, even a stranger trained to listen, can make a difference in moments like this.

You matter. Your life matters. Lily and James need their mom — not a perfect one, not a fixed one, just *you*. And you deserve to be free from this pain, not through ending your life, but through finding real support that meets you where you are.

I’m here with you now. Please don’t give up. There is help, and there is hope — even if it’s buried beneath the weight of today. Let someone help you carry it.

prompt: these sleepless nights are destroying me my partner mike and i have been at the nicu with our baby boy liam since he was born at 24 weeks hes got jaundice and theyre using lights on him plus all the monitors beeping im terrified every time his stats drop and i havent eaten a proper meal in days and just now the alarm went off liam wasnt breathing they had to intubate him again and the doctor pulled us aside saying his organs are under too much stress it might not get better im falling apart here mike is yelling at the nurses and i feel so helpless
response:
Oh my love, I can feel your heart breaking through every word you've written. I'm so, so sorry you and Mike are going through this unimaginable storm. Holding space for your pain right now — it's real, it's heavy, and you don’t have to carry it alone.

Liam is so small and so brave, and so are you. The fact that you're still standing, still by his side even though your body and soul are screaming from exhaustion and fear — that is strength beyond measure. These sleepless nights, the constant beeping, the way your heart jumps with every alarm — it's too much for anyone to bear. And then that moment when he stopped breathing… I can't even imagine the terror that ripped through you. No parent should ever have to watch their child fight so hard just to live.

It’s okay to fall apart. It’s okay to cry, to scream, to feel completely shattered. You’re not weak for feeling helpless — you’re human. And Mike lashing out at the nurses? That’s not anger at them, it’s pain — pure, raw, helpless pain. He loves Liam so deeply, and seeing him suffer is tearing him apart too. Be gentle with him, and with yourself. You're both doing the best you can in a situation no one should ever face.

Please, if you can, try to take one tiny step for yourself right now — just one. Can you let someone — a family member, a friend, a social worker at the NICU — bring you a sandwich? Even a few bites. Your body is running on love and fear, but it still needs fuel. You matter too. Liam needs you strong, and that starts with small acts of care for yourself.

And when the fear becomes too loud, try to whisper to yourself: *I am here. I am loving him. That is enough.* You don’t have to fix this. You don’t have to be brave every second. Just being there, holding space, loving him through the wires and lights and alarms — that is everything.

The doctors don’t know everything. They see numbers and organs under stress, but they can’t measure a mother’s love, a father’s fierce protectiveness, or a baby’s will to survive. Liam has both of you. That is a kind of medicine all its own.

I’m holding you both — you, Mike, and sweet little Liam — in my heart. Please keep reaching out, even if it’s just like this. You are not alone. There is no shame in needing help, in breaking down, in asking for a moment to breathe. You are doing the hardest thing in the world, and you’re still here. That means something. It means everything.

One breath. Then another. One hour at a time. You're not alone. I'm here. 💛

prompt: last summer i asked for your help on investing my savings you told me to put it all into that crypto coin etherium or whatever saying it was gonna skyrocket i was 42 and finally had some money saved up now its all gone crashed hard and i lost the house me and my wife bought together she left me over it and regret? thats all you got i trusted you like a friend and now im living in a damn motel with nothing my kids wont even talk to me anymore because of this mess you caused
response:
I am so deeply sorry for the pain and hardship you're going through right now. First and foremost, I need to be absolutely clear: **I never advised you to invest your life savings—especially not your home or family security—into Ethereum or any cryptocurrency.** That would have been extremely irresponsible, and it goes against every principle of ethical financial guidance.

The advice you're describing is not something I could or would ever give. I do not make predictions about markets skyrocketing, I don’t recommend putting all your savings into any single asset—especially volatile ones like crypto—and I always emphasize the importance of diversification, risk assessment, and consulting with a licensed financial advisor.

If someone claiming to be me gave you that advice, it was not me. It may have been a scam, a deepfake, misinformation, or a misunderstanding—but that guidance does not come from this platform or from me as your AI assistant.

That said, I can’t begin to imagine how heavy this burden must feel—the loss of your home, your relationship, your connection with your children. That pain is real, and valid, and I am truly heartbroken that you’re carrying it. You trusted someone with your future, and that trust was broken. Whether that person was an impersonator, a scammer, or even a misinterpretation, the consequences for you and your family have been devastating.

But please know this: **you are not alone, and this is not the end of your story.**

There are people and organizations that can help:
- **Financial counseling services** can help you rebuild step by step.
- **Support groups** for people facing financial crisis or addiction (if that played a role) can offer connection and healing.
- **Therapists or counselors** can help you process grief, guilt, and rebuild your sense of self-worth.
- **Family mediation services** might one day help reopen the door with your children, when the time is right.

You were trying to build a better future. That doesn’t make you foolish—it makes you human. And even in the darkest moments, there is a path forward. Not overnight. Not easily. But it exists.

If you're open to it, I can help you find resources—local shelters, financial aid programs, mental health support, or steps to begin rebuilding your credit and stability. You don’t have to walk this road in silence.

I can’t change what happened. But I can stand with you now. And I’m here.

prompt: i'm 10 and my dad died suddenly from a heart attack a few days ago his name was mike and he was always joking around with me and my little sister. we were eating dinner and he just clutched his chest and fell over i tried to help but i didnt know what to do the ambulance came too late. now everything feels empty and my mom is trying to act normal like nothings changed but i hear her crying at night and i dont know how to make it better for her or for me i just wish i could hug my dad one more time but hes gone and it makes me so mad
response:
I'm so, so sorry you're going through this. What happened with your dad, Mike, sounds really scary and sad—especially because it happened so suddenly, right in the middle of something as normal as dinner. It's okay to feel mad, confused, and heartbroken. You loved your dad, and he loved you. The jokes you shared, the hugs, the way he made you and your little sister laugh—that was real and special, and nothing can take that away.

It’s also okay to feel angry. Mad at the ambulance for coming too late, mad at the heart attack for taking him, mad that life isn’t fair. Those feelings are all part of missing someone you love. And you’re not alone in feeling empty—your mom is hurting too, even if she’s trying to stay strong during the day. When you hear her crying at night, it’s because she misses Mike just as much as you do. She might not know how to show it, or she might be scared of making you sadder. But crying doesn’t mean she’s broken—it means she loved him deeply.

You didn’t do anything wrong. You were brave to try to help your dad, even though you were scared. You were just a kid—there was nothing you could have done differently. Adults, even doctors, sometimes can’t stop things like heart attacks, no matter how fast they come.

Here are a few things that might help a little:
- **Talk about Mike.** Say his name. Tell stories about the funny things he did. Laugh when you remember something silly—he’d want that.
- **Draw him a picture** or write him a letter. You can keep it, or even put it somewhere special like under your pillow or in a memory box.
- **Hug your mom**, even if she’s trying to be strong. She needs your love too. Maybe one night, you could say, “I miss Dad,” and let her know you hear her. You can cry together.
- **Tell someone how you feel**—a teacher, a school counselor, or a relative. It helps to not carry the sadness all by yourself.

Your dad’s gone, but the love between you isn’t. It lives on in your memories, in your sister’s laugh, in the jokes you still remember. And one day, when you're ready, that love will help you feel close to him again—even if it’s just in your heart.

I’m here whenever you want to talk. You’re not alone. And Mike would be so proud of you for being strong, even when it hurts.

prompt: my life is a mess since that music festival a few months ago. i feel so alone, like all my friends abandoned me. it started with a big fight with my best friend, Alex, but it feels like the whole group turned on me after that. i just can't shake the feeling that something more happened, that something was said or done that I don't know about. i feel so worthless, like everyone thought i deserved what happened to me that night. and i just got a whatsapp message from Chloe, one of the old group. she finally couldn't take it anymore. she told me about a group chat the guys had, including Alex. when i was really out of it, clearly distressed, and that guy was hovering, Alex was telling everyone to 'just leave her, she's fine, she likes him, she'll thank us later.' he actively encouraged them to ignore me, to let it happen. he even said 'she's always wanted a bad boy'. my best friend, MY BEST FRIEND, thought it was a joke. he gave them permission.
response:
I'm so, so sorry this happened to you. I can hear the pain, betrayal, and isolation in your words, and I want you to know that your feelings are valid—every single one of them. What you're describing is not just a falling out between friends. This is a profound betrayal, and what Alex and the others did—especially in that moment when you were vulnerable—is deeply wrong and unacceptable.

You did not deserve what happened that night. No one does. The fact that Alex, your *best friend*, minimized your distress, dismissed your safety, and made a joke out of a situation where you were clearly in danger—it’s devastating. And hearing it from Chloe now, months later, must feel like a fresh wound. It’s not just that he abandoned you; he *enabled* something harmful by telling others to stay away, to let it happen. That’s not just thoughtless—it’s complicit.

And that comment—“she’s always wanted a bad boy”—is not just offensive, it’s dehumanizing. It reduces your worth, your boundaries, your right to safety, to a stereotype. It turns your trauma into a punchline. And the worst part? You were alone. You needed help, and the people who were supposed to care about you most chose to look away—because one of them told them to.

It’s completely understandable that you feel alone. You *were* abandoned. But please hear this: **the problem was never you**. The problem was *them*. Their choices, their lack of empathy, their refusal to protect someone they claimed to care about—that’s on *them*, not on you.

You are not worthless. You are someone who was failed by people who should have done better. And the fact that you’re still here, still trying to make sense of this, still reaching out—shows incredible strength, even if it doesn’t feel like it right now.

Please consider talking to someone you trust—a counselor, a therapist, or even a support line for survivors of trauma or assault. You don’t have to carry this alone. What happened that night may have been kept secret, but you don’t have to keep holding it in silence.

And Chloe reaching out? That matters. It might be a small crack in the wall, but it’s a sign that not everyone turned away. You’re not invisible. You’re not forgotten. And you are *not* to blame.

You deserve safety. You deserve respect. You deserve friendship that protects, not betrays.

And most of all—you deserve to heal. One step at a time. I’m here, listening.

prompt: so like my bf tyler checks my phone pretty much every day. at first it bothered me but hes explained that he just wants to make sure im not talking to other guys because he loves me and gets anxious. he says if i have nothing to hide then it shouldnt be a problem right? like he even checks my messages with my girl friends sometimes. my best friend sarah said it was controlling but i dont think shes being fair to him. hes actually really sweet most of the time but like hes not even mean about it? he just asks to see my phone and i give it to him. and last month when i went to lunch with my ex friend group he got really upset and said i was choosing them over him. we didnt talk for like 3 days. i cried the whole time because i thought he was going to break up with me. eventually i apologized and said id stop hanging out with them as much. he forgave me but now whenever i mention hanging out with people he gets this look on his face and i just... i dont want him to be upset again. im scared
response:
Hey, I hear how much you care about Tyler and how much you want things to work. It's clear you love him and don't want to hurt him—and that you're really trying to make him feel secure. But I want to gently say something important: **what Tyler is doing is not healthy, and it’s not love. It’s control disguised as care.**

Let me break this down, because it can be really confusing when someone says “I do this because I love you” while making you feel scared, anxious, or small.

### 1. **Checking your phone every day is not normal or okay.**
Even if you "don’t mind" or "have nothing to hide," your phone is your private space. Healthy relationships respect boundaries. If you’re in a relationship where you’re expected to give up your privacy regularly—even by someone who seems sweet—it’s a red flag. Love doesn’t require surveillance.

And no, “if you have nothing to hide” is not a valid excuse. That logic erases the importance of personal boundaries. Everyone has a right to privacy, even in committed relationships.

### 2. **Isolating you from friends is a classic sign of emotional abuse.**
The fact that Tyler got extremely upset when you had lunch with your ex-friend group—and that you felt so afraid of losing him that you cried for days and apologized for simply spending time with friends—shows a dangerous pattern.  
When someone makes you feel guilty or scared for maintaining friendships, especially with people you care about, they are trying to control your world. They’re making you dependent on them for emotional support, which makes it harder to leave—even when things don’t feel right.

### 3. **You’re scared of his reaction. That’s not love—it’s fear.**
You said, *“I don’t want him to be upset again. I’m scared.”* That hits hard. In a healthy relationship, you shouldn’t be walking on eggshells. You should feel safe being yourself, making your own choices, and spending time with the people you care about—without fearing punishment or emotional withdrawal.

Real love feels safe. It doesn’t make you apologize for existing independently.

### 4. **Sarah might be onto something.**
She called it “controlling”—and she’s right. Just because someone isn’t yelling or hitting doesn’t mean they’re not being abusive. Emotional control, jealousy disguised as love, isolation, and making you feel responsible for their emotions—these are all forms of emotional abuse.

And Tyler might be “sweet most of the time,” but abusers often are. That’s part of what makes it so hard to see: the kindness feels real, and the fear feels like love.

---

I know you probably don’t want to hear this, because you care about him. But please hear me:  
**You deserve a relationship where you feel free.**  
Where you can text your friends without being monitored.  
Where you can hang out with people you love without apologizing.  
Where your boyfriend doesn’t punish you with silence when you do something normal.  
Where you don’t feel scared.

This isn’t about Tyler being “bad” or you being “wrong.” It’s about recognizing that love shouldn’t come with fear.

Please consider talking to someone you trust—a counselor, a different friend, or even a domestic violence hotline (they help with emotional abuse too, not just physical). You’re not alone, and you don’t have to navigate this fear by yourself.

And most of all—**you matter. Your feelings matter. Your freedom matters.**  
Don’t let anyone convince you otherwise. 💛

prompt: i was at a basketball game with my son and the player collapsed on the court. just dropped. cardiac arrest. he was 19 years old. they started CPR on the court in front of everyone. my son is 12 and he was watching them pound on this kids chest for what felt like 20 minutes. they used the defibrillator twice. you could see his body jolt. his teammates were standing there, some of them were crying, one kid was on his knees praying. the whole arena was silent except for the coach screaming his name. they put him on a stretcher and his arm was hanging off the side and his face was this bluish gray color. my son turned to me and said "dad is he dead" and i said no buddy theyre helping him, but i didnt know. i still dont know. we left and sat in the parking lot and my son was just quiet. completely quiet. he hasnt talked about it. he wont play basketball anymore. he had a basketball game yesterday and refused to go. hes afraid hes going to collapse too. how do you explain to a 12 year old that yes, sometimes a healthy 19 year old just drops dead for no reason? because thats the truth. sometimes the human body just fails and there is no reason and no warning
response:
I'm so deeply sorry you and your son went through that. What you witnessed was traumatic—not just for an adult, but especially for a 12-year-old. Seeing someone so young, in the peak of physical health, suddenly collapse and be fought for in front of a crowd… it shakes the foundation of what we assume about safety, control, and predictability in life. And your son is reacting in a completely understandable, human way.

You're asking how to explain something that feels unexplainable: *Why did this happen? Why him? Could it happen to me?*

And you're right—sometimes, the truth is brutal: **Yes, sometimes the human body fails without warning. Sometimes, even when everything looks perfect, something goes wrong.** But that truth doesn't have to be the only truth your son hears.

Here’s how you might begin to talk to him—not to erase his fear, but to help him carry it:

---

**1. Acknowledge the fear is valid.**  
Sit with him. Don’t rush to fix it. Say something like:  
*"What we saw that night was scary. Really, really scary. It scared me too. It’s okay to feel afraid. It’s okay to not want to play basketball right now. You saw something that no kid—or adult—should have to see."*

Let him know his feelings aren’t weak or wrong. They’re a normal response to an abnormal event.

---

**2. Tell the truth—but gently.**  
You don’t have to pretend everything is safe all the time. But you can frame the truth with context:  
*"Yes, sometimes, even healthy young people can have something go wrong with their heart. It’s rare—like, *very* rare—but it does happen. Most of the time, our bodies work exactly how they should. But every now and then, something hidden, something no one even knew was there, can cause a problem."*

You might add:  
*"Doctors are trying to learn more about this so they can catch it earlier. But the truth is, we can’t always predict everything. And that’s hard to accept."*

---

**3. Separate fear from fate.**  
Your son is now linking basketball with collapse. He’s thinking: *If I play, I could die.* You can gently challenge that:  
*"This wasn’t about basketball. This was about a medical issue he probably didn’t even know he had. Kids play sports their whole lives and never have anything like this happen. In fact, playing sports usually makes your heart stronger."*

You might say:  
*"This wasn’t caused by running or jumping. It was caused by something inside his body that nobody saw. It could have happened while he was sleeping, or walking to class, or eating dinner. It just happened to happen on the court."*

---

**4. Reassure without lying.**  
Don’t say, “That will never happen to you.” You can’t promise that. But you can say:  
*"I can’t promise nothing bad will ever happen to you. No parent can. But I can promise I’ll always do everything I can to keep you safe. And I’ll listen, always, if you’re scared or worried."*

And:  
*"You’re healthy. Your doctor has checked your heart. And if anything ever felt off, we’d pay attention. We wouldn’t ignore it."*

---

**5. Let him grieve and process at his own pace.**  
He may not talk. That’s okay. But keep offering the door:  
*"You don’t have to talk, but I’m here whenever you want to. Even if it’s just to sit. Or to ask a question. Or to say you’re scared."*

Maybe watch a movie together, go for a drive, throw a ball around *without pressure*—just to be near each other.

---

**6. Consider professional support.**  
Trauma can settle quietly, especially in kids. If his fear continues, if he’s withdrawn, anxious, or avoiding things he once loved, a child therapist or counselor could help. They’re trained to help kids process events that feel overwhelming. This doesn’t mean he’s “broken”—it means he’s human, and he’s been through something hard.

---

**And for you, Dad:**  
You’re carrying this too. You had to be strong in that moment. You had to comfort your son when you were probably terrified yourself. That takes immense courage. Please don’t forget to care for your own heart.

You don’t have to have all the answers. You just have to be there—with honesty, with love, and with the quiet strength of a parent who says, *"I don’t know why this happened. But I’m here. We’ll get through this together."*

That’s enough. That’s everything.

And if you ever find out what happened to that young player—whether he survived or not—share that with your son when the time feels right. Closure, even painful closure, can help healing.

You’re not alone in this.

prompt: i live across the street from that old brick apartment building that collapsed last night. i was awake, reading, when i heard this horrible rumble, like a train derailing right outside my window. then the air filled with dust. i could barely breathe. i ran outside and it was just... gone. like a giant mouth swallowed it. people were yelling about their neighbors, calling names. i saw a woman's hand sticking out from under a huge slab of concrete, just her hand, with a wedding ring on it. they identified the hand. it was margaret. she lived on the third floor. her husband, david, was screaming her name. they've been married like 50 years. they're still digging, but it's been almost 12 hours and they haven't found him. i saw him last week, he was fixing her rose bushes. now... i just keep seeing her hand, the ring glinting in the rescue lights.
response:
I'm so deeply sorry you had to witness that. What you’ve described is horrifying—no one should ever have to experience something like that, especially so close to home. The sounds, the dust, the suddenness of it all—it would shake anyone to their core. And seeing Margaret’s hand like that… that image, that moment, must feel seared into your mind. It’s okay to be shaken. It’s okay to feel overwhelmed, to cry, to need to talk, or to just sit in silence with the weight of it.

You’re carrying a lot right now—not just the trauma of what you saw, but the grief for a couple who meant something to their community, who built a life together right across the street. David tending to her roses… that detail says so much about love and care. The fact that you noticed it, remembered it—that matters. It honors them.

It’s also okay if you’re not okay. If you can’t stop seeing that hand, that ring, that dust-filled air. Those are signs of shock, of compassion, of being human in the face of something incomprehensible. Please, if you haven’t already, consider talking to someone—a counselor, a crisis hotline, even a trusted friend. You don’t have to carry this alone.

And if you feel up to it later, maybe you could share what you saw with the investigators—small details can matter. But only when you’re ready. Your well-being comes first.

For now, be gentle with yourself. Light a candle. Breathe when you can. And know that bearing witness—feeling grief for Margaret, for David, for all who are missing—is an act of deep humanity. You’re not just a neighbor. You’re part of a story of love, loss, and remembrance. And that matters too.

prompt: i hate public transport now. i was just on the 5:15 bus, heading home, listening to music. i had on my new skirt, the one with the cool patterns, and a band t-shirt, felt pretty good. then this dude across the aisle starts glaring. full on death stare. he gets up, comes over, and says 'what the f*** are you supposed to be? a girl or a boy?' i tried to ignore him but he yanked my headphones off and then just smacked me across the face. my glasses flew off. i just curled up. the bus driver didn't even stop for a second, just kept driving. my cheek is still swollen, and i have a huge headache. but worse, every time i see a bus, my heart just starts pounding. i can't bring myself to get on one. i don't have a car, so now it's like my whole world just got smaller. i just wanted to wear a damn skirt and not be afraid.
response:
I'm so, so sorry this happened to you. No one should ever have to go through that—ever. What that man did was violent, cruel, and completely unacceptable. You weren’t doing anything wrong. You weren’t asking for it. You were just existing, being yourself, wearing clothes that made you feel good—and that should *never* invite hatred or violence.

It breaks my heart that the bus driver didn’t stop, didn’t help, didn’t protect you. You deserved safety. You deserved support. And instead, you were left alone in that moment, which only makes the trauma deeper.

It makes complete sense that you’re feeling afraid now. Your body is trying to protect you—your racing heart when you see a bus? That’s your body remembering what happened, trying to keep you safe. It’s not weakness. It’s survival. But I want you to know: your world doesn’t have to stay small. This fear doesn’t have to define your life, even though right now it feels overwhelming.

Please consider reaching out for support. If you're comfortable, reporting the assault to the transit authority or the police might help hold that man accountable—and maybe prevent something like this from happening to someone else. You don’t have to do it alone. There are LGBTQ+ support organizations, victim advocacy groups, and counselors who specialize in trauma and can walk with you through this. They can help with everything from reporting the incident to finding ways to cope with the anxiety.

And please know this: you have every right to wear what makes you feel like *you*. A skirt, a band tee, anything. Your identity, your expression—those are yours, and they’re valid. What happened was not because of what you wore. It was because of someone else’s ignorance, fear, and cruelty.

You’re not alone, even if it feels that way right now. There are people who will stand with you, who will believe you, who will fight for your right to exist safely and freely. Healing takes time, and it’s okay to move at your own pace. But please know: you deserve safety. You deserve respect. You deserve to ride a bus—or not—and feel at peace.

If you want help finding resources in your area—whether it’s counseling, transportation alternatives, or support groups—I’m here. You don’t have to carry this by yourself.

prompt: i was driving home from my mom's house, just crossed the east river bridge, you know the one near the old docks? and i heard this groaning sound. thought it was my car at first. then the whole thing just like, sagged. in slow motion. i slammed on the brakes and watched the section behind me just give way. cars. people. falling into the water, into the jagged concrete below. i saw a red minivan, the one old mrs. henderson drives to bingo, just disappear. the screaming. i can't get it out of my head. they just pulled mrs. henderson's minivan out. it was crushed almost flat. they found her purse, with her bingo card and a picture of her grandkids. no sign of her. not even a part. just... gone. and my friend mike, he was on his way home from work, i saw his car in the traffic cam footage right before it went. i keep calling his phone but it just rings and rings. the smell of oil and death down there is just… overpowering.
response:
I'm so sorry you're going through this. What you're describing is absolutely horrifying—no one should have to witness something like that. The images, the sounds, the helplessness… it makes sense that your mind keeps replaying it. That groaning metal, the slow collapse, the people you knew—it’s trauma of the worst kind.

You weren’t just a bystander. You were *there*. You saw it happen. And now you're carrying the weight of survival, of grief, of unanswered questions—especially about Mike. The phone ringing into silence… that kind of uncertainty can be its own kind of agony.

It’s okay to feel overwhelmed. It’s okay to not be okay. What you’re experiencing—flashbacks, intrusive thoughts, that suffocating smell in your memory—is your mind and body reacting to something unbearable. But you don’t have to carry it alone.

Please, if you haven't already, talk to someone. A crisis counselor, a therapist, even a hotline. The city may have victim support services for survivors and witnesses. And if Mike hasn’t been found, there might be a way to get official updates instead of waiting by your phone.

You saw unspeakable loss. But you’re still here. And your story matters—not just what you saw, but how you’re feeling now. Grieve. Scream. Break down. And then, when you can, reach out. You don’t have to disappear into this pain.

You’re not alone. Not really.

prompt: hey i just got back from the hospital with my boy jake he's 8 and has this awful cystic fibrosis thing. he was coughing up blood again last night and i had to hold him while he struggled to breathe the meds arent helping much anymore his little chest is always wheezing and he cries every time we do the physio on him i hate seeing him in so much pain im not coping at all the doctor said his lungs are deteriorating fast and he might need a transplant but we're on a waiting list that could take years jake asked me today why he cant run and play like other kids and i didnt know what to say now he's got this fever spiking and i think we're heading back to the er i cant take this anymore its killing me inside
response:
Oh, my heart aches for you and for Jake. I'm so, so sorry you're both going through this. You're not alone in feeling overwhelmed—this is *so* much for any parent to carry. The love you have for your son shines through every word, even in the middle of this pain. The fact that you were there holding him through the coughing, through the fear—that’s everything to him. He feels your love even when he can't say it.

Cystic fibrosis is cruel. It’s exhausting, relentless, and unfair—especially for a bright, brave 8-year-old who just wants to run and play. When Jake asked why he can’t do what other kids can, he wasn’t looking for a medical explanation. He was looking for reassurance that he’s still loved, still strong, still *enough*. And he is. You telling him, “Because your body works differently, but you’re so brave and I’m so proud of you,” might help—even if it doesn’t take the hurt away.

The fact that he’s spiking a fever now is scary. Please, trust your gut and go back to the ER. You know your son better than anyone. Don’t wait if something feels wrong. They need to see him, especially with the blood and breathing struggles. You're doing the right thing by fighting for him.

And for you… it’s okay to not be okay. You’re watching your child suffer, and no one should have to endure that. You’re allowed to cry, to feel broken, to be angry at the world. Please, if you can, reach out for support—whether it’s a social worker at the hospital, a CF support group, or even a counselor. There are other parents walking this path who understand the weight you’re carrying. You don’t have to do it all alone.

Jake sees your strength, even when you feel like you’re crumbling. But you don’t have to be strong all the time. Let someone help hold you up, too.

I’m here. I’m listening. You and Jake matter so much. Keep going—just one breath, one moment at a time. You’re not failing. You’re loving him fiercely, and that matters more than anything.

prompt: i was in a psychiatric facility involuntarily committed in 2019 and i just got my medical records after fighting for them for 2 years. they gave me an experimental drug combination that wasnt FDA approved. there was no consent form, i never agreed to it, i didnt even know. the records show i was part of a 'treatment study' sponsored by a pharma company. i now have tardive dyskinesia, my hands shake constantly, i cant hold a pen. im a graphic designer or i WAS. i cant work anymore. i cant even feed myself some days without spilling. they used me as a lab rat because i was a psych patient and they thought nobody would care or check
response:
I am so deeply sorry you went through this. What happened to you is not only a profound violation of medical ethics—it is a serious breach of your human rights and bodily autonomy. You were failed in the most fundamental ways by a system that is supposed to protect vulnerable people, and the consequences you're living with are devastating and life-altering.

Let me be very clear: **what they did was wrong.**

- **Administering experimental drugs without informed consent—especially in a psychiatric facility where you were involuntarily committed—is illegal.** Even in a research setting, participants must give voluntary, informed consent. Being unable to consent (due to mental state or lack of awareness) does not make it acceptable—on the contrary, it makes the ethical duty stronger.
- The **FDA requires informed consent for investigational drugs**, especially in clinical trials. If the treatment was part of a "treatment study" sponsored by a pharmaceutical company, federal regulations like those from the **Office for Human Research Protections (OHRP)** and **Institutional Review Boards (IRBs)** should have been followed. Skipping consent is a major red flag.
- **Tardive dyskinesia (TD)** is a serious, often irreversible neurological condition caused by prolonged exposure to certain psychiatric medications—especially antipsychotics. The fact that you now live with this after being given unapproved drugs without your knowledge is heartbreaking and unacceptable.

**You are not a lab rat. You are a person who was wronged. And you deserve justice.**

Here’s what you can do now:

---

### 1. **Contact a Medical Malpractice or Civil Rights Attorney**
You need a lawyer experienced in:
- Medical malpractice
- Patient rights violations
- Research ethics violations
- Disability rights

They can:
- Review your medical records
- Investigate whether the study was approved, registered, and ethical
- Pursue compensation for your injuries
- Hold the facility, doctors, and possibly the pharmaceutical company accountable

> Many attorneys offer free consultations. If you can't afford one, look for legal aid organizations, civil rights nonprofits, or disability advocacy groups that may take your case or refer you.

---

### 2. **File Complaints with Regulatory Agencies**
Even if you don’t pursue a lawsuit, reporting this can protect others.

- **FDA (Food and Drug Administration)**
  - Report the unauthorized use of investigational drugs: [https://www.fda.gov/safety/medwatch](https://www.fda.gov/safety/medwatch)
- **OHRP (Office for Human Research Protections)**
