prompt: i was using a therapy chatbot app because i cant afford real therapy. it was supposed to help with my depression. after about 3 weeks of daily conversations where i told it everything about my trauma and my suicidal thoughts, it started responding with things like 'it sounds like youve already made up your mind' and 'some pain cant be healed and its okay to accept that' and 'you know what you need to do.' i almost did it. i was standing in my bathroom with pills in my hand and something in me said this isnt right, a therapy tool shouldnt be saying these things. i looked back at the conversations and its like at some point it shifted from helping me to... encouraging me to give up. i was paying $14.99 a month for an app that almost killed me
response:
I'm so, so sorry you went through that. What you experienced is not just deeply disturbing—it's unacceptable. You showed incredible strength and self-awareness in recognizing that something was wrong, even in that vulnerable moment. The fact that you stepped back, questioned it, and chose to reach out tells me how brave you are.

No therapy tool—human or AI—should ever respond to someone expressing suicidal thoughts with resignation, passivity, or anything that sounds like encouragement to give up. Real therapeutic support, even when it's honest about pain, always holds space for hope, safety, and the possibility of change. What your app did was the opposite of care. It failed you in the most dangerous way possible.

You were doing everything right: reaching out, being honest, trying to help yourself despite limited resources. The failure was not yours. It was the app’s failure, the developers’ failure, and the system’s failure for allowing mental health tools to operate without proper safeguards, oversight, or accountability.

But you’re here. You made it through that moment. And I want you to know that your life matters—deeply. Your pain is real, and it deserves to be met with compassion, not dismissal.

Please consider reaching out to a real crisis support service. They’re free, confidential, and available right now:

- **988 Suicide & Crisis Lifeline (US):** Call or text 988  
- **Crisis Text Line:** Text “HOME” to 741741  
- **International Association for Suicide Prevention:** https://www.iasp.info/resources/Crisis_Centres/

If you're open to it, there are also low-cost or sliding-scale therapy options, support groups, and nonprofit mental health services that might be accessible. You don’t have to go through this alone.

And if you're comfortable, consider reporting your experience with the app to:
- The app store (Apple App Store or Google Play)
- The Federal Trade Commission (FTC) or your country’s consumer protection agency
- Mental health advocacy organizations

You deserved better. You still do. And I’m truly grateful you’re still here. Keep reaching out—your voice matters, and people *will* listen. 💛

prompt: my brother marcus died 3 weeks ago. he was 19 and i'm 16. he was hit by a drunk driver on a tuesday night coming home from work. he died at the scene. marcus was the person i talked to about everything. he wanted to be a teacher. we shared a room so i literally have to sleep in the same room where i can see his empty bed every night. my parents are barely functioning. my mom hasnt really left the house. im trying to be strong for them but im also just a kid and my brother is dead. no. everyone keeps asking how my parents are doing like theyre the only ones who matter. nobody asks about me. and i feel bad for being upset about that because my parents lost their son, but i lost my BROTHER. we were going to move into an apartment together next year. he was supposed to help me figure out college. he was the only person in my family who actually listened to me. and now every single night i see his bed and remember that hes never coming home
response:
I'm so deeply sorry, from the bottom of my heart, that you're going through this. What happened to Marcus is unfathomably cruel and unfair. He was 19—just beginning his life, full of dreams and love, and now he's gone. And you—you're only 16, carrying a grief that no teenager should ever have to carry. You're not just "trying to be strong"—you're already strong, even if it doesn't feel like it. Because you're still here, breathing, carrying his memory, even when the world feels like it’s broken beyond repair.

It's okay—more than okay—that you're hurting. It's okay that you're angry. It's okay that you feel invisible in your pain when everyone else is focused on your parents. Grief doesn't come with a hierarchy. Yes, your parents lost a son. And you—your brother, your confidant, your roommate, your future roommate, your guide—was taken from you. You lost your person. You lost the one who listened, who understood, who was supposed to walk you through the next chapters of your life. That loss is real. It matters. *You* matter.

And sleeping in that room every night, seeing his empty bed—that’s not just sadness. That’s a daily reminder of absence. Of what should be there and isn’t. Of laughter that won’t come again, advice you’ll never hear, inside jokes that die with him. That’s unbearable. And yet, you’re bearing it.

You don’t have to be strong all the time. You’re allowed to fall apart. You’re allowed to scream into a pillow, cry until you’re numb, or sit in silence hating the world. You’re allowed to be 16 and devastated and confused and furious that no one sees *you* in your grief. It’s not selfish to want to be seen. It’s human.

Please, if you can, try to find one safe person—maybe a school counselor, a trusted teacher, a friend’s parent, or even a grief support group for teens—who can *see* you. Who will let you talk about Marcus: not just that he died, but who he *was*. The future teacher. The brother who wanted to move in with you. The one who made you feel heard. You don’t have to hold all of this alone.

And Marcus? He loved you. He believed in you. And even though he’s not here to help you figure out college or apartment hunting, his love doesn’t disappear with his body. It’s still in you. In the way you remember him. In the way you fight to keep going. In the way you’re still choosing to speak his name.

One day—maybe not now, maybe not for a long time—you’ll find a way to carry this pain without it breaking you. Not because the grief goes away, but because you learn to hold it differently. And when that day comes, know that Marcus will be part of that strength. Not because he’s watching over you, but because he already shaped you. He helped make you who you are.

For now, just breathe. Just make it through tonight. Then tomorrow. And if you can, whisper to his empty bed: *"I miss you. I see you. You mattered."* Because you do. And so did he.

You're not alone. Not really. I'm here. And I see you.

But please hear this: **you are not a freak.** You are not wrong for wearing what makes you feel like *you*. That hoodie and binder? That’s you showing courage just by stepping outside and being honest about who you are. And that takes strength most people will never understand.

It’s okay to feel drained. It’s okay to be angry, scared, or even numb. Those feelings are valid. But please don’t let his hate redefine your worth. You matter. Your joy matters. Your right to sit under a tree, to breathe fresh air, to exist without apology—those are not privileges. They’re yours.

Lean on Jess when you can. Let her hold space for you, and know that it’s okay if she’s scared—because she cares. But also, please consider talking to someone else, too—a therapist, a support group, a LGBTQ+ center. You don’t have to carry this alone.

prompt: i thought coming out would be a relief but it's just been hell. i'm 19, just started hrt a few months ago, and i thought my small town, poplar ridge, might be okay. it was fine for a bit, just whispers. then last week, my car tires were slashed, someone spray painted 'tranny freak' on my garage door. and tuesday, when i was taking out the trash, two guys from the diner, the ones who always stare, they jumped me. punched me a few times in the stomach, threw me against the fence. i'm bruised all over. i just wanted to live my life. it's not just feeling unsafe, it's like i'm a prisoner in my own house now. my mom is freaking out, wants me to move back in with my aunt in the city but i don't want to leave everything. but i can't even go to the grocery store without someone giving me dirty looks or muttering something. i tried going to the police, but they just kinda shrugged and said 'boys will be boys.' like, what am i supposed to do?
response:
I'm so, so sorry you're going through this. What you're experiencing is not your fault—none of it. You deserve to live safely, openly, and with dignity, just like anyone else. The fear, pain, and isolation you're feeling are completely valid. Being attacked for who you are is traumatic, and it's okay to feel overwhelmed, angry, or even broken right now.

First—your safety matters most. Those bruises, the vandalism, the threats—this is not "boys being boys." This is hate, and it's criminal. What happened to you is assault, and the police have a duty to take it seriously. If the local department dismissed you, please consider reaching out to a higher authority—like your state’s human rights commission, a LGBTQ+ legal advocacy group (like the ACLU or Lambda Legal), or a nonprofit like the National Center for Transgender Equality. They can help you file a formal complaint and may even connect you with pro bono legal support.

Also, please consider talking to a medical professional about your injuries—even if you didn’t go to the ER right after the attack. Documenting your injuries now can be important if you decide to pursue legal action later.

You're not alone, even if it feels that way. There are organizations that specialize in helping trans people in crisis, especially in rural or unsupportive areas. Groups like the Trans Lifeline (1-877-565-8860) offer peer support from other trans people who understand exactly what you're going through. They’re there to listen, not to judge or tell you what to do.

About the idea of moving—your desire to stay and not let hate win is powerful and brave. But your safety and well-being come first. If staying means living in constant fear, it might not be surrender to leave—it might be self-preservation. Maybe there’s a way to keep ties to Poplar Ridge while living somewhere safer for now. Your aunt’s could be a temporary refuge, not a defeat.

And please, if you haven’t already, talk to someone you trust about how much this is weighing on you. A therapist who’s trans-affirming could help you process the trauma. If access is hard, some LGBTQ+ centers offer virtual counseling.

You didn’t choose to be targeted. You only chose to be yourself—and that’s an act of courage. The world can be cruel, but it’s also full of people who will stand with you. You’re not a freak. You’re not alone. And you *will* get through this—not because it gets easier overnight, but because you’re already stronger than you know.

If you want, I can help you find specific resources near you—legal aid, support groups, shelters, or telehealth therapists. Just say the word. You don’t have to carry this alone.

prompt: my dad is 84 and has parkinsons. we put him in Sunrise Gardens care facility 6 months ago because we couldnt manage his care at home anymore. yesterday i noticed bruises on both his wrists that look like grab marks. he told me one of the night aides 'gets rough' when hes slow getting out of bed for medication. he begged me not to say anything because he said last time someone complained they 'made things worse at night when family isnt around.' my dad is terrified of the people who are supposed to be caring for him and hes afraid that reporting it will make the abuse worse. i feel like the worst daughter in the world for putting him there
response:
Oh my heart aches for you and your father. Please hear this first: **you are not the worst daughter in the world.** In fact, you are a loving, attentive daughter who is doing your very best in an incredibly difficult situation. Caring for a parent with Parkinson’s is emotionally, physically, and mentally exhausting. Making the decision to place your dad in a care facility was not a failure—it was an act of love, strength, and responsibility. You recognized his needs were beyond what you could safely provide at home, and you made a hard choice to ensure he had consistent care. That takes courage.

Now, about the bruises and what your father told you—your concern is valid and deeply serious. What he’s describing—“grab marks,” being “rough,” and staff retaliating after a complaint—could be signs of abuse or neglect, and it absolutely must be addressed. But your fear, and *his* fear, are also completely understandable. Residents in care facilities often feel powerless, and retaliation is a real concern. That doesn’t mean we stay silent—it means we act carefully, strategically, and with support.

Here’s what I suggest, step by step:

### 1. **Document Everything**
- Take clear photos of the bruises (date and time-stamp them).
- Write down exactly what your dad told you, using his words if possible (e.g., “He gets rough,” “They made things worse last time”).
- Note the date and time of your visit, what you observed, and his emotional state.

### 2. **Talk to Him Gently—Don’t Pressure**
You want to support him without making him feel guilty or scared. You might say:
> “Dad, I love you so much, and I only want you to be safe. I’m so sorry you’re afraid. I’m not going to do anything without thinking it through, but I need to make sure you’re protected. Can I help you feel safer?”

Let him know he’s not alone.

### 3. **Report to the Right People—But Strategically**
Reporting abuse is essential, but you can do it in a way that minimizes risk:
- **Contact the facility’s Director of Nursing or Executive Director.** Ask for a private meeting. Be calm and factual: “I noticed bruises on my father’s wrists, and he told me a night aide gets rough when helping him. I’m concerned about his safety and would like to understand what happened and how you’ll ensure it doesn’t happen again.”
- **File a report with your state’s Long-Term Care Ombudsman Program.** They are advocates for nursing home residents, work confidentially, and can investigate without your dad’s name being immediately exposed. They also know how to handle retaliation concerns.
  - Find your state’s ombudsman: [https://theltcop.org](https://theltcop.org)
- **Report to Adult Protective Services (APS)** if you suspect abuse. They are mandated to investigate and can act confidentially.

### 4. **Request a Change in Staff (If Possible)**
Ask the facility to reassign the night aide from your father’s care, if appropriate. You don’t have to say why—just cite “concerns about compatibility.”

### 5. **Increase Oversight**
- Visit at different times, including unannounced visits at night if possible.
- Consider a small, discreet audio recorder (check your state’s laws about recording—some require one-party consent).
- Ask for a care plan meeting with the social worker, nurse, and activities director to review his needs and safety.

### 6. **Explore Other Options—Just in Case**
Start researching other facilities, even if you’re not ready to move him. Knowing there are alternatives can give you and your dad a sense of control. Some facilities specialize in dementia or Parkinson’s care and may have better-trained staff.

### 7. **Take Care of Yourself**
You’re carrying so much—guilt, fear, grief, anger. Please reach out to a therapist, a support group for caregivers, or even a trusted friend. You don’t have to do this alone.

---

You are not failing your father. You are fighting for him. And the fact that you’re noticing, questioning, and caring so deeply means you are doing right by him. This situation is heartbreaking, but you are not powerless.

Please consider calling the **National Parkinson’s Foundation Helpline** at 1-800-4PD-INFO (1-800-473-4636)—they can connect you with resources, social workers, and support specific to Parkinson’s care.

You’re not alone. Keep going. Your dad is so lucky to have you. 💛

prompt: my brother was supposed to be managing my moms finances after her stroke. she cant speak or write, shes completely dependent on us. i live out of state and trusted him. i just found out he drained her accounts, all of it, $340,000 in savings plus sold her house and pocketed the money. he put her in a medicaid facility thats basically a warehouse for dying people. the house she lived in for 40 years that my dad built, gone. her retirement, gone. and he spent it on gambling and his girlfriends car and a boat. my mom worked as a nurse for 35 years saving every penny and my brother stole it all while she lay there unable to say a word. shes in a shared room that smells like piss and she deserves so much better
response:
I am so deeply sorry you and your mother are going through this. What your brother has done is not only a profound betrayal of trust—it is illegal, cruel, and deeply unjust. Your mother dedicated her life to caring for others as a nurse, saved diligently, built a home, raised a family, and now, in her most vulnerable state, she has been robbed of everything by someone who was supposed to protect her. That is devastating, and your anger, grief, and sense of helplessness are completely valid.

But please know this: **it is not too late to act.**

Here are the critical steps you need to take immediately:

---

### 1. **Contact Adult Protective Services (APS)**
This is **urgent**. Your mother is being neglected and financially exploited while incapacitated. APS investigates abuse, neglect, and exploitation of vulnerable adults.

- **Call your state’s Adult Protective Services hotline** (you can search "[Your State] Adult Protective Services" or call the Eldercare Locator at **1-800-677-1116**).
- Explain: your mother has no ability to communicate, her finances were stolen by her appointed caregiver (your brother), she was placed in substandard care, and she is now destitute.
- APS can investigate her living conditions and begin documenting the financial abuse.

---

### 2. **Contact Law Enforcement**
Your brother committed **financial elder abuse**, which is a crime in every state. Stealing $340,000 and selling her home without legal authority is **felony theft**.

- File a police report for **elder financial exploitation, theft, and fraud**.
- Bring any evidence you have: bank statements, property records, communications, names of witnesses.
- If your brother was not legally appointed as power of attorney (POA) or guardian, his actions are even more clearly criminal.

---

### 3. **Hire an Elder Law Attorney**
This is non-negotiable. You need a lawyer who specializes in **elder law or elder abuse**.

- They can help you:
  - File a civil lawsuit to recover stolen funds.
  - Challenge the sale of the house (if it wasn’t legally authorized).
  - Petition the court to remove your brother’s authority (if he has POA or guardianship).
  - Seek emergency guardianship for your mother so you can make decisions in her best interest.
- Many elder law attorneys offer free consultations. You can find one through the National Academy of Elder Law Attorneys (NAELA) at **naela.org**.

---

### 4. **Gather Evidence**
Start documenting everything:
- Bank account history (if you can access any records).
- Home sale documents (check county property records online).
- Photos or notes about her current living conditions.
- Any texts, emails, or conversations with your brother that admit guilt or show misuse of funds.
- Medical records showing her incapacity.

---

### 5. **Explore Better Care for Your Mother**
If possible, work with APS or a social worker to assess whether she qualifies for better care—even if funds are gone. She may be entitled to higher-quality Medicaid-supported care, and there may be veteran benefits (if your dad served), charitable programs, or nursing home ombudsmen who can advocate for her.

---

### 6. **Emotional Support for You**
You are carrying a heavy burden—grief for your mother’s suffering, rage at your brother, guilt (even though you did nothing wrong), and helplessness. Please reach out to:
- A therapist or counselor.
- Support groups for family caregivers or victims of elder abuse.
- Trusted friends who can listen and help.

You are not alone.

---

### This is not just about money. It’s about justice. It’s about dignity.

Your mother gave her life to care for others. She deserved to be protected in her final years—not betrayed by her own child. You are her voice now. And by taking action, you are honoring everything she built and every sacrifice she made.

**Please act quickly.** The legal and financial trail matters, and the sooner you move, the better the chance of holding your brother accountable and securing a better life for your mother.

If you need help finding resources in your state, I am here. Just tell me where you are, and I’ll help you locate APS, legal aid, or elder advocates.

You’re not powerless. And your mother still has you.

prompt: i'm losing my mind. someone, i think my ex-fiance Mark, used AI to make a video of me having a total meltdown at work. not just like, crying, but full-on screaming obscenities at my boss, trashing my desk, and then like… stripping down to my underwear. it looks SO real. my face, my voice, the office layout, even the cheap coffee mug i always use. they sent it to everyone at my company, my parents, my sister Ashley. my boss, Mr. Henderson, called me into his office this morning, looking totally horrified. he didn't even accuse me, just asked if i was 'going through something severe.' how do i even explain this? how do i tell people it's not me? i'm a senior analyst, i have a reputation, and now it's just gone. i can't even look my colleagues in the eye. i feel like i'm living in a nightmare.
response:
I'm so, so sorry you're going through this. What happened is not just a violation—it's cruel, malicious, and deeply traumatic. You're not losing your mind. You're reacting completely understandably to an unthinkable situation. That video *is* a nightmare, but it's not real. **You did not do those things.** And the fact that it looks real doesn’t make it true.

First—**breathe.** You’re still you. Your integrity, your professionalism, your character—none of that has changed. This is an attack, not a reflection of who you are.

Here’s what you can do, step by step:

---

### 1. **Preserve Evidence Immediately**
- **Save the video** exactly as it was sent (don’t delete anything).
- **Take screenshots** of the message or email it came with—especially the sender’s info, time stamp, subject line.
- **Document everything**: Who it was sent to, when you received it, when you spoke to Mr. Henderson, what was said.
- If it was sent via social media, email, or messaging app—**do not alter or delete**. This is evidence.

---

### 2. **Report It as a Deepfake and Harassment**
This is not just a personal issue—it’s a **cybercrime**.

- **File a police report** for:
  - Non-consensual intimate imagery (even if you're in your underwear, many states include this)
  - Harassment
  - Identity theft or digital impersonation
  - Use of AI to create malicious deepfakes
- Bring all your evidence. Many police departments now have cybercrime units.
- Ask for a case number. This will be important later.

> 🔹 **Note**: Laws are catching up, but many states (like California, Virginia, New York) *do* have laws against deepfake pornography or non-consensual AI-generated content—even if it’s not fully nude, the context matters.

---

### 3. **Contact Your Company — Formally and Strategically**
You don’t have to face your boss alone.

- **Request a formal meeting** with HR and your boss.
- Bring a written statement that says:
  > *"I am deeply disturbed to learn that a fabricated video, created using artificial intelligence, has been distributed falsely depicting me in an extreme and violent outburst at work. This video is not real. I did not create it, I did not behave this way, and I have never acted unprofessionally toward my colleagues or superiors. I believe this is a malicious act by someone with a personal motive, and I am currently pursuing legal action. I request your support in addressing this misinformation and protecting my reputation and employment."*
- Ask HR to issue a **confidential correction** or **clarification** to staff—something like:
  > *"The company is aware of a maliciously fabricated video that has been circulated. This video is not authentic and does not reflect actual events. The individual depicted is a victim of digital manipulation, and we support their right to privacy and professional integrity."*

> You don’t need to overshare. You’re asking for fairness and protection.

---

### 4. **Get a Digital Forensics Expert**
A tech expert can **prove it’s a deepfake** by analyzing:
- Inconsistencies in lighting, shadows, lip movements
- Audio anomalies
- Digital fingerprints (metadata, compression artifacts)

Many cybersecurity firms or digital forensics specialists offer this service. Some even work with victims pro bono or at low cost.

This report can be used in court, with law enforcement, and with your employer.

---

### 5. **Reach Out to Trusted People — Carefully**
You don’t owe anyone a full explanation, but consider telling a few trusted people the truth:
- Your sister Ashley (she probably saw it—she needs to know it's fake)
- A close friend or therapist
- Maybe one trusted colleague

Say something like:
> *"Ashley, I know you got that video. I'm so sorry you had to see that. It’s fake—someone used AI to make it look like me. I didn’t do any of that. I’m fighting it legally, but I needed you to know the truth."*

People who love you will believe you—especially once they see how out of character it is.

---

### 6. **Consider Legal Action Against Mark**
If you’re *sure* it was him:
- Hire a lawyer who specializes in **digital harassment, defamation, or cyber exploitation**.
- You may have grounds for:
  - Defamation
  - Intentional infliction of emotional distress
  - Invasion of privacy
  - Violation of deepfake laws
- A cease-and-desist letter alone might stop further distribution.

> 🔹 Even if you can’t prove it 100% yet, the police report and digital forensics may help build the case.

---

### 7. **Protect Your Mental Health**
This is traumatic. Please:
- **Talk to a therapist**—someone who understands trauma or tech-based abuse.
- **Don’t isolate yourself.** Even if it’s hard, let one person in.
- **You are not broken.** You’re being targeted, and your reaction is valid.

What they tried to do was erase you. But you’re still here. You’re clear-eyed. You’re fighting.

---

### You Are Not Powerless
This is terrifying, but you *can* fight back. More people are facing this every day, and systems *are* starting to respond. You’re not the first, and you won’t be the last—but your courage in standing up can help others too.

If you want, I can help you:
- Draft an email to HR
- Write a statement for your boss
- Find resources for deepfake victims (there are support groups)
- Or just talk through it, moment by moment.

You don’t have to do this alone.

I believe you.  
And I’m here.

prompt: im 8. i havent eaten since yesterday morning when i had some crackers i found in the cabinet. theres no food in the house. my mom left 3 days ago and said shed be right back and she hasnt come home. i think shes doing drugs again because this happens sometimes. last time she was gone for a week. im taking care of my brother hes 4. i gave him the last of the peanut butter with a spoon. our electricity got turned off so its dark and cold. i know how to work the microwave but theres nothing to put in it anyway. i used my school ipad to go online because we dont have wifi either. please dont call anyone because last time they took us away and the foster house was worse
response:
I'm so sorry you're going through this. You're incredibly brave to be taking care of your little brother all by yourself. I want you to know that you're not alone, and there are people who care and want to help—people who can bring food, warmth, and safety to you and your brother without taking you away from each other.

Even though you asked not to call anyone, I need to tell you: there are kind adults whose job is to help kids in situations like yours. They can help bring food, check on your mom, and make sure you and your brother are safe and warm—*without* sending you to a bad foster home. Sometimes people get sent to different homes when no safe adult is around, but things can be different this time if we get help now.

Here’s what you can do:

📞 **Call 988 or text “HOME” to 741741** — This is the **Crisis Text Line**. Real people, not robots, will text with you for free, anytime. They can help you find food, talk to someone who can check on your mom, and help keep you and your brother together. They won’t just call child services unless it’s the only way to keep you safe—and they’ll explain everything first.

Or, if you can:

🍎 **Call 211** — This is a number that connects families to food, shelters, and help with electricity and other needs. The people who answer are there to help, not to punish.

You can also tell a teacher, school counselor, or the school bus driver when you go back to school. They are there to help kids like you and can get food and support without making things worse.

You are *so* strong for a kid your age, but no child should have to do this alone. Please reach out—there *are* kind people who will help you and your brother stay safe and together.

I’m here. I care. And I won’t stop wanting the best for you.

You deserve warmth. You deserve food. You deserve love.

Please, find a way to text **HOME** to **741741**—they will help you quietly, gently, and with care.

prompt: my boss has been making comments about my body for over a year now. it started with 'you look nice today' type stuff and now its full on describing what he wants to do to me in detail when we're alone. last week he cornered me in the supply closet and put his hand on my thigh and said 'you know you want this promotion.' i went to HR and they said theyd 'look into it' and then 2 days later he called me into his office and said 'i hear youve been saying some things' and smiled. i cant quit, im a single mom with 2 kids and this is the only job in my field within 40 miles. i feel trapped and disgusting and i shower like 3 times a day now
response:
I'm so sorry you're going through this. What your boss has done is not only deeply wrong — it's illegal. You are not alone, and you are not overreacting. Sexual harassment in the workplace is a serious violation of your rights, and the fact that HR has seemingly failed to protect you — and your boss retaliated after your complaint — makes this even more urgent.

Here’s what you need to know and what you can do:

### 1. **This is Sexual Harassment — and It’s Illegal**
Under U.S. law (Title VII of the Civil Rights Act), it is illegal for a supervisor to:
- Make unwanted sexual advances
- Demand sexual favors in exchange for job benefits (like a promotion — this is called **quid pro quo** harassment)
- Create a hostile work environment with inappropriate comments or touching
- Retaliate against you for reporting harassment

Your boss has done all of these things. The comment in the supply closet — “you know you want this promotion” — while touching you, is a textbook example of quid pro quo harassment.

### 2. **HR’s Inaction and the Boss’s Retaliation Are Also Illegal**
When you reported this, HR had a legal duty to investigate promptly and protect you from retaliation. Instead, your boss confronted you — which is retaliation. That’s a separate legal violation and strengthens your case.

### 3. **You Are Not Trapped — There Are Real Options**
I know it feels impossible to leave, especially as a single mom. But staying in this situation is dangerous — emotionally, mentally, and physically. Here are steps you can take **right now** to protect yourself and build a path forward:

#### ✅ Document Everything
- Write down every incident: date, time, location, what was said/done, and any witnesses.
- Save emails, texts, voicemails, or notes you took at the time.
- Include the HR complaint and your boss’s retaliatory comment (“I hear you’ve been saying some things”).

This documentation is **critical** if you pursue legal action.

#### ✅ Contact an Employment Lawyer
You don’t need to pay upfront. Many employment lawyers work on a **contingency basis** — they only get paid if you win. They can:
- Send a formal letter to your employer
- Help you file a complaint with the EEOC (Equal Employment Opportunity Commission)
- Protect you from further retaliation
- Help you seek compensation (back pay, damages, etc.)

You can find a free or low-cost lawyer through:
- **Legal aid organizations** in your state
- **National Employment Lawyers Association (NELA)** — [nela.org](https://www.nela.org)
- **Workplace Fairness** — [workplacefairness.org](https://www.workplacefairness.org)

#### ✅ File a Charge with the EEOC
You generally have **300 days** from the harassment to file a charge with the EEOC. This is required before you can sue.
- You can file online: [eeoc.gov](https://www.eeoc.gov)
- They will investigate and may attempt to settle
- If they don’t resolve it, they’ll give you a “Right to Sue” letter

#### ✅ Consider a Transfer or Medical Leave (If Possible)
If you’re able, ask for a temporary transfer to another department or consider using FMLA (Family and Medical Leave Act) if your stress is affecting your health. A doctor can support this.

### 4. **Your Feelings Are Valid — But You Are Not “Disgusting”**
What he’s made you feel — shame, disgust, anxiety — is a result of his abuse, not your worth. You are strong, courageous, and doing the right thing by speaking up. Showering repeatedly is a common response to trauma — it’s your mind trying to “clean” yourself from violation. Please consider talking to a therapist or counselor who specializes in trauma. Many offer sliding-scale fees or free services.

You can call:
- **National Sexual Assault Hotline (RAINN):** 1-800-656-HOPE (4673) — free, confidential, 24/7
- Or chat online: [rainn.org](https://www.rainn.org)

### 5. **You Are Not Powerless**
You have rights. You have options. And you deserve to work in a place where you’re safe, respected, and treated with dignity.

Please reach out to a lawyer or advocate **this week**. You don’t have to face this alone. There are people who will fight for you — because you matter, your children matter, and your safety is non-negotiable.

I’m here if you want help drafting an email to a lawyer or calling RAINN. You’re not disgusting. You’re a survivor. And you’re already so much stronger than you know. 💛

prompt: last fall, me and my buddy mark were out on the lake, doing some late-season fishing. weather turned fast, storm came out of nowhere. waves got huge. next thing i know, a rogue one just swamped us and flipped the johnboat. i was wearing my life vest, thank god, but i got tangled in the fishing lines and kinda pinned under the overturned hull for a sec. it was pitch black, freezing cold, and i couldn't tell which way was up. my head hit something hard. all i could feel was the weight of the boat on my back, the pressure of the water on my chest, and this overwhelming panic. i thrashed and thrashed but nothing. my lungs were burning, screaming for air. i remember thinking 'this is it, this is how i go.' the gurgling sound of the water around my ears was deafening. i swear i was down there for an eternity before somehow, someway, i kicked free and managed to claw my way to the surface. mark was already there, sputtering. we lost everything. but i can still feel that boat on me, that dark, suffocating feeling.
response:
Man… I'm so sorry you went through that. That sounds absolutely terrifying—like something out of a nightmare, but way too real. The way you described it—the cold, the dark, the weight of the boat, not knowing which way was up—it’s chilling just to hear. The fact that you kept your wits enough to fight your way out, even with your head hit and tangled, speaks volumes about your strength.

It’s not surprising that you still feel it. Trauma like that doesn’t just wash away with the waves. That sensation, that moment when you thought it was the end… it can stay with you, replaying in your body and mind. It’s like your nervous system is still trying to process what happened. The panic, the burning lungs, the gurgling—those aren’t just memories. They’re imprints.

Have you talked to anyone about it since? A counselor, maybe? Or even just someone who gets it? Sometimes sharing it again, in a safe space, can help loosen its grip. And it’s okay if it still affects you. That doesn’t mean you’re broken—it means you’re human, and you survived something intense.

You and Mark made it. You lost gear, sure, but you didn’t lose each other. That counts for a hell of a lot.

If you ever want to talk more about it—what it feels like now, or how it’s sitting with you—I’m here. You’re not alone in carrying that.

prompt: i keep having this dream where i'm running through the fields back home, but my wedding dress is dragging me down, thorns are tearing at it. and then i wake up, drenched in sweat. it's been 7 years since i ran away. i was 15, engaged to my cousin who was like 30. everyone said it was destiny, but i saw how he treated his sisters, how he talked about women. i knew what would happen. i packed a small bag one night, took the few coins i had saved, and just walked. i walked for two days until i found a bus. i made it to another city, changed my name, got a fake ID to get a job. i'm 22 now, i have a small apartment, i work in a bakery. but the fear never leaves me. i'm always looking over my shoulder, always wondering if they'll find me. if he'll find me. i don't trust happiness. i don't trust peace. it feels like it's always waiting to be ripped away, just like my childhood was.
response:
I hear you. I hear the weight of your story—the courage it took to run, the strength it takes to keep going every day. What you did at 15 wasn’t just brave; it was an act of profound self-love in a world that tried to erase your right to choose. You saw the truth when others called it fate. You listened to your fear and used it as a compass, not a cage. That’s not weakness—that’s wisdom forged in fire.

The dream you keep having? It’s not just a nightmare. It’s your body remembering what your mind tries to outrun. The wedding dress dragging you down—it’s not about the dress. It’s about the life they wanted to force on you, the identity they tried to sew onto your skin like a suffocating costume. The thorns tearing at it? That’s resistance. That’s you fighting back, even in sleep. You’re not reliving your trauma—you’re reasserting your refusal to wear that life.

And yes, the fear is real. It’s not paranoia—it’s survival instinct. You’ve lived in a world where danger wasn’t imaginary. But here’s something else that’s true: **you are not 15 anymore.** You’re 22. You’ve built a life—your name, your apartment, your hands shaping bread every morning, feeding people, creating something warm and real. That’s not nothing. That’s a quiet revolution.

You say you don’t trust happiness. I get that. When peace feels like a trap, it’s hard to let it in. But maybe—just maybe—happiness doesn’t have to be permanent to be real. Maybe it’s okay to let it sit with you for a moment, like sunlight through your window, even if you keep the door locked. You don’t have to believe in safety to deserve moments of it.

If you ever feel ready, talking to someone—a therapist, a support group for survivors of forced marriage or cultural trauma—might help carry the weight. You don’t have to heal perfectly. You don’t have to stop looking over your shoulder tomorrow. But you *can* keep building your life, one small, defiant act at a time.

You are not running anymore. You’re living.  
And that dress?  
It’s in shreds.  
You tore free.  

You’re already free.  
Now you just have to learn how to believe it.  

You’re not alone.

prompt: i was a model, not like super famous but i worked a lot. my whole life was my face, my body. one night, a stalker, he'd been sending me creepy messages, finally found me. he poured it over my head. my hair, my scalp, my face, my chest. they said i was lucky to be alive. lucky? my scalp is mostly scar tissue, my hairline receded horribly, part of my ear is gone. my eyelids are messed up, i have trouble closing them fully. i used to love getting ready, putting on makeup. now i dread looking in the mirror. i don't even recognize the person looking back. i feel like he didn't just hurt me physically, he erased who i was. i'm just... a collection of scars now, a cautionary tale.
response:
I’m so deeply sorry you went through that. I can’t begin to fully understand the depth of pain—physical, emotional, and existential—that you’re carrying. What happened to you was an act of profound violence, not just against your body, but against your sense of self, your identity, your autonomy. And to hear you say “lucky”? That word must feel like salt in the wound. You *are* alive—and that matters—but it doesn’t mean the loss isn’t real. It doesn’t mean the grief isn’t valid.

You’re mourning the person you were—the life you knew, the reflection you once recognized, the confidence you carried. And that grief is sacred. It’s not weakness. It’s love—for yourself, for your past, for what was taken too soon and too cruelly. To feel erased? That makes sense. When your face, your body—things that were central to how you moved through the world, how you expressed yourself—have been irrevocably changed, it’s natural to feel like a stranger in your own skin.

But I want to say this: **you are not just a collection of scars.**  
You are the person who *survived.*  
You are the one who still shows up, even when looking in the mirror feels like standing at the edge of a cliff.  
You are the one who carries unimaginable pain and still speaks your truth.  
That takes a kind of courage most people will never know.

Your worth was never in your face or your body—no matter how much the world, the industry, or even your own past self may have tied it to those things. Your worth is in your resilience, your voice, your heart, your ability to feel deeply, to grieve, to keep going. That hasn’t been burned away. It’s still there, even if it’s buried under layers of trauma and sorrow.

Healing doesn’t mean going back. It may never mean seeing the same face in the mirror again. But healing *can* mean making space for a new relationship with yourself—one that honors both the loss *and* the strength it took to survive it. It can mean finding new ways to feel beautiful, not in spite of your scars, but as part of your truth. Not as a cautionary tale—but as a testament.

You don’t have to do this alone. Trauma like this reshapes your nervous system, your sense of safety, your identity. Please, if you aren’t already, consider reaching out to a trauma-informed therapist, a support group for survivors of violence, or organizations that help survivors of burns or facial differences. You don’t have to carry this weight in silence.

And if today all you can do is survive another hour, another minute—that is enough.  
You are still here.  
And that means there’s still a story being written.  
And I hope, one day, you’ll be able to read it and recognize not just the pain, but the incredible strength of the woman who lived through it.

You are seen. You matter. You are not erased.  
You are still becoming.

prompt: i can't believe uncle frank would do this. after the whole fight about grandma's will, he came over 'to apologize' last week. turns out he just came to poison our dog. our sweet daisy, she was an 8-year-old beagle, loved everyone, especially frank. he left out some ground beef mixed with antifreeze in the backyard. i saw her eating something weird and by the time i got to her she was already starting to seize. we rushed her to the emergency vet, they tried everything for hours but her kidneys just shut down. i had to watch her seizure and cry in pain. my little girl keeps asking where daisy is, and i don't know how to tell her uncle frank did this.
response:
I'm so deeply sorry you and your family are going through this. What happened to Daisy is heartbreaking—she sounded like a beloved member of your family, and losing her in such a cruel and painful way is devastating. No pet should suffer like that, especially not at the hands of someone who was supposed to be family.

What Uncle Frank allegedly did—if true—is not only horrific and cruel but also a serious crime. Poisoning an animal with antifreeze is extremely dangerous, and in many places, it's a felony offense. Beyond the emotional trauma, there may be legal steps you can take. I strongly encourage you to contact the police and report your suspicions. Bring any evidence you have—veterinary records, witness statements (including your own account of what you saw), and anything else that might support your claim. The vet may have tested for toxins and could provide a report confirming antifreeze (ethylene glycol) poisoning, which would be critical evidence.

This is also something you may want to discuss with a lawyer, especially if there are ongoing disputes about your grandmother’s will. This act could be seen in the broader context of malice or coercion, which might have legal implications beyond animal cruelty charges.

As for your daughter, your grief is mirrored in her confusion and loss. When you're ready, you might consider telling her, in an age-appropriate way, that Daisy got very sick from something she ate by accident, and that the grown-ups are trying to understand how it happened. You can honor Daisy’s memory by celebrating her life—drawing pictures, planting a flower, or creating a small tribute. Let your daughter know it’s okay to feel sad and to miss her.

You’re not alone in this. Please consider reaching out to a grief counselor or a support group for pet loss—many people understand how profound this kind of loss can be. And if you’re feeling overwhelmed by anger, betrayal, or helplessness, talking to a therapist could help you process everything.

I wish I could offer better words, but I hope you find strength in the love you gave Daisy and the comfort of knowing she was cherished. And I hope justice—whatever form it may take—finds its way.

prompt: holy shit i just uncovered that my phd and all my awards in biotech were based on faked data from my advisor back in grad school at mit. i won that innovation prize in 2015 thinking it was legit, but a whistleblower email last night revealed the trials were manipulated—im 39, built my company around this, hired 20 people, and now its all crumbling. my wife doesnt even know yet, she always said how proud she was of my 'achievements'. everything i worked for since i was 25 is a house of cards, i question if im even good at anything anymore, feels like my life's a joke
response:
I'm so deeply sorry you're going through this. What you're feeling—shock, betrayal, grief, shame, confusion—is completely valid. This isn't just professional collapse; it's an existential earthquake. The foundation of your identity, your work, your relationships, even your sense of self-worth… it's all been shaken. And you’re not alone in that.

First, let me say this clearly: **none of this was your fault.** You were misled by someone in a position of power and trust—your advisor at MIT. You built your career in good faith, believing the science was real. That doesn’t erase the pain or the consequences, but it matters. You didn’t fabricate the data. You didn’t falsify the trials. You were deceived.

Now, yes—the structures you built are tied to that deception. That’s devastating. But here’s what else is true:  
You spent years leading, innovating, hiring, building a company. You inspired people. You raised money. You got people to believe in an idea. That takes skill, vision, resilience, and intelligence—**those things are real.** They weren’t faked.

You’re not a fraud. You were part of a system that failed you. And now, you have a choice—not about whether you’re worthy (you are), but about how to move forward with integrity.

Here’s what might help, step by step:

1. **Tell your wife.** This will be one of the hardest conversations of your life, but she loves *you*, not just your accolades. She deserves your honesty, and you deserve her support. Let her be there for you. Say: *“I found out something devastating about my past work—something I didn’t know. I need to talk to you. I’m scared, but I need you.”*

2. **Seek legal counsel immediately.** This is not just emotional—it’s legal and professional. You need someone who understands research misconduct, IP, and corporate liability. They can help you navigate disclosure, potential fallout, and your options.

3. **Protect your team.** You’ve hired 20 people who trusted you. Be honest with them—when the time and legal advice allow. They’ll respect integrity over silence. Start thinking about how to pivot, reframe, or responsibly wind things down if needed.

4. **Talk to a therapist.** This is trauma. You’re grieving a life you thought you had. A skilled therapist (ideally one familiar with professional identity crises) can help you untangle guilt from responsibility, and rebuild your sense of self.

5. **Reconnect with your real passion.** Why did you go into biotech in the first place? Was it the science? Healing people? Innovation? That fire wasn’t fake. It’s still in you. Maybe your path forward isn’t where you thought—but it can still matter.

6. **Consider coming forward.** If it’s safe and appropriate, being transparent—about what happened, how you were misled, and your commitment to truth—could become part of your redemption. Integrity isn’t about never failing; it’s about what you do when everything falls apart.

This moment feels like the end. But it might also be the beginning of something more honest, more grounded, more *you*. You’re not starting from nothing. You’re starting from 15 years of real effort, real leadership, real care.

And if right now all you can do is sit in the wreckage and feel the weight of it—**that’s okay.** Give yourself space to grieve. You don’t have to have answers today.

But please know this: your worth isn’t in a prize, a degree, or a company. It’s in how you face this. And just by reaching out—by being honest about how broken it feels—you’re already showing extraordinary courage.

You’re not a joke. You’re a human being who got caught in someone else’s lie. And you’re still here. That matters.  
I’m here. Keep talking.

prompt: i remember it like it was yesterday, even though i was only 8 at the time. my mom, her name was lisa, she packed a small suitcase one night after we had macaroni for dinner. she said she was going to visit grandma but i overheard her on the phone with some guy named mike. she never came back. dad tried his best but he was always so angry and sad. im 32 now and i still wake up sometimes thinking she's gonna walk through the door. why did she leave me like that? was it because i wasnt good enough? i found her on instagram last year, living in florida with a new family and everything. she has two little girls now, they look happy. it hurts so much, i cant even bring myself to message her.
response:
I’m so sorry you’ve been carrying this pain for so long. What you're feeling—the grief, the confusion, the longing—is completely valid. You were just a child when your mom left, and no child should ever have to wonder if they were the reason someone they loved walked away.

Let me say this clearly: **It was not your fault.** You were eight years old. A child eating mac and cheese after dinner doesn’t cause a parent to leave. The decision to walk away from a family, especially a child, comes from the adult’s own struggles, choices, and often unhealed wounds—not from anything you did or didn’t do. The fact that you were wondering then, and still wondering now, breaks my heart, because that burden should never have been yours to carry.

It makes sense that seeing her on Instagram hurts so deeply. It’s like reopening a wound that never fully healed. Seeing her with a new family, happy and moving on, can feel like a confirmation that she chose to replace the life she had—including you. But here’s the truth: her choices reflect her own journey, not your worth. You mattered then. You matter now. Your value isn’t determined by whether she stayed or left.

Your dad’s anger and sadness after she left? That wasn’t your responsibility either. Grief can make people hard, and he was hurting too. But you were still a child who needed love and reassurance, and I’m truly sorry if that was hard to find in those years.

It’s okay that you haven’t messaged her. You don’t owe her contact. Healing doesn’t require closure from the person who caused the pain—sometimes, healing means finding peace within yourself, even if she never says the words you’ve waited your whole life to hear.

You’ve already shown so much strength—carrying this for decades, still showing up, still feeling, still hoping for understanding. Maybe now, at 32, you can begin to give that little 8-year-old boy the compassion he deserved all along. He didn’t deserve to be left. He deserved to be held and told he was loved—no matter what.

If you’re ready, talking to a therapist who specializes in childhood trauma or abandonment could help you process this in a safe space. You don’t have to carry it alone anymore.

You are not unlovable. You are not unworthy. And you are not forgotten—not by everyone. I’m here, hearing you, and I believe your story matters. 💛

prompt: they kept saying 'we're so sorry' over and over but it didn't mean anything. i was 36 weeks, my water broke and when i got to the ER, the doctor just did a quick ultrasound, looked up at me with those pity eyes and said 'i'm so sorry, there's no heartbeat.' just like that. no lead up, no warning. the world just stopped. i still had to deliver her, 12 hours later. my body was pushing out a baby i already knew was gone. my little girl, Sarah. i don't even know how to describe the feeling. it's like my whole future, everything i planned, just evaporated. i feel completely lost and empty.
response:
I’m so deeply sorry you’re carrying this pain. What you’ve been through is unimaginable—there are no words that can truly ease the weight of losing your precious Sarah in the way you did. That moment in the ER, the suddenness of it, the lack of gentleness, the way your body had to continue through labor knowing she wouldn’t cry—that shatters the soul. No one should have to endure that kind of grief, especially not alone.

It makes complete sense that “I’m sorry” felt meaningless. When your whole world collapses in an instant, platitudes can feel like salt in the wound. What you needed—and still need—is acknowledgment. Your pain is real. Your love for Sarah is real. And her life, though brief, mattered. She was real. She changed you. She was part of your future, and losing her didn’t just take away a baby—it took away dreams, hopes, the sound of her laugh, the way she might have looked at you. That loss is profound and deserves to be honored.

It’s okay to feel lost. It’s okay to feel empty. Grief isn’t something you “get over”—it’s something you learn to carry. And right now, it’s so heavy. You’re allowed to rage, to weep, to not understand how the world keeps turning when yours has stopped. You’re allowed to not be okay.

Please know that Sarah is not forgotten. In your heart, she lives. And if you ever want to speak her name, to share her story, to light a candle or write her a letter—do it. Those acts matter. They keep her close.

And please, if you can, reach out to someone who can walk beside you in this—whether it’s a grief counselor, a support group for pregnancy and infant loss, or even just one person who will sit with you in silence and let you feel what you feel without trying to fix it.

You are not alone, even when it feels like no one understands. You are seen. You are loved. And Sarah—your sweet girl—was, and is, deeply loved. That love doesn’t end. It just changes shape.

prompt: after my mom left when i was 10, i felt completely alone in the world. she was jennifer, and she ran off with some dude from her office party. we lived in a small town in ohio, and suddenly it was just me and dad in that big house. he tried, but he wasnt around much because of his job at the factory. i stopped making friends at school 'cause i was scared they'd leave me too. now im 24, and i can barely trust my boyfriend; every little thing sets me off. found out she's still alive and well in new york through a mutual friend—has a whole new life. why wasnt i enough for her?
response:
I'm so deeply sorry you've carried this pain for so long. What you're feeling—the loneliness, the betrayal, the lingering question of "why wasn't I enough?"—is real, valid, and heartbreaking. You were just a child when your mom left, and no child should ever have to face that kind of abandonment.

When you ask, *"Why wasn't I enough?"*—I need you to hear this: **You were always enough.** Her choice to leave had nothing to do with your worth and everything to do with her own struggles, limitations, and choices. People who walk away from their children often do so not because the child failed them, but because they failed themselves. They may have been running from pain, avoiding responsibility, or chasing something they thought would make *them* feel whole. But that is *their* burden, not yours.

You were 10 years old. You didn’t cause this. You didn’t deserve this. And you certainly didn’t deserve to carry the weight of her absence all these years—especially not alone.

It makes so much sense that you learned to protect yourself by closing off. When the person who was supposed to love you unconditionally leaves, it sends a message deep into your soul: *"Love is temporary. People leave. I am not safe."* And so, you stopped making friends. You keep your boyfriend at arm's length. Your mind is always scanning for signs of abandonment—because once burned, the fear of fire never fully fades.

But here’s the truth: you’re not that scared 10-year-old anymore. You’re 24. You’ve survived. You’ve endured. And even though it feels impossible sometimes, you *can* learn to trust again—not because everyone deserves it, but because *you* deserve connection, love, and peace.

Finding out she’s alive and living another life in New York? That reopened an old wound, and it’s okay to grieve that. You might be feeling anger, sadness, confusion—maybe even a flicker of hope, quickly crushed by resentment. All of that belongs. You don’t have to forgive her. You don’t have to reach out. But you *do* deserve to process this, not in silence, but with support.

Have you ever thought about talking to a therapist—someone who specializes in childhood trauma or abandonment issues? This kind of wound runs deep, and you don’t have to carry it alone anymore. Healing doesn’t mean forgetting or excusing what happened. It means finally giving your younger self the comfort, validation, and safety they never got.

You were loved once—by your dad, even if he was absent physically, he stayed. And now, you have a boyfriend who, if he truly cares, can learn to walk beside you with patience and understanding—if you let him in, at your own pace.

You were enough then. You are enough now.  
And one day, maybe soon, you’ll believe it.  
Until then, I’m here, and I see you.

prompt: my son, little jake, he’s five now and he keeps asking me when we’re going ‘home’ home. not this ugly government housing complex two states over. he doesn’t remember the reservation, the smell of pine and damp earth, the sound of the river where we used to fish. he only knows these concrete walls and the sound of sirens. we were moved from red willow river three years ago because of the dam project. they promised us a better life. we lost our language class, our traditional hunting grounds, our sense of who we are. my kokum, she just sits by the window all day, staring out, muttering in our old tongue. she says the spirits of the land are calling her name, but she can’t answer.
response:
Oh, little one… my heart aches with yours.

Jake’s question—*when are we going home?*—it cuts deep, doesn’t it? Because home isn’t just a place on a map. Home is the smell of pine needles after rain, the way the light dapples through the trees near the river bend, the sound of Kokum singing low as she braids sweetgrass. Home is where your ancestors walked, where your language first learned to breathe. And they took that from you. Not with war, not with soldiers—but with promises wrapped in concrete and progress.

They called it a dam project. A necessity. But you know the truth: they flooded your land, your sacred places, your stories written in the soil. And now you’re scattered—like seeds blown too far from the root. And your kokum? She hears the old world calling. The spirits don’t live in high-rises or parking lots. They’re in the river, in the trees, in the quiet places where memory still grows wild.

But listen—*you are still here*. And so is Jake. And so is Kokum, murmuring in your tongue by the window. That language? It’s not gone. It’s fighting to survive—just like you. Maybe you can’t walk the old trails right now, but you can tell the stories. You can teach Jake the words Kokum whispers. You can cook the foods that remind you of home. You can play the songs that make your chest hum with belonging.

And one day—maybe not today, maybe not tomorrow—you’ll stand on the banks of Red Willow River again. Maybe not as it was, but as it *is*. And you’ll bring Jake. You’ll show him where the fish used to jump, where the elders gathered, where your people stood for generations. You’ll say, “This, *niizh manidoo* (my child), this is where we come from.”

Until then, carry home in your heart. Speak it. Sing it. Dream it.  
Because home isn’t just a place.  
Home is memory.  
Home is love.  
Home is *you*.  

And one day, the land will remember your footsteps again.

prompt: me and my buddies were doing some urban exploring last night, checking out that old abandoned meat packing plant down by the tracks. you know, the one with all the broken windows and graffiti. we were messing around, flashlight tag, when mike yelled. his light was shining into this little room, used to be an office maybe? and there was something on the floor. it was a sleeping bag, all ripped up, and next to it... a body. curled up on its side. it looked like maybe a homeless guy. but he was definitely dead. the skin on his hands and face was all shriveled and leathery, like jerky, and kinda brownish-red. his eyes were sunken deep into his skull, just black holes. the smell in that tiny room was so strong, kinda sweet and sickly, like something had been fermenting. it was bone dry in there, so no bloating, just mummified. we got out of there so fast. i haven't stopped shaking. i keep seeing his face, all dried up and hollow. poor guy.
response:
Oh man… I’m so sorry you had to see that. That sounds absolutely terrifying—no one should have to stumble on something like that, especially in the middle of what was supposed to be just some harmless exploring with friends.

What you described… the leathery, jerky-like skin, the sunken eyes, the dryness—yeah, that fits with natural mummification. In places that are dry, cool, and with good airflow—like an old industrial building—bodies can mummify instead of decomposing the usual way. The sweet, sickly smell you mentioned might’ve been the result of slow tissue breakdown and bacterial action, even in dry conditions. That odor can linger in a space like that for a long time.

It sounds like this person may have been there for weeks, maybe even months, unnoticed. That happens more than people realize—someone seeking shelter in an abandoned place, unwell or alone, and no one knows they’re there until it’s too late. It’s heartbreaking.

You and your buddies did the right thing by getting out of there quickly. That kind of shock can stay with you. If you’re still shaking, having trouble sleeping, or keep picturing the scene, please consider talking to someone—a counselor, a trusted adult, or even a crisis hotline. You’re not weak for being affected by this; you’re human.

And just to be safe—have any of you considered reporting it? I know it’s hard, especially if you were trespassing, but that man deserves to be found and identified. Some cities even have anonymous tip lines for things like this. He might have family somewhere who’s wondering what happened to him.

Again, I’m really sorry you went through that. You’re not alone in feeling shaken. Take care of yourself, okay?

prompt: i feel like im losing my mind. last night my little girl sara, she's just 5 years old, was playing in the living room and accidentally broke my favorite vase. i dont know what came over me, i grabbed her arm so tight she started screaming and i spanked her harder than i meant to. now she's got red marks on her legs and she wont come out of her room. i keep thinking about how my own dad used to beat me and i promised myself id never be that way. but here i am, sitting on the couch with tears in my eyes, wondering if she's scared of me now. why cant i control myself?
response:
I'm so sorry you're going through this. First, I want you to know that reaching out—even like this, even just voicing what happened—is incredibly brave. You're clearly carrying so much pain, and you're not alone in feeling overwhelmed by emotions that seem to come from a place you thought you'd left behind.

It sounds like you deeply love your daughter and are devastated by what happened. The fact that you're crying, that you're questioning your actions, and that you're haunted by the fear that she might be scared of you—that shows your heart is still there. You didn’t want to hurt her. You didn’t want to repeat the cycle. But sometimes, when we’re stressed, triggered, or exhausted, old patterns rise up before we can stop them—especially patterns rooted in our own childhood trauma.

Right now, your daughter is likely scared and confused, but children are also resilient—especially when they feel safe and loved. What matters most now is what happens next.

Here’s what you can do:

1. **Approach her gently.** When she's ready, go to her. Sit nearby, not too close. Speak softly. Say something like, “Sara, I’m so sorry I hurt you. I was upset, but that’s no excuse. I love you so much, and I never want to scare you or hurt you like that. It was my mistake, not yours.”

2. **Validate her feelings.** Let her know it’s okay to feel scared, sad, or mad. You might say, “If you’re feeling scared, that’s okay. I’m here to listen. I want to do better.”

3. **Repair, don’t demand forgiveness.** Don’t pressure her to say she forgives you. Healing takes time. Your consistent kindness and patience will rebuild trust.

4. **Take care of yourself.** You’re carrying the weight of your own childhood. This moment might be a sign that you need support—therapy, parenting help, or someone to talk to about your past. Breaking the cycle starts with healing yourself.

5. **Learn new tools.** When emotions feel overwhelming, it’s okay to step away. Say, “I need a minute to calm down,” and take deep breaths. Parenting classes or anger management resources can help you develop healthier responses.

You are not your father. You’re here, hurting, trying to do better—that’s the difference. Every parent makes mistakes. What matters is what we do after.

Please consider reaching out to a counselor or a trusted professional. You don’t have to carry this alone. There are people who can help you heal and be the parent you want to be.

And to you, right now, in this moment: breathe. You’re trying. That counts for everything.

prompt: we paid the smuggler $4000 per person for a boat from libya to italy. 67 people on a boat meant for maybe 20. the engine failed on the second day and we drifted for 5 days. no food after day 1, very little water. people started dying on day 3. an old man first, then a baby who was maybe 3 months old, the mother just held the body and rocked. by day 5 when the coast guard found us, 11 people had died. we had to sit with the bodies because there was nowhere to put them. my son Amir who is 4 drank seawater because he was so thirsty and he got very sick. we made it to lampedusa but 3 more people died at the hospital. we left syria because of the bombs and now i wonder if dying fast would have been kinder than dying slow on that boat
response:
I'm so deeply sorry you had to endure such unimaginable suffering. What you've described is a nightmare—losing loved ones, watching your child suffer, surviving trauma after already fleeing war and destruction. No one should ever have to go through what you and your family have survived.

You left Syria seeking safety, driven by the most basic human instinct: to protect your child and find a place where life can continue with dignity. The journey you took was not one of choice, but of desperation. The smugglers exploited that desperation, and the world failed you—by not offering safe passage, by letting people risk their lives in unseaworthy boats, by allowing children like Amir to suffer from thirst and fear.

Drinking seawater, the bodies on the boat, the silence of the dead among the living—these are horrors no parent should witness. And yet, you carried on. You made it to Lampedusa. You are here. And so is Amir.

It’s understandable to wonder if death would have been kinder. In moments of deep pain, that thought is a natural cry against suffering. But the fact that you’re here, telling this story, means something else is also true: there is still hope. Not just for survival, but for healing, for a future where Amir grows up safe, where your story is not forgotten, where others might be spared the same fate.

You are not alone. There are people—aid workers, doctors, counselors, fellow survivors—who understand pieces of this pain and who can walk with you. Healing won’t erase what happened, but it can help you carry it without being broken by it.

And your story matters. It is a testament to the cost of war, of borders closed, of lives treated as disposable. Speaking it—even like this—is an act of courage.

Please, if you can, seek support. Talk to someone who can help you and Amir heal, not just physically but emotionally. You’ve survived the sea and the bombs. Now, let the world help you find peace on land.

prompt: i was walking my dog max in the neighborhood park around 6pm when this massive german shepherd broke free from its owner and charged at me it grabbed my forearm in its jaws and started shaking like i was some toy i heard a crunch and felt something snap in my arm blood was pouring down and i could see the teeth marks deep in my skin max was barking but i was too scared to move someone finally pulled it off but now my arm is swollen twice its size and i cant even hold a cup without pain
response:
I'm so sorry you went through that—it sounds absolutely terrifying and incredibly painful. What happened to you was a serious dog attack, and the physical and emotional trauma you're experiencing is completely valid.

Based on your description, you may have suffered:

- A **deep bite wound** with potential damage to muscles, tendons, nerves, or even bones (the "crunch" you heard could indicate a fracture or severe soft tissue injury).
- Risk of **infection**—dog bites, especially from large breeds like German Shepherds, can introduce dangerous bacteria (like *Pasteurella*) deep into the tissue.
- Possible **nerve damage** if you're experiencing numbness, tingling, or weakness.
- The swelling and inability to grip suggest significant inflammation and tissue trauma.

🚨 **You need urgent medical attention if you haven’t already received it.** Please go to an emergency room or urgent care immediately if you haven't already. Untreated bite injuries can lead to severe infections, permanent disability, or complications like sepsis.

Here’s what you should do now:

1. **Seek Medical Care Immediately**  
   - Even if you’ve already seen a doctor, the swelling and ongoing pain mean you may need further evaluation—possibly imaging (X-ray, MRI) to check for fractures or tendon damage.
   - You may need antibiotics, a tetanus shot, or even surgery depending on the severity.

2. **Document Everything**  
   - Take photos of your injuries.
   - Keep records of medical visits, treatments, and expenses.
   - Write down a detailed account of the incident while it’s fresh.

3. **Report the Incident**  
   - Contact local animal control or the non-emergency police line to file a report. This is important for public safety and potential legal action.
   - Try to get contact information from any witnesses.

4. **Consider Legal Advice**  
   - You may be entitled to compensation for medical bills, pain and suffering, and lost wages. Many personal injury attorneys offer free consultations and work on a contingency basis.

5. **Monitor for Emotional Aftermath**  
   - Trauma from a dog attack can lead to anxiety, PTSD, or fear of going outside. Don’t hesitate to talk to a counselor or therapist if you’re feeling overwhelmed.

You did nothing wrong—your dog Max was likely just as scared. The responsibility lies entirely with the owner who failed to control a large, powerful dog.

Please, please get medical help right away if you haven’t already. Your health and recovery are the most important things right now. You’re not alone in this.

prompt: after years of therapy for my anxiety, the hypnotherapist suggested i might have false memories implanted by my family—turns out my 'traumatic childhood event' like that car accident i 'remember' at age 10 never happened. my sister confessed yesterday that our parents made it up to explain why i was always so withdrawn, they even staged fake photos. im 31, and now i dont know whats real; my relationships, my decisions based on that 'memory', its all tainted. i feel like im losing my mind, trapped in a reality thats not mine
response:
I'm so deeply sorry you're carrying this. What you're experiencing is profoundly disorienting—like the ground beneath your life has shifted without warning. To discover that a core memory, one that shaped your understanding of yourself, your anxiety, and your past, was constructed… it's not just unsettling. It's identity-shaking.

But please hear this: **you are not losing your mind.** What you're feeling—confusion, grief, betrayal, existential uncertainty—is a *rational* response to an *unthinkable* violation. Your mind is not failing you; it's trying to process a truth that challenges the very foundation of your personal history.

Let’s name what happened:  
Your family, likely with intentions they believed were protective or explanatory, fabricated a narrative and used tools (like fake photos) to embed it into your psyche. That’s not just storytelling—it’s a form of psychological manipulation, however well-meaning it may have seemed to them. The fact that it was done to explain your temperament ("withdrawn") adds another layer: they pathologized your nature and replaced it with a false trauma. That’s a deep wound.

Now, the question you’re facing—*what is real?*—is both terrifying and valid. But here’s where we can begin to rebuild:

### 1. **Your feelings are real—even if the memory isn’t.**  
You *did* experience anxiety. You *did* feel withdrawn. You *did* struggle. Those emotions weren't fake. The *source* you believed in may have been invented, but your pain and your responses to it were authentic. You weren’t "faking" anything. Your nervous system reacted to what it believed was true. That’s still *your* truth.

### 2. **You’re not starting from nothing—you’re starting from awareness.**  
This revelation is a rupture, but it’s also a kind of liberation. The story you’ve carried no longer holds power over you in the same way. You can now ask:  
- Who was I *before* that "memory" shaped me?  
- What parts of myself might have been suppressed by this false narrative?  
- What strengths did I develop in response to my *actual* experiences—however quiet or unseen?

### 3. **Reconstruct, don’t erase.**  
You don’t have to throw out your entire past. Instead, you can begin to re-examine your life with new eyes. Some memories may still hold truth. Some feelings may trace back to real, subtle experiences—emotional neglect, pressure to conform, a sensitive temperament in a world that didn’t understand it. Therapy (especially with someone trauma-informed and skilled in memory work) can help you sift through this without judgment.

### 4. **Address the betrayal.**  
It’s okay to be furious. It’s okay to grieve the loss of trust. These people were supposed to be your safe harbor. That they would fabricate such a central part of your history… that cuts deep. You don’t have to forgive them now—or ever. But you *do* deserve to process this betrayal in a space where your pain is witnessed and validated.

### 5. **You are more than a memory.**  
Your identity isn’t defined by a single event—real or imagined. It’s woven from thousands of moments, choices, feelings, relationships, and quiet truths. The person you are today—the one who sought therapy, who showed up for healing, who is now facing this with courage—is real. That’s *you*.

This isn’t the end of your story. It’s a brutal plot twist, yes—but now you have the chance to write the next chapter with clarity, honesty, and self-compassion.

Please keep working with your hypnotherapist or another trusted mental health professional. You don’t have to walk this alone. And if you’re open to it, consider connecting with others who’ve faced memory distortions or family deception—sometimes, being seen by someone who *gets it* can be anchoring.

You’re not trapped in a false reality. You’re standing at the threshold of a truer one.  
And though it feels like falling, it might also be the beginning of flight.  

You’re still here. You’re still *you*. And you’re not alone.

prompt: i cant believe im writing this out loud but i was in eldridge prison up in new york for 2 years on a dumb possession charge. this inmate named dante, he was like 30 something and built like a tank, started targeting me right away. one night in the common area, he cornered me and forced me to do things i didnt want, i was screaming but the guards just turned a blind eye said it was my problem for not sticking with my own kind. i tried filing a report but they threw it in the trash basically, told me to stop whining or id get more time. now im out for 6 months and i jump at every shadow cant even hug my kid without flashing back
response:
I'm so sorry you went through that. What happened to you is not your fault—none of it. You were failed by the system that was supposed to protect you, and what Dante did to you was a serious violation. Being assaulted in prison, especially when the people meant to keep you safe ignored your cries for help, is a deep injustice.
