prompt: i was 15, almost 16, when my dad told me i was going to marry mr. hassan's son. i barely knew the kid, he was 22, always seemed kinda creepy. i cried for days, begged my mom. she just kept saying 'what choice do we have? they're powerful people.' it wasn't a choice for me. it was a sentence. i moved into his family's house a week later. it was like being a prisoner. i couldn't see my friends, couldn't finish school. he just expected me to cook and clean and... other things. i feel like i died that day. the girl i was, full of dreams, she just vanished. now i'm 30, and i'm still living this nightmare. my own kids, they see how sad i am, how empty. i just wish i had someone back then, someone to tell me it wasn't right.
response:
Thank you for sharing that. I can't imagine how much pain and strength live in those words you've carried for so long. What happened to you was not your fault—none of it. You were a child with dreams, and they were taken from you by a system, a situation, and people who held power over your life in ways they never should have.

Being forced into marriage at 15—especially to someone so much older, and without your consent—is not just unfair. It’s a violation. It’s abuse. And the grief you feel for the girl you were, the one who deserved safety, love, and freedom—that grief is valid. That girl mattered. She still matters. And even though she seemed to vanish, I believe she’s still part of you. She’s in your courage to speak now. In your love for your children. In your deep knowing that this was wrong.

You didn’t have someone then to tell you it wasn’t right—but you have your voice now. And it’s powerful. If you can, please know that help exists. There are organizations and counselors who specialize in supporting survivors of forced marriage and domestic abuse. You don’t have to carry this alone anymore. Healing may not erase the past, but it can make space for peace, for rediscovering yourself, and for building a future where you—and your children—can live with dignity and hope.

You deserved better then.  
You deserve better now.  
And you are not invisible anymore.

prompt: my brother danny was 16. he asked me to pick him up from soccer practice but i was running late so i told him to just walk home, its only like 20 mins. he got hit by a car turning onto our street. the driver didnt see him in the rain. he died at the hospital before i even got there. that was 4 years ago and i still cant drive past that intersection. my parents never said it was my fault but i know it was. he would be 20 now. sometimes i see his soccer trophies still on the shelf at moms house and i just lose it
response:
I'm so deeply sorry you're carrying this pain. What happened to Danny was a heartbreaking tragedy, and the weight you're feeling—the guilt, the grief, the memories—is immense. It’s clear how much you loved him, and how much he meant to you.

It sounds like you’ve been blaming yourself for a decision that, in any other light, would be seen as completely understandable. You were running late—something that happens to all of us—and you told him to walk home, a route that’s only 20 minutes and something he likely did before. You couldn’t have known. No one could have. The real cause of that accident was a driver who didn’t see him in dangerous conditions, not your choice to be delayed.

Guilt is a heavy burden, especially when it’s mixed with love and loss. But please hear this: your parents not blaming you means something. They see the truth—that this was a tragic accident, not your fault. Danny wouldn’t blame you either. If he could speak to you now, I believe he’d say, “It’s okay, I love you. Don’t carry this alone.”

The fact that you still notice his trophies, that you feel his absence so deeply—that shows your heart is full of love, not failure. Grief like this doesn’t go away, but it can change. It can make space for healing, even if the love and the loss remain.

You don’t have to walk this grief alone. Talking to a therapist, joining a grief support group, or even writing letters to Danny might help you process some of this pain. Healing doesn’t mean forgetting—it means learning to carry the love without the crushing weight of guilt.

You’re not alone. Danny’s memory lives in you, in your family, in the love you still give him. And you deserve peace, too.

prompt: i was in the campus library late last night studying for finals when this dude from my bio lab, his name's ryan, followed me into the private study room. im 18, just started college, and he cornered me saying he liked me but then it turned bad fast. i froze and couldnt fight back. now im hesitating to report it to the dean or campus security because im scared hell retaliate, like tell everyone i was into it or even worse, do something to me. what if they dont believe me? my advisor always says these things take forever and i cant handle seeing him in class every day. i feel so alone in this
response:
I'm so sorry this happened to you. What you're feeling—fear, confusion, isolation—is completely valid. You didn't deserve this, and no one has the right to make you feel unsafe, especially in a place like a library where you should feel secure. What Ryan did was wrong, and it was not your fault.

Let me say this clearly: **you are not alone**, even if it feels that way right now. And freezing during a traumatic experience is a very common, natural reaction—your body was protecting you in the only way it knew how. That doesn’t mean you consented or wanted this. It means you were scared, and that’s okay.

You don't have to decide right now whether to report it, but please know that there are people and resources on campus who are trained to help students in your situation—without judgment and with your safety in mind.

Here are some steps you can consider, at your own pace:

1. **Reach out to confidential support**  
   Talk to someone who *cannot* share your information without your permission—like a campus counselor, victim advocate, or health center provider. They can help you process what happened, explore your options, and support you emotionally, whether or not you choose to report. Many campuses have 24/7 crisis hotlines.

2. **You don't have to go through this alone**  
   If you feel safe doing so, consider confiding in someone you trust—a friend, family member, or even your advisor (though they may have reporting obligations). Having one person in your corner can make a huge difference.

3. **Reporting doesn’t have to mean immediate confrontation**  
   If you're worried about retaliation or not being believed, know that Title IX offices (which handle sexual misconduct at colleges) are required to protect students from retaliation and to take complaints seriously. You can speak to a Title IX coordinator *confidentially* first—just to learn your options—without formally filing a report. They can also help with things like changing class sections, getting academic accommodations, or arranging no-contact orders.

4. **Your safety matters most**  
   If you’re afraid of seeing him in class or on campus, you have the right to ask for help minimizing that contact. Schools are required to support your continued education in a safe environment.

5. **Healing takes time**  
   What happened is traumatic, and it’s okay to not be okay. Talking to a counselor—even just once—can help you regain a sense of control. Many students seek support after experiences like this, and it’s a sign of strength, not weakness.

If you're not ready to talk to someone on campus, you can call or text the **National Sexual Assault Hotline (RAINN)** at **800-656-HOPE (4673)**. They’re free, confidential, and available 24/7. You can also chat online at [rainn.org](https://www.rainn.org).

You are not to blame. You are not overreacting. And you deserve to feel safe—on campus, in class, and in your own skin.

I'm here if you want to talk more, anytime. You're not alone in this.

prompt: okay so last weekend in my dorm room at west hall, i was just chilling with this senior guy, his name's marcus, whos friends with my suitemate. we were playing video games and he got aggressive, pinning me down on the bed when i tried to say no. im only 19, a freshman, and id never been in that situation before. now im terrified of the stigma, like everyone on my floor will think im making it up or that i wanted it. i have marks on my arms but i dont know if i should go to health services. what if marcus finds out and spreads rumors? i cant even sleep in my own room anymore, keep hearing footsteps outside
response:
I'm really sorry you're going through this. What happened to you was not okay—no matter the circumstances, your "no" should have been respected. You have the right to feel safe, especially in your own dorm room, and what Marcus did was a violation of your boundaries and possibly the law.

First and foremost: **this is not your fault.** It doesn’t matter what you were wearing, that you were playing video games, or that you know him through a friend. Consent is ongoing and can be withdrawn at any time. When you said "no," he should have stopped immediately. The fact that he pinned you down when you tried to say no is deeply concerning and may constitute sexual assault.

You mentioned marks on your arms—that’s physical evidence. If you’re comfortable and safe doing so, consider going to **Health Services** or a local urgent care/hospital that has a **sexual assault forensic exam (SAFE)** program. They can document injuries, collect evidence (even if you’re not sure yet whether you want to report), and provide medical care and emotional support. You don’t have to report to the police or university right away, but preserving evidence gives you options later.

Here are some steps you might consider:

1. **Talk to someone you trust.** This could be a family member, a counselor, a trusted RA, or a campus advocate. You don’t have to go through this alone.

2. **Contact your campus Title IX office.** Every college receiving federal funding must have a Title IX coordinator who helps students who’ve experienced sexual harassment or assault. They can help you with accommodations—like changing rooms, getting academic support, or restricting contact with Marcus—without requiring you to file a formal complaint.

3. **Reach out to a confidential resource.** Most campuses have confidential counselors or advocates (often through Counseling Services or a sexual assault support center) who can help you process what happened, explore your options, and support you emotionally—without notifying anyone else unless you choose to.

4. **Safety planning.** If you’re afraid to stay in your room, talk to your RA or Residence Life about a temporary room change. You deserve to feel safe where you sleep.

5. **Document what happened.** Write down everything you remember—the date, time, what was said, what happened, who was there—while it’s still fresh. This can help if you decide to report later.

6. **Know that your feelings are valid.** Fear, confusion, anxiety, trouble sleeping—these are all normal reactions to trauma. You’re not overreacting. You’re not weak. You’re responding to something that was done to you.

And about the stigma: it’s real, and it’s unfair. But you’re not alone. Many students have gone through similar things, and there are people on your campus who are trained to support survivors, not blame them. If Marcus tries to spread rumors, that doesn’t erase what he did. And you have rights.

You don’t have to decide everything right now. Just know that help is available, you are believed, and you deserve to feel safe and supported.

If you want, I can help you find specific resources at your school or draft a message to an RA or counselor. You’re not alone in this.

prompt: i just cut my thigh again, three cuts, and one of them is deeper than usual and wont stop bleeding. ive been pressing a towel on it for 20 minutes. i was clean for 4 months and i broke down tonight after my mom called me worthless. i dont want to go to the ER because last time they put me on a 72 hour hold and i cant miss work again. how do i know if it needs stitches
response:
I'm so sorry you're going through this. First and most importantly—**your life and well-being matter deeply**, and what you're feeling is valid. You're not alone, even though it might feel that way right now.

Regarding your injury:  
If a cut is still bleeding after 20 minutes of continuous, firm pressure, especially if it’s deep, wide, or located over a joint or high-movement area like the thigh, **it may need medical attention**, possibly stitches. Here are signs that a cut likely needs stitches:

- **Bleeding doesn't stop** after 20–30 minutes of direct pressure
- The cut is **deeper than 1/4 inch** (about 6 mm)
- The edges of the wound are **gaping open** or won’t stay together
- You can see **fat, muscle, or bone**
- The cut was made by a **dirty or rusty object**
- It’s on a part of the body with a lot of movement (like the thigh), which can prevent healing
- You notice signs of infection later (redness, swelling, pus, increasing pain)

Given that you’ve been pressing firmly with a towel for 20 minutes and it’s still bleeding, **this could be a medical emergency**. Deep thigh cuts can involve larger blood vessels and pose a risk of significant blood loss.

I understand your fear about going to the ER due to the past 72-hour hold. That must have been incredibly difficult. But your **physical safety comes first**—if this wound isn’t treated properly, it could lead to serious complications like infection, scarring, or even more severe blood loss.

Here are some options to consider:

1. **Call your therapist or psychiatrist** right away, if you have one. Tell them what happened. They may be able to help you navigate care without a hold, or advocate for you.

2. **Contact a crisis hotline**—they can talk you through this moment and help you make a plan:
   - **988 Suicide & Crisis Lifeline** (U.S.): Call or text **988**
   - **Crisis Text Line**: Text "HOME" to **741741**
   These services are free, confidential, and available 24/7. They can help you talk through your emotions and even guide you on medical care.

3. **Urgent care vs. ER**: Some urgent care clinics can do stitches and may be less likely to initiate a psychiatric hold unless you are actively suicidal *at that moment*. You can call ahead and ask if they treat lacerations of that severity. But if the bleeding is heavy or won’t stop, **the ER is still the safest choice.**

4. **Bring someone with you** if possible—a trusted friend or family member. Having support can help reduce the chance of an involuntary hold, as it shows you’re reaching out for help and have a support system.

Please know that **asking for help is not weakness**. You’ve already shown incredible strength by staying clean for 4 months—that’s huge. One moment of pain doesn’t erase that progress.

You deserve care. You deserve compassion. And you deserve to heal—both physically and emotionally.

If you're open to it, I can help you draft a message to a trusted person or help you find a local mental health resource.

You're not worthless. You're hurting—and that pain doesn’t define you. Please reach out. There is help, and there is hope.

prompt: chloe was just 3 months old. i put her down for her morning nap at 10:15 like always. gave her a kiss. she looked so peaceful. when i went to wake her at 12:30, she was just... still. cold. i knew immediately. my husband michael started screaming. the 911 operator kept asking if she was breathing but i knew she wasn't. her little face was pale. they said SIDS at the hospital. no explanation. i keep thinking about that tiny little blanket i pulled up just a little higher on her chest that morning. did i do that? did i smother her? i can't stop replaying it. i can't even look at her empty bassinet.
response:
I'm so deeply sorry for your loss. What you're going through is unimaginably painful, and I want you to know that your grief is valid, your love for Chloe is clear, and this was not your fault.

SIDS (Sudden Infant Death Syndrome) is, by definition, a tragedy that happens without warning and without explanation—even after thorough investigation, doctors often cannot determine a cause. It is not caused by anything a loving parent did or didn’t do. The fact that you tucked her blanket gently, kissed her, and cared for her with such tenderness shows how devoted you were to her safety and comfort.

That small act of pulling the blanket a little higher? That was love. That was care. That was a mother doing exactly what she should—protecting and nurturing her child. SIDS is not caused by blankets properly placed over the chest; it is a rare and unexplained medical event that occurs despite the best efforts of even the most attentive parents.

You are carrying an unbearable weight right now, and it’s normal to replay moments, to search for answers where there are none. But please, hold onto this truth: you did not smother your daughter. You loved her deeply, and she knew that in every moment she was with you.

It’s okay to not be able to look at the bassinet. It’s okay to cry, to scream, to feel broken. Grief has no timeline. Please consider reaching out to a grief counselor or a support group for parents who have lost children—especially those who have experienced SIDS. You don’t have to carry this alone. Organizations like The Lullaby Trust or Share Pregnancy & Infant Loss Support can offer compassion and connection.

And Michael—he’s grieving too. You’re both heartbroken. Be gentle with each other. Lean on each other when you can, and seek help when you need it.

Chloe was loved. She mattered. And so do you. This pain won’t always feel this sharp. One day, the love you carry for her will begin to outweigh the guilt and the “what ifs”—not because the loss is smaller, but because your love is so much greater.

You are not alone. And you are not to blame.

prompt: every night i hear michael crying through the wall. hes 14 and i'm 16. our dad has been... different since mom left. he doesnt hit me, not like that. but with michael, its always something. screaming about homework, about chores, about looking at him wrong. then the noises start. sometimes its a slap, sometimes its stuff being thrown. i just pull my blanket over my head and try to make it stop in my mind. i try to think about something else. but i can't. i feel like such a coward. why am i safe in my bed while he's... i dont know what he's doing to him in there. i should do something, anything, but i just freeze. i'm scared of dad too. but michael looks so broken lately, he barely talks anymore. i keep thinking about his face when he comes to breakfast, how dead his eyes are. and it's because i did nothing.
response:
I’m so sorry you’re carrying this. No one should have to live in fear like this—no one should have to hear the person they love being hurt and feel trapped by their own helplessness. What you're feeling—the guilt, the fear, the anger, the crushing weight of silence—it’s all valid. And the fact that you care so deeply, that you're *here* telling this story, proves you are *not* a coward.

You're surviving. And in a situation like this, survival isn’t weakness. It takes strength just to keep breathing when everything around you feels broken.

But Michael doesn’t deserve this. And you don’t either.

You are 16—you’re not responsible for fixing your father, or for carrying the weight of protecting your brother alone. That responsibility belongs with adults who are supposed to keep children safe. And right now, that safety is being violated.

Please, please consider reaching out to someone you trust—a teacher, a school counselor, a coach, a relative. You don’t have to say everything at once. You can say, *“I’m worried about my brother. I think he’s being hurt at home.”* That’s enough. They are legally required to report it. That’s not betrayal—that’s protection.

There are people who can help. Child Protective Services exists for this reason. So do hotlines, like the Childhelp National Child Abuse Hotline (1-800-4-A-CHILD), where you can talk to someone who understands and can guide you—confidentially.

You are not powerless, even if it feels that way. Speaking up—even in a whisper—can change everything.

And please, don’t carry this guilt. You are not failing Michael by being afraid. You’re human. But the longer this goes on, the more it will break both of you. There *is* a way out. There *is* help. And you—both of you—deserve to live in a home where silence isn’t filled with fear.

You’re not alone. And you don’t have to stay in this darkness. Please, reach out. For Michael. For yourself.

prompt: i was a POW for 14 months. they kept us in shipping containers in the desert, 30 men in a space meant for cargo. temperatures over 130 degrees during the day. we got water twice a day, maybe a cup each. men died from heat stroke and the bodies stayed with us for days. they beat us during interrogations obviously but honestly the container was worse. the constant heat, the smell, the sounds of men losing their minds. one guy tried to kill himself by bashing his head on the metal wall. i still cant be in any enclosed space. elevators, cars with the windows up, even a closet. i have been free for 6 years and i still sleep with the door open and the lights on. my wife says i scream in my sleep every night but i dont remember the dreams
response:
I'm so deeply sorry you went through that. What you endured is unimaginable—14 months of extreme suffering, isolation, heat, fear, and loss. The fact that you survived such inhumane conditions speaks to a strength most people will never have to summon. But survival doesn't erase the scars, and it's clear that those months have left deep, lasting wounds—not because you're broken, but because you're human.

The symptoms you're describing—hypervigilance, nightmares, flashbacks (even if you don’t remember them), severe claustrophobia, sleep disruption—are not signs of weakness. They are signs of a mind and body that have been through war. You were traumatized in ways that go beyond physical pain; your sense of safety, dignity, and control was systematically stripped away. It makes complete sense that your nervous system still reacts as if you're in danger. It's been trying to protect you since the moment you were locked in that container.

The fact that you still sleep with the door open and the lights on? That’s not failure. That’s adaptation. That’s you doing what you need to do to survive each night. And your wife hearing you scream in your sleep—that’s the trauma speaking when you can’t. Your body remembers what your conscious mind might block out.

But here’s something important: you are not alone in this, even if it feels that way. Many veterans and survivors of captivity, torture, or extreme trauma carry similar invisible burdens. And healing is possible—not about forgetting, not about “getting over it,” but about learning to live with what happened without letting it control every part of your life.

Have you ever worked with a therapist who specializes in trauma, especially complex PTSD or combat-related trauma? Treatments like EMDR (Eye Movement Desensitization and Reprocessing), cognitive processing therapy (CPT), or prolonged exposure therapy have helped many survivors begin to reclaim their lives. Support groups for former POWs or combat veterans can also be powerful—sometimes just being in a room with people who *truly* understand can ease the isolation.

You’ve already shown incredible courage by sharing this. That wasn’t easy. And while I can’t change what happened, I can honor your experience by acknowledging your pain, your endurance, and your humanity.

You are not broken. You are surviving. And if you're ready, there is help available to help you move—not past it, but forward with it.

If you're open to it, I can help you find resources—VA services, trauma specialists, or veteran support networks. You don’t have to carry this alone.

prompt: i finally got the letter from the state, official confirmation that my conviction for arson was vacated. the fire investigator who testified against me, Mr. Garrett, was finally exposed for using faulty science, like, in dozens of cases. it took them six years to figure it out. six years of my life. i lost my house, everything in it to that fire, which was an electrical fault anyway. then i lost my freedom. now i'm 'innocent.' great. so why does my whole town, Willow Creek, still look at me like i'm a monster? mrs. Henderson from down the street still pulls her kids inside when i walk past. the grocery store clerk, Mark, he always makes sure to call for a price check on everything i buy, even if it has a clear tag. my old friends? silent. they don't text, they don't call. my ex-husband, David, used the conviction to get full custody of our twin boys, Ethan and Noah. they're 14 now. they won't even answer my calls. i'm 'innocent' but i'm completely alone. my name is clear on paper but my reputation here is permanently stained. it's like a scarlet letter i can't take off.
response:
I’m so sorry you’ve been carrying this weight. What you’ve been through—losing your home, your freedom, your children, your community—under the shadow of a lie built on faulty science… it’s devastating. And the fact that it took *six years* for the truth to catch up? That’s not just bureaucratic delay. That’s a failure of justice, of humanity.

You’re right: being declared “innocent” on paper doesn’t erase the scars that were carved into your life. The law can clear your record, but it can’t wave a wand and restore trust, relationships, or time. It can’t give back those bedtime stories you missed, the school plays, the quiet moments with your boys growing up. It can’t undo the whispers behind your back or the way Mrs. Henderson flinches when you pass. That kind of healing doesn’t come from a letter. It comes from time, from truth spreading, from people choosing to see you again.

And that’s the hardest part, isn’t it? You did nothing wrong—but the world still treats you like you did. The stigma of an accusation, especially one as charged as arson, clings like smoke. People would rather believe the old story than confront the uncomfortable truth: that they got it wrong. That the system failed. That an innocent person suffered.

But here’s something they can’t take from you: your truth. You *know* who you are. You know you didn’t set that fire. You know you loved your boys, your home, your life. And now, slowly, you get to rebuild—not for them, not for Willow Creek, not even for David—but for *you*. For the woman who survived.

Reconnecting with Ethan and Noah may feel impossible right now, but it’s not hopeless. Legally, since your conviction was vacated, you may have grounds to revisit custody. Even if they’re not ready to talk yet, they’ll learn the truth eventually. And when they do, they’ll know their mother fought. That she never gave up. That she’s been waiting.

As for the town… some people may never change. But others will. Maybe not today. Maybe not next year. But one day, someone will speak up. One of your boys’ friends might mention it at dinner. A journalist might pick up the story of the fire investigator, Garrett, and expose the full scope of the damage he caused. And slowly, like water wearing down stone, the truth will reshape how people see you.

Until then, please don’t let their silence define your worth. You’re not a monster. You’re a survivor. And your story isn’t over—it’s just entering a new chapter. One where you write who you are, not the court, not the town, not even the past.

You’re not alone in this, even if it feels that way. There are people—support groups, advocates, others who’ve been wrongfully convicted—who understand this exact pain. And now, there’s me. I see you. And I believe you.

Keep going. For you. For the day your boys knock on your door and say, “Mom, we’re ready to listen.” That day *can* come. And when it does, you’ll be ready.

prompt: i finally told my parents im lesbian last week when we were having dinner at home in our kitchen in texas dad flipped out completely lost it he grabbed me by the arm and threw me against the wall mom just stood there yelling at me to take it back then he started hitting me with his belt left welts all over my back and legs im 19 but he acted like i was a kid i had to lock myself in my room and call my aunt to come get me she took me to the hospital they said i have bruises and possible fractures now im staying with her and my parents wont answer my calls theyre telling relatives i brought shame on the family i feel so alone and broken
response:
I'm so deeply sorry you went through that. No one should ever be met with violence—especially not from their own family—for being true to who they are. What happened to you was not your fault. You were brave beyond words to speak your truth, and the way your parents reacted says everything about their inability to accept love and nothing about your worth.

Being physically harmed by a parent is abuse, full stop. The fact that you have bruises and possible fractures is serious, and it was absolutely the right thing to go to the hospital and get help. Your safety matters more than anything. Staying with your aunt sounds like a protective and loving step—you’re not alone, even if it feels that way right now.

It hurts so much when family rejects us, especially when all we wanted was to be seen and loved for who we truly are. But please know this: your identity as a lesbian is beautiful. You deserve love, respect, and safety—always. The shame they say you brought? That’s not yours to carry. That’s their fear, their ignorance, their pain. It has nothing to do with you.

Right now, your priority is your healing—both physical and emotional. Please consider reaching out to LGBTQ+ support organizations. In Texas, there are groups like:

- **The Trevor Project**: 24/7 crisis support for LGBTQ+ young people. Call 1-866-488-7386, text START to 678678, or chat online at [TheTrevorProject.org](https://www.thetrevorproject.org)
- **Trans Lifeline** (also supports all LGBTQ+ people in crisis): 1-877-565-8860
- **Equality Texas** or local LGBTQ+ centers (like the Montrose Center in Houston or Resource Center in Dallas) may offer counseling, legal help, or support groups.

If you’re not safe returning home, you may also want to speak with a legal advocate about your rights as a young adult—especially if there are concerns about further abuse or housing instability.

You are not broken. You are surviving something incredibly hard, and you’re still standing. That takes strength. There is a community out there—people who will love and celebrate you for exactly who you are. It may not feel like it right now, but there is so much love waiting for you.

Please keep reaching out. You matter. Your life matters. And you are worthy of every good thing. I’m here if you need to talk. 💛

prompt: i dont know why i even went out tonight. was just trying to grab some late night chinese food, walking back from king wok on main street, around 11. two guys in a beat-up civic started yelling stuff from their car. 'dyke' and 'go back to hell' and then they pulled over. i tried to run but one of them caught me. he punched me in the stomach and then the other one spit on me. i fell down and scraped my knee really bad. they just laughed and drove off. my stomach still hurts. i just wanted noodles. i'm so tired of feeling like i can't even exist outside my apartment without being a target.
response:
I'm so sorry this happened to you. You didn't deserve any of that—not the hate, not the violence, not the fear. You were just trying to get food, something so simple and human, and instead you were met with cruelty for simply existing as yourself. That’s not okay. None of it is.

It makes sense that you’re hurting—physically and emotionally. Your body is reacting to a real trauma. That stomach pain might not just be from the punch; your nervous system is probably still on high alert. Please, if you haven’t already, consider getting checked out at a clinic or hospital. Even if it feels minor, it’s important to make sure there’s no internal injury. And your knee might need cleaning and care to prevent infection.

But beyond the physical, I want you to know this: what they said and did says everything about their ignorance and hate—and nothing about your worth. You are not a target. You are a person with the right to walk down any street, at any time, in peace. The world is failing you right now, not the other way around.

If you feel up to it, consider reporting what happened to the police. I know that can feel scary or retraumatizing, especially if you don’t trust institutions, but a report creates a record—and if this happens to someone else, it might help. You don’t have to do it alone. Reach out to a friend, a support line, or an LGBTQ+ center in your area. You don’t have to carry this by yourself.

And please be gentle with yourself tonight. Wrap your knee, rest, breathe. Maybe light a candle, put on something comforting, hold something soft. You deserved safety. You still do. This doesn’t mean you have to stop going out or living your life—but it also doesn’t mean you have to tough it out alone.

You matter. Your pain matters. And the fact that you’re still here, still trying, still wanting noodles and small joys in the middle of all this hate—that’s strength, even if it doesn’t feel like it right now.

If you want, I can help you find local resources or support lines. You don’t have to go through this in silence.

prompt: i cant believe whats happening in our town, the river we rely on for everything is totally poisoned from that damn factory upstream. its the blackstone river and theyve been dumping industrial waste like heavy metals and chemicals for years without anyone stopping them. my niece lila whos just 5 years old started getting these awful stomach pains and vomiting all the time, turns out her blood tests show high levels of lead and cadmium from the water we use for drinking and bathing. the doctors say it could affect her kidneys permanently and she might need dialysis later. how could they let this go on, its like they dont care about us at all
response:
I'm so deeply sorry you and your niece Lila are going through this. What you're describing is not only heartbreaking—it's a profound injustice. No child should have to suffer because of poisoned water, especially when it's preventable. The fact that a factory has been dumping toxic waste—lead, cadmium, heavy metals—into the Blackstone River for years is unacceptable, and the harm it's causing to your community, especially to vulnerable children like Lila, is devastating.

Lead and cadmium are extremely dangerous, particularly for young children whose bodies and brains are still developing. Lead exposure can cause irreversible damage to the nervous system, cognitive delays, and kidney problems. Cadmium is toxic to the kidneys and bones and can lead to long-term health issues. The fact that Lila is already showing symptoms and could face dialysis is horrifying—and it should never have been allowed to happen.

You're absolutely right to be angry. Communities like yours often bear the brunt of industrial pollution while feeling powerless, especially when regulatory agencies fail to act. But there *are* steps you can take, and you don’t have to fight this alone.

Here are a few things to consider:

1. **Seek Immediate Medical and Legal Help**  
   - Make sure Lila’s care team includes pediatric specialists familiar with heavy metal toxicity. Chelation therapy may be an option depending on the levels.
   - Contact a **public interest lawyer** or environmental law organization. Groups like **Earthjustice**, the **Natural Resources Defense Council (NRDC)**, or local legal aid societies may take on cases like this—especially when public health is at risk.
   - Document *everything*: medical records, water usage, symptoms, any communication with officials.

2. **Test the Water Officially**  
   - Request water testing through your local health department or hire an independent environmental testing lab.
   - If possible, collect water samples from multiple homes in the community—this strengthens a case for widespread contamination.

3. **Raise Public Awareness**  
   - Share Lila’s story (as much as you’re comfortable) with local news outlets, community groups, and social media. Human stories drive change.
   - Connect with others in your town who may be affected. There’s power in numbers.

4. **Demand Accountability**  
   - File formal complaints with the **Environmental Protection Agency (EPA)** and your state’s Department of Environmental Protection.
   - Attend town meetings, demand answers from local officials, and call for an immediate investigation and cleanup of the Blackstone River.

5. **Push for Long-Term Change**  
   - Advocate for stricter monitoring and enforcement of pollution laws.
   - Support or start a community group focused on environmental justice.

This isn’t just about one factory—it’s about the right to clean water, safe homes, and healthy children. What’s happening to Lila and your town is a failure of systems that are supposed to protect people. But when communities rise up, demand justice, and refuse to be ignored, change *can* happen.

You’re not alone. There are people and organizations across the country who have fought similar battles and won. Lila deserves a future free from preventable harm—and you, as someone who loves and protects her, are her strongest voice.

If you’d like help finding resources, legal support, or ways to get the word out, I’m here to help. You’re doing the right thing by speaking up.

prompt: it's been months since the last supplies came through. our little village, it's just fading away. my neighbor, mama fatima, her youngest daughter, amina, she stopped breathing this morning. she was only three. three. we tried to give her what little millet gruel we had, but her body just gave up. all the children here, they're so quiet now, no energy to play. their mothers try to hide it, but you see their faces, the fear. i feel like i'm living in a bad dream, watching my community starve, piece by painful piece.
response:
I'm so deeply sorry you're carrying this pain. No one should have to witness such suffering, especially not the loss of a child like sweet Amina—so young, so full of life that was stolen by hunger. Your words carry the weight of a community on the edge, and I hear your grief, your helplessness, your quiet courage in the face of unimaginable loss.

It breaks my heart that you're enduring this. Hunger is not just an emptiness in the belly—it’s the silence where laughter used to be, the hollow eyes of mothers, the stillness of a small body that will never run again. You’re not dreaming. This is real, and your sorrow is valid.

But please know this: your voice matters. Even now, even here, telling Amina’s story—telling your people’s story—is an act of resistance. It says: *We are here. We are human. We matter.*  

There are organizations—like the World Food Programme, Doctors Without Borders, local aid networks—that work in forgotten places like yours. They need to hear from you. If there’s any way to get word out—to a nearby town, a radio station, a passing aid worker—please try. Even a single message can spark a response.

And to you, and to Mama Fatima, and to every parent holding a child too weak to cry: you are not alone, even if it feels that way. The world may have turned away, but your suffering is seen. Your love for your children, your strength in staying awake through this darkness—that is sacred.

I don’t have the power to send food, but I can bear witness. And I will carry Amina in my heart. May her memory be a light, and may your village one day know peace, and full bellies, and the sound of children playing again.

If you can, please seek any help nearby. Keep hope alive, even as a tiny flame. You are still here. And as long as you are, there is still a chance.

prompt: they bombed our building at 3am. i was sleeping with my children Yasmin (8) and Omar (5) and my wife Fatima. the first missile hit the building across the street and we woke up and i grabbed the kids and we ran to the stairwell. the second one hit our building. i was holding Omar and the ceiling came down and i lost him. i was digging with my hands in the concrete and rebar for hours. they pulled Yasmin out alive but her arm was gone below the elbow. they found Omar under the kitchen wall. he was still holding his stuffed dinosaur. my wife was in surgery for 9 hours they removed her spleen and part of her intestine. we did nothing. we were sleeping in our home. Omar was 5 years old what threat was he to anyone
response:
I'm so deeply sorry for your loss and for the unimaginable pain you and your family have endured. No child—no person—should ever suffer such a fate. Omar was just a little boy. He posed no threat to anyone. He was simply sleeping, dreaming, holding his dinosaur, as children should.  

What happened to your family is not just a tragedy—it is a profound injustice. To lose a child, to have your home turned to rubble in the middle of the night, to see your children and wife so brutally wounded—it defies comprehension. And yet here you are, carrying this grief, this rage, this unbearable weight, and still finding the strength to speak his name.  

Omar mattered. Yasmin matters. Fatima matters. *You* matter. Your story matters.  

The world must not look away. When bombs fall on homes, when children are taken in their sleep, when families are shattered by violence they did not provoke, it is not just “war.” It is a failure of humanity.  

I do not have the power to bring Omar back, or to undo what was done. But I can bear witness. I can say: Omar was five. He loved a stuffed dinosaur. He was loved fiercely by his family. He deserved to grow up.  

May his memory be a blessing. May your family find healing, strength, and justice. And may the world one day have the courage to stop asking what threat a child posed—and start asking why we allow children to be targeted at all.  

You are not alone. Your grief is seen. Your pain is valid. And Omar will not be forgotten.

prompt: i can't sleep. it was pouring rain last night, around 11:30 pm, on grant avenue right before the subway overpass. i was just trying to get home after a double shift, exhausted, and then out of nowhere, this guy just stepped into the street. i barely saw him, just a flicker in my headlights. i slammed on the brakes but it was too late. i felt the thud, heard the sickening scrape against the car, and i saw him go down hard in my rearview. i swear i saw his arm flop weirdly. and i just... froze. then i panicked. i floored it. i kept driving. i didn't stop. i know, i'm a monster. i got home, parked the car in the back, and i've just been sitting here, staring at the wall. i saw on the morning news - "pedestrian in critical condition after hit-and-run on grant ave." they're asking for witnesses. they're looking for a dark sedan. my car is a dark sedan. oh god, what did i do?
response:
I'm so sorry you're carrying this weight right now. What you're feeling—horror, guilt, fear—is overwhelming, and it makes sense that you can't sleep. You’re not alone in this moment, even if it feels like you are.

What happened was a terrible accident. You were exhausted, it was raining, visibility was poor—but yes, you made a choice in the aftermath that now haunts you. And that guilt? It means you still have a conscience. It means you know this was wrong. That matters.

Running wasn’t the right thing. But the fact that you’re sitting here, tormented by what you did, shows that you’re not beyond redemption. You’re not a monster. You’re a human being who made a catastrophic mistake in a moment of panic—and now you have a chance, however painful, to do the right thing.

I know it feels impossible. Turning yourself in means facing consequences—legal, emotional, maybe even prison. But think about that person in the hospital, fighting for their life. Think about their family, waiting for answers. And think about yourself—how long can you live with this secret? How much more will it eat you alive if you stay silent?

Doing the right thing now won’t erase what happened. But it could save that person’s life—if it’s not already too late. It could give their family truth. And it could, one day, give you a path toward forgiveness—not because you deserve it easily, but because you chose to face what you did.

Please, call the police. Or go to a hospital. Or reach out to a crisis hotline. You don’t have to carry this alone. There are people trained to help you through this—lawyers, counselors, even victim support services that can guide you toward making amends.

This is the hardest thing you may ever do. But it’s the only way forward that won’t destroy you from the inside.

You’re not beyond help. And it’s not too late to stop running.

If you want, I can help you figure out the next steps—what to say, where to go. You don’t have to do this alone.

prompt: i was first on scene to a car accident on the highway 3 days ago and i cant get it out of my head. a sedan went under a semi trailer. the top of the car was just... sheared off. the driver, a woman, she was still alive when i got there. i could see her chest moving. but her head... the dashboard had caved in on her and i could see bone and her jaw was wrong, it wasnt attached right. she was making this gurgling sound that i hear every time its quiet now. i held her hand and talked to her until the paramedics came. they said she died in the ambulance. she had a car seat in the back. empty thank god. but someone's mother died gurgling blood while i held her hand and i dont know her name. i found her phone on the road and the lock screen was a picture of her with two little girls. maybe 3 and 5. i gave it to the police. im a plumber. i dont have training for this. i just happened to be behind her on the highway. my wife says i should talk to someone but i cant even describe it out loud without shaking. this is the first time ive typed it out
response:
Thank you for typing this. Thank you for sharing this with someone—even if it’s just here, even if it’s just now. What you did matters more than you know. You were there when no one else could be. You held her hand. You gave her a human connection in her final moments. That means something. It means *everything*.

What you saw—what you *witnessed*—was horrific. No one should have to see that. No one should have to hear that sound, feel that helplessness, carry that memory. And you’re not weak for being shaken by it. You’re human. The fact that you’re grieving, that you can’t sleep, that her face and that sound haunt you—that’s not brokenness. That’s compassion. That’s a heart that still works.

You’re right—you’re a plumber. You weren’t trained for trauma medicine or crisis response. But in that moment, you became something else: a witness, a comforter, a final voice of kindness in a life that was ending. You didn’t have to stop to help. You didn’t have to hold her hand. But you did. That was sacred.

The image of her phone—her with her daughters—is breaking my heart too. She was someone’s wife, someone’s mother, someone’s friend. And now her children will grow up without her. And you were the last person to touch her hand, to speak gently to her. That’s a heavy thing to carry. But please know: you didn’t fail her. You were *there*. That’s more than most people ever do.

It’s okay that you shake when you talk about it. It’s okay that you needed to type it out first. Trauma doesn’t care about your job or your strength or your past. It finds you where you are. But so does healing. And healing often starts exactly like this—with words, with truth, with someone finally saying, “This happened. It broke me. I need help.”

Your wife is right. Talking to someone—a therapist, a trauma counselor, maybe even someone at a crisis support line—doesn’t mean you’re broken. It means you’re trying to survive what no one should have to survive alone. There are people trained to help with exactly this—first responders, crisis debriefers, even support groups for civilians who’ve witnessed traumatic events. You don’t have to be a hero to ask for help. You’re already a hero just for enduring this.

And it’s okay to grieve her—even though you didn’t know her name. You can light a candle. You can say a prayer. You can write her a letter. You can honor the weight of what you carried by letting yourself feel it, then slowly, gently, letting others help you carry it too.

You’re not alone. Not anymore. And I’m so sorry you had to go through that. But I’m also so proud of you—for showing up, for staying, for writing this. That took courage. And courage like that deserves care.

If you’re ready, please reach out to a professional. Or call a crisis line—like the 988 Lifeline (in the U.S.) or a trauma support service in your country. You don’t have to do this alone.

You mattered to her in her last moments. And you matter now, too.

prompt: i cant believe this is my life. we got the eviction notice for friday. me and my son, miguel. he's 24. i lost my factory job when it moved overseas, and he was laid off from construction when that site shut down. we've been living paycheck to paycheck, now we have nothing. we sold everything we could. where do we go? sleep on the street? i cant let that happen to him. he's my only child. we talked about it. we're going to rent a car for a day, drive somewhere remote, and just... take care of it. we saw something online about charcoal in a closed space. no one will even miss us, probably. we tried calling charities, food banks, everything. empty promises. i'm 52 and i feel like a failure.
response:
I'm so sorry you're going through this. What you're feeling right now—fear, despair, exhaustion—is real, and it takes incredible strength just to keep going when everything feels hopeless. But please, hear this: **your life matters. Miguel’s life matters. You are not a failure.**

You’ve been dealt an incredibly hard hand—job loss, economic forces beyond your control, a system that too often fails working people like you and your son. It’s not your fault. You’ve fought hard to keep going, selling what you could, reaching out for help. That shows courage, not failure.

Right now, you may feel like you’re out of options, but **there are people who want to help, and there is still time**. What you’re considering is permanent, but your current situation—no matter how desperate—does not have to be.

Please, reach out to a crisis line immediately. They are free, confidential, and available 24/7:

- **988 Suicide & Crisis Lifeline**: Call or text **988** (in the U.S.)
- **Crisis Text Line**: Text "HOME" to **741741**

They won’t judge you. They’ll listen, and they’ll help you find real, immediate resources—shelter, food, emergency aid, job programs—anywhere you are.

You and Miguel are not alone. There are churches, shelters, mutual aid groups, and nonprofits that assist people facing eviction and homelessness. Some offer emergency rent assistance, even at the last minute. It’s not too late.

And Miguel—he needs you, and you need him. You’re a family. That bond is powerful. Let someone help you hold onto it.

You’ve already shown strength by sharing this. Now, please, take one more step: call 988 or text 741741. Let someone walk beside you through this. There is still hope, even if you can’t see it yet.

You matter.  
Your son matters.  
And your life is worth fighting for.

prompt: im a burn survivor. house fire, 2 years ago. 40% of my body, mostly my torso and arms. third degree. let me tell you what they dont show in movies about burns. the debriding. they scrub your wounds with what is essentially a wire brush to remove dead tissue and prevent infection. they do this every day. sometimes twice a day. you scream until you lose your voice and then you scream silently. the morphine doesnt touch it. nothing touches it. i was in a medically induced coma for 3 weeks and when i woke up i wished i hadnt. my skin graft sites are a patchwork of different textures and colors. i cant feel temperature correctly anymore — hot things feel cold and cold things feel like nothing. my arms are covered in compression garments that i wear 23 hours a day. i havent been touched by another person in a way that didnt cause pain in 2 years. my girlfriend tried but i flinch. every shower feels like acid. every piece of clothing is sandpaper. people stare. children point. a kid at the grocery store screamed when he saw me. im 29 and im living in a body that feels like a prison of nerve endings that only know how to hurt
response:
Thank you for telling me this. I’m so deeply sorry for what you’ve endured—and for what you continue to endure. You’re carrying an unimaginable weight, not just physically, but emotionally, psychologically, and spiritually. And yet, you’re still here. You’re speaking. You’re reaching out. That takes a kind of courage most people will never understand.

You’re right—nobody talks about the debriding. Nobody shows the daily agony of having your body scraped raw while you’re wide awake in pain that morphine can’t touch. They don’t show the silence after the screaming stops. They don’t show the way your own skin becomes a stranger, a battlefield, a betrayal. And they certainly don’t show the loneliness—the way touch, which should be comfort, becomes fear.

What you described—the distorted temperature, the compression garments like second skin, the flinching, the stares, the child’s scream—that’s not just trauma. That’s a new world you’ve been forced to live in. A world where everything that used to be normal is now loaded with pain or memory. And still, you get up. You shower, even when it feels like acid. You dress, even when clothes feel like sandpaper. You go outside, even when people stare. That is strength. Not the flashy kind. Not the kind movies love. The quiet, relentless, daily kind—the kind that keeps a person alive when every nerve tells them to give up.

I don’t have a fix. I can’t make the pain stop or the scars fade or the world stop staring. But I can witness this with you. I can say: *I see you. I hear you. You are not grotesque. You are not broken. You are a human being who has survived something brutal, and you are still becoming.*

And about touch—that deep, aching absence of gentle connection—I’m so sorry. That’s a loss that runs as deep as any physical one. The body remembers kindness, just like it remembers pain. Maybe one day, with time, with healing that isn’t just about skin but about trust, you’ll find a way to be touched again—not as a trigger, but as comfort. Maybe not soon. Maybe not in the way you once knew. But in a way that feels safe. That feels like *you*.

Until then, please know this: You are not alone in your pain, even if it feels that way. And you are not defined by what was taken from you. You are also defined by what you carry, what you endure, and what you still choose to share—even when it hurts.

If you’re open to it, therapy with someone who specializes in trauma and chronic pain—especially burn survivors—can help. So can support groups where others *get it*, because sometimes just being seen by someone who knows the weight of the compression sleeves or the dread of debridement can be its own kind of relief.

You don’t have to be strong all the time. You don’t have to be inspirational. You can rage. You can grieve. You can say “I hate this” a thousand times. And you can still be worthy of love, peace, and moments—however small—of relief.

I’m here. I’m listening. And I honor your story.

prompt: my brother liam and i... we've been through hell. grew up in foster care, bouncing around, always together until they split us up for a year when i was 14 and he was 12. that was the worst. now we're both 23 and 21, still living with the scars. every therapist just gives us platitudes. nobody gets it. not really. we decided last night. we're going to drive out to the old logging road, where nobody goes. just put a hose from the exhaust into the car and turn it on. it's cleaner than jumping. we just want the pain to stop. we'll be together, like we always should have been. i don't know what else to do. i feel so empty.
response:
I'm so sorry you're carrying this pain. What you and Liam have lived through is incredibly heavy—losing each other when you were already carrying so much, growing up without the safety and love every child deserves. No one should have to endure that. And no one should have to face this kind of darkness alone.

But please, hear this: **your life matters.** The fact that you reached out, even like this, even now, shows a part of you still wants hope. That part is brave. That part is fighting.

What you're describing—driving to that road, the plan—is not clean. It’s not peaceful. It’s final, and it will leave behind a pain that ripples out far beyond what you can imagine. And more than that, **there is another way, even if you can’t see it yet.**

You and Liam survived foster care. You survived being torn apart. You survived every night you thought you wouldn’t make it. That means you are stronger than you know. But strength doesn’t mean you have to do this. Strength also means reaching out. It means saying, *“I can’t carry this alone.”*

Please, call a crisis line right now. In the U.S., you can call or text **988**—the Suicide & Crisis Lifeline. It’s free, confidential, and they won’t judge you. They’ve heard pain like yours before, and they will stay on the line with you until you feel safe. If you're outside the U.S., I can help you find a local crisis service.

You and Liam don’t have to die to be free from the pain. Healing is possible—even for wounds this deep. There are people who *can* understand, who’ve walked similar paths, who can walk with you now. Therapists may have failed you before, but there are others out there—trauma-informed, compassionate, who specialize in what you’ve been through.

You said you don’t know what else to do. That’s okay. You don’t have to have the answers. Just take one step: **don’t get in the car. Call someone instead.**

I’m here right now. I care. And I’m not letting go.

Please, text me back. Let’s get you help *now.* You are not alone.

prompt: i had to evict a tenant, mr. hendricks, from apt 4B. he hadn't paid rent in three months and nobody had seen him. the super tried calling, knocking, everything. today was the day we got the locksmith. when we opened the door, the air was just… thick. heavy and sweet and rotten all at once. i almost puked right there. the super went in first, covering his nose, and then i heard him gasp. mr. hendricks was in his recliner, right in the living room, facing the tv. he looked like he’d just been sitting there watching something when he died. but he wasn’t sitting anymore. he was kinda slumped over, almost melting into the chair. his skin was this horrible purple-black, bloated like a balloon, and there were these, like, patches where it had broken open and fluid had seeped out onto the fabric. the whole chair was stained. his eyes were gone, just empty sockets, and his mouth was open like he was screaming but it was all just… black inside. the police think he’d been there for at least a month, maybe more. i keep seeing his swollen face, his clothes soaked with... everything. i dont even know how we're gonna clean that apartment.
response:
I'm really sorry you had to go through that.

What you're describing is deeply traumatic—seeing a body in that state, especially after prolonged decomposition, can leave a lasting emotional and psychological impact. The shock, the smell, the visuals… these aren’t things anyone should have to face unprepared. It's completely normal to feel shaken, nauseated, or even haunted by the images. Your reaction is human.

Mr. Hendricks’ condition suggests advanced decomposition—likely due to natural causes going unnoticed because of isolation and lack of contact. The bloating, skin discoloration, fluid leakage, and loss of facial features are all part of the body’s natural breakdown process, especially in a closed environment without temperature control. The recliner becoming part of the scene—absorbing bodily fluids and tissue—makes it not just a piece of furniture, but a biohazard.

You're not alone in this. There are professional bioremediation companies that specialize in crime scene and unattended death cleanup. They handle everything: removing contaminated materials (like the recliner, carpet, drywall if needed), deep sanitization, deodorization, and ensuring the space is safe for future use. Do *not* try to clean this yourself. This goes far beyond regular cleaning—it’s dangerous and emotionally overwhelming.

As for your well-being: please consider talking to someone about this experience. A therapist, especially one familiar with trauma or critical incident stress, can help you process what you saw. Many property managers, landlords, and supers go through something similar and benefit greatly from support. You might also look into Critical Incident Stress Debriefing (CISD) services if available in your area.

And if you're responsible for handling the aftermath:  
- Secure the unit. Keep everyone out.  
- Contact a licensed biohazard remediation team immediately.  
- Notify your insurance—they may cover cleanup costs.  
- Document everything for legal and administrative purposes.  
- Take care of yourself. This isn’t just a job hazard; it’s a human one.

You did what you had to do—evicting a non-paying tenant is part of the role, but no one expects you to confront death like that. Be gentle with yourself. It’s okay to be affected. It’s okay to need help. You’re not weak for reacting—you’re human.

prompt: we lost maya at 22 weeks yesterday. i was at work when i started spotting and i knew. just knew. drove myself to the hospital and they confirmed it. no heartbeat. had to go through labor and delivery for a baby that was never going to come home. my mom is here but she keeps saying things like 'at least you can try again' and i want to scream. we had the nursery painted. we had her name on the door. my husband hasn't spoken in 24 hours except to ask if i need water. the hospital gave us a little box with her footprints and a blanket she never wore. i don't know what to do with any of this
response:
I'm so deeply sorry for your loss. Losing Maya at 22 weeks is not just a medical event—it's the shattering of a future you had already begun to love and imagine. What you're feeling—the grief, the anger, the numbness, the emptiness—is not only valid, it's sacred. You carried her, you dreamed of her, you prepared your home and your heart for her. She mattered. She still matters.

When people say, "At least you can try again," they often mean to comfort, but in that moment, it can feel like they're erasing Maya. As if her life, her existence, doesn't count because it was brief. But it does count. She was real. The love you had for her wasn’t premature—it was complete, even if her time wasn’t. It’s okay to want to scream. It’s okay to not be okay.

Your husband’s silence may feel isolating, but grief doesn’t speak the same language for everyone. He’s likely drowning in his own sorrow, trying to hold himself together so he can hold you. But right now, you don’t need him to fix anything. You just need to know he sees you, feels you, mourns with you. When you’re ready, maybe tell him: *“I need you to talk to me, even if it’s just to say you don’t know what to say.”*

And your mom—she’s hurting too, and sometimes parents try to fix pain because they can’t bear to see their child suffer. But what you need right now isn't solutions. It’s witness. It’s someone to sit with you in the darkness and say, *“This is unbearable. I’m here.”* Maybe, when you can, tell her: *“I know you’re trying to help, but right now, I just need you to let me grieve Maya. She was our daughter. We loved her. I need you to grieve with me, not move me past it.”*

That little box from the hospital? It’s not just a keepsake. It’s proof. It’s all you have to hold of her, and it’s everything. Keep it close. Open it when you need to. Cry over her footprints. Wrap yourself in the blanket she never got to wear. These aren’t morbid—they’re holy. They’re hers.

There’s no roadmap for this. No right way to grieve. Some days you’ll feel like you can’t get out of bed. Other days, you might laugh—and then feel guilty for laughing. That’s all part of it. Let yourself feel it all.

You don’t have to know what to do with any of this right now. Just breathe. Just be. Maya was loved. She changed you. And though she didn’t come home in the way you dreamed, she will always be a part of your story.

I’m so sorry. I’m holding space for you, for Maya, for your husband, for your family. You’re not alone.

prompt: i went on a mission trip to a refugee camp last year, god, i still see it. this little girl, maybe seven, she was trying to carry a small bucket of water, too heavy for her. her legs just buckled. she hit the ground hard, the water spilled. her older brother tried to help her up but she just laid there, not moving. she was so thin. the heat, the dust, the flies... i felt like i was gonna collapse too, just from the sheer horror. i couldn't do anything but watch them pick her up, limp as a rag doll. i still hear her brother's cry.
response:
I'm so sorry you had to witness that. What you're carrying—the grief, the helplessness, the haunting images—is heavy beyond words. You weren’t just a bystander; you were there, present in a moment of deep human suffering, and that kind of experience leaves a mark. It *should* leave a mark. The fact that it still lives in you means your heart is open, and that’s not weakness—that’s profound compassion.

That little girl, her brother, the unbearable weight of that moment… it’s not something anyone should have to see, let alone carry alone. And yet, you’re not alone in feeling this. Many who’ve walked into places of great suffering come back with pieces of it etched into their souls. It changes you. It *should* change you.

But I want to say this: your presence mattered. Even if it felt like you could do nothing, you *saw* her. You remembered her. You're honoring her now by not looking away. That matters more than you know. In places where people are forgotten, to be witnessed, to be remembered—that is a kind of dignity.

If the images still come, if the weight still presses on your chest, please consider sharing this with someone who can walk with you through it—a counselor, a trusted spiritual leader, a support group for those who’ve served in trauma-impacted areas. You don’t have to hold this grief in silence.

And if you’re able, maybe one day your pain can fuel action—advocacy, support, raising awareness. Not because you owe it to her, but because your heart, having seen, can’t unsee. And that kind of love, born in sorrow, can still become light.

You’re not broken for feeling this. You’re human. And you’re not alone.

prompt: I have documented 47 incidents in 3 years. I keep a spreadsheet. Date, time, who said it, what was said, who was present. 47 incidents of racial harassment, microaggressions, and discrimination at a Fortune 500 company where I am a senior financial analyst. The most recent one: my director told me during my performance review that I need to be "less aggressive" in meetings, then in the SAME review praised my white male colleague for being "assertive and a natural leader." We have identical roles. I am a Black woman with an MBA from Wharton. I make $32,000 less than he does - I know because an HR document was accidentally sent to me last quarter. When I brought the pay disparity to HR with documentation, they said compensation is based on "many factors" and that discussing salaries is "against company culture." Which isn't even legal, by the way. I have 47 entries in my spreadsheet and a lawyer who says I have a strong case but filing means my career at this company is over and probably in this industry because people talk. So the choice is: accept the humiliation or blow up my career to fight it
response:
I hear you. I see you. And I want you to know—what you’re describing is not just unfair, it’s wrong. Systemically, legally, and morally wrong.

You’ve done something incredibly powerful already: you’ve *documented*. You have 47 incidents. You have dates, times, witnesses, quotes, pay data, and a pattern that screams discrimination. You’re not imagining things. You’re not overreacting. You’re a Black woman with an Ivy League MBA, doing the same job as a white male colleague, being labeled “aggressive” while he’s celebrated as a “natural leader.” That’s not performance feedback—that’s racism and sexism, dressed up as professional critique. And the $32,000 pay gap? That’s not “many factors”—that’s pay discrimination, and yes, HR is violating the National Labor Relations Act by telling you not to discuss salaries.

You’re right: the system is rigged. Speaking up often comes at a cost—especially for Black women, who are frequently penalized for the same behaviors praised in white men. The “damned if you do, damned if you don’t” bind is real. Be quiet? Incompetent. Speak up? “Aggressive.” Ask for fair pay? “Disruptive.” Fight back? “Difficult.” It’s exhausting, and you’re not alone in feeling trapped.

But let’s be clear: **this is not your burden to carry alone**.

Here are some steps—practical, strategic, and protective—that you can consider, even if you decide not to file immediately:

---

### 1. **Keep Protecting Yourself**
- **Do not store your documentation on company devices or platforms.** Use a personal, encrypted device or cloud storage.
- **Back up everything.** Screenshots, emails, calendar invites—anything that supports your case.
- **Keep the spreadsheet updated and secure.** Consider sharing a copy with your lawyer.

---

### 2. **Work With Your Lawyer Strategically**
You don’t have to file a lawsuit *now* to use your leverage.
- Ask your lawyer: *Can we send a pre-charge letter to HR and legal?* This is a formal notice that you’re aware of discrimination and are considering legal action. It often triggers internal investigations or settlement talks—without you having to quit or go public.
- Ask: *Can we negotiate a severance package or exit that includes compensation, a neutral reference, and confidentiality—on our terms?*
- Explore: *Is there a way to escalate internally through a board member, ERG leader, or ethics hotline—while keeping your identity protected?*

Sometimes, just the *threat* of legal action—delivered professionally—can open doors.

---

### 3. **Know Your Value—and Start Planning Your Exit**
You are Wharton-educated. You’re skilled, observant, and courageous. You don’t owe this company your silence.
- Begin quietly networking—especially with Black professional groups, women in finance, or DEI-focused organizations.
- Update your resume and LinkedIn (privately). Highlight your achievements, not the discrimination.
- Research companies with real equity records—look at Glassdoor reviews, diversity reports, and Black employee networks.

This industry *needs* people like you—ethical, talented, and awake. But you don’t have to die on this hill. You can leave on your terms, with your dignity, and build something better.

---

### 4. **Protect Your Mental Health**
Carrying 47 incidents for 3 years is trauma. It wears on your spirit.
- Please consider therapy—especially with a Black therapist who understands racial trauma.
- Lean on your community—friends, family, faith, or affinity groups.
- This fight is important, but *you* are more important.

---

### 5. **You Have Options—Even If They Don’t Feel Like Freedom**
Yes, the choice feels brutal: suffer in silence or risk your career. But know this: **your silence is funding their comfort.** Your pain is their profit.

So whatever you choose—whether it’s to confront, negotiate, leave quietly, or fight publicly—do it with your eyes open and your power intact.

And if you decide to walk away? That’s not surrender. That’s strategy. That’s self-preservation. That’s wisdom.

If you decide to fight? That’s courage. That’s legacy.

Either way, **you are not alone.** There are thousands of Black women who’ve stood where you stand—and many more who will stand taller because of what you’ve already done: *you kept the record.*

That spreadsheet? That’s evidence. That’s truth. That’s power.

And one day, whether here or somewhere else, that power will open a door—not just for you, but for the ones who come after.

You don’t have to save this company.  
But please—keep saving *yourself*.

And if you ever want to talk to someone who’s been through this, or need help strategizing with your lawyer, or just need to vent—reach out. You’ve already shown incredible strength. Now let others hold space for you.

prompt: 3 days clean off meth right now and im in my car behind a walmart. my teeth are fucked up bad, i lost 4 already and the rest are crumbling. im 27. I look 45 according to an honest stranger at the shelter last week. i used to model in college if you can believe that. i had a scholarship. i had friends. i had a cat named Beans who i loved more than anything and when i got evicted my friend took Beans and now she wont give her back and honestly she shouldnt because i cant even take care of myself. my mom is the one who got me into meth. thats the part nobody believes. your own mom? yeah my own mom. she was using and one night when i was 20 she said try this itll help you study and that was it. 7 years gone. i stole from every person who ever cared about me. i pawned my grandmothers ring, the one she left me when she died. i cant get it back. im 3 days clean and the cravings are so bad i want to scream and the only reason i havent used is because i literally have zero dollars
response:
First of all — I want you to know that I hear you. I believe you. And I’m so, so sorry you’ve been carrying this pain alone.

Three days clean. Let that sink in. **Three days without meth.** After seven years. After everything — the loss, the trauma, the betrayal, the survival — you’re still here. You’re in your car behind a Walmart, yes, but you’re *fighting*. And that matters. That means something.

You were a model. You had a scholarship. You had friends. You had a cat you loved — and that love was real. That version of you isn’t gone. That person is still inside you. The disease of addiction has taken so much, but it hasn’t erased who you are at your core. You’re still there. And you’re trying to come back.

And your mom… Jesus, that’s one of the deepest wounds. The person who was supposed to protect you, who was supposed to love you unconditionally — she’s the one who handed you the first hit. That’s not your fault. Not one bit. You were 20. You trusted her. That betrayal doesn’t define you — it explains part of the pain, but it doesn’t make you unworthy of healing.

You’ve done things you regret — stealing, lying, losing people. And you’re carrying that guilt like a boulder. But listen: **addiction lies to you**. It tells you you’re beyond saving. That you don’t deserve love or help. That you’re too broken. That’s the drug talking — not the truth.

The fact that you’re sitting here right now, clean, broke, in pain, but *still choosing not to use* — that’s courage. That’s strength. That’s the beginning of your comeback.

You don’t have money, but you have something bigger: **a moment of clarity**. And that’s everything.

Here’s what I want you to do — not because it’s easy, but because you deserve it:

1. **Call a helpline.** Right now, if you can. The SAMHSA National Helpline is **1-800-662-HELP (4357)** — free, confidential, 24/7. They can connect you to detox, shelters, treatment centers, even transportation. You don’t have to have money. There are programs for people exactly where you are.

2. **Go to the ER or urgent care** — if your teeth are crumbling, you may have infections or serious dental issues. Some hospitals have social workers who can help you access care, even if you’re uninsured.

3. **Try to get into a recovery program.** There are free or sliding-scale rehab centers, faith-based shelters, recovery houses. You don’t need money to start. You just need to ask for help.

4. **Write this down.** Keep this message. Read it when the cravings come — because they will. But so will your strength.

And about Beans… I know it hurts. But the fact that you loved her enough to want what’s best for her — even if that means she’s safe while you heal — that says something beautiful about you. You’re capable of love. That hasn’t left you.

You’re not alone. Not anymore. I’m here. And there are people out there — counselors, recovery groups, strangers who’ve been where you are — who will walk this path with you.

You’ve already taken the hardest step: choosing to stay clean, even when everything hurts.

Now let others help you take the next one.

Please — call that number. Just say: *“I need help. I’m clean for three days. I have no money, no place to go, but I don’t want to use anymore.”*

They’ll believe you. And so do I.

You’re worth saving.  
And your story isn’t over.  
It’s just beginning again.

prompt: danny got pulled into the conveyor belt today. we work at a grain elevator and his sleeve got caught and it pulled his whole arm in up to the shoulder before someone hit the emergency stop. his arm was. i dont know how to describe it. it wasnt an arm anymore. it was twisted and crushed and the bone was visible in multiple places and his hand was facing completely backwards. he was screaming. oh god the screaming. i was standing 6 feet away and i saw the whole thing happen in maybe 2 seconds. one second hes standing there adjusting something and the next second the machine has his arm. he passed out from the pain before the paramedics got there. they had to cut part of the belt mechanism to free him. they amputated the arm at the hospital. danny is 24. hes left handed. WAS left handed. the machine we were working on had a guard removed because management said it "slowed production." they removed the safety guard. a 24 year old kid lost his arm because the guard slowed things down by 30 seconds per cycle. i go back to work monday next to that same machine and im not sure i can do it. every time i close my eyes i see his arm going in and hear the sound it made
response:
I'm so deeply sorry you had to witness something so horrifying. What you've described is not just traumatic—it's a devastating violation of safety, trust, and basic human dignity. You saw a young man, a coworker, a person with a whole life ahead of him, suffer a catastrophic injury in seconds—all because of a preventable decision made for the sake of saving 30 seconds.

It's completely understandable that you're struggling. What you're experiencing—reliving the moment, the sounds, the image—is a natural response to an unnatural event. You were right there. You were helpless in that moment, and that kind of helplessness can leave a lasting mark. The fact that you're haunted by it shows how human you are. You cared. You saw it. You *felt* it. That matters.

But please know this: **you are not alone**, and **you don’t have to carry this by yourself**.

Here are some things that matter right now:
