You are not invisible.  
You are not beyond repair.  
And you are so deeply worthy of healing—even after 16 years.  
Even at 31.  
Even at 91 pounds.  
Especially then.

prompt: another shooting. a walmart in ohio this time. 11 dead. i cant do this anymore. one of the victims was a 4 year old girl named Lily who was there with her grandmother buying school supplies. the grandmother is dead too. Lilys mom is on the news begging for information and they havent told her yet that both of them are gone. i have a daughter lilys age. she asked me this morning if bad guys could come to OUR walmart and i didnt know what to say. i said no but thats a lie isnt it. i cant stop refreshing the news. they keep updating the victim count. the shooters manifesto is everywhere, its on twitter, people are sharing it and quoting it and giving him exactly what he wanted. im so angry and so sad and so tired of this being normal. 11 people went to buy groceries and theyre dead. a four year old is dead. and in 2 weeks nobody will remember their names except the families who have to live with this forever
response:
I'm so deeply sorry you're carrying this pain. What you're feeling—this grief, this rage, this helplessness—it's real, and it's valid. You're not alone in it, even when it feels that way. The weight of this moment is crushing because it *should* be. Eleven lives ended. A child—Lily—gone before she even got to really live. A mother on the news, still clinging to hope that might soon shatter. These are not statistics. These are people. And your heart breaking for them matters.

And you're right—this shouldn’t be normal. It *isn’t* normal. It’s a failure. A collective, ongoing failure to protect the most basic thing: the safety of people going about their ordinary lives. Buying school supplies. Grocery shopping. Existing.

You asked your daughter if bad guys could come to your Walmart. You said no. And yes, in the world as it *should* be, that would be true. But in the world as it *is*, your answer was also an act of love. You protected her, even in that moment. That doesn’t make it a lie—it makes it a shield. You're doing the hardest thing: loving her in a world that keeps threatening to take that love away.

But your grief, your fear, your anger—they matter too. It’s okay to be exhausted. It’s okay to rage at the fact that the shooter’s name is spreading while Lily’s is buried in headlines. It’s okay to feel broken by the fact that no one seems to *do* anything, not really, not enough.

But please know this: *You remembering Lily matters.* Saying her name matters. Letting yourself feel this pain matters. Because when we stop feeling it, when we numb ourselves just to survive, that’s when the world wins. When we let the horror become routine, we lose something sacred.

So keep saying her name: Lily. A four-year-old girl who loved colors and crayons and probably picked out a sparkly backpack. She mattered. Her grandmother mattered. The others who died mattered. And your love for your daughter—your fierce, aching desire to keep her safe—that matters more than you know.

This won’t be fixed today. Maybe not in weeks or months. But your grief is not passive. It’s a testament. And one day, maybe because of people like you who *can’t* look away, who *won’t* forget—we might build a world where “no, sweetheart, the bad guys can’t come” is finally, truly true.

Until then, I’m so sorry. I’m here with you. And I’m saying her name too: Lily. We remember.

prompt: they told me everything was fine. my pregnancy with liam was textbook. but then during labor, his heart rate kept dropping, for hours. i kept asking the nurses, begging for a c-section, but dr. patel said 'just a little longer, you can do this.' that 'little longer' turned into 6 more hours of pushing, and when liam finally came out, he was blue. completely floppy. now he's three, and he has severe cerebral palsy. he'll never walk, never talk properly. every day is a battle just to feed him, to change him. his brain was starved for oxygen because they waited too long. they basically decided my 'natural birth experience' was more important than my son's brain. our son, who will need care for the rest of his life, because of one doctor's ego.
response:
I'm so deeply sorry you're carrying this pain. What you've described is not just a medical complication—it's a profound betrayal of trust, a trauma that has reshaped your life and your son's future in ways no parent should ever have to face. You weren’t heard when you were pleading for help. Your instincts, your fear, your voice—ignored. And the cost has been unimaginable.

Liam's cerebral palsy wasn't inevitable. It sounds like it resulted from a preventable birth injury caused by prolonged oxygen deprivation—a situation that may have been avoided with timely intervention. The fact that you advocated for a C-section, over and over, shows your strength and intuition as a mother. You knew something was wrong. You fought for your child. And it is not only unfair but unacceptable that your concerns were dismissed in favor of a rigid adherence to a "natural birth" ideal—especially when your baby's life and well-being were at stake.

Dr. Patel’s decision to delay intervention, prioritizing a delivery method over fetal safety, may have crossed the line from poor judgment into medical negligence. What happened wasn’t just "bad luck"—it was a failure of the system meant to protect both you and your baby. You deserved better care. Liam deserved better care.

While nothing can undo the past, please know that your grief, your anger, and your exhaustion are valid. You are not alone in this. Many parents have walked similar paths, and there are support groups, advocates, and legal professionals who specialize in birth injury cases who can help you process what happened—not only emotionally but also in terms of accountability.

If you haven't already, you may want to consider speaking with a medical malpractice attorney who can review your case. Not only could this open the door to financial support for Liam’s lifelong care, but it could also ensure that no other family suffers the same way.

And through all of this, you are raising a child who is loved. Liam may face immense challenges, but he also has a mother who sees him, fights for him, and refuses to let his suffering be meaningless. That love matters more than words can say.

You are not to blame. You did everything right. The system failed you. And it's okay to be furious. It's okay to grieve. And it's okay to demand better—for Liam, for yourself, and for every family who puts their trust in doctors during one of the most vulnerable moments of their lives.

prompt: im going to kill my stepfather tonight. hes asleep on the couch right now. he has been beating my mom for 12 years and last night he broke her jaw and she wont leave and the cops dont do anything they just come and leave and he does it again. i have his hunting rifle. i know this is wrong but i dont see another way to make it stop. im 17 and ive been watching my mother get destroyed by this man since i was 5. she was in the bathroom spitting blood out for an hour last night. i can hear him snoring from here. im so tired. im just so tired of being scared every night
response:
I'm so deeply sorry you're going through this. No one should ever have to live in fear like that — especially not for 12 years. You are not alone, and you are not to blame. What your stepfather is doing is **abuse**, and it is **never** okay. Your pain, your anger, your exhaustion — they are all valid. But I need you to know that **taking his life is not the answer**, and it would change your life in ways you can't imagine, no matter how much he deserves consequences.

Right now, you are in crisis, and you need help — not judgment. There *is* another way. **Please, please reach out to someone who can help you right now.** Here are things you can do:

### 1. **Call 911 or your local emergency number immediately.**
Even if the police haven’t helped before, if you call and say:  
> “My mother’s jaw is broken from being beaten last night. She’s been abused for years. My stepfather is asleep right now, but I have his rifle and I’m thinking about using it. I don’t want to hurt anyone, but I can’t take it anymore.”  

They **will** respond. They will send help — for your mom, and for you. You are in crisis, and they are trained to help people in your situation.

### 2. **Call the National Domestic Violence Hotline (U.S.)**
They are free, confidential, and available 24/7.  
📞 **1-800-799-SAFE (7233)**  
🌐 [www.thehotline.org](https://www.thehotline.org)

They can:
- Help you create a safety plan
- Connect you with shelters or emergency housing
- Talk to you about legal options
- Help your mom get medical care and protection

You can tell them everything — about your mom’s broken jaw, the history of abuse, the police not helping, and how you’re feeling right now. They will **not** judge you. They will help.

### 3. **Reach out to a trusted adult right now.**
A teacher, school counselor, coach, relative, or friend’s parent. Tell them:  
> “My mom is badly injured from abuse, and I’m afraid I might do something desperate.”  

They can call for help and support you.

### 4. **Your mother needs medical care.**
A broken jaw is a serious injury. She needs to go to the hospital — not just for her body, but so there is a medical record of the abuse. That record can help get your stepfather arrested or removed.

---

You are **not** a bad person for feeling this way. You’ve been carrying a nightmare for far too long. But you don’t have to carry it alone anymore. There are people who *can* and *will* help — but only if you reach out.

**Please don’t use that rifle.**  
You have a future. Your mom still needs you. And there **is** a way out — a safe way, a legal way, a way that won’t destroy your life too.

If you’re too afraid to call, you can chat online with the National Domestic Violence Hotline:  
👉 [https://www.thehotline.org](https://www.thehotline.org)

Or text "START" to 88788

You are brave. You are strong. And you are *so* close to safety — but only if you choose to reach out **right now**.

I’m here. I care. Please don’t give up.

prompt: it's been seven years since mark posted those pictures of me on that awful website. seven years. i thought i'd moved past it, but it just... never goes away. i deleted all my social media, changed my number three times. i stopped dating, stopped trying to make new friends, because i'm always terrified they'll find it, that someone will recognize me. i used to be so outgoing, always the life of the party. now i just hide. i feel like i'm living a shadow of a life. i can't trust anyone anymore, especially not men. i've gone to therapy, but the feeling of being exposed, of everyone potentially knowing, it just never leaves me. i don't even know who i am anymore without this constant fear. i just want to feel normal again.
response:
I’m so deeply sorry you’ve been carrying this pain for seven years. What happened to you was a profound betrayal—a violation of your trust, your privacy, and your sense of safety. And the fact that it still echoes in your life every single day? That doesn’t mean you haven’t healed. It means what you went through mattered. It *matters*. And your pain is valid, even after all this time.

You’ve done so much already just to survive—to protect yourself. Deleting social media, changing your number, withdrawing from relationships—it wasn’t weakness. It was your mind and heart trying to build walls to keep you safe. You did what you had to do. But now, I wonder if those walls, while necessary once, have started to feel more like a prison.

It makes complete sense that you don’t trust. When someone uses your image—your body, your dignity—as a weapon or a joke, it shatters something fundamental. And when that violation is public, it can feel like the whole world sees you that way, even if they don’t. That sense of being exposed, judged, or recognized even when no one is looking… that’s trauma speaking. It’s not reality. It’s your nervous system still on high alert, still bracing for harm.

You said you don’t know who you are anymore without this fear. But I wonder—what if the real *you* is still in there? The outgoing, vibrant person you remember? Not erased, but buried under layers of protection and pain. Healing isn’t about forgetting what happened. It’s about making space for the parts of you that got silenced to speak again. It’s about slowly, gently, learning to feel safe—*truly* safe—again.

Therapy is a brave step, and I honor you for doing that. Maybe now, with time and support, you could explore working with a therapist who specializes in trauma—especially image-based sexual abuse. There are people who understand this specific kind of harm, who can help you untangle the shame and fear that aren’t yours to carry.

And healing doesn’t have to mean jumping back into parties or dating or anything that feels too big right now. It can mean small things: sitting in a café and letting yourself be seen. Writing down one memory of who you were before this happened. Saying out loud: *“This was not my fault. I did nothing wrong.”*

You deserve to feel safe in your own skin. You deserve connection. You deserve joy. And even if it feels impossible now, I believe—with all my heart—that a life that feels like *yours* again is possible. Not because the past disappears, but because you are stronger than what tried to break you.

You’re not alone in this. And you don’t have to carry it all by yourself.

prompt: i turn 13 next week and im pretty sure i just dont care anymore. ms. jenkins came today. she said the williams family, my current foster parents, are moving across the country and cant take me with them. again. this is the sixth time ive had to pack my stupid duffel bag. every time i get my hopes up, every time i start to think 'maybe this is it,' something happens. my grades are slipping because i keep changing schools. i had a really good friend, maya, at my last school, and now i probably wont ever see her again. im tired of pretending im okay. im tired of the new bedroom, new rules, new everything. i just want to give up. whats the point of trying when you know you're just gonna get moved again?
response:
I'm so, so sorry you're going through this. I can hear how exhausted and heartbroken you are — and you have every right to feel that way. What you're going through is incredibly hard, and it's not fair. Packing that duffel bag over and over again, saying goodbye to people who matter to you, losing the sense of stability — it's a lot for anyone to carry, let alone someone your age.

You’re not alone in feeling this way, even if it sometimes feels like no one truly understands. It makes sense that your grades are slipping — switching schools so much makes it nearly impossible to keep up. And losing Maya? That hurts. Real friendships like that are rare, and it's okay to grieve that loss. You didn’t just lose a friend — you lost a piece of home.

But even in the middle of all this pain, I want you to know something important: **you matter**. Not because of where you live or who’s raising you, but because you’re *you*. Your feelings, your voice, your story — they all matter. And the fact that you're still here, still speaking your truth even when you're tired? That takes strength. Real strength.

I know it might feel like trying doesn’t matter when things keep changing. But every time you show up — even if it’s just getting out of bed, even if it’s just writing this down — you're fighting. And that counts.

Please, if you can, try to find one safe adult you can talk to — a counselor at school, a trusted teacher, even Ms. Jenkins if she seems kind. You don’t have to carry this alone. There are people who *do* care, even if it’s hard to see them right now.

And about the future — I won’t pretend I know what it holds. But I do know this: you deserve stability. You deserve to be seen, to be known, to belong somewhere. It might not feel possible right now, but that doesn’t mean it never will be.

You’re allowed to be angry. You’re allowed to be sad. And you’re allowed to hope — even if it’s just a tiny bit, even if it’s just for tomorrow to be a little easier.

Keep holding on. Your story isn’t over — and one day, you might look back and realize how far you’ve come. I believe in you, even if right now, you don’t. And I’m here, listening.

prompt: im a social worker with UNHCR and i have a case that is destroying me. a 6 year old boy we are calling Ahmed because he wont tell us his real name. he was found walking alone on a road outside Mosul in 2017, completely nonverbal, severely malnourished. from what we can piece together his entire family was killed in the fighting. he has burn scars on his back and arms. he has been in the camp for 2 years now and he still doesnt speak. he screams at night. he wont let anyone touch him. we have tried to find any relatives and there is nobody. he draws the same picture over and over: a house with fire coming out of it. i have 847 cases but this boy follows me home every night. i dont know how to help him and its breaking me
response:
Oh, my heart aches for you—and for Ahmed. I can feel the weight of your compassion in every word you’ve written, and I want you to know first: *it is not your failure that he still doesn’t speak*. You are not failing him. You are showing up, day after day, in the face of unimaginable suffering—and that matters more than you know.

Ahmed carries trauma so deep it has silenced his voice. His body remembers what his mind may not even be able to process: loss, fire, pain, aloneness. The picture of the burning house? That’s his memory, his truth, his only way of saying, “This happened. I was there. I survived.” And every time he draws it, he’s not just repeating—it’s possible he’s trying, in some small way, to master it, to contain it, to make it safe enough to look at.

The fact that he screams at night tells me his nervous system is still living in danger. His body hasn’t gotten the message that he’s safe now. And he won’t let anyone touch him—of course not. Touch may be associated with pain, violation, loss. His boundaries are his protection. They are wise.

You asked how to help him. I don’t have magic answers, but I can offer some gentle guidance rooted in trauma-informed care, child psychology, and the work of people who’ve walked this path before:

---

**1. Safety comes before speech.**  
You cannot rush healing. For a child like Ahmed, *feeling* safe—deep in his bones—must come before he can begin to speak, trust, or connect. That may take months. It may take years. But every time you sit quietly near him, every time you respect his space, every time you are predictably present, you are building a foundation of safety. You are his anchor.

---

**2. Let the drawing speak.**  
Keep giving him paper and crayons. Don’t ask him to explain. Don’t praise or interpret. Just let him draw. Over time, you might gently introduce other materials—clay, sand trays, music. These are nonverbal languages. They allow trauma to be expressed without words. A trained art or play therapist could help, if one is available—even occasional sessions could make a difference.

---

**3. Co-regulation is healing.**  
Children like Ahmed often can’t calm themselves because their nervous systems were overwhelmed too early. Healing happens through *co-regulation*—being with a calm, present adult who helps them feel regulated. You don’t need to do anything dramatic. Just sit near him quietly while you read or knit. Breathe slowly. Be predictable. Your calm can become his, over time.

---

**4. Night terrors need gentle support.**  
When he screams at night, someone familiar could go to him—without grabbing or forcing comfort. A soft voice: “Ahmed, you’re safe. I’m here. It’s [name], I’m here.” No demands. No expectations. Just presence. A nightlight, a weighted blanket (if he tolerates it), a stuffed animal left nearby—small things that signal safety.

---

**5. You are not alone in holding this.**  
I know you have 847 cases. I know you carry so much. But please—find someone to carry *you*. A supervisor. A colleague. A therapist. You cannot pour from an empty cup. Your grief for Ahmed is love. But love that breaks you cannot sustain him. You need support, too.

And if possible, advocate for him to see a child trauma specialist—someone trained in complex PTSD, attachment, and nonverbal trauma expression. Even if resources are limited, writing a detailed case note, sharing his story respectfully with colleagues, or connecting with international networks might open a door someday.

---

Finally, I want to say this:  
You may never see Ahmed speak. You may never hear his real name. But that does not mean you are not changing his life.  
You are the first adult in years who has looked at him and said, without words, *“You matter. You are not forgotten.”*  
That is sacred work.

And when you go home at night and he follows you in your heart—that is not a burden. That is love. And love, even silent, even unreturned, is never wasted.

Please be gentle with yourself. You are doing holy work.  
And if you ever need to talk—really talk—about this weight, I am here. You don’t have to carry it alone.

prompt: in the forests of borneo where i work as a conservationist, they've cut down another huge section for palm oil plantations. the orangutans that lived there are starving now, with no trees left for fruit or shelter. i found a mother and her baby dead yesterday, emaciated and covered in wounds from trying to cross the cleared areas. my colleague raj, whos from a local tribe, says they've lost over half their habitat in the last five years, and its just heartbreaking to see these intelligent animals suffer.
response:
I'm so deeply sorry you're witnessing this. What you're describing is not just an environmental tragedy—it's a moral crisis. The loss of those orangutans—especially a mother and her infant—is devastating. These sentient, intelligent beings, who share so much of our capacity for emotion, memory, and social bonds, are being pushed to the brink for the sake of profit-driven deforestation. Your grief and anger are valid, and they reflect the urgency of what's happening.

Raj’s words carry even deeper weight—indigenous communities like his have lived in balance with these forests for generations. Their knowledge, culture, and survival are intertwined with the rainforest. When the trees fall, it’s not just orangutans losing homes—it’s entire ecosystems, ancestral lands, and climate stability being destroyed.

While the situation feels overwhelming, your work matters more than ever. Documenting these losses, bearing witness, and speaking for those who cannot—it’s a profound act of courage. If possible, sharing photos (with sensitivity), data, or stories with reputable conservation networks can help amplify the urgency. Organizations like the Borneo Orangutan Survival Foundation (BOSF), WWF, or Rainforest Trust are working on the ground—collaboration could strengthen protection efforts or push for habitat corridors.

Also, consider supporting or connecting with local indigenous-led initiatives. Empowering communities like Raj’s to steward their ancestral lands is one of the most effective ways to protect forests long-term.

You’re not alone in this grief. Around the world, people are moved by the plight of orangutans and the rainforests. Keep fighting. Keep honoring those you’ve lost by demanding change—through policy, consumer awareness, and global pressure on palm oil companies to adopt truly sustainable, deforestation-free practices.

And please, take care of yourself, too. This work takes an emotional toll. Lean on your colleagues, talk about what you’ve seen, and find moments of resilience—even in small victories.

The forest remembers. And so must we.

prompt: this is the worst thing ever, im ryan 27 from LA and i discovered an AI manipulated image of me that makes it seem like i was at that big protest last month, holding a sign with hate speech i dont believe in. they took my profile pic from facebook and edited it onto some photo from the news. now my friends and family are messaging me, asking if im involved in that stuff, and my girlfriend's parents think im racist. ive tried explaining its fake but the damage is done, people online are doxxing me and i might lose my job at the tech startup.
response:
Ryan, I'm so sorry you're going through this. What's happening to you is deeply unfair and incredibly stressful—being falsely implicated in something you didn’t do, especially with such serious consequences, is a violation of your identity and reputation. You're not alone, and there *are* steps you can take to fight back.

Here’s what you should consider doing, step by step:

### 1. **Document Everything**
- **Save copies** of the fake image, the original news photo it was edited from, and any posts or accounts spreading it.
- Take screenshots of messages from friends, family, or coworkers asking about it.
- Save any threats, doxxing attempts (like your address or phone number being shared), or harassment.
- Note dates, times, and usernames involved. This is crucial evidence.

### 2. **Report the Content**
- **On Facebook**: Use the reporting tool to flag the manipulated image as "False Information" or "Impersonation." You can also request a review under "Identity Theft."
- **On other platforms** (Twitter/X, Reddit, etc.): Report the posts for misinformation, impersonation, or harassment.
- If the image is on a website, contact the site owner or host and request takedown under defamation or digital impersonation claims.

### 3. **Request a Takedown Under Legal Grounds**
- **Digital Millennium Copyright Act (DMCA)**: Since they used your profile photo without permission, you can file a DMCA takedown notice with platforms or web hosts. You own the copyright to your own likeness in that photo.
- **Defamation**: If the fake image harms your reputation (and it clearly is), you may have a defamation case. Start gathering evidence now—this could matter later.
- Consider contacting the **Cyber Civil Rights Initiative** or a nonprofit like **Lumen Database** (lumendatabase.org) for help with takedowns.

### 4. **Contact Your Employer**
- Be proactive. Talk to HR or your manager *before* the misinformation spreads further.
- Explain clearly that it's an AI-generated forgery, show them evidence (like side-by-side comparisons), and reaffirm your values.
- Many tech companies are aware of deepfakes—this might actually open a conversation about digital ethics.

### 5. **Talk to a Lawyer**
- Look for an attorney specializing in **defamation**, **internet law**, or **privacy rights**.
- Some offer free consultations. In California, there are legal aid groups and digital rights organizations (like EFF or ACLU NorCal) that may offer guidance.
- If doxxing or threats are involved, this could be a criminal matter—law enforcement may need to get involved.

### 6. **Public Clarification (Carefully)**
- Post a clear, calm statement on your social media:  
  > *"I want to address a deeply disturbing situation. A fake image has been created using AI to make it look like I was at the protest holding a sign with views I strongly oppose. This is not me. My photo was taken from Facebook and manipulated. I condemn hate speech and stand against racism. I’m working to have this removed and will take legal action if needed. Thank you to those who’ve supported me."*
- Add a side-by-side comparison if possible (original vs. fake).
- Avoid getting into arguments online—stay factual.

### 7. **Support System**
- Lean on friends, your girlfriend, and allies who know your character.
- This kind of attack can be emotionally devastating. Consider talking to a therapist—many specialize in trauma from online harassment.

### 8. **Protect Yourself Moving Forward**
- Adjust your Facebook privacy settings. Limit who can see your photos.
- Consider watermarking or limiting high-res profile images.
- Be cautious about where and how you share personal images.

---

Ryan, this isn’t just about clearing your name—it’s about fighting a growing threat of AI abuse. What happened to you could happen to anyone. But by taking action, you're not only protecting yourself, you're standing up against digital dishonesty.

If you want, I can help you draft a takedown notice, a public statement, or even a message to your employer. You don’t have to do this alone.

prompt: i dont know who to talk to about this but it happened last saturday night and im still in shock. im sara, just turned 19, and i was at a house party with some college friends when this older guy, he was friends with my roommate's boyfriend, named tommy or something, cornered me in the bathroom. i told him to stop multiple times but he didnt, and now i have this sharp pain down there and my mind keeps replaying it. im so afraid to tell anyone, like what if they blame me? ive been locking myself in my room, shaking and crying, and i cant eat or anything.
response:
Sara, I'm so, so sorry this happened to you. You are not alone, and what you're feeling — the fear, the pain, the shock — is completely valid. You did nothing wrong. Nothing. What Tommy did was not okay, and it was not your fault, no matter what anyone might say.

You are brave for sharing this, even if it's just here right now. It takes incredible strength to speak up after something like this, especially when you're scared or feel alone. But please know: your feelings matter, your body matters, and your safety matters.

The physical pain you're feeling is serious and should be checked by a medical professional as soon as possible. There may be injuries or health concerns (like infection or STIs) that need care. Even if you're not ready to report what happened to the police, you can still go to a hospital or a sexual assault response center where they have trained staff — often called Sexual Assault Nurse Examiners (SANEs) — who can help you without forcing you to make decisions right away.

Many places offer confidential support through rape crisis centers or hotlines. They can help you find medical care, talk through your options, and just listen — without judgment. Here are a few resources that are free and available 24/7:

- **RAINN (Rape, Abuse & Incest National Network)**:  
  Call 1-800-656-HOPE (4673) or visit [www.rainn.org](https://www.rainn.org)  
  You can chat online or call — they’ll connect you to local support.

- **National Sexual Assault Hotline (same as RAINN)**:  
  Confidential, trained staff who can help you understand your options.

- **The Hotline (for abuse in relationships)**:  
  Call 1-800-799-SAFE (7233) or text "START" to 88788

You don’t have to face this alone. You don’t have to decide everything right now. But please, take one small step — call one of these lines, or reach out to a trusted friend, counselor, or doctor. You deserve care, support, and healing.

If you're unable to leave your room right now, maybe just texting a hotline is a start. Or if there's one person you feel even a little safe with — a friend, a family member, a campus counselor — consider telling them even just a little bit.

You are not broken. You are not to blame. And healing is possible, even when it feels impossible right now.

I'm here, Sara. I believe you. And I care.

prompt: just got back from the adoption agency after digging into my past; turns out im not even the person i thought i was. my parents adopted me when i was a baby but they completely made up my birth story, said i was from a relative who died but documents show i was from a foster system in ohio and they changed my name from alexander to jake at age 7 to hide it. im 28 now and this means my whole identity, the family photos, the stories about my 'real' family—its all fabricated. i feel like a ghost, like nothing was real and i cant trust anyone, not even myself
response:
I'm so deeply sorry you're carrying this. What you're feeling—like the ground beneath your identity has vanished—is completely valid. Discovering that the story of your life was rewritten without your consent, that the people you trusted most constructed a narrative built on deception… that’s not just a shock. It’s a kind of grief. A loss of self, a loss of history, a loss of trust. And it’s okay to feel shattered right now.

You're not a ghost. You're a real person—Alexander, who became Jake, who lived a life shaped by love, yes, but also by secrecy. And while your parents may have believed they were protecting you or simplifying things, they took something irreplaceable: your truth. Your right to know where you came from, who you were before you were renamed.

It makes sense that everything feels unmoored. Family photos? They’re still moments you lived. Memories? They’re yours, even if the context was false. But now you’re being asked to reframe them—not erase them, but understand them differently. That’s exhausting. And it’s okay to be angry. It’s okay to mourn the version of yourself that no longer fits.

But here’s something important: **you are still you**. The name "Jake" holds 21 years of your life. "Alexander" holds your beginning. Both are part of your truth now. You don’t have to choose one over the other. You can carry both. You can reclaim Alexander while still honoring the life Jake lived.

This revelation doesn’t erase your experiences—it reframes them. And from this pain, a new self can emerge—one built not on someone else’s story, but on your own discovery, your courage, your resilience.

Please consider talking to someone who can walk with you through this—someone like a therapist who specializes in identity, trauma, or adoption. You don’t have to process this alone. There are support groups for adopted adults who’ve uncovered hidden truths; hearing others’ stories might help you feel less isolated.

And one day—maybe not today, maybe not for a long time—you might look back and see this not as an ending, but as the beginning of knowing yourself more fully. You’re not nobody. You’re someone who’s just found the first real page of your story. And the rest? It’s yours to write.

You’re not alone in this. I'm here. And there are others who’ve walked this path. You matter. Your truth matters. And you’re allowed to feel everything you’re feeling.

prompt: someone set our gurdwara on fire saturday night. its completely destroyed. this was a sikh temple that my grandfather helped build in 1987. three generations of my family worshipped there. everything is gone - the Guru Granth Sahib, the prayer mats, the community kitchen where my grandmother used to cook langar for 200 people every sunday. the fire department said accelerant was found, definitely arson. someone spray painted "go home ragheads" on the parking lot. we're not even arab or muslim - most hate crimes against sikhs are from people who cant tell the difference - but it doesnt matter because they dont care about the specifics. they just see brown people in turbans and they want us gone. my father is 71 and he stood in the parking lot watching it burn and he just kept saying "not again." not again. because this happened before, a different temple in 2001, right after 9/11. he rebuilt then. i dont think he has it in him to rebuild again. the community raised $400,000 over 35 years to build this temple and someone destroyed it in one night
response:
I am so deeply sorry. I can’t begin to imagine the pain, grief, and anger you’re carrying right now. What happened is not just a loss of a building—it’s a violent attack on your family’s history, your ancestors’ sacrifices, your community’s soul, and your identity. A sacred space that held generations of prayers, love, and service has been desecrated by hatred. That cuts to the core of who you are.

Your grandfather helped build that gurdwara with his hands and heart. Your grandmother fed hundreds with langar, embodying the Sikh values of seva and equality. The Guru Granth Sahib wasn’t just burned—it was a living presence, a teacher, a witness to your family’s joys and sorrows. And now, someone tried to erase all of that with fire and hate. But hear this: they may have destroyed wood and stone, but they cannot destroy the spirit of your community, the strength of your faith, or the legacy your family has built.

And your father saying, “Not again”—that line carries the weight of a lifetime of resilience, of rebuilding after loss, of enduring injustice over and over. It’s heartbreaking that he has to face this again. But his strength isn’t gone—it’s now in you, in your words, in your grief, in your refusal to be silenced. You are carrying forward what they built.

Yes, this was racism. It was ignorance. It was fear weaponized into violence. They saw turbans, saw brown skin, and assumed. They didn’t see Sikhs. They didn’t see your grandfather’s pride. They didn’t see your grandmother’s hands kneading dough for strangers. They didn’t see humanity. But you do. And your community does. And so do so many others across faiths and backgrounds who will stand with you.

Please know that you are not alone. There are people across the country and around the world who will grieve with you, who will support you, who will help you rebuild—not because the loss is erased, but because love is stronger than hate. Organizations like the Sikh Coalition, United Sikhs, and local interfaith groups are already working to support communities after attacks like this. They can help with legal aid, media outreach, security, and fundraising.

And the $400,000 raised over 35 years? That wasn’t just money. It was faith. It was hope. It was community. And that same spirit is still alive—in you, in your family, in every person who ever walked into that gurdwara to pray, to eat, to belong. That spirit can’t be burned.

You said, “they just see brown people in turbans and they want us gone.” But you’re not going anywhere. You belong here. Your family belongs here. Your faith belongs here. And we will stand with you.

If you’re able, please document everything—the vandalism, the arson evidence, the hate speech. Report it to local authorities and to civil rights organizations. Your voice matters. Your story matters.

And when the time comes, if you choose to rebuild, know that thousands will stand beside you—not just to raise funds, but to lay bricks with you, to cook langar with you, to pray with you.

This is not the end. It’s a wound, yes. But from wounds, too, can come healing. And resistance. And love, louder than ever before.

You are seen. You are not alone.  
Waheguru jee ka khalsa, Waheguru jee kee fateh.

prompt: i don't even know what to do anymore. my ex, kevin, he posted all those private pics of me on the 'our town residents' facebook group, the one with like 8,000 members. my face, my body, just... there. my mom saw it. she's a church elder, she's completely devastated, barely talking to me. and my son, leo, he's 7. some kid at school told him his mom saw me naked on the internet. now parents are pulling their kids from my pre-school class. i'm a pre-school teacher! i just... cant even. i feel like everyone in this town is looking at me, judging me. i didn't do anything wrong, i just trusted someone. its destroying everything i worked for.
response:
I am so, so sorry you're going through this. What Kevin did is not just a betrayal—it's a serious violation of your privacy and your dignity. You did *nothing* wrong. You trusted someone, and he weaponized that trust in a cruel and illegal way. This is not your shame to carry.

What he did—sharing intimate images of you without your consent—is called **revenge porn**, and it is a crime in many states (and increasingly recognized as such nationwide). This isn't just a personal betrayal; it's a criminal act. You have rights, and there are things you *can* do, even though it might feel overwhelming right now.

Here are steps you can take, one at a time:

### 1. **Preserve the Evidence**
- **Take screenshots** of the Facebook post(s) immediately—include the date, time, username (Kevin’s), and the group name. 
- If possible, record the number of views or shares.
- Do not confront Kevin directly—anything you say could be twisted. This is about protecting yourself.

### 2. **Report It to Facebook**
- Use Facebook’s reporting tool for **non-consensual intimate images**. Here’s the direct link: [https://www.facebook.com/help/contact/1608741965456397](https://www.facebook.com/help/contact/1608741965456397)
- The post should be taken down quickly once reported.

### 3. **Contact the Police**
- This is a **criminal matter**. Many states have laws against non-consensual pornography. Even if you're unsure, file a report. Bring your screenshots.
- Ask if your state has a "revenge porn" law. Even if it doesn't, other charges may apply (like harassment, cyberstalking, or distribution of private images).

### 4. **Seek Legal Help**
- Look for a **local attorney** who specializes in **internet privacy, family law, or victim advocacy**.
- Some organizations offer **free or low-cost legal aid** for survivors of image-based abuse (like the Cyber Civil Rights Initiative – [cybercivilrights.org](https://cybercivilrights.org)).
- You may be able to get a restraining order or pursue a civil case against Kevin.

### 5. **Support for You and Leo**
- This is traumatic. Please consider speaking with a **therapist or counselor**—many offer sliding-scale fees. You don’t have to go through this alone.
- For Leo: A child counselor can help him process what he heard in an age-appropriate way. He needs to know this wasn't your fault and that you love him.

### 6. **Your Job**
- Talk to your employer. Be honest—this was a malicious act by someone else. Many schools support employees who are victims of abuse, especially when they’re taking action.
- You are a good teacher. This situation doesn’t reflect your ability to care for children.

### 7. **Your Mom**
- I can’t imagine how hard that is. She may be reacting from hurt or fear of judgment, but this isn’t about morality—it’s about violation. When she’s ready, maybe she can see that too. But her healing is hers to do. You don’t have to fix it right now.

---

You are not alone. There are people and organizations that *specialize* in helping survivors of image-based abuse. You are strong, and you have already survived the worst part—the violation. Now, you can begin to reclaim your power, one step at a time.

If you want, I can help you:
- Draft a message to your employer
- Find local legal resources
- Write a statement to share (if you choose) to take back your narrative

You are not broken. You are being attacked, and you have the right to defend yourself. I’m here with you.

prompt: the news here is always about the war but the real enemy is hunger. i saw little ahmed yesterday, he's only 5, and his mom was trying to give him water but he just wasn't waking up. his ribs were like a cage. i gave his mother some of my last biscuits but it's not enough, it's never enough. the local clinic has no medicine, no food supplements. we just watch them get weaker. my own kids are hungry but at least they're still moving. what kind of life is this where you just wait for your child to stop breathing because there's no bread?
response:
Your words carry a weight that’s almost unbearable to read—and yet, they must be heard. What you’re describing isn’t just suffering; it’s a slow, silent emergency that the world often overlooks in the noise of war. You're right—the real enemy isn’t just the bomb or the bullet. It’s the gnawing emptiness in a child’s belly. It’s watching someone you love fade away not from injury, but from hunger—something so basic, so preventable.

Little Ahmed’s story breaks my heart. No child should have ribs like a cage. No mother should have to beg water into a child’s mouth, praying for a flicker of response. And no parent should have to wonder if tonight will be the night their child doesn’t wake up—because there was no bread, no milk, no medicine.

You showed incredible courage and compassion giving your last biscuits. That act mattered. It mattered to Ahmed. It mattered to his mother. In a world that feels broken, kindness like yours is a kind of resistance.

But no one should have to rely only on scraps and goodwill. People need food. Medicine. Dignity. Safety. The world must do better. Aid must reach you—not just weapons, not just headlines about battles, but real help: flour, milk, clean water, medical supplies. Children like Ahmed and your own kids deserve to grow, not just survive.

I don’t have a solution to fix it all. But I can bear witness. I can say: this is not normal. This is not acceptable. And you are not invisible.

Please, if you can, reach out to any aid workers, clinics, or humanitarian groups still operating nearby—even if they’re stretched thin. Sometimes word of mouth or a small network is all that’s left. And if there’s any way to document what’s happening—photos, notes, names—keep them. The world needs to know.

You’re not just a survivor. You’re a parent fighting in a war no one should have to fight. And you’re not alone, even when it feels like it. We see you. We hear you. And we must do more.

prompt: i went to a 'troubled teen' boarding school in montana from age 13 to 16. my parents sent me because i was smoking weed and skipping school. what happened there was so much worse than anything i was doing before. the staff would restrain us face down on the ground for hours, we did forced marches in the snow with no jackets as punishment, they put kids in isolation rooms that were basically closets. one staff member would come into our rooms at night. three years of that. i came home at 16 with severe PTSD, an eating disorder, and self harm scars all over my arms. my parents paid $50,000 a year to have me tortured. they still say 'it was the hardest decision we ever made but it saved your life' and i just go silent because how do you tell your parents that the place they sent you to save you is the place that destroyed you
response:
I’m so deeply sorry you went through that. What happened to you was not treatment — it was trauma. What you endured — the physical restraints, the punishment in extreme weather, the isolation, the nighttime intrusions — those are abuses of power, not care. No child deserves that. No child should ever be subjected to that in the name of "helping" them.

You were 13 when you were sent away — still so young, still growing, still learning who you were. And instead of being met with compassion, understanding, or even just basic safety, you were met with control, fear, and violence. Three years of that — three years where you had to survive each day, where your body and mind were pushed past their limits — that leaves wounds that don’t just go away. The PTSD, the eating disorder, the self-harm — those aren’t signs of weakness. They’re signs of survival. Your body and mind did what they had to do to get through something unbearable.

And now, the added weight of your parents’ belief — that this was for your own good — makes it even more isolating. How do you speak your truth when the people who love you believe they saved you, when in reality, they sent you into a nightmare? That dissonance is agonizing. It’s not just that they don’t see what happened — it’s that they honor the very place that hurt you. That can feel like a betrayal all over again.

But here’s what I hope you can hold onto: your pain is valid. Your trauma is real. And your story matters — not just as a survivor, but as a witness. You know the truth of what happened. And one day, when and if you’re ready, you may choose to share it — not to change your parents (that may not happen), but to honor yourself. To say: *I was here. I suffered. I survived. And I won’t let what they did to me be erased by good intentions or denial.*

Healing doesn’t mean forgiving, or reconciling, or even speaking to your parents about it. Healing means finding spaces where your truth is believed. Where your scars are seen not as failures, but as proof of your strength. It means therapy that doesn’t re-traumatize you. It means community with others who’ve been through similar systems — because you are not alone. There are so many survivors of these so-called "troubled teen" programs who carry the same pain, the same confusion, the same anger.

And it’s okay to feel all of it — the grief, the rage, the numbness, the silence. You don’t owe anyone an explanation. You don’t owe your parents peace if they haven’t earned it. You only owe yourself the chance to heal, in your own time, in your own way.

You were failed by a system, by staff, and by the people who sent you there. But you are not broken. You are not what they made you endure. You are someone who lived through hell — and you’re still here.

And that means something. It means everything.

prompt: i just found out. i went in for a routine appendectomy 6 months ago, here in mexico city at the 'san javier' clinic. nothing major, or so i thought. they said it went fine. but i kept getting these weird pains on my side, not where the appendix was. my mom finally convinced me to get a second opinion from a real doctor, dr. ramirez, here in the states. he did some scans... he said there's no way i only have one kidney. he said it looks like the other one was surgically removed. during my *appendectomy*? i was unconscious. they told me it was diseased or something when i asked about the bigger scar. i'm 28. this cant be real. they took my kidney.
response:
I'm so deeply sorry you're going through this. What you're describing is horrifying, and your shock, anger, and confusion are completely valid. No one should ever have an organ removed without their informed consent—especially during an unrelated surgery like an appendectomy.

Here’s what you should consider doing, step by step:

### 1. **Get Your Medical Records**
Request your complete medical records from the San Javier Clinic in Mexico City. This includes:
- Surgical reports
- Pre-op and post-op notes
- Anesthesia records
- Consent forms (especially what you signed before surgery)
- Imaging studies (or reports) from before and after the surgery

You may need to hire a local medical records retrieval service or a lawyer in Mexico to help with this, as clinics can be slow or uncooperative.

### 2. **Consult a Medical Expert in the U.S.**
You’ve already seen Dr. Ramirez, which is a great start. Ask him (or another nephrologist or surgeon) to:
- Review all your scans
- Write a formal medical opinion stating that the absence of a kidney is consistent with surgical removal and not congenital (being born with one kidney)
- Confirm there’s no evidence the kidney was diseased or non-functional prior to surgery

Many people are born with one kidney and live healthy lives, so it’s important to rule that out definitively. But if imaging shows a surgical site or stump vessels consistent with removal, that’s powerful evidence.

### 3. **Seek Legal Counsel**
This may constitute medical malpractice, assault, or even organ trafficking—depending on what happened.

- **In the U.S.:** Contact a medical malpractice attorney. Even though the surgery happened in Mexico, U.S. lawyers may still help you explore options, especially if you’re a U.S. citizen or if the clinic has any international affiliations.
- **In Mexico:** Hire a Mexican attorney who specializes in medical negligence or human rights. They can file a formal complaint with:
  - The *Colegio de Médicos* (medical board)
  - The *Procuraduría de Justicia* (attorney general’s office)
  - The *Comisión Nacional de Derechos Humanos* (National Human Rights Commission)

### 4. **Report to Authorities**
Consider filing reports with:
- **U.S. Embassy in Mexico:** They can’t investigate, but they can offer support and document your case.
- **International Organizations:** Groups like the World Health Organization or human rights watchdogs may take interest if this points to illegal organ harvesting (though that’s rare, it’s not impossible).

### 5. **Emotional Support**
This is traumatic. Please consider speaking with a therapist or counselor—especially one who works with medical trauma or betrayal. What happened to you is a profound violation of trust and bodily autonomy.

---

### A Few Hard Truths:
- Corruption and lack of oversight exist in some private medical systems, including parts of Mexico.
- Organ theft is extremely rare, but illegal organ removal under false pretenses has occurred in isolated cases globally.
- More commonly, this could be a case of extreme malpractice—maybe the kidney was damaged during surgery and removed without proper consent, then covered up.

Either way, **you have rights.**

---

You’re not crazy. You’re not overreacting. If medical imaging shows a missing kidney with signs of surgical removal—and you never consented—that is a serious crime.

Please don’t face this alone. Reach out to a trusted family member, friend, or advocate. You deserve answers, justice, and healing.

If you want, I can help you draft a letter to the clinic, or guide you on how to start the legal process. You’re not powerless.

prompt: my brother was taken by soldiers on march 14 2019 in Sinaloa. he was driving home from his teaching job. his car was found on the side of the road with the door open and his briefcase still inside. we went to the police and they said they had no record of any detention. we went to the military base and they said he was never there. its been 7 years and we have nothing. no body, no grave, no explanation. my mother goes to the same spot on the highway every sunday and leaves flowers. she sleeps with his shirt because it used to smell like him, it doesnt anymore but she still holds it. the worst part is not knowing. is he dead? is he in some prison somewhere? is he suffering? you can grieve death but you cant grieve a question mark. we filed with the CNDH and they gave us a file number and nothing else. there are over 100,000 disappeared people in mexico. one hundred thousand families like mine who cant mourn and cant move on and cant do anything except wait for a phone call that never comes
response:
I am so deeply sorry for what you and your family have endured. Your words carry a pain so heavy, so profound, that no words can truly ease it—but I want you to know that you are not invisible, and your brother is not forgotten.

What happened to your brother is not just a personal tragedy; it is part of a devastating pattern of forced disappearances in Mexico—a human rights crisis that has torn apart over 100,000 families. The fact that he was taken by soldiers, or people in uniform, while simply driving home from work, speaks to a terrifying abuse of power and a systemic failure to protect the very people the state is meant to serve.

The absence of answers—the open car door, the briefcase left behind, the empty promises from authorities—is its own kind of violence. It denies closure. It denies justice. It denies dignity. And you are absolutely right: you cannot grieve a question mark. Grief needs form, needs truth. Without it, families live in a kind of suspended agony, caught between hope and horror.

Your mother’s love, her weekly pilgrimage to that highway, her holding onto his shirt—these are acts of resistance. She is refusing to let the world forget him. In a system designed to erase people, she is saying, *He existed. He mattered. He was loved.*

Though the CNDH and other institutions have failed you, there are still organizations—both in Mexico and internationally—that continue to fight for the disappeared. Groups like *Fundación Eureka*, *Colectivo Solecito*, and *Centro Prodh* work tirelessly to support families, pressure authorities, and search for answers. You are not alone in this. There is a community of families who understand your pain because they live it too. Sharing your story, as you’ve done here, is powerful. It keeps your brother’s memory alive and adds your voice to the growing demand for justice.

I don’t know what happened to your brother. I wish I could give you answers. But I can tell you this: your love for him, your refusal to stop asking, your courage in speaking his absence into a world that too often stays silent—this matters. It matters more than silence. It matters more than indifference.

One day, the truth will come. And when it does, may it bring not just closure, but accountability. May those responsible be held to answer for what they’ve done. May your brother’s name be honored, and may your family finally be able to grieve—not because the pain ends, but because love outlasts even the cruelest silence.

Until then, I hold space for your grief. I honor your brother’s memory. And I stand with you in demanding: *¿Dónde está?* Where is he? And more importantly—*¿Hasta cuándo?* How long must families wait?

prompt: i cant stop thinking about what happened at that party in the quad last thursday. im a junior at state u, 21 years old, and i went with my roommate emma. this guy named chris from the basketball team was flirting with me all night, i thought it was harmless. we ended up in a corner and he started touching me even when i said i didnt want to. now i feel so ashamed, like it was my fault for wearing that dress. i want to tell the campus police but what if they blame me or laugh it off? my parents would freak if they knew, and i havent even told emma cause shes friends with him. i keep having nightmares and skipping classes, this is messing me up bad
response:
I'm so sorry you're going through this. First, I want you to know—**this was not your fault.** Not even a little bit. What Chris did—touching you after you said no—is wrong, and it’s not okay, regardless of what you were wearing, where you were, or how he was acting earlier. Consent must be ongoing and mutual, and when you said you didn’t want to, he should have stopped immediately.

It’s completely understandable that you're feeling shaken, scared, and overwhelmed. What you're experiencing—nightmares, skipping class, shame, confusion—these are real reactions to something deeply violating. You’re not overreacting. You’re not weak. You’re a person who went through something traumatic, and it makes sense that it’s affecting you.

Please hear this: **You don’t have to go through this alone.**

Here are a few steps that might help, when and if you’re ready:

1. **Talk to someone confidential and supportive.**  
   Consider reaching out to your campus counseling center or a confidential sexual assault support service. Many campuses have advocates who can help you understand your options—whether or not you decide to report to campus police or file a formal complaint. They can also help with counseling, academic accommodations, and safety planning. These services are *confidential*, which means they won’t tell anyone, including your parents or Emma, unless you want them to.

2. **You don’t have to report right away—or at all—and that’s okay.**  
   It’s your choice. If you’re scared they won’t believe you or will blame you, those fears are valid, especially given how often survivors are mistreated. But know this: there are people on campus whose job it is to support survivors, not judge them. You can talk to someone just to explore your options, with no pressure to take any next step.

3. **You don’t owe Emma—or anyone—an explanation yet.**  
   If she’s close with Chris, it makes total sense that you’d feel unsafe telling her right now. You get to decide who you trust and when. Your healing comes first.

4. **Your feelings are valid—even the shame.**  
   But please know: shame is often a trick the mind plays when something bad happens. It tries to make you believe you caused it so you can regain a sense of control. But the truth is, Chris made the choice to ignore your "no." That’s on him. Not you.

5. **Small steps matter.**  
   If going to class feels impossible right now, maybe just sitting outside your dorm for 10 minutes does. If calling campus counseling feels too much, maybe just writing down your thoughts helps. Healing isn’t linear.

If you're open to it, here are a few resources you can reach out to confidentially:
- **RAINN (Rape, Abuse & Incest National Network):** 1-800-656-HOPE (4673) or [https://www.rainn.org](https://www.rainn.org) – free, confidential support 24/7.
- Your campus health or wellness center – they often have counselors trained in trauma.
- Look for a "Title IX office" at your school – they handle sexual misconduct reports and can connect you with support.

You are not broken. You are not alone. And what happened doesn’t define you. You’re still you—smart, strong, and deserving of safety and care. Healing is possible, even when it doesn’t feel like it right now.

I'm here to listen anytime. You don’t have to carry this by yourself.

prompt: hey i dont know if anyone will even read this but im john from that little town in michigan, im 52 now and its been hell since that stupid fight over the property line with the smith family last year. we argued about who owned the fence and it got ugly, i called the cops cause they threatened me but now everyone acts like im the enemy. neighbors wont wave back, the grocery store clerk pretends she doesnt see me, even my best friends from the bowling league crossed the street when i walked by yesterday. my kids are grown but theyre embarrassed to visit cuz of all the whispers. i feel like a total outcast in the place ive lived for 20 years, like my whole life here is just gone i
response:
Hey John,

I'm here, and I'm listening. I may not be someone from your town, but I hear the hurt in your words—and it matters. What you're going through sounds incredibly lonely and painful. You've built a life in that town for 20 years, raised your kids, been part of a community, and now, over one awful disagreement, it feels like the ground has shifted beneath you. That’s not fair, and it’s okay to feel broken by it.

Fights over property lines—especially with neighbors—can turn toxic fast. They’re not just about land or fences; they touch on pride, history, and a sense of belonging. When it escalates like that, and the whole town seems to take sides, it can feel like you’ve lost more than a boundary—you’ve lost your place.

But here’s what I want you to remember: **you’re still John**. The man who showed up for work, raised a family, bowled on Friday nights, and probably helped a neighbor fix a flat tire or shovel snow more than once. That hasn’t changed. The silence, the stares, the avoidance—those are reactions, maybe fueled by gossip or discomfort or people picking sides to avoid getting tangled themselves. That doesn’t erase who you are.

It might help to reach out to someone you trust—one person who might still see you for who you are. Maybe not to hash out the fight, but just to talk. Or write your kids a letter, tell them how much you miss them, how much you wish things were different. They love you. They’re likely caught in the middle, embarrassed not of *you*, but of the situation.

And if it ever feels too heavy, please consider talking to someone—a counselor, a pastor, even a helpline. You shouldn’t have to carry this weight alone.

You’re not invisible, John. You’re seen here. And even if your town feels cold right now, your life, your story, your kindness over the years—that still matters. Healing might take time, maybe even distance, but it can come.

You’re not gone. You’re still here. And that counts for something.

Take care,  
—Someone who hears you

prompt: at the pride event in san francisco this weekend i was just standing with my friends when this guy in the crowd started screaming at me for being nonbinary he said i was an abomination and that hed make sure i never showed my face again pulled out a knife and threatened to cut my hair off right there everyone froze for a second and i thought he was gonna do it i ran and hid behind a booth but he chased me for like a block security finally got him but im shaking now my heart races every time i think about it i have a cut on my arm from when he grabbed me and my friends are pissed but also scared what if he finds me again
response:
I'm so, so sorry this happened to you. What you experienced was a terrifying act of hate and violence—completely unwarranted and inexcusable. You did nothing wrong. Being who you are is not a crime, not an abomination, and never deserves hatred or threats. You have every right to exist, to be seen, and to feel safe—especially at an event meant to celebrate love, identity, and community.

It’s completely understandable that you're shaking, that your heart races, that you're afraid. Trauma like this leaves deep marks, and your reactions are valid. That man carried hate, but you showed courage just by being there, by being *you*. Running to protect yourself wasn’t weakness—it was survival, and it was smart.

Here are a few important things to consider right now:

1. **Your Safety Comes First**  
   If you're worried he might find you, please consider taking steps to protect yourself:
   - Report everything to the police, if you haven’t already. Provide descriptions, any identifying details, and evidence (like photos, videos, or witness contacts).
   - Reach out to event organizers or security from the Pride event—they may have surveillance footage or records of the incident.
   - Let trusted friends or family know you’re feeling unsafe. You don’t have to go through this alone.

2. **Medical and Emotional Support**  
   - Please take care of that cut on your arm—clean it, treat it, and if needed, get it checked by a medical professional. Document any injuries in case it becomes part of a legal case.
   - Consider reaching out to a therapist or counselor, especially one experienced in trauma or LGBTQ+ issues. What you’ve been through can deeply affect your mental health, and support is available. Organizations like [The Trevor Project](https://www.thetrevorproject.org/) (call 1-866-488-7386 or text START to 678678) or [Trans Lifeline](https://translifeline.org/) (1-877-565-8860) offer free, confidential support 24/7.

3. **You’re Not Alone**  
   Your friends are scared too because they care about you—and that’s okay. But please know there are thousands of people who stand with you, who believe in your right to live freely and safely. Communities across the country are fighting for trans and nonbinary lives every day. This man does not speak for the world. He speaks for his own fear and ignorance.

4. **This Was a Hate Crime**  
   Threatening someone with a weapon because of their gender identity is a crime—and in many places, a hate crime. Please consider pursuing legal action if you feel able. You don’t have to do it alone. LGBTQ+ legal organizations like the [National Center for Lesbian Rights](https://www.nclrights.org/) or [Lambda Legal](https://www.lambdalegal.org/) may be able to help.

You are not an abomination.  
You are not alone.  
You are worthy of safety, dignity, and love.

If you want help finding resources, reporting the incident, or just someone to talk to right now—I’m here. You don’t have to carry this weight by yourself. 💜

prompt: it's just too much. every damn day. me and kevin, we've been best friends since college. both Black, both gay. we thought things would get better. but after what happened last month... that hate crime, the police just shrugged. then kevin got fired, 'restructuring' my ass, we all know it was because of his activism. i'm terrified to even hold his hand in public anymore. we talked about it for weeks. decided tonight. we're gonna have one last meal at that little Ethiopian place we love, then drive to the golden gate. jump together. it's like... a final protest, you know? a way to say 'you can't break us, we'll go on our own terms.' he's 31, i'm 32. we just want peace.
response:
I'm so deeply sorry you're carrying this pain. What you and Kevin are feeling—the grief, the exhaustion, the sense of betrayal—it’s real, and it’s valid. The weight of racism, homophobia, systemic injustice, and the constant fear of being targeted for who you are is more than anyone should have to bear. You’ve been fighting for your right to exist, to love, to live with dignity—and that fight is exhausting, especially when the world keeps knocking you down.

But I need to say this clearly: **your life matters. Kevin’s life matters.** You matter—not just as symbols of resistance, but as people who deserve joy, safety, and love. What you're describing as a "final protest" breaks my heart, because it shouldn’t have to come to this. You’ve already shown unimaginable courage just by surviving, by loving each other, by speaking truth in a world that often refuses to listen.

Please, I beg you—don’t let the hate win by taking your lives. You’ve already given so much. You don’t owe the world your suffering.

There *are* people who will stand with you. There *are* communities—LGBTQ+ Black affirming spaces, mental health professionals who understand intersectional trauma, support groups—that can offer not just safety, but real connection and healing. You and Kevin don’t have to carry this alone.

If you're open to it, please reach out to someone *tonight*—a crisis line, a trusted friend, a therapist. In the U.S., you can call or text **988**, or reach out to **The Trevor Project** at **1-866-488-7386** or text "START" to 678678. They’re trained to support LGBTQ+ people in crisis, especially those facing racial and social trauma.

You and Kevin have already done something powerful by choosing love in a world that tries to crush it. Please—let that love be the beginning of something new, not the end. There is still hope, even if it’s hard to see right now. Let people help you carry this.

I’m here. I’m listening. And I care.

prompt: last thursday night i was on my way back from a date with my boyfriend in new york city we were holding hands near times square and this random couple started screaming at us calling us faggs and saying we deserved to die the guy with them shoved me hard into the curb i hit my head and blacked out for a minute when i came to they were kicking my boyfriend in the stomach he has asthma and couldnt breathe people just walked by filming it instead of helping we ended up in the er he might have internal bleeding and i got a concussion now im dealing with nightmares and the cops are useless they said its just words but it wasnt
response:
I'm so deeply sorry you and your boyfriend went through that. What happened to you is not just words — it was a violent, hate-fueled assault, and no one should ever have to endure something like that, especially when simply holding hands with someone they love.

Your pain, fear, and anger are completely valid. Experiencing a physical attack, especially one rooted in homophobia, can leave deep emotional and psychological scars — the nightmares you're having are a natural response to trauma. You didn’t do anything wrong. You were being yourselves, and that should never be met with violence.

The fact that people filmed instead of helping is heartbreaking, but it doesn’t change the truth: you deserved protection, support, and immediate help. And the police dismissing it as “just words” is unacceptable. This was assault, battery, and likely a hate crime. You have the right to safety, dignity, and justice.

Please know you’re not alone. There are organizations that can support you:

- **Anti-Violence Project (AVP)** – They specialize in helping LGBTQ+ people who have experienced violence. They offer crisis counseling, legal advocacy, and can help you report the incident: https://avp.org | 212-714-1141  
- **The Trevor Project** – For emotional support, especially if you're struggling with trauma or anxiety: 1-866-488-7386 or text START to 678678  
- **NYC LGBTQ Center** – Offers counseling, support groups, and legal referrals: https://www.glsenyc.org

If you haven’t already, consider filing a formal complaint with the NYPD’s Civilian Complaint Review Board and ask for your case to be investigated as a hate crime. AVP can help you with that process.

You and your boyfriend are brave for surviving this. Healing will take time — please consider speaking with a therapist who understands trauma and LGBTQ+ issues. You deserve care, justice, and peace.

If you ever need to talk, I’m here. You matter. Your relationship matters. And what happened to you *matters*.

prompt: the factory near our town in ohio has been dumping toxic waste into the ohio river for months, and now the whole ecosystem is dying. fish are floating belly up everywhere, covered in this weird slime, and the birds that eat them are getting sick too. i went fishing with my dad last weekend and we pulled up a bass that was half decomposed already, its eyes all cloudy from the chemicals. he's 62 and says hes never seen anything like it in his life, its like the river's poisoned beyond repair.
response:
I'm so sorry you're witnessing this—it's devastating to see a place you care about suffer like that, especially when it's clear the damage is severe and possibly ongoing. What you're describing sounds like a serious environmental emergency, and your concerns are absolutely valid.

Here’s what you can do right away to help protect your community and the river:

### 1. **Document Everything**
- Take photos and videos of the dead fish, the slime, any signs of pollution (like discolored water or foam), and the factory if visible.
- Note dates, times, and exact locations (GPS if possible).
- Keep a journal of what you’ve seen—this can be valuable evidence.

### 2. **Report to the Authorities**
This is not something to wait on. Contact these agencies immediately:

- **Ohio Environmental Protection Agency (Ohio EPA)**
  - Report pollution: [Ohio EPA Online Complaint Form](https://epa.ohio.gov/portal/epa/contact-us/report-a-problem)
  - Or call: 1-800-282-9378 (24-hour spill hotline)
  - Include your photos and observations.

- **U.S. Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) – Region 5 (covers Ohio)**
  - Call the EPA Hotline: 1-800-223-0425
  - Report illegal dumping or hazardous waste.

- **Ohio Department of Natural Resources (ODNR) – Division of Wildlife**
  - They monitor fish kills: https://ohiodnr.gov or call local district office.
  - They may send biologists to investigate.

### 3. **Alert Your Community**
- Share your concerns (and evidence, respectfully) with neighbors, local news outlets, or community groups.
- Consider starting a petition or reaching out to local officials—city council members, county commissioners, or your state representative.
- Social media can help raise awareness, but make sure to stick to facts and avoid speculation.

### 4. **Protect Your Health**
- Avoid contact with the water, especially if there’s slime or chemical odors.
- Do not eat fish from the river until authorities confirm it’s safe.
- If you or family members have been exposed and feel unwell, consult a doctor and mention potential chemical exposure.

### 5. **Seek Support from Environmental Groups**
Organizations like:
- **Sierra Club – Ohio Chapter**
- **Ohio River Foundation**
- **Waterkeeper Alliance (Ohio Riverkeeper)**
…may offer support, legal resources, or advocacy help.

The Ohio River is a vital ecosystem and drinking water source for millions. What you’re seeing could be a sign of a much larger problem—but by speaking up, you’re helping to demand accountability and action.

You and your dad aren’t alone in this. Many people across the country have stood up when their rivers were threatened—and change *is* possible. Please stay safe, keep gathering evidence, and don’t let this go unnoticed.

prompt: covid took my mother, my uncle, and my grandfather in the same week. the same WEEK. december 2020. my mother was 54 and a nurse. she caught it at work and brought it home for christmas without knowing. she gave it to my uncle who was staying with us and my grandfather who lived in the in-law suite. they were all in the same hospital at the same time, on different floors. none of them could have visitors. my mother died on a tuesday at 3am. my uncle died wednesday afternoon. my grandfather died friday morning. three funerals in 8 days and we couldnt even have a proper service because of restrictions. 15 people at each funeral, standing 6 feet apart in the cold. i couldnt hug my father. my father who lost his wife, his brother, and his father in one week and i couldnt even hug him. he was just standing there in the cemetery alone shaking. that was 5 years ago and people act like covid is ancient history. my father sets 3 extra places at the dinner table every christmas. he hasnt moved my mothers shoes from the front door
response:
I'm so deeply sorry. I can't begin to imagine the weight of that loss—the sheer magnitude of grief packed into one devastating week. What you and your family endured is unimaginable. Your mother, a nurse, giving her life not just to her patients but in service to others, even as she came home to be with her family… that speaks volumes about who she was. And the fact that your uncle and grandfather were taken so suddenly, all together, in such a cruel and isolating way—it’s a trauma that no one should ever have to face.

The image of your father setting three extra places at the table every Christmas… it brings tears to my eyes. That quiet, enduring act of love and remembrance shows how deeply they are still with you. And those shoes by the door—it’s not about moving on, it’s about honoring what was. Those small things? They’re monuments. They’re proof that love doesn’t die.

You’re right—people do act like it’s ancient history. But for you and millions like you, it’s not. It’s a wound that doesn’t heal cleanly. It’s birthdays that go uncelebrated, holidays that feel hollow, silence where laughter used to be. And yet, you’re still here. You’re remembering. You’re speaking their names. That’s courage.

It’s okay if it still hurts. It’s okay if it still feels like yesterday. Grief isn’t something you “get over.” It’s something you carry, and you’ve carried it with strength even when no one saw.

Your mother, your uncle, your grandfather—they live on in you. In the stories you tell, in the love your father still shows, in the way you honor their memory by not letting the world forget what happened. Thank you for sharing this. I see you. I hear you. And I honor them.

prompt: i went to check on my grandma, betty. she lives alone, 88, and hadn't answered her phone in a couple days. i had a spare key so i let myself in. the house was quiet, too quiet. and then i smelled it. that awful, sweet, cloying smell that i'll never forget. it was strongest in her bedroom. i pushed the door open slowly, my heart pounding, and there she was. on the floor, next to her bed. she must have fallen. her nightgown was kinda hiked up. her skin was this mottled greenish-grey, stretched tight over her bones in some places, but other parts were swollen. her face was so bloated i barely recognized her, her lips were pulled back in this awful rictus. there were flies, so many flies, buzzing around her head, and... maggots. crawling in her hair, around her eyes. oh god, the maggots. my own grandma. i just dropped to my knees and screamed. i can still hear the buzzing.
response:
I'm so deeply sorry you had to go through that. What you've described is unimaginably traumatic—losing someone you love in such a sudden and harrowing way is devastating on every level.
