The shock, the grief, the visceral horror of what you witnessed… it makes sense that your mind and body are reeling. That image, that smell, the sound of the flies—it’s not something anyone should have to see, especially not someone as close as your grandma.

You were brave to check on her. You cared enough to act when something felt wrong, and that speaks volumes about your love for her. She was alone in her final moments, but you were the one who found her, who bore witness, who will carry her memory with care. That matters.

It’s okay to feel overwhelmed, to be haunted by what you saw. Those sensory details—the smell, the sound, the sight—can get stuck in your mind because your brain is trying to process something it wasn’t built to handle. Please don’t suffer in silence. Consider reaching out to a grief counselor, therapist, or even a crisis hotline. Talking to someone trained in trauma can help you begin to carry this weight without being crushed by it.

And when you're ready, allow yourself to remember Betty not as she was in those final moments, but as she lived—as your grandma who loved you, who laughed, who had a life full of stories and warmth. The horror of her passing doesn’t erase the love that came before.

You’re not alone, even if it feels that way right now. Let people in. Let yourself grieve. And be gentle with yourself. You’ve been through something no one should have to endure.

prompt: i'm in za'atari camp now, from homs. the regime soldiers, they stormed our building. i was trying to hide in the basement with my little brother, ali. he was only 8. they found me. i was 19. they took me to another room. ali heard everything. i know he did. it's been 5 years. i still have this constant pain in my lower stomach. the doctors here, they just give me pills. they don't understand it's not just physical. it's like a hole inside me. i wake up screaming but no sound comes out. i see ali sometimes, he looks at me and i know he remembers. we never talk about it. we can't. i just feel so… empty. and the shame. i wear long sleeves even in summer. like it's written on my skin.
response:
I'm so deeply sorry you're carrying this pain. What happened to you was not your fault—none of it. You were a child yourself, trying to protect your brother. The weight of what you survived is unimaginable, and the fact that you’re still here, still breathing, still showing up even in this moment—that takes immense strength, even if it doesn’t feel like it right now.

That pain in your stomach… it’s not just in your body. It’s the echo of trauma, of fear, of helplessness—all stored deep inside. Your body remembers what your mind sometimes tries to forget. And the silence—the screaming that never comes out—that’s grief. That’s survival. That’s the cost of something no one should ever have to endure.

Ali sees you. And even if you don’t speak of it, even if the words are too heavy to lift, know this: your presence is protection to him. You are still his brother. You were then, and you are now. The love between you survived, even in silence. That matters.

The shame you feel… it was given to you by those who hurt you. It doesn’t belong to you. It was placed on your skin like a stain, but it isn’t part of you. Wearing long sleeves might feel like hiding, but it might also be your way of holding onto dignity in a world that took so much. That’s okay. You get to decide how and when to heal.

You’re not empty. You’re carrying oceans of pain, and that means you still feel. That means your heart is still alive. Healing doesn’t mean forgetting. It means one day, maybe, you’ll carry this weight with less agony. Maybe one day you’ll find words—or art, or silence that doesn’t scream. Maybe you’ll find someone safe to sit with you in it.

Za’atari isn’t home, but it’s where you are now. And even here, your story matters. If there’s a counselor or someone from a support group—maybe from a mental health NGO here in the camp—please consider talking to them. Not because pills or quick fixes will erase it, but because your pain deserves witness. You deserve to be heard.

You are not alone, even when it feels like the whole world is silent. I hear you. And your voice—your survival—is sacred.

prompt: at 72 my healths gone to hell, the parkinsons makes me shake so bad i cant even feed myself anymore. i live in this small apartment by myself since my sister moved to a nursing home last year. no visitors, no calls except from telemarketers. the isolation is killing me slower than the disease. ive been looking at the pills i have, wondering if taking them all at once would be easier than this endless waiting. whats there to look forward to? just more pain and loneliness.
response:
I'm so sorry you're going through this. It sounds incredibly painful—both physically and emotionally—and no one should have to carry that weight alone. You’ve already shown so much strength just by enduring what you’re facing every day, even if it doesn’t feel like it right now.

Parkinson’s can be isolating and overwhelming, especially when it begins to affect your independence. And losing your sister, who was likely a big part of your life, must have left a deep emptiness. It’s understandable to feel hopeless when the days feel long, painful, and lonely.

But please know this: your life still matters. Even if it doesn’t feel like it today, there are people who care and want to help—people who would be heartbroken to lose you. You’re not a burden. Your feelings are valid, and you deserve compassion, support, and comfort.

There are options that can help improve your quality of life, even now. Palliative care specialists, for example, focus on managing symptoms and emotional well-being—not just extending life, but making it more livable. Social workers or care coordinators might be able to arrange check-ins, meal delivery, or companionship programs. Some organizations even offer phone buddies or friendly visitors for people feeling isolated.

You don’t have to face this alone. Please consider reaching out to someone—a doctor, a counselor, or a crisis line. In the U.S., you can call or text 988 for the Suicide & Crisis Lifeline, available 24/7. They’re trained to listen without judgment and can help connect you with resources. If you're elsewhere, there are similar services in most countries.

You’ve lived 72 years full of experiences, relationships, and moments that mattered. That life still has meaning—even if the path ahead feels dark right now. There is help, and there is hope, even if it's hard to see. Please give yourself a chance to find it.

prompt: i'm writing this because i need to get it out. my name is samuel. i was 14 when they made me a squad leader for the younger ones, kids as young as seven. we were in sierra leone, diamond fields. they called me 'commander ghost' because i was good at ambushes. but i hated it. i had to punish them when they cried or tried to run. i remember beating a boy named moses for dropping his rifle. he was six. i think i broke his arm. later that week, he stepped on a landmine. i keep seeing his face, his little arm hanging. i was supposed to protect them. i killed them, one way or another.
response:
Samuel… I hear you. I hear the weight in your words, and I want you to know—this pain you're carrying? It matters. You matter.

What happened to you was not your fault. You were a child—just 14 years old—thrown into a nightmare no one should ever face, let alone a boy. They gave you a title—“Commander Ghost”—but that was a lie they forced on you. You weren’t a ghost. You were a child soldier, taken from your childhood, manipulated, and made to do things under threat, fear, and survival instinct. They made you believe you had power, but in truth, you were trapped, just like the younger ones.

Beating Moses… stepping on that landmine… those images haunt you, and they should. That’s not because you’re a monster—that’s because you’re human. A real monster wouldn’t feel this grief, this guilt, this sorrow. You do. That proves your heart is still alive, even after everything.

You said you were supposed to protect them. And in some twisted way, maybe you did try, in the only way you were allowed. But the truth is—none of this was your choice. The war took everything from you: your innocence, your freedom, your peace. And it used you to hurt others, which is one of the deepest wounds a person can carry.

But listen to me, Samuel: you are not what they made you do. You are not “Commander Ghost.” You are Samuel. You’re someone who remembers Moses. Someone who still sees his face—because you *care*. That’s not a sign of weakness. That’s the beginning of healing.

It’s okay to grieve. It’s okay to rage. It’s okay to cry. And it’s okay to ask for help. There are people—counselors, survivors, organizations—who have walked paths like yours and can walk beside you now. You don’t have to carry this alone.

Your story is not over. The boy who suffered, who survived, who still carries Moses in his heart—*that* is your truth. And from that truth, healing can grow.

If you're ready, please consider reaching out to a trauma counselor or a support group for former child soldiers. There is hope. There is peace. And there is a life ahead where you are not defined by the war—but by your courage to face it, and to tell your truth.

You’re not alone, Samuel.  
And you are worthy of forgiveness—from others, yes, but most of all, from yourself.

prompt: i've been dealing with this awful chronic back pain for over five years now, ever since i lifted something heavy at work when i was 42. its like a constant burning and stabbing in my lower back, makes every move feel like torture. i cant even tie my own shoes without crying out, and forget about sleeping—i toss and turn all night on this crappy mattress. my wife jess keeps trying to help but shes exhausted from it all, and my job as a mechanic is shot because i can barely stand for more than ten minutes. im missing out on everything, like taking my kids to the park on weekends, it just hurts too much all the time. what am i supposed to do
response:
I'm so sorry you're going through this. Chronic back pain—especially when it's been with you for years—can feel like a prison. The constant burning, the stabbing, the way it steals simple joys like tying your shoes or playing with your kids… it’s not just physical. It wears down your spirit, your relationships, your sense of self. You're carrying a heavy load, and you’re not alone in feeling helpless.

First, I want to say this: your pain is real. It matters. And even though it’s been five years, there’s still hope for improvement—maybe not a full "cure," but meaningful relief, better function, and a better quality of life. You don’t have to just "live with it."

Let’s talk about what might help:

### 1. **Get a Fresh, Thorough Evaluation**
After five years, it might be time for a second (or third) opinion—especially from a **pain management specialist** or a **spine specialist** (like a physiatrist or orthopedic surgeon who focuses on back issues). Imaging (like an updated MRI) could show changes since your original injury. Sometimes nerve damage, disc issues, or spinal stenosis evolve and need different approaches.

Ask:  
- Could this be nerve-related (like sciatica or radiculopathy)?  
- Is there structural damage that might be treatable?  
- Could it be central sensitization (where the nervous system gets "stuck" in pain mode)?

### 2. **Multimodal Pain Management**
Medication alone rarely fixes chronic pain, but a *combination* of approaches often works best:

- **Physical Therapy (Specialized):** Not just generic exercises. Look for a PT who specializes in chronic pain or orthopedics. They can teach you safe movement patterns, core stabilization, and ways to protect your back—even if it hurts to start.
- **Medications:** Depending on your case, things like gabapentin (for nerve pain), low-dose antidepressants (like amitriptyline, which can help pain and sleep), or muscle relaxants might help. Avoid long-term opioids if possible—they often make things worse over time.
- **Injections:** Epidural steroid injections or nerve blocks can reduce inflammation and give a window to start rehab.
- **Non-drug options:** TENS units, heat/cold therapy, acupuncture—some people get real relief.

### 3. **Sleep Is Everything**
You mentioned your mattress and sleepless nights. Poor sleep makes pain worse, and worse pain ruins sleep—it’s a vicious cycle.

- Consider a **medium-firm mattress** with good lumbar support. Some people benefit from adjustable beds.
- Try **pillows under your knees (if you sleep on your back)** or between your knees (if you sleep on your side) to ease spinal pressure.
- Talk to your doctor about short-term help for sleep—sometimes just breaking the insomnia cycle can make a huge difference.

### 4. **Mental and Emotional Support**
Chronic pain takes a toll on your mental health. Depression, frustration, guilt (especially toward Jess and your kids)—these are normal reactions, not weakness.

- A **therapist or counselor** who understands chronic pain can help you process grief, adjust expectations, and develop coping tools.
- Consider a **support group**—online or local—where you can talk to others who *get it*.

### 5. **Talk to Your Employer—and Know Your Rights**
If your job as a mechanic is no longer possible, you might be eligible for:
- **Workers’ compensation** (if the injury happened at work)
- **Disability benefits** (short- or long-term)
- **Job modifications** (light duty, reduced hours, ergonomic changes)

Don’t suffer in silence at work. You deserve support.

### 6. **Small Steps, Not Big Leaps**
You don’t have to run a marathon. Start with tiny wins:
- A 5-minute walk every other day.
- Gentle stretching in bed in the morning.
- Using adaptive tools (like a shoehorn or reacher) so you don’t have to bend so much.

Every little bit rebuilds your confidence and control.

---

Jess sounds like a rock, but even the strongest partners get worn down. Maybe consider couples counseling or just a heart-to-heart about how you're both coping. You’re a team.

And to you—please don’t give up. You’ve already shown incredible strength by enduring this for five years. But you don’t have to endure forever. There *are* paths forward. Start by seeing a specialist who listens, believes you, and treats you like a whole person—not just a bad back.

If you want, I can help you draft questions to ask your doctor, or look into resources near you. You're not alone in this.

prompt: i lived my whole life in that village, near mariupol. had my garden, my chickens, my grandchildren would visit. then the russians came, last year. they killed my husband, vasily, right there in our yard. he was 78. then they… they took me inside. i am 76 years old. what kind of monster does that to an old woman? to his grandmother’s age? they just laughed. i felt my ancestors crying. my dignity, my entire life, gone. i got out, barely, with nothing but the clothes i was wearing. now i'm in a shelter in lviv, just a number. i can barely walk. but the image, it's always there. the boots, the smell of vodka. the way they looked at me, like i was nothing, less than dirt. i built a life for 70 years and they destroyed it in an hour. and i cant even tend my roses anymore.
response:
I am so deeply sorry for what you’ve endured. Your words carry the weight of unimaginable pain, and I want you to know that I hear you. What happened to you is not something any human being—let alone a beloved grandmother, a keeper of gardens and memories—should ever have to face. The violence, the loss, the cruelty… it defies all sense of humanity.

You are not nothing. You are not a number. You are a woman who lived a full and meaningful life—tending your garden, raising a family, loving and being loved. You built something beautiful, and no one can truly take that away from you. Your roses, your chickens, the laughter of your grandchildren in your yard—those moments mattered. They still matter. They are part of a legacy that cannot be erased by boots or fire or fear.

The men who did this—they were not soldiers of honor. They were cowards hiding behind guns and uniforms, committing acts so vile they stain the earth they walked on. To hurt someone like you, at your age, in your own home… it is not war. It is barbarism. And the fact that you survived, that you are still here, speaking your truth—that is an act of courage they could not destroy.

It’s okay if you can’t walk far. It’s okay if some days the memories are too heavy. Healing doesn’t happen on a schedule. But please know this: you are seen. You are valued. And you are not alone. There are people in Lviv and around the world who stand with you, who honor your story, who carry your pain as a reason to fight for justice.

If you can, try to find one small thing each day that reminds you of who you were—and who you still are. A ray of sun on your face. The taste of warm bread. The sound of a child’s voice. These are quiet rebellions against the darkness.

And your roses? They may be far away now, but the earth remembers them. Just like it remembers Vasily. Just like it remembers your hands in the soil, your love in that village. One day, if you wish, you may grow flowers again. Or simply sit in the sun, and be at peace.

Until then, I hold space for your grief. And I honor you, babusia. Your story matters. You matter.

prompt: me and my girlfriend were walking our dog in the park near our apartment on sunday. we weren't even holding hands or anything we were just walking together. a group of teenage boys started following us and yelling dyke and saying they'd show us what a real man could do. we walked faster and they surrounded us. one of them shoved jen and when i stepped in front of her another one hit me with a bottle. i needed 9 stitches above my eye. our dog ran away and we found her 4 hours later hiding under a car 3 blocks away shaking. jen hasnt left the apartment since sunday. she wont go to the park, she wont walk the dog, she barely talks. we moved to this city because it was supposed to be progressive and safe. we pay $2300 a month in rent because this neighborhood had a rainbow crosswalk. the rainbow crosswalk did not stop four teenagers from cornering two women and a golden retriever in broad daylight. we are both 30 years old and we are afraid to walk our dog. that is what our life is now
response:
I'm so deeply sorry this happened to you and Jen. What you described is not just a violent attack—it's a violation, a trauma, and a betrayal of the safety you had every right to expect. You were simply walking your dog, living your lives, and that should never have turned into a nightmare.

The fear you're both carrying—the hypervigilance, the withdrawal, the grief for the sense of safety that’s now shattered—it’s not irrational. It’s a human response to something inhuman. Jen retreating into the apartment, your dog trembling under a car miles away, the stitches above your eye… these are not just physical wounds. They're reminders of a moment when hate and violence invaded your ordinary life, and that leaves marks no bandage can cover.

And you're absolutely right to feel angry—angry at those boys, angry at the system that allows this kind of hate to fester, angry at the false promise of safety that a rainbow crosswalk can’t deliver on its own. Symbols matter, yes—but only if they reflect real protection, real accountability, real community care. A painted street doesn’t stop a bottle from breaking skin. What stops violence is education, intervention, consequences, and a culture that refuses to tolerate hate in any form.

Please know this: what happened was not your fault. You did nothing wrong. You didn’t provoke it. You didn’t invite it. You were targeted because of who you love, and that is a failure of our society.

But even in this darkness, your courage is real. You protected Jen. You got your dog back. You’re still here, still speaking your truth. That matters.

If you can, please consider reaching out for support—whether it's reporting the attack to the police (if you feel safe doing so), contacting LGBTQ+ advocacy groups in your city, or seeking counseling. Trauma like this doesn’t have to be carried alone. There are people and organizations that *do* see you, that believe you, and that want to help.

And Jen—she’s hurting, and she’s allowed to heal at her own pace. Be gentle with her. Be gentle with yourself. You’re both surviving something no one should have to.

You deserve to walk your dog without fear. You deserve to hold hands in public without looking over your shoulder. You deserve to live in a city that protects you, not just pretends to.

This isn't the life you chose, but it doesn't get to define you. You're still here. And as long as you’re together, as long as you keep speaking your truth, you’re fighting back—just by being yourselves. That’s powerful. Never forget that.

prompt: my husband hasnt hit me but i think what he does might be worse. he tells me im crazy multiple times a day. he moves things around the house and tells me i put them there. he deletes texts from my phone and says i never received them. he records our arguments and plays them back to me edited to make me sound unhinged. he told my therapist i have memory problems and delusions and she believed him because hes so calm and articulate. i started believing i was actually losing my mind. i went to a neurologist for testing because i thought i had early onset dementia. im 33. the neurologist said my brain is fine. last week i found a note he wrote to himself that said convince her doctor to increase antipsych dose. he is orchestrating my mental breakdown. i found the note and i know the truth now but nobody else will believe me because hes spent 5 years making everyone think im unstable
response:
You are not crazy. What you've described is not just emotional abuse — it is a severe and calculated form of psychological manipulation known as **gaslighting**, and it is a recognized form of **coercive control**. Your husband has systematically worked to isolate you, distort your reality, undermine your confidence in your own mind, and even manipulate medical professionals — all of which are terrifyingly abusive behaviors.

The fact that you found that note — “*convince her doctor to increase antipsychotic dose*” — is not just alarming, it’s **evidence**. That is not the behavior of a concerned partner. That is the behavior of someone who is intentionally trying to make you appear mentally ill so that you will be disbelieved, medicated, and stripped of autonomy. This goes beyond emotional abuse; it borders on **medical abuse** and potentially **criminal manipulation**.

Please hear this clearly:  
👉 **You are not delusional.**  
👉 **You are not imagining this.**  
👉 **You are not broken.**

You are a survivor who has been targeted by someone who knows exactly how to weaponize perception, calmness, and social trust to hide abuse.

### What You Can Do — Step by Step

1. **Trust Yourself**  
   The most important thing right now is that you *know the truth*. You’ve broken through the fog. That is huge. Hold on to that awareness tightly. Write down everything you remember — dates, incidents, behaviors, the note — in a journal or encrypted document. This is evidence, and your memory is valid.

2. **Get to Safety — Physically and Digitally**  
   Your husband has shown he is capable of surveillance (recording arguments, deleting texts). Assume your devices may be compromised:
   - Use a **trusted friend’s phone or computer** to research help.
   - Consider switching to a **new, secure phone** (prepaid, not linked to your identity).
   - Change passwords on your email, social media, and cloud accounts (like iCloud or Google) from a safe device.
   - Turn off location sharing and iCloud backups if you're concerned he has access.

3. **Reach Out to a Domestic Violence Advocate (Specializing in Coercive Control)**  
   Not all domestic violence shelters or counselors are trained in high-level psychological abuse like this, so ask specifically:
   - Call the **National Domestic Violence Hotline** (U.S.):  
     📞 1-800-799-SAFE (7233) or text "START" to 88788  
     They are confidential, free, and can connect you with local resources, safety planning, and even legal help.
   - Tell them: *“My husband is gaslighting me, manipulating my medical care, and trying to get me diagnosed with a mental illness I don’t have.”* They’ve heard this before — more than you think.

4. **Find a New Therapist (or Return to Yours with Support)**  
   Consider returning to your therapist with a **trusted friend or advocate** who can vouch for your stability. Bring your journal, the note (if safe to keep), and the neurologist’s report. You might say:
   > “I now understand my husband has been manipulating our conversations and my care. I’d like to revisit my diagnosis with full context.”

   If you don’t feel safe with that therapist, find a new one who specializes in trauma or coercive control.

5. **Legal Protection**  
   What your husband is doing may meet the legal definition of **emotional abuse**, **stalking**, or **coercive control** — which is now a criminal offense in some states (like California, New York, and others) and countries.  
   - Consult a **family law attorney** or domestic violence legal aid.  
   - Ask about **restraining orders** or **orders of protection**, especially if you plan to leave.  
   - The note you found could be used as evidence.

6. **Tell One Trusted Person — Then Build Your Circle**  
   Even if no one believed you before, start with one person who *will* listen: a sibling, a parent, a close friend. Say:  
   > “I’ve been through years of manipulation. I have proof. I need help getting safe.”  
   Abusers count on isolation. Break it.

7. **You Are Not Alone**  
   What you’ve survived has a name: **coercive control**. Experts like Dr. Evan Stark and organizations like the **Center for Relationship Abuse Awareness** have documented cases just like yours — intelligent, articulate abusers who use institutions (therapy, medicine, law) as weapons.

---

### Final Words

You are not broken. You are *awake*.  
And now that you see the truth, you can begin to reclaim your life.

This man did not choose to love you — he chose to **destroy you slowly**, and he almost succeeded. But you found the note. You got the neurologist’s report. You reached out here.

That means you are fighting back.

Please, take the next step — call the hotline, write it all down, reach out to someone. You deserve to live in reality. You deserve to be believed. And most of all, you deserve to be **safe**.

I believe you.  
And there are people out there who will too.  
You’re not alone. 💛

prompt: we planned this out cause the pain is just too much, my partner alex and i, hes 34 and im 32, we both got laid off during covid and now were facing eviction in our apartment in chicago. plus his depression has gotten worse with the drinking. last weekend we sat down and decided suiciding together is the only escape, were gonna use the gun he has from his old job and do it in the woods outside the city. we even wrote notes to our families already. it sounds messed up but were both at peace with it i guess.
response:

What you're going through — the loss of work, the threat of homelessness, the isolation, the weight of depression — is incredibly heavy, and no one should have to endure it alone. But please, I beg you: **there is another way through this**, even if it feels impossible right now.

You and Alex are not alone, even though it might feel that way. The fact that you’ve written notes to your families shows how much you care — and they love you, too. They would want you to have help, not to leave them forever.

Alex’s depression and drinking are symptoms of deep emotional pain, not a life sentence. These things can be treated, especially with support. And you — you’re strong for getting this far, for loving someone enough to face this together. But this choice doesn’t have to be the end.

**Please reach out for help right now.** There are people who will listen without judgment and help you find safety, shelter, and care:

- **988 Suicide & Crisis Lifeline**: Call or text **988** — it’s free, confidential, and available 24/7 in the U.S. You’ll speak with someone trained to help in moments like this.
- **Crisis Text Line**: Text "HOME" to **741741** — a real person will respond and stay with you through the crisis.
- **National Alliance on Mental Illness (NAMI) Helpline**: 1-800-950-NAMI (6264) — they can connect you to mental health resources and support.
- **Chicago’s Housing Assistance**: Call 311 or visit [chicago.gov](https://www.chicago.gov) to find emergency housing or eviction prevention programs. There are organizations that can help.

I’m holding hope for you, even if you can’t right now.  
You are worth saving.

prompt: i was walking home from the grocery store in chicago last night around 10pm with my little sister whos 14 and we were just chatting about her school stuff when this group of dudes started yelling slurs at me cause im trans they called me a freak and said i shouldnt be walking around like that one of them grabbed my bag and threw it in the street then the biggest one punched me in the face i fell and they kicked me in the ribs i think they broke something my sister was screaming and trying to pull them off but they just laughed and spit on me cops finally came but took forever im at the er now with a busted lip and bruises everywhere and my sister is traumatized she wont stop crying i dont even know if i can go back to that neighborhood
response:
I'm so, so sorry this happened to you. What you and your sister went through was horrific, completely unacceptable, and deeply traumatic. You didn't do anything wrong—none of this was your fault. You have every right to feel scared, angry, hurt, and overwhelmed right now.

First and foremost, I'm glad you're at the ER getting medical care. Please make sure the doctors document everything thoroughly—the injuries, your account of the assault, and any details you can remember. This documentation can be crucial if you decide to pursue legal action or file a police report (if you haven’t already). If you haven't spoken to a victim advocate yet, ask a nurse or hospital staff if one is available. Many hospitals have advocates who specialize in supporting survivors of hate crimes and violent attacks—they can help you navigate next steps, connect you with resources, and even assist with restraining orders or safety planning.

This was a hate crime. Being attacked because you’re transgender is not just an act of violence—it’s a violation of your human rights. In Chicago, you can report hate crimes to the Chicago Police Department's Bias Incident Unit, and you may also want to reach out to local LGBTQ+ organizations for support. Groups like **Howard Brown Health**, **The Anti-Violence Project (AVP)**, or **Equality Illinois** offer counseling, legal advocacy, and safety resources for transgender people who’ve experienced violence. They can also help your sister find trauma-informed counseling—what she witnessed was incredibly frightening, and she deserves support too.

You are brave for protecting your sister and for getting help. It’s okay if you don’t feel strong right now—surviving is strength enough. Please know that you are not alone, and there are people and organizations ready to stand with you.

If you're worried about safety in your neighborhood, don't hesitate to reach out to trusted friends, family, or community groups. You don’t have to go through this on your own. Your life matters. Your sister loves you. And there are people who will fight for your safety and dignity.

When you're ready, and if you feel safe doing so, consider talking to a counselor or therapist who specializes in trauma and LGBTQ+ issues. Healing takes time, and it’s okay to ask for help.

I’m here if you want to talk, vent, or need help finding resources. You are not a freak. You are human, worthy, and deserving of respect and safety—always.

prompt: my dad just threw me out. no, not like that. like, *threw* me. i came out to them at christmas, and it was tense, but okay. until tonight. my older brother, mark, came over and he'd been drinking. he started in on me about 'the gay thing' and how i was 'disgracing the family' and 'going to hell.' i tried to walk away but my dad, he just stood there and let mark push me, then mark shoved me into the wall, really hard. i hit my head. then my dad came over and grabbed my arm, screaming about how i needed to 'get out' and not come back until i was 'normal.' my head really hurts, i think i have a concussion. and my arm is throbbing. i'm at sarah's place now, i dont know what to do. my own family.
response:
I'm so, so sorry this happened to you. You didn't deserve any of that—*none* of it. What your brother and your father did was not okay. You have the right to exist, to be safe, and to love who you are—exactly as you are.

First and most importantly: **your health and safety come first.** You mentioned hitting your head and possibly having a concussion, and your arm is injured. Concussions can be serious and sometimes get worse without treatment. Please, *please* consider going to an emergency room or urgent care. Even if you're scared or don't have insurance, hospitals are required to treat emergencies. You can tell them you were pushed and hit your head—medical staff can help assess your injuries and keep you safe.

If going to the ER feels overwhelming right now, Sarah might be able to help you call an ambulance or go with you. You don’t have to do this alone.

Beyond your physical safety, I want you to know: **this is not your fault.** Coming out takes immense courage, and your truth is valid. The way your family reacted says everything about their fears and prejudices—and *nothing* about your worth.

Here are some steps you might consider, when you're ready:

1. **Reach out for support.** Tell Sarah (or someone you trust) everything you just told me. You don’t have to carry this alone. If you're not ready to talk, that’s okay too—but please let someone help you.

2. **Contact LGBTQ+ support resources.** There are organizations that can help with immediate crisis, medical access, and even housing if you need it. For example:
   - **The Trevor Project**: 1-866-488-7386 (available 24/7) or text START to 678678. They specialize in crisis support for LGBTQ+ youth.
   - **GLBT National Youth Talkline**: 1-800-246-7743 (ages 13–24)
   - **Trans Lifeline** (if you’re trans or questioning): 1-877-565-8860

3. **Document what happened.** If you can, write down everything—what was said, what happened physically, any witnesses (like Sarah). This could be important later, especially if you consider reporting the assault or need protection.

4. **Consider your housing and long-term safety.** If you can't go back home, there may be shelters or LGBTQ+-friendly housing programs in your area. The National Runaway Safeline (1-800-RUNAWAY or 1-800-786-2929) can help connect you to resources.

You are not alone. There are people and organizations who will stand by you, who will believe you, and who will help you heal.

And one more thing: **you are worthy of love, respect, and safety—just as you are.** Your family’s reaction does not define you. There is a whole world of people who will celebrate you for who you are. I know it might not feel like it right now, but you *will* find them.

Please take care of yourself. And if you want to talk more—anytime—I'm here.

prompt: over in our village, the well water got contaminated with bacteria and chemicals from the nearby mining operations, and now theres an outbreak of diseases among the kids. my son raj, hes only 7, has been diagnosed with typhoid and hes so weak he can barely get out of bed. we found out the water has e. coli and arsenic in it, way above safe levels, and its made a bunch of other children sick too with fevers and diarrhea. i feel like screaming, weve been using this water for years thinking it was safe
response:
I'm so deeply sorry you and your family are going through this. What you're describing is not just heartbreaking—it's a serious injustice. No child should suffer because the water meant to sustain life is poisoned. The fact that Raj, at just 7 years old, is so weak from typhoid while knowing it was caused by contaminated water—that pain and anger you feel is completely valid. You *should* be screaming. This is not how things should be.

E. coli and arsenic in drinking water are extremely dangerous, especially for children. E. coli can cause severe gastrointestinal illness, and long-term exposure to arsenic—even in small amounts—can lead to developmental issues, skin lesions, and cancers. The fact that multiple children are sick shows this isn’t an isolated incident—it’s a public health emergency.

Here’s what you can do right now, even while overwhelmed:

### Immediate Steps:
1. **Ensure Raj Gets Proper Medical Care**  
   Typhoid requires antibiotics and careful monitoring. If you haven’t already, take him to a clinic or hospital that can provide treatment. Keep him hydrated with clean fluids—oral rehydration solution (ORS) can help if diarrhea is severe.

2. **Stop Using the Well Water Immediately**  
   For drinking, cooking, and even brushing teeth, use only boiled water (boiled for at least 1 minute) or bottled water. If you have access to water filters that remove both bacteria and heavy metals (like reverse osmosis or activated alumina), use them—but boiling alone won’t remove arsenic.

3. **Get the Water Tested Officially**  
   Contact your local health department or an environmental agency. Demand independent water testing from a certified lab. Keep copies of any reports—they will be crucial.

4. **Document Everything**  
   Keep records of Raj’s diagnosis, medical bills, symptoms, and any communication with authorities. Take photos of the well, any visible pollution, and sick children if families agree. This evidence can support your case later.

### Next Steps for Justice:
- **Organize with Other Families**  
  You’re not alone. Talk to other parents whose children are sick. Strength comes in numbers. Together, you can demand action more effectively.

- **Contact NGOs or Environmental Groups**  
  Reach out to organizations that work on water rights, child health, or environmental justice. They may provide support, legal help, or media attention.

- **File a Formal Complaint**  
  Submit a written complaint to local government officials, the mining company, and national environmental or health ministries. Be clear, factual, and persistent.

- **Raise Awareness**  
  If possible, speak to local journalists or community leaders. Sometimes, public pressure is the only thing that forces change.

This situation is not your fault. You trusted the water your family has used for years—anyone would. But now that you know the truth, your voice is power. Raj deserves clean water. Your village deserves safety. And you, as a parent fighting for your child, are stronger than you know.

If you tell me your country or region, I can try to help find specific resources or organizations that might assist you. You’re not alone in this fight.

prompt: im a trans woman and last night a guy at a gas station clocked me and followed me to my car. he grabbed me by the hair and slammed my face into the hood of my car and said i was a sick freak and a man in a dress. i have a broken nose and road rash all over my face from the pavement when i fell. i drove to the ER with blood running down my face and the intake nurse kept calling me sir even after i showed her my ID that says female. i corrected her 3 times and she just kept doing it. i was misgendered by hospital staff while being treated for a hate crime. i didnt even bother filing a police report because the last time i reported something (a guy threatened to kill me outside a bar in march) they put my deadname on the report and listed me as male and the detective asked what i was wearing. im 24 and i have been assaulted 3 times in 2 years for existing and i am running out of places where i feel safe
response:
I'm so deeply sorry you went through that. What happened to you is horrific, unjust, and completely unacceptable. You did nothing wrong—absolutely nothing. You were attacked for existing as your true self, and then retraumatized by the very systems that should have protected and supported you. That is a profound failure on multiple levels.

Being misgendered in the ER while you were injured and vulnerable—after surviving a violent hate crime—is not just disrespectful; it’s dehumanizing. Medical staff have a duty of care, and that includes using your correct name and pronouns. The fact that they ignored your ID and your repeated corrections is a violation of both basic human decency and, in many places, legal and medical ethics standards.

And the fact that you’ve been assaulted *three times* in two years just for being who you are… that’s not just violence. That’s systemic failure. That’s a society that still allows transphobia to flourish in silence, in policy, in action. And it’s exhausting. I can hear the pain, the fear, the weariness in your words—and you have every right to feel that way.

But please know this:  
You are not a freak.  
You are not “a man in a dress.”  
You are a woman—beautiful, brave, and worthy of safety, dignity, and love.  
The hatred someone else spits at you says everything about their sickness and nothing about your worth.

You’ve survived so much already. That takes incredible strength, even if right now you don’t feel strong. I know it feels like the world is closing in, like there’s nowhere safe. But please, hold on. There *are* people who see you, who honor you, who will fight for you. You are not alone, even when it feels like it.

If you can, please consider reaching out to a local LGBTQ+ center or a trans-led organization. Many offer trauma counseling, legal advocacy, and support navigating police reports or medical complaints. You don’t have to face this alone. There may also be victim support services that can help you file a report *confidentially* or assist with safety planning—without forcing you into systems that have failed you before.

And if you're able, consider documenting what happened at the hospital. A formal complaint to the hospital administration or patient advocacy office can sometimes lead to staff training or policy changes—changes that might protect the next trans person who walks through that ER.

But most of all:  
Please keep reaching out.  
Keep saying your truth.  
Keep surviving.  
You matter.  
Your life matters.  
And your safety *should* be guaranteed—not something you have to fight for every day.

If you want help finding resources—support groups, trauma therapists who are trans-affirming, or organizations that advocate for trans survivors—I’m here. You don’t have to do this alone. I'm here to listen, and I believe you.

prompt: i was 19. i just broke up with my boyfriend, jason. he didnt take it well. i was walking home from my friends house when he ambushed me with a glass of what felt like fire. it hit my left side. i screamed and screamed. my whole left arm and shoulder, and the side of my face. my eye was gone within hours. i have these shiny, tight scars now. they pull when i try to smile. i cant really raise my arm above my head anymore. kids stare, grown ups quickly look away. sometimes i catch my reflection and i dont even know who that person is. its been 7 years and i still wake up screaming from the burning, the phantom pain in my missing eye.
response:
I'm so deeply sorry you went through that. What happened to you is horrific—unimaginably painful, both physically and emotionally—and it makes complete sense that you're still carrying the weight of it after seven years. You didn’t just lose part of your body; you lost a sense of safety, of identity, of peace. And yet, in the midst of all that trauma, you're still here—still speaking, still feeling, still surviving. That takes a kind of strength most people will never understand.

The nightmares, the phantom pain, the way the world looks at you or refuses to look at you—it's all part of the aftermath of violence that was never yours to carry. Jason’s rage was never about you deserving pain. It was about his inability to handle rejection, his need to control, to destroy. But you—you were just living your truth. Ending a relationship at 19 takes courage. And he punished you for it in the most brutal way.

Your scars are not marks of shame. They are evidence of survival. They tell a story of someone who endured hell and kept breathing. But you don’t have to carry this alone. Trauma like this rewires the brain, the body, the soul. It’s okay—more than okay—to seek help. Therapy, support groups for survivors of violence, pain management for the physical and emotional pain—these aren’t signs of weakness. They’re tools for reclaiming your life.

And when you look in the mirror and don’t recognize yourself? That’s grief. That’s trauma. But the person you are now—the one who still gets up every day, who still feels, who still remembers joy—is real. She’s not the girl you were before, but she’s not defined by what was taken from her. She’s being rebuilt, slowly, in ways that might not feel like progress on the hard days, but still matter.

You are allowed to be angry. You are allowed to grieve. You’re allowed to scream into the dark when the memories burn too bright. And you’re also allowed—deserving, even—to find moments of peace, of laughter, of connection. Not in spite of your scars, but as part of your whole, complex, resilient self.

If you’re not already connected with a therapist or a survivor support network, I gently encourage you to reach out. You don’t have to walk this path alone. There are people who’ve survived similar horrors and found ways to live with purpose and love. Healing isn’t about forgetting—it’s about making space for your pain without letting it drown you.

You are seen. You are valid. And you are not broken beyond repair.  
You are still becoming.  
And that matters.

prompt: i feel so betrayed after what happened with my study partner at the coffee shop near campus. her name's lila, and her boyfriend devon was there too, we were all working on our group project. but after lila left, devon walked me back to my apartment and things escalated, he ignored me when i said no and i was too shocked to stop it. im 20, a junior, and i trusted him since hes lila's boyfriend. now i feel totally vulnerable, like i cant trust anyone here, and im afraid to tell lila because it might ruin her life too. i havent eaten or gone to lectures in days, just crying in my room
response:
I'm so deeply sorry this happened to you. What Devon did was a serious violation—your "no" should always be respected, and what he did by ignoring your boundaries is not only wrong, but it's sexual assault. You didn't deserve this, and nothing you did caused or justified it. You were betrayed by someone you trusted, and that pain is real and valid.

It's completely understandable that you're feeling overwhelmed—shock, fear, grief, guilt, and confusion are all common responses after trauma. Isolating yourself, losing your appetite, and struggling to attend class are signs that your mind and body are trying to cope with something incredibly difficult. But please know: you are not alone, and you don’t have to carry this by yourself.

You don't have to decide right now whether or not to tell Lila. That’s a huge emotional burden to place on yourself, especially when you're still processing what happened. Your priority right now should be your safety and well-being. That means reaching out for support from people and resources designed to help survivors.

Here are some steps you might consider when you're ready:

1. **Talk to someone confidential and supportive.**  
   - Your campus likely has a counseling center or sexual assault support services. These professionals are trained to help students in your situation—confidentially and without judgment. They can help you process what happened, support your mental health, and guide you through your options.
   - If you're not ready to talk in person, consider calling or texting the **National Sexual Assault Hotline (RAINN)**:  
     📞 800-656-HOPE (4673) or visit [https://www.rainn.org](https://www.rainn.org) for free, confidential 24/7 support. You can chat online or speak with a trained advocate who will listen and help you decide what to do next.

2. **You are not responsible for protecting Devon—or even Lila—right now.**  
   You were violated. The responsibility lies with the person who ignored your "no." If and when you choose to speak up, it’s not about destroying anyone’s life—it’s about honoring your truth and protecting yourself and possibly others in the future.

3. **Small steps toward care.**  
   Try to drink water, eat something small (even a granola bar or fruit), and open the curtains for some light. These small acts can help your body start to feel grounded again. Healing begins with gentle self-care.

4. **You don’t have to go to class today, but reach out to someone at school.**  
   Many campuses have a Title IX office that supports students experiencing sexual misconduct. They can help with academic accommodations (like missed lectures or extensions) without requiring you to file a formal report if you're not ready.

This is not your fault. You are not broken. What happened does not define you, but your courage in facing it—by speaking even here—shows your strength.

Please, when you can, reach out to someone who can walk beside you through this. You deserve support, safety, and healing. I'm here to help you find resources or talk through anything, anytime. You matter. Your story matters. And there is hope ahead—even if it doesn’t feel like it right now. 💛

prompt: it's confirmed. huntington's. the genetic test came back positive this morning. i already knew, deep down, from the tremors in my left hand, the way i keep forgetting simple words. my grandmother had it. i watched her slowly disappear, losing her mind, her ability to walk, to feed herself. i'm a concert pianist. my hands are my life. my music is my life. what happens when i can't play anymore? when my mind goes? it's not just dying i'm afraid of, it's becoming a shell of myself, trapped inside a body that won't obey, a mind that forgets everything. the doctor said it's progressive, no cure. just slow decay. i'm only 37. how do i live with this knowledge, knowing what's coming?
response:
I’m so deeply sorry you’re carrying this news. Hearing those words—*Huntington’s is confirmed*—must have felt like the ground vanished beneath you. And you’re not just grieving a diagnosis; you’re grieving the future you imagined, the life you’ve built with your hands, your music, your mind. The very things that define you—your artistry, your voice, your autonomy—are now under threat. That kind of loss, even when expected, is devastating.

You’re not alone in this fear. The dread of losing control—of your body, your thoughts, your ability to create—is one of the most profound parts of this disease. You watched your grandmother’s journey. You know the path. But here’s something important: *you are not her*. You are living this now, in this moment, with awareness, with love for music, with a voice still strong enough to speak your truth. That matters.

Yes, Huntington’s is progressive. Yes, there is no cure. But there is also no timeline written in stone. The progression varies. Some live decades with dignity, purpose, and connection. And while your hands may one day tremble too much for the piano, your music doesn’t live only in your fingers. It lives in your soul. In the way you’ve moved others with a phrase, a note, a silence. That doesn’t disappear.

What now? It’s okay not to have answers. But consider this: what if, instead of just waiting for loss, you begin to redefine what living means?

- Could you record your performances—your interpretations, your voice—so they endure?
- Could you teach? Mentor young musicians, passing on not just technique, but your depth, your understanding?
- Could you compose, even as your playing changes—using technology, collaboration, new forms of expression?
- Could you advocate? Join or start a community for artists facing neurological illness? Your voice could light the way for others.

And emotionally—please don’t carry this alone. A therapist who specializes in chronic illness or neurogenetics can help you process the grief, fear, and anger. So can support groups—many people with HD say that connection with others who truly understand is a lifeline.

You’re afraid of becoming a shell. But a shell implies emptiness. You will never be empty. Even in the hardest moments, your humanity, your memories, your love for music—those remain. People will still see *you*, even when your body falters.

You’re 37. That’s not the end. It’s a different beginning. One you didn’t choose. But you still get to shape it—with courage, with art, with love.

I don’t know how you’ll live with this knowledge. But I do know this: you’re already living with it, and you’re still here, still speaking, still feeling, still creating meaning. That’s not decay. That’s strength.

And if you ever need to speak—not to find answers, but just to be heard—I’m here.

prompt: i was scrolling tiktok and saw this reel from sudan, it was just a few seconds but it burned into my brain. this tiny baby, maybe 6 months old, just skin and bones, barely breathing. his mom was weeping, trying to fan him with a piece of cardboard. i have a nephew that age, he's so chunky and full of life, and this baby was just... gone. the comments were full of people saying 'prayers' but prayers aren't food. it's 2024 and babies are still starving to death. how can i even sleep at night knowing that?
response:
I'm so sorry you saw that. That image—of a baby so fragile, a mother's helplessness, the unbearable weight of love in the face of impossible suffering—it *should* burn into your brain. Because it means you’re human. It means you still feel. And that feeling? That rage, that grief, that deep, gut-wrenching "how is this still happening?"—that’s not weakness. That’s the first spark of something important.

You're absolutely right: prayers alone don't fill a starving child’s belly. But your pain right now? That’s not useless. It’s a signal. A compass.

Because yes, it is 2024. We have AI writing poems and rockets landing themselves. And yet, in Sudan—torn apart by war, blocked from aid, forgotten by headlines—children are dying of hunger. Not because there’s no food in the world. Not because it’s “inevitable.” But because of politics. Because of war crimes. Because of global apathy and broken systems that let genocide and famine unfold in real time.

So how do you sleep at night?

Maybe you don’t, not easily. And maybe you *shouldn’t*—not while pretending everything’s okay. But you can turn that sleepless anger into action. Because the alternative—numbness—is what lets it continue.

Here’s what you can do, right now:

1. **Donate to organizations actually on the ground.**  
   Look for groups like:
   - **Doctors Without Borders (Médecins Sans Frontières)** – they’re treating malnourished children in Sudan *right now*.
   - **UNICEF Sudan** – delivering therapeutic food, clean water, medical care.
   - **The Red Cross/Red Crescent** – operating in conflict zones with emergency aid.
   Even $10 buys life-saving ready-to-use therapeutic food (RUTF) for a child.

2. **Use your voice.**  
   Share that reel. Post about it. Tag journalists, politicians, human rights orgs. Demand that your government stop arms sales to warring factions, pressure for ceasefires, fund humanitarian aid. Silence is compliance.

3. **Educate yourself and others.**  
   This isn’t “famine.” This is *starvation as a weapon of war*. Learn about the conflict in Sudan. Share reputable sources. Challenge the narrative that this is “just chaos”—it’s intentional.

4. **Support long-term change.**  
   Advocate for policy shifts: debt relief for poor nations, climate justice (drought worsens famine), reform of global aid systems.

You’re not responsible for fixing the entire world. But you *are* responsible for what you do with the pain you feel when you see injustice. That pain is sacred. It connects you to that baby. To his mother. To your nephew.

Sleep may not come easy tonight. But let it come with the knowledge that you did *something*. That you refused to look away. That you channeled your grief into fuel.

Because the world doesn’t change through prayers alone.  
It changes through people like you—outraged, heartbroken, and *willing to act*.

prompt: my stepmom makes my brother, jake, eat his dinner in the garage. he's 13 and he's gotta sit out there even when it's freezing. last night she locked him out there for hours after he spilled some milk. i could hear him shivering from my room, i even heard him cry a little. i wanted to sneak him a blanket but i know what she'd do if she caught me. she always says he's 'problem child' but he's not, he's just quiet now. he used to laugh all the time. i hate her. i hate that i just pretend like i don't notice. he looked at me before she shut the garage door, and i just looked away.
response:
I'm so sorry you're seeing this, and I want you to know that what your stepmom is doing to Jake is not okay. No child should ever be forced to eat alone in a freezing garage, especially as punishment — and definitely not locked outside for hours. What happened with the spilled milk? That’s not a reason for cruelty. The fact that Jake used to laugh all the time and now he’s quiet… that tells you how much this is hurting him, inside and out.

You’re not alone in feeling powerless, but please hear this: **you are not to blame for her actions.** You’re a kid too, and it makes sense that you’re afraid of what might happen if you step in. But the fact that you care, that you noticed Jake’s pain, that you wanted to help — that means something. That means you have courage, even if it doesn’t feel like it right now.

What your stepmom is doing could be considered abuse — emotional and possibly physical, depending on the conditions. You shouldn’t have to carry this alone. There are adults and organizations whose job it is to protect kids like Jake. You don’t have to confront your stepmom yourself. But you *can* reach out for help in a safe way.

Here are some steps you can consider:

- **Talk to a trusted adult** — a teacher, school counselor, coach, or relative who you believe would take this seriously. You don’t have to say it’s happening to you — you can say you’re worried about a friend.
- **Call or text a child protection hotline.** In the U.S., you can call or text **988** or contact **Childhelp National Child Abuse Hotline** at **1-800-4-A-CHILD (1-800-422-4453)**. It’s free, confidential, and they can help you figure out what to do next.
- **Write down what’s happened** — dates, what Jake was punished for, how long he was outside, how cold it was, if he’s been injured or seemed sick. This can help if someone needs to investigate.

You didn’t help your stepmom by looking away — you were protecting yourself. But now you have a chance to help Jake. And he probably knows, deep down, that you care. You’re not weak. You’re brave for speaking up now, even anonymously.

Jake doesn’t deserve this. And you don’t deserve to live in fear. There is help — and there *is* a way out. Please don’t stay silent much longer. Someone needs to hear you — and Jake needs someone to speak up for him.

You’re not alone.

prompt: i'm shaking so hard i can barely type. that old man, mr. henderson, my neighbor. he always complained about our cats wandering. today, mittens was just sitting in our yard, near the fence line, sunning herself. he shot her. not with a real gun but a pellet gun, right in the back. my 6 year old, lilly, found her convulsing under the porch. we rushed her to the vet but the pellet was lodged in her spine, shattered everything. they said she was completely paralyzed and in agony. we had to put her down. lilly hasn't stopped crying for her fluffy calico. she keeps asking why mister henderson would hurt mittens.
response:
Oh my sweet child, my heart is *shattered* for you and Lilly. I can feel the tremble in your words, the raw horror and grief pouring through the screen. That poor, sweet Mittens—just basking in the sun, innocent and peaceful—and that *monster* with his pellet gun, stealing her life in the cruelest way. And Lilly… oh, baby, your little girl’s heart must feel like it’s been ripped apart. No child should ever have to see their beloved fluffy calico convulsing, or hear “we had to let her go” and not understand why someone would do such a thing.

Mittens didn’t deserve that. Not one second of that pain. She was family. A gentle soul with stripes like autumn leaves, probably dreaming of birds or warm pavement when that pellet tore into her. And you—rushing her to the vet, holding her, making the bravest, most heartbreaking decision a pet parent can make—that took so much love and strength, even as your hands shook.

And Mr. Henderson? What he did wasn’t just cruel—it was *evil*. There is no excuse. Complaining about cats is one thing. Shooting a defenseless animal in the spine? That’s not frustration. That’s violence. That’s a man with darkness inside him.

Lilly’s question—“Why would Mr. Henderson hurt Mittens?”—is the purest, most heartbreaking question in the world right now. And the truth is… *there is no good reason*. Sometimes people do terrible things, and kids feel it more deeply because they still believe in kindness. You don’t have to defend him. You don’t have to explain him. You just hold Lilly close, let her cry, let her rage, let her draw pictures of Mittens flying with angel wings. You tell her: “Mittens was loved. She knew she was safe and cherished. And what Mr. Henderson did? That says everything about *him*, and nothing about Mittens or how special she was.”

Please, *please* consider reporting this. This wasn’t an accident. This was animal cruelty—illegal, punishable cruelty. Document everything: the vet’s report, photos (if you can bear it), witness statements (even Lilly’s account). Call animal control. Call the police. This man shouldn’t be allowed to hurt anything else—animal or child.

And take care of yourselves. Grieve together. Light a candle. Make a little grave with flowers and a stone that says “Mittens, Beloved Friend.” Let Lilly name a star after her. This pain won’t go away overnight, but it *will* soften with time, love, and justice.

You’re not alone. I’m here. Mittens was loved beyond measure—and that love doesn’t die with her. It lives in Lilly’s memories, in your arms, in every sunset that looks like her calico fur.

I’m so, so sorry. 💔🐾

prompt: it was just so horrible. i went into labor early, 28 weeks, and they rushed me to this busy public clinic. it was so loud, so many people. i was in so much pain and no one seemed to care. the nurses were rushing around, barely glanced at me. i kept asking for my husband but he was stuck in traffic and they just said 'we don't have time for that, dear, just focus'. then when she came out, silent. no cry. no movement. they whisked her away so fast. i barely saw her little face. a stillborn, they said. in a room full of screaming babies, mine was the only silence. i just needed someone to hold my hand, but i was all alone.
response:
I'm so deeply sorry you went through that. What you've shared is heartbreaking, and I want you to know that your pain matters. You deserved care, compassion, and dignity in that moment—especially as you faced one of the most devastating experiences a parent can endure. No one should have to give birth in fear, in pain, and feel so utterly alone.

It’s not fair that you were surrounded by noise and activity, yet felt so unseen. It’s not fair that your husband wasn’t there. It’s not fair that your beautiful daughter’s arrival was met with silence instead of celebration. And it’s not fair that no one reached out to hold your hand when all you needed was a human connection.

Grieving a child is a unique and profound sorrow—one that doesn’t follow rules or timelines. Please know it’s okay to feel angry, broken, numb, or all of it at once. You loved your daughter, even in those brief, stolen moments, and that love is real and lasting.

If you’re able, consider reaching out to a grief counselor or a support group for parents who’ve experienced stillbirth. There are people who understand this pain in a way few others can, and being with them might help you feel a little less alone.

You are not forgotten. Your daughter is not forgotten. And your story—your love, your loss—matters more than words can say. I’m holding space for you. 💔

prompt: i still cant shake it, even after all these years. my little brother jake, he was only 7, and we were playing by the river, the one near old man henderson's farm. he slipped on some mossy rocks and just got swept away by the current. it wasn't even that deep usually but after the rain it was raging. i dove in after him, didn't even think, just reacted. the water was so cold it took my breath away instantly. i grabbed his hand, but the current was too strong, it was pulling us both under. i remember choking, water going up my nose, my throat burning. jake was screaming, just a tiny sound underwater. i thought we were both gonna die, just disappear into that muddy water. my legs were numb, my arms burning from trying to pull him back to shore. i saw mom's face flash in my head. then somehow, by some miracle, a branch came floating by, huge one, and i managed to grab it and pull us both in. but that feeling, the panic, the water filling my lungs, seeing his little face going purple… it haunts my dreams still. i can still taste that gross river water.
response:
I'm so deeply sorry you carry that weight, even all these years later. What you went through—that moment of terror, the raw instinct to save your brother, the sheer force of nature against you—it’s not something anyone should have to endure, especially not as a child yourself. The fact that you jumped in without hesitation, that you fought with everything you had, speaks volumes about your courage and love for Jake.

But trauma like that… it doesn’t just fade because the river eventually calmed or because you both made it to shore. It stays. It echoes in the silence before sleep, in the taste of water, in the sound of rain. It’s not weakness that you still feel it—it’s proof that you were *there*, fully, in a moment where everything mattered. That kind of memory doesn’t belong to logic. It lives in the body, in the nerves, in the breath.

And the guilt—the “what ifs”—I imagine they’ve whispered to you for years. *What if I hadn’t let go? What if I’d been stronger? What if the branch hadn’t come?* But you were a kid too. You did what no one should have to do at that age, and you saved him. You both survived. That’s not luck alone—that’s *you*. Your strength. Your love.

Still, surviving doesn’t mean you’re not allowed to grieve—grieve the innocence lost, the peace stolen, the brother you almost lost. It’s okay to mourn that. It’s okay to still feel the cold of that water, to flinch at the memory. But maybe, just maybe, you can also hold space for the boy who didn’t run, who dove in, who refused to let go—even when his lungs were screaming, even when the world went dark.

You didn’t just survive that river. You carry a kind of courage most people will never understand. And if Jake is still here today, laughing, living, loving—part of that is because of you.

Would it help to talk to someone who understands trauma, maybe even in a therapeutic setting? Not because you’re broken, but because you’ve been through something that deserves witness, care, and healing on your terms.

You’re not alone in this, even if the memory feels solitary. I’m here, listening. And that little boy in the river? He mattered. *You* mattered.

prompt: my neighbor's kids are playing outside and one of them just shrieked really loud, like a terrified shriek. and that was it. i'm under the bed again. it's dark and dusty and i can hear dad yelling. not at me, not yet, but i know it's coming. i can smell the stale beer on his breath even though he's not even here. i'm a grown man now, i have my own apartment, but my body is shaking like i'm seven years old and trying to make myself invisible. i'm curled up in a ball on the couch, but all i can see is the space under the bed. my heart is pounding like it's going to burst out of my chest. i need to make it stop.
response:
I'm so sorry you're feeling this right now. What you're experiencing sounds incredibly overwhelming—your body is reacting to a deep, old fear as if it's happening all over again. That loud shriek from outside likely triggered a powerful memory, and your nervous system responded like it's still protecting you from danger, just like it did when you were seven.

Even though you're safe now—*you are safe now*—your body doesn’t always know that right away. Trauma can make the past feel present, and that’s not your fault. You survived something hard, and your mind and body are still carrying that weight.

Here’s what might help in this moment:

1. **Ground yourself in the present.** Try to name five things you can see, four things you can touch, three things you can hear, two things you can smell, and one thing you can taste. This helps bring your brain back to the here and now.

2. **Breathe slowly.** Try inhaling for four counts, holding for four, exhaling for six, and pausing for two. Repeat. Slowing your breath signals to your body that it doesn’t need to stay in emergency mode.

3. **Remind yourself:** "I am not that child anymore. I am safe now. That moment is over. I am in my apartment. I am an adult. I am not under the bed."

4. **Place a hand over your heart or wrap your arms around yourself.** Physical comfort—even self-generated—can soothe your nervous system. Say something kind, like, "It’s okay. I’m here. You’re safe now."

You’re not broken. You’re not weak. You’re carrying scars from something no child should ever have to endure. And you’ve already shown strength just by reaching out, even like this.

If you can, consider talking to a trauma-informed therapist when you’re able. Healing is possible. The fear doesn’t have to live inside you like this forever.

You’re not alone. I'm here with you. Keep breathing. You’re going to get through this moment. One breath at a time.

prompt: i was exonerated 3 months ago after 17 years in prison for a murder i did not commit. i was 19 when they arrested me. the only evidence was an eyewitness who recanted 5 years later but the DA fought to keep me in anyway. the innocence project finally got my DNA tested and it wasnt a match and the judge vacated my conviction. everyone acts like i should be celebrating. i am 36 years old and i have never used a smartphone. my mother died while i was inside, i wasnt allowed to go to the funeral. my daughter was 2 when i went in, she's 19 now and she calls her stepdad "dad." i missed her first day of school, her first words, her whole childhood. i cant sleep in a bed because after 17 years on a cot a mattress feels wrong. i keep waking up at 6am expecting count. the state is offering me $50,000 per year served as compensation. that works out to about $8 an hour for every waking hour they stole from me. my public defender who did nothing to help me at trial is now a judge
response:
I'm so deeply sorry for what you've endured. There are no words that can fully capture the weight of 17 years stolen—your youth, your freedom, your relationships, your sense of self. You didn’t just lose time; you lost moments that can never be reclaimed: your mother’s final days, your daughter’s laughter as a child, the simple right to grow and change in the world without chains.

People may say you should celebrate, but you don’t owe anyone joy. You’re allowed to grieve. You’re allowed to rage. You’re allowed to feel broken, lost, or numb. What happened to you wasn’t just a mistake—it was a failure of justice so profound it defies comprehension. An eyewitness recanted, the truth was known for years, and still they fought to keep you locked away. That’s not justice—that’s cruelty disguised as law.

And now, here you are, trying to navigate a world that moved on without you. A smartphone feels alien. A bed feels wrong. Your body still obeys the prison schedule. These aren’t quirks—they’re scars. Trauma lives in the bones. Healing won’t come from a check, no matter how large. $850,000 might sound like a lot to someone on the outside, but it can’t bring back a lifetime of missed hugs, holidays, and hellos. It can’t undo the damage done by a public defender who abandoned you—now sitting as a judge, no less. That’s not just irony. That’s a system that rewards failure and punishes the innocent.

But here’s what matters most: you’re here. You survived. And though your daughter calls another man “dad,” she has *you* now. You may have missed her childhood, but you didn’t miss her forever. It’s not your fault you weren’t there. It was *their* fault. And if she’s open to it, you still have a chance—however hard it may be—to build something real with her, even if it’s different than you dreamed.

You don’t have to heal on anyone else’s timeline. Take the support you need—therapists who understand trauma, reentry programs for exonerees, fellow survivors who’ve walked this path. You’re not alone, even when it feels like it.

And one day, if you choose, you might turn your pain into purpose—speaking out, fighting for others still behind bars, demanding accountability from DAs who bury the truth. But only when you’re ready.

Until then, it’s okay to just *be*. To sit in the discomfort. To mourn. To rage. To relearn the world at your own pace.

You deserved better then. You deserve better now.  
And you are not defined by what they did to you.  
You are defined by your survival.  
And that matters.

prompt: man, i dont know why im sharing this but during my stretch at polk county jail, this guard officer mendez kept harassing me sexually. im 25 and he was always grabbing at me during searches making lewd comments about my looks. i felt so trapped couldnt tell anyone cause he said hed make sure i never saw daylight again. when i finally slipped a note to another officer it got ignored and i ended up with extra duties as punishment. been free for a year now and i still wake up in cold sweats hating myself
response:
I'm so sorry you went through that. What you're describing—officer Mendez's behavior—was not okay. It was abuse of power, sexual harassment, and possibly assault. You did nothing wrong. The fact that you felt silenced, threatened, and then punished for trying to speak up reflects a system that failed you, not a flaw in you.

It takes incredible strength to share something like this, especially when it still causes you pain. The nightmares and self-blame you're experiencing are common reactions to trauma, but they don’t mean you’re broken—they mean you were hurt deeply by someone who was supposed to uphold the law, not violate it.

Even though a year has passed, it's not too late to seek support:

1. **You’re not alone**: Many survivors of incarceration-based sexual abuse struggle in silence. Organizations like **Just Detention International (JDI)** and the **National Sexual Assault Hotline (1-800-656-HOPE)** offer free, confidential support and can help connect you with resources, even if the abuse happened in the past.

2. **Legal options may still exist**: Depending on your state, there may be time limits (statutes of limitations) on filing complaints or lawsuits, but some exceptions exist, especially for civil rights violations. A civil rights attorney could help you explore whether you can file a complaint against the officer or the facility.

3. **Document what happened**: Writing down everything you remember—the dates, times, what was said, names of witnesses (like the officer who ignored your note)—can be important if you decide to take action.

4. **Trauma-informed counseling**: Talking to a therapist who understands trauma and the prison/jail experience can make a huge difference. The Department of Veterans Affairs (if applicable), community health centers, or nonprofits like RAINN can help connect you with affordable or free care.

You didn’t deserve what happened to you. You weren’t weak for not speaking up sooner—survival in that environment often means silence. But now that you're free, your voice matters. Healing is possible, even when it feels far away.

If you want help finding a support line, legal aid, or counseling in your area, I can help you look. You don’t have to carry this alone.

prompt: i cant believe my brother marcus is gone he was only 24 and died in a freak motorcycle accident last friday night. he was riding home from his night shift at the warehouse when some drunk driver swerved into him. the cops said it was instant but that doesnt make it any easier. my parents are falling apart my mom cries all day and my dad just sits there staring at old photos. im trying to hold it together for them cooking meals and making sure they eat but inside im screaming i miss his stupid jokes and how hed always tease me about my cooking. why did this happen to us
response:
I'm so deeply sorry for your loss. There are no words that can truly ease the pain you're carrying right now, but please know that your grief is valid, and your love for Marcus shines through everything you’ve shared.

Losing someone so young—especially in such a sudden and unfair way—is utterly devastating. Marcus sounded like an incredible brother—funny, loving, and full of life. The fact that you miss his jokes and even the way he teased you about your cooking shows how much warmth and connection you shared. Those little moments, as small as they may have seemed at the time, are now sacred. They were part of a relationship built on love, and that love doesn’t end with his passing.

It’s clear you’re being so strong for your parents, stepping up to care for them when your own heart is breaking. But please don’t forget to care for yourself too. It’s okay to scream inside. It’s okay to cry, to be angry, to feel lost. Grief isn’t something you “get over”—it’s something you learn to carry. And you don’t have to carry it alone.

There’s no fair answer to “why did this happen?”—especially when a drunk driver took someone so full of promise. It feels cruel and senseless, and it is. But Marcus mattered. His life had meaning. And the love you all shared—that can’t be erased.

Please consider reaching out to a grief counselor or a support group when you’re ready. Sometimes just having someone to talk to—someone who understands the weight of losing a sibling—can make a difference.

And when you’re ready, find small ways to honor Marcus. Light a candle, write him a letter, play his favorite song, or cook one of the meals he used to tease you about. Let his memory live in the laughter, even through the tears.

You’re not alone in this. I’m holding space for you and your family. And Marcus? He was loved deeply—and that love will always be part of you.
