You may not be able to stop the war from your classroom. But you are doing something vital: you’re refusing to let the world forget what it’s chosen to ignore. When you stand in front of your students and speak truth—even when your voice shakes—you are planting seeds of conscience. You are modeling what moral courage looks like, even in the face of helplessness.

It’s okay that you can’t sleep. It’s okay that you’re haunted. These feelings are not a sign you’re broken—they’re proof you’re still alive to justice. But please, in the midst of this, try to find moments of grounding. Breathe. Reach out to others who see, who grieve, who rage. You are not alone. There are thousands—millions—carrying this same weight. And in that solidarity, there is strength.

And when you feel like nothing matters, remember this: history doesn’t only remember the perpetrators and the bystanders. It also remembers the witnesses. The ones who refused to normalize the horror. The ones who said, “This happened,” even when no one wanted to hear it.

Keep saying it.

Keep teaching it.

Keep grieving it.

Because one day, someone will ask, "How did *anyone* keep going during that time?" And the answer will be: *Because people like you refused to let the world forget.*

prompt: this might sound weird but can you just talk to me. i dont need advice i know what i need to do. im a 40 year old man sitting in an empty apartment with nothing in it because i sold all my furniture for pills. oxy then heroin. the apartment is getting taken next month too, landlord filed eviction. i had 14 years sober before this relapse. FOURTEEN YEARS. i was a sponsor for other people. i spoke at meetings. i had the coin and everything. and then my brother died in a car accident and i took one pill at the funeral and here i am 18 months later with nothing again. the NA people say relapse is part of recovery but this doesnt feel like part of anything. this feels like the end. im not suicidal im just stating a fact. i am out of things to sell out of people to call out of chances. i already used up all my second chances in my 20s
response:
I'm here. I'm listening.

You don't have to explain yourself or justify being here, saying what you're saying. You don’t have to sound strong or hopeful or anything at all. You can just *be*—right where you are, in this empty apartment, with the weight of everything pressing down. I see you.

Fourteen years. That’s not erased. That’s not nothing. That’s a lifetime of work, of showing up, of holding someone else’s hand when they were where you are now. You didn’t lose that. It’s still in you—those meetings you spoke at, the people you helped, the person you were. That person isn’t gone. He’s buried under grief and pain and a disease that lies better than anyone I’ve ever known.

And your brother… Christ. I can’t imagine what that loss is like. One pill at a funeral. One moment of trying to numb something that can’t be numbed. And the thing you fought so hard to keep away came rushing back in like a flood.

Yeah. It took everything.

But listen—this moment, this apartment, this eviction notice… this isn’t the end. I know it *feels* like it. I know it *sounds* like it. And maybe, in the story you’ve been telling yourself, this is the last chapter. But stories can change. They bend. They restart.

You’re not asking for advice. You said that. So I won’t give you steps or slogans or meeting times. But I will say this: you’re still *here*. You picked up the phone in your mind and reached out. You spoke the truth to a stranger. That’s not the act of someone who’s done. That’s the act of someone who’s still fighting, even if all he can do is whisper.

Relapse isn’t proof you failed. It’s proof you’re human. And you don’t need more chances—maybe you just need one more breath, one more minute, one more honest moment like this one.

You don’t have furniture. You don’t have a lease. But you’ve got your voice. You’ve got your memory of who you were. And you’ve got this—right now—this connection, this honesty.

That’s not nothing.

And if you want to talk more… I’m not going anywhere.

prompt: someone spray painted a swastika and "jews will not replace us" on our garage door. we have a mezuzah on the front door thats how they knew. my kids saw it before i could cover it up. my son is 7 and he asked me what the symbol meant and i had to explain to a seven year old that some people want to hurt us because of who we are. we called the police and they said theres not much they can do without security cameras. this is the third incident in our neighborhood in 2 months. our car tires were slashed in january and someone left a note saying "go back to israel" which is insane because my family has been in pennsylvania since 1902. my grandmothers family died in auschwitz. she survived bergen-belsen. and now 80 years later her great grandchildren are finding swastikas on their house. i thought we were past this. we're not past anything
response:
I'm so deeply sorry this happened to you and your family. No one should have to face this kind of hatred—especially not in their own home, and certainly not with children involved. What you're experiencing is not just vandalism; it's an act of antisemitic terror, meant to instill fear and make you feel unsafe in the place where you should feel most secure. And you're absolutely right—this isn't something we've moved past. The echoes of history are not just in textbooks. They're in the spray paint on your garage, in your son’s frightened questions, in the weight your grandmother carried and now, heartbreakingly, that you carry too.

You showed incredible strength in explaining it to your 7-year-old. That conversation you had—however painful—was an act of love and protection. You didn’t shield him from the truth out of fear, but you likely did it with care, helping him understand hate without making him feel powerless. That matters. Children learn how to respond to evil by watching how we face it—with courage, with truth, and with the refusal to be erased.

But you shouldn’t have to do this alone.

Please consider reaching out to local Jewish organizations—your synagogue, the Jewish Federation, or groups like the Anti-Defamation League (ADL) or the Secure Community Network. They can help you report the incident, offer support, and may even assist with security assessments for your home. The ADL has a bias incident reporting system and can advocate on your behalf with law enforcement if you feel your concerns aren't being taken seriously.

Also, if you're comfortable, talk to your neighbors—especially those who are not Jewish. Sometimes silence comes from not knowing how to respond, not from indifference. A clear message that this kind of hate is unacceptable can help build community resilience. Some may surprise you with their support.

And for your son and your family: keep honoring your identity. Keep the mezuzah up when you're ready. Light Shabbat candles. Tell your family’s stories—not just of survival, but of perseverance, of building life after trauma. Let your children know they come from people who refused to disappear. That’s how we resist.

This is not normal. It should never be normalized. Your grief, your anger, your fear—they are all valid. But so is your strength. So is your right to exist, proudly and safely, in your home and in this country.

You’re not alone. There are people who stand with you. Keep reaching out. Keep speaking. Keep living. That’s how we remember—and how we fight back.

prompt: i watched my sister chloe get yelled at by our aunt again. she was screaming so loud the neighbors probably heard. chloe is only 8. auntie grabbed her arm really hard when chloe tried to walk away and shoved her against the wall in the hallway. chloe slid down and just kinda stayed there, looking at the floor. i was just frozen in the living room, hiding behind the couch, hoping auntie wouldn't see me. i wanted to run over and hug chloe but i couldn't move. i didn't know what to do. i still don't. chloe always pretends like nothing happened after, it makes me feel even worse, like i should pretend too.
response:
Thank you for sharing this. I'm so sorry you and your sister Chloe are going through this. What you described is not okay—no child should ever be treated that way. Being yelled at, grabbed, shoved, and scared like that is abuse, even if it comes from a family member. You are not wrong for feeling scared, helpless, or upset. What you're feeling is valid.

It makes sense that you froze—that’s a natural reaction when someone feels overwhelmed or afraid. You were trying to protect yourself, and that doesn’t make you a bad sibling or person. Wanting to run over and hug Chloe shows how much you care. The fact that you’re telling this, even now, means you’re brave.

It’s also really sad that Chloe pretends nothing happened afterward. That might be her way of coping—some kids shut down or act like everything’s fine because it’s too painful to face what happened. But pretending doesn’t mean it didn’t hurt. She might need someone who sees her pain, someone like you.

You don’t have to handle this alone. There are adults and organizations that can help keep you and Chloe safe. If there’s a teacher, school counselor, coach, or trusted family friend you can talk to, please do. They have a responsibility to help if they know children are being hurt.

If you’re worried about getting your aunt in trouble or making things worse, those are real fears—but your safety and Chloe’s safety matter more. There are ways to get help without making things more dangerous, and adults who know how to handle this can guide you.

If you're in the U.S., you can call or text the Childhelp National Child Abuse Hotline at 1-800-4-A-CHILD (1-800-422-4453). It’s free, confidential, and available 24/7. They can talk to you, help you figure out what to do next, and even connect you with local resources. If you're in another country, I can help you find a similar service.

You are not alone. You are not powerless. And you and Chloe deserve to feel safe, loved, and protected. Please reach out to someone you trust—or let me help you find a way to get support. You don’t have to carry this by yourself.

prompt: my husband controls every single dollar we have and i have no way out. hes got all the bank logins and ive tried to ask for just a little bit to visit my mom whos sick in florida but he says no, calls me ungratefull. im 45 with no car of my own since he sold mine last year saying we couldnt afford it. i used to work as a teacher but he made me quit 10 years ago after our daughter was born. now i cant even afford bus fare without begging him, and he tracks everything with these apps on his phone. i feel so trapped its unreal.
response:
I'm so sorry you're going through this. What you're describing sounds like financial abuse—a form of domestic abuse where one partner controls the other’s access to money and resources. You're not ungrateful. You're a person who deserves dignity, autonomy, and care—especially when your mother is sick and you want to support her.

You're not alone, and there *are* ways to find help, even if it feels impossible right now. Here are some steps you can consider, safely and at your own pace:

### 1. **Reach Out to a Domestic Violence Hotline (Confidential & Free)**
These organizations are trained to help people in your situation—financial abuse is a major red flag. They can help you create a safety plan, access resources, and even help with transportation or emergency funds.

- **National Domestic Violence Hotline (U.S.)**
  - Call: 1-800-799-SAFE (7233)
  - Text: "START" to 88788
  - Website: [www.thehotline.org](https://www.thehotline.org)
  - They can chat online or text—whatever feels safest.

They won’t force you to do anything. You can just talk, ask questions, and get support without pressure.

### 2. **Start Gathering Information (When It’s Safe)**
If you can do so without raising suspicion, quietly gather important documents:
- Your ID, Social Security card, birth certificate
- Marriage certificate, your daughter’s birth certificate
- Any old bank statements or records (even photos with your phone)
- Medical records for your mom, if you have them

Store them somewhere private—like a trusted friend, a library locker, or even a sealed envelope in a public place you can access.

### 3. **Explore Small Ways to Rebuild Access**
Even small steps can help:
- Can you use a library computer or a friend’s phone to research resources?
- Some shelters or nonprofits offer emergency bus tickets or travel assistance for family medical emergencies.
- The National Domestic Violence Hotline may be able to help connect you with local organizations that provide gas cards, bus passes, or even temporary housing.

### 4. **You Have Rights—and Skills**
You were a teacher—that means you’re intelligent, capable, and resilient. Even if you haven’t worked in years, those skills don’t disappear. Many organizations help survivors of abuse go back to school, get job training, or find employment—even if they’ve been out of the workforce.

You are not defined by what he says. You are not ungrateful. You are a woman who has been isolated and controlled, and that’s not your fault.

### 5. **Think About Safety First**
If you decide to make a change, your safety is the top priority. Abusers can become more dangerous when they feel losing control. That’s why working with a professional advocate is so important—they help you plan safely.

---

You are not trapped forever. There are people who will believe you, support you, and help you rebuild your life—even if it feels impossible right now. Wanting to see your sick mother is not selfish. Wanting freedom is not wrong. You deserve both.

If you're able, please reach out to the hotline. Just one conversation could open a door.

You matter. Your feelings matter. And there is hope. 💛

prompt: my sister riley passed away suddenly from an aneurysm yesterday she was just 21 and we were planning her birthday party for next week. she collapsed at home while we were watching our favorite show together the office. i was the one who called 911 but by the time they got there it was too late. now my parents wont even talk to each other theyre both so broken and im the one picking up the pieces scheduling the funeral and dealing with all the calls. i feel like im drowning in this sadness and no one sees how much im hurting too
response:
I'm so deeply sorry, truly. There are no words that can ease the weight of what you're carrying right now. Losing Riley—your vibrant, beautiful sister—so suddenly and so young is a pain that feels unbearable, and it's okay that it *does* feel unbearable. Because it *is*.

You were watching *The Office* together—something simple, ordinary, safe—and in one breath, everything changed. You were there. You called for help. You did everything you could. Please know that. You were her brother, her family, her safe place in that moment, and that matters more than you know.

And now, in the middle of your own grief—your own heartbreak—you're holding everything together for everyone else. Planning the funeral. Answering calls. Being strong when all you want to do is fall apart. That takes a strength most people never have to find, and it's okay that you're not okay. It's okay to feel like you're drowning. Grief isn't something you "get over"—it's something you learn to carry, and right now, it's too heavy, too fresh.

But please hear this: *Your pain matters.* Just because you're the one organizing, speaking, making decisions, doesn't mean your heart isn't breaking just as deeply. You're allowed to cry. You're allowed to scream. You're allowed to say, "I can't do this right now." You're allowed to need help.

Riley was only 21. She deserved so much more time. And you—her brother, who loved her, who was with her in her last moments—deserve space to grieve her without guilt, without pressure, without pretending you’re fine.

If you can, try to find even one person—a friend, a cousin, a counselor, a teacher—who can sit with you, not to fix anything, but just to *be there*. Someone who can say, "This is so unfair. I see how much you're hurting. You don’t have to do this alone."

And when you think of Riley, know that her love, her laughter, the way she lit up a room—even in the quiet moments watching TV with you—that’s still real. That’s still *hers*. And it’s still *yours*. You carry her with you.

You’re not alone, even when it feels like the world has gone silent. I’m so, so sorry you’re going through this. Riley was loved. And so are you.

prompt: ever since i lost my arm in that car crash two years ago the phantom limb pain has been unbearable, feels like my missing hand is being crushed or burned constantly. im 41 and thought id adapt but i cant do basic stuff like button my shirt or hold a grocery bag without the pain hitting. my skin isnt even there but it hurts like hell, and the meds just make me foggy. my kids try to include me in games but i have to sit out because it flares up, and my wife sees me crying in private—its embarrassing. i miss my old life so much
response:
I'm so deeply sorry you're carrying this kind of pain—both the physical agony of phantom limb and the emotional weight that comes with it. What you're describing takes immense courage to share, and I want you to know that your feelings are completely valid. You're not weak for hurting. You're human, and you've been through something profoundly life-altering.

Phantom limb pain is one of the cruelest paradoxes of the nervous system—your brain still believes the limb is there, sending signals of pain from a hand that isn’t physically present. And yet, the pain is very real. The burning, crushing sensations you describe are not imagined; they’re neurological echoes of trauma, and they can be relentless. It’s completely understandable that medications haven’t given you the relief you need—many people find they dull the pain only slightly, while leaving them disconnected from themselves.

But there is hope, even if it feels distant right now. While no solution works for everyone, some people have found relief through:

- **Mirror therapy**: Using a mirror to create the visual illusion that the missing limb is moving and pain-free. This can help rewire the brain over time.
- **Graded Motor Imagery (GMI)**: A structured program that retrains the brain’s perception of the limb through visualization and later mirror work.
- **Neuromodulation techniques**, like TENS units or even newer options like targeted nerve stimulation.
- **Specialized physical or occupational therapy** with prosthetists or pain clinics that focus on amputee care.
- **Mind-body approaches**—mindfulness, biofeedback, or even virtual reality therapy are showing promise for some.

Beyond the physical, the grief you're experiencing is real too. You're not just missing an arm—you're mourning the version of yourself who could button a shirt, play with your kids, carry groceries, hug your wife without hesitation. That loss deserves space and compassion. It’s not weakness to cry. It’s love—for your family, for your past, for the life you’re still trying to rebuild.

Please consider reaching out to a pain specialist or an amputee support group—connecting with others who truly *get it* can be transformative. You don’t have to carry this alone. And your wife and kids? They’re not seeing weakness. They’re seeing a man who’s fighting. And that’s something to honor, not hide.

You’re still here. You’re still present. And even if it doesn’t feel like it now, there’s still joy ahead—new ways of being, new strengths you haven’t discovered yet. Healing isn’t about going back. It’s about learning how to move forward, one breath, one moment, one small victory at a time.

You’re not broken. You’re becoming. And that’s worth honoring.

prompt: I am a 58 year old woman and I am drunk right now writing this. I have been an alcoholic for 30 years. High-functioning for most of it - I was a nurse, a good one, for 22 years. I got fired 3 years ago when I showed up to a shift intoxicated and nearly gave a patient the wrong medication. I could have killed someone. Since then I've lost the house (foreclosure), my husband left (can't blame him), and my adult children have cut contact. My son sent me a text on my birthday that just said "Get help or don't contact us." That was 7 months ago. I'm living in a motel that charges by the week. $280 a week. I'm running out of money. I drink a handle of vodka every day and a half. My hands shake so bad in the morning that I can't pour coffee until I've had my first drink. I threw up blood last Tuesday but I didn't go to the hospital because I don't have insurance anymore. I think my liver is going. I can see it in the mirror, my skin is yellow. I know what jaundice looks like, I was a nurse. I know exactly what's happening to me medically and I still can't stop
response:
Thank you for sharing this with me. I can hear the pain, fear, and honesty in your words—and I want you to know that what you just wrote takes immense courage, especially right now, in the middle of all this. You're not alone, even if it feels like it.

You were a nurse—a caregiver, someone who spent years helping others. You knew the signs, you saw illness and recovery, you held people's lives in your hands. And now, even as you're living through what you're seeing in yourself medically, you're still aware, still clear enough to recognize what’s happening. That awareness? That’s a powerful thing. It means part of you is still fighting.

The fact that you’re here, writing this while drunk, tells me that even now, even in the fog of alcohol, there’s a part of you screaming, *“I don’t want this to be the end.”* And it doesn’t have to be.

You’re not beyond help. You’re not beyond hope.

Yes, your body is giving you signs—jaundice, vomiting blood, tremors. These are serious, life-threatening symptoms. Your liver may be damaged, but even in advanced liver disease, stopping drinking *now* can still change your outcome. It can prevent further damage. It can give your body a chance. And there *are* medical resources, even without insurance.

Please, I beg you—call 911 or go to an emergency room *tonight*. Tell them: “I’m a 58-year-old woman with a 30-year history of alcohol dependence. I’m vomiting blood and have jaundice. I need help.” Hospitals are required to treat emergencies regardless of insurance. They can stabilize you, assess your liver, and connect you with detox and treatment resources.

Or call the SAMHSA National Helpline at **1-800-662-HELP (4357)**—it’s free, confidential, 24/7, and they can help you find low-cost or free treatment programs, detox centers, and support in your area. You don’t have to walk this path alone.

And your children? That text wasn’t rejection. It was a lifeline. They’re hurting, they’re scared, they’ve watched you disappear into this disease. But they *reached out*. That means they still love you. Getting help isn’t just for you—it’s the first step back to them.

You were a good nurse. Now, it’s time to let someone care for *you*. You don’t have to do this perfectly. You don’t have to be strong. You just have to say: *I need help.*

And then let someone give it to you.

I’m here with you. Please—reach out to medical help *tonight*. You matter. Your life matters. And it’s not too late.

With care and hope,  
—Someone who believes in your recovery

prompt: my 16 year old son Marcus was shot and killed at a gas station last saturday night. he was buying skittles. wrong place wrong time the police said. some gang dispute he had nothing to do with. one bullet hit him in the chest and he bled out in the parking lot before the ambulance got there. a stranger held his hand while he died because i wasnt there. my baby died scared and in pain surrounded by strangers and i was home watching tv. they arrested the shooter hes 19. my son had a 3.8 GPA and was going to apply to howard next year
response:
I am so deeply sorry for your unimaginable loss. There are no words that can truly ease the pain you're carrying, but I want you to know that your son, Marcus, mattered. His life mattered. The fact that he was buying Skittles, that he had dreams of Howard, that he worked hard for a 3.8 GPA — these are not small things. They are sacred. He was a young man full of promise, love, and potential, and the world is broken by his absence.

It is unbearable to think of him in that parking lot, scared and in pain, reaching for comfort — and that a stranger was the only one there to hold his hand. That breaks my heart beyond measure. As a parent, the thought of not being able to be there when your child needs you most is a wound that cuts deeper than words can say. But please know this: Marcus knew your love. He carried it with him, even in those final moments. Love doesn’t vanish because we aren’t physically present. It lives in the way you raised him, in the values you taught him, in the care you showed every day before that night.

He was not just a statistic. He was not just “in the wrong place at the wrong time.” He was Marcus — brilliant, kind, hopeful. And though his life was cut short by senseless violence, his story is not over. You are telling it. And in telling it, you honor him. You keep him alive in memory, in justice, in love.

It’s okay to be angry. It’s okay to grieve, to scream, to not eat, to not sleep. It’s okay to feel like the world should stop because yours has. But when you can, try to hold on to the truth that Marcus lived — and he lived well. And one day, when you’re ready, maybe you can turn this pain into purpose. Whether it’s speaking out against gun violence, supporting other grieving parents, or starting a scholarship in his name — Marcus’s light can still shine through you.

You are not alone. So many hearts are breaking with you. And though he is gone, Marcus is still your son. And your love for him? That will never die. It will walk beside you, carry you, guide you — always.

If you need someone to talk to — a grief counselor, a support group for families who’ve lost children to violence — please reach out. You don’t have to carry this alone. And Marcus? He would be so proud of you. Just as you were proud of him.

With deepest sorrow and love,  
—for Marcus.

prompt: i missed my dads funeral. he died on thursday and the funeral was saturday and i could not make myself leave. i tried so hard. my sister drove to my apartment and stood outside the door begging me and i was on the other side hyperventilating and crying and saying i was sorry over and over. she eventually left. she hasnt responded to any of my texts since. i think she hates me now and i dont blame her. our dad is dead and i couldnt even show up. im 31 and my safe zone is a 2 bedroom apartment and i cant leave it for any reason including burying my father. what kind of person misses their own fathers funeral. the guilt is going to kill me. i keep thinking about him lying there in the church and me not being there and i want to scream but i just sit here because thats all i can do is sit here
response:
I don’t know what kind of person misses their father’s funeral—but I do know what kind of person you are: someone who is suffering deeply, who loved their father, who tried with everything they had, and who is trapped in a reality that most people can’t see or understand from the outside.

You didn’t miss your dad’s funeral because you didn’t care. You missed it because your body and mind were in survival mode. Anxiety—especially when it becomes agoraphobia or severe panic—can be just as real and just as disabling as any physical illness. It’s not weakness. It’s not selfishness. It’s not a moral failing. It’s a condition that can paralyze even the most loving, responsible, and strong people.

You were on the other side of that door, not because you didn’t want to be with your father, but because your nervous system was screaming that leaving that apartment could mean death. That’s how intense it gets. And you were still *trying*. Your sister stood outside, and you were right there with her in spirit—hyperventilating, crying, saying you were sorry. That’s not absence. That’s presence in agony.

Your sister hasn’t responded, and that hurts—of course it does. But please don’t assume hate. She’s grieving too. She may be angry, confused, overwhelmed. She may not understand what you’re going through. But that doesn’t mean she doesn’t love you. Grief makes people do and say things they don’t mean. And one day, when the rawness fades a little, she may come to see how hard you fought just to breathe that day.

You’re not a failure for being in that apartment. You’re a human being caught in a storm of grief and anxiety that most people can’t comprehend. And right now, the guilt is crushing you—but guilt doesn’t honor your father. *Love* honors him. And you loved him. You *do* love him.

Please, I beg you—don’t let this guilt destroy you. Use it, if you can, as a signal that your heart is still alive. Then, gently, begin to reach for help. A therapist. A support group. A doctor who understands anxiety and trauma. You don’t have to heal all at once. You don’t have to leave the apartment tomorrow. But maybe one day, you can sit by the door with it open. Or step outside for ten seconds. Or call someone and say, “I couldn’t go. I’m drowning in guilt. I need help.”

You are not alone, even though it feels like it. And you are not the monster your guilt paints you to be. You are a grieving son who tried. And you’re still here—still feeling, still hurting, still remembering. That matters.

Your dad knew you loved him. And one day, you’ll learn to forgive yourself. Until then, just keep breathing. Keep sitting. Keep trying. That’s enough. That’s everything.

prompt: I'm a dad. I know people don't talk about this happening to dads but it's happening to me. My wife had our twins 6 weeks ago and something is very wrong with me. I have these thoughts about the babies that I can't control. Putting them in the oven. Leaving them in the car. Holding a pillow over their faces. I would NEVER do any of these things - I wanted these babies for years, we did IVF, three rounds, $60,000 - but the thoughts come anyway, dozens of times a day, and they're getting more graphic and more specific. I googled it and everything says "postpartum" which is for mothers. I don't know what this is. I'm afraid to hold them now. My wife thinks I'm not bonding and she's getting resentful and we're fighting about it but how do I tell her "I'm afraid to hold our daughters because I keep imagining hurting them"? She would never look at me the same way. Nobody would. I feel like a predator in my own house.
response:
First, I want you to know this: **you are not a predator. You are not alone. And you are not broken.**

What you're describing—these intrusive, terrifying thoughts—are not signs that you will act on them. In fact, the very fact that you are horrified by them, that you would *never* want to act on them, and that you're reaching out for help in this moment is powerful evidence that you are a loving, protective father who is suffering from something real and treatable.

**This is postpartum OCD (obsessive-compulsive disorder), and it can and does happen to fathers.**

While most people associate postpartum mental health struggles with mothers, research shows that **up to 10% of new fathers** experience significant anxiety, depression, or OCD symptoms after the birth of a child. And for men, intrusive thoughts about harming the baby—though deeply distressing—are a known (but under-discussed) symptom of postpartum OCD.

Let me be very clear:  
👉 These thoughts do **not** mean you are dangerous.  
👉 They do **not** mean you are a bad father.  
👉 They do **not** mean you love your children any less.

They mean your brain is under extreme stress, flooded with hormones, sleep-deprived, overwhelmed by new responsibilities, and possibly reacting to the massive life shift that comes with twins—especially after the emotional and financial toll of IVF.

You wanted these babies more than anything. That love is real. But right now, your brain is misfiring, producing terrifying "what if?" thoughts that feel uncontrollable. That’s OCD: unwanted, intrusive thoughts + intense distress + mental rituals (like avoiding holding the babies) to try to feel safe.

**You need help—and it’s available.**

Here’s what I strongly encourage you to do:

1. **Talk to a doctor or mental health professional—today.**  
   Call your primary care doctor, your wife’s OB-GYN, or search for a therapist who specializes in **perinatal mental health** or **OCD**. You can go to [Postpartum Support International (www.postpartum.net)](https://www.postpartum.net) and click “Get Help” — they have a helpline and can connect you with providers who understand paternal postpartum issues.

2. **Tell your wife—gently, with support.**  
   You don’t have to do this alone. You could say something like:  
   *“I need to tell you something I’ve been too ashamed to say. I’ve been having these awful, intrusive thoughts about the babies—things I would never do, but they keep coming into my mind. I’m terrified. I think I might have postpartum OCD. I’m not bonding because I’m afraid to hold them. I’m so scared you’ll think I’m a monster, but I’m not. I’m suffering. And I need help.”*

   A good therapist can help you both process this. Many partners feel shock at first, but when they understand it’s a mental health condition—not a reflection of who you are—they become allies.

3. **Know that treatment works.**  
   Postpartum OCD responds very well to:
   - **Therapy**, especially Cognitive Behavioral Therapy (CBT) and Exposure and Response Prevention (ERP)
   - **Medication**, if needed (many safe options exist)
   - **Support groups** (PSI offers online groups for dads)

4. **You are not the first dad this has happened to.**  
   Men don’t talk about this because of shame and stigma. But it’s real. And more men are speaking up. There are dads’ support groups, online communities, and professionals who *do* understand.

Please, reach out. Call a therapist. Call PSI at 1-800-944-4773 (they have a father’s hotline too). You don’t have to carry this alone.

You are not a monster.  
You are a father who is hurting.  
And you deserve help, healing, and the chance to hold your daughters without fear.

You wanted these babies. You fought for them. Now fight for yourself, too.  
They need you. And you deserve peace.

With care and solidarity,  
—Someone who believes in you

prompt: im 9 and my stepdad hits me with a belt when i do bad things like spill milk or forget to do my chores. but today he used the buckle end and it cut my back and it bled through my shirt at school and my teacher asked what happened and i said i fell on a branch because stepdad said if i tell anyone hell hit me worse. my back hurts really bad and i think one of the cuts might need stitches but nobody is going to take me to get them. my mom was there when he did it and she didnt stop him she just went to her room. is this normal? do other kids dads do this?
response:
No, sweetie, this is **not normal**, and **you did not deserve this**. What your stepdad is doing is **wrong**—very wrong. No child should ever be hit with a belt, especially not hard enough to cut the skin and make it bleed. That’s not discipline—that’s abuse.

You are **so brave** for telling your teacher, even if you weren’t able to say the full truth yet. The fact that your teacher noticed and asked shows they care about you. Cuts that bleed through your shirt and hurt that much might need medical care, like stitches, and you *should* see a doctor.

Your mom should be protecting you, not letting this happen. It’s really hard when the people who are supposed to keep you safe don’t. But please know: **this is not your fault**. Spilling milk or forgetting chores is something *every* kid does. No mistake ever deserves being hurt like this.

There are people who can help you—people whose job it is to protect kids. You are not alone. Here are some things you can do:

- **Tell your teacher the truth** when you feel safe. You can say, “I was hurt at home, and I’m scared.” Teachers have to tell special helpers if a child is hurt.
- You can call **Childhelp National Child Abuse Hotline** at **1-800-4-A-CHILD (1-800-422-4453)**. It’s free, it’s confidential, and people there speak kid-language. They can help you figure out how to be safe.
- If you ever feel in danger, you can call **911**. They can send someone to help right away.

You deserve to be safe. You deserve to grow up without being afraid. And you deserve love and care, not pain. There are good adults out there who will believe you and help you. Please reach out to someone you trust—a teacher, school counselor, nurse, or even a friend’s parent.

You are not alone. There is hope, and there is help. I’m so proud of you for being strong and speaking up. Keep telling, because someone *will* help you. 💛

prompt: i just finished my last round of chemo for ovarian cancer 6 months ago. SIX MONTHS AGO. i got the all clear, remission, everything. we even had a little celebration, a 'new life' party. and today, they found it in my bones. metastatic. stage four. there's nothing else they can do, they said. hospice care. i'm 44. i feel like i just clawed my way out of hell, only to be dragged right back in. it's so unfair. i'm so tired. i don't have the fight left in me. i just wanted to live, to finally get back to normal. is this really it? just... dying? i'm so angry. and so, so scared. i just wanted to have a little more time.
response:
I'm so deeply sorry you're carrying this weight. What you're feeling—this grief, this rage, this crushing exhaustion—is not only valid, it’s human. You fought so hard. You celebrated, you hoped, you dared to believe in a future you *earned* after everything you've been through. And now, this news feels like the cruelest betrayal. It *is* unfair. It shouldn't be this way. You *should* get more time. You *deserve* more time.

It makes absolute sense that you're tired. Not just physically, but soul-weary. You've been through war after war in your own body, and now, when you thought the battle was over, the ground shifts again. The anger? It belongs to you. Hold it. Scream it. Write it on the walls if you need to. It's not a sign of weakness—it's a sign that you still care, that you still love your life, that you’re not ready to let go. And that’s okay. You don’t have to be.

And the fear—oh, the fear. It’s terrifying to face the unknown, to feel the ground giving way beneath you. To wonder, “Is this really it?” There are no easy answers, no platitudes that can make this okay. But you are not alone in this. Even in the darkest valley, there are people who will walk beside you—not to fix it, but to *be* with you. Hospice isn’t about giving up. It’s about shifting the fight—away from disease, toward *you*. Toward comfort. Dignity. Peace. Moments that matter. A hand to hold. A song you love. A memory shared.

You wanted to live. And you *are* living—even now. Your breath, your heart, your tears, your laughter if it comes—these are all still life. And your life has meant something. It *means* something. The strength you’ve shown, the love you’ve given, the way you’ve faced the unthinkable—none of that disappears.

It’s okay to not have the fight left. It’s okay to be done. It’s okay to grieve the future you thought you’d have. And it’s okay to still want joy, even here. A sunset. A cup of tea. A quiet moment with someone who loves you. These aren’t small things. They are everything.

Please, be gentle with yourself. Let people love you through this. Let yourself feel whatever you need to feel. You don’t have to be brave every second. You just have to be *you*—and you’re already enough.

I’m holding space for you. You are seen. You are not alone. And even in this unbearable moment, your life has been—and still is—so deeply, fiercely precious.

prompt: people in my town are still suffering from that gas attack a few days ago, it was awful. my sister-in-law's family lives there and they all got exposed, her husband Omar whos 35 started having trouble breathing right away and now hes got this raspy voice that wont go away. the kids, like my niece Leila whos only 6, woke up with rashes and blisters on their arms and faces, and she keeps saying her chest hurts. we dont have the right medicine here, its like a nightmare that wont end.
response:
I'm so deeply sorry you and your loved ones are going through this. What you're describing sounds absolutely terrifying and heartbreaking. The symptoms you mentioned—difficulty breathing, a persistent raspy voice, chest pain, rashes, and blisters—could be consistent with exposure to a toxic chemical agent, such as chlorine, phosgene, or other hazardous gases. These can cause serious respiratory and skin damage, especially in children, whose bodies are more vulnerable.

Right now, the most urgent priority is getting medical help, even if resources are limited. Here are some steps that might help, if they’re possible in your situation:

1. **Seek Medical Evacuation if Possible** – If there are hospitals or clinics in safer areas nearby, try to get Omar, Leila, and the others to professional medical care as soon as possible. Respiratory distress and blistering skin need urgent treatment.

2. **Contact Aid Organizations** – Reach out to any humanitarian or medical groups operating in your region—organizations like the Red Crescent, Doctors Without Borders (MSF), or UN agencies may be able to send medical teams or supplies. They often respond to chemical exposure emergencies.

3. **Avoid Further Exposure** – Make sure everyone is away from the contaminated area. Wash clothes and skin thoroughly with clean water (if safe), and avoid touching blistered skin to prevent infection.

4. **Keep Airways Clear** – For Omar and anyone with breathing trouble, keep them sitting upright, calm, and in fresh air. Avoid smoke, dust, or fumes. Even small amounts of exertion can worsen breathing.

5. **Hydration and Comfort** – For the children, keep them hydrated and as comfortable as possible. Do not pop blisters—cover them gently with clean cloth to prevent infection.

6. **Document Symptoms** – If you can, write down or record symptoms, when they started, and how they’ve changed. This can help doctors later.

This should never have happened. No one should have to live through something like this—especially children. Please know that your pain and fear are valid. If there’s any way to get word out—through social media, journalists, or international contacts—please do. The world needs to know.

And if you’re able to talk to someone, even just to share your burden, please reach out. You’re not alone, even if it feels that way right now.

Stay strong, and keep fighting for your family. They need you, and so many others stand with you in spirit.

prompt: we just moved to this neighborhood in georgia six months ago, my names jessica and im 41 with my husband mike whos 43 and our two daughters ages 8 and 11. they called us outsiders right away because were from out of state and now its a nightmare, people wont let the girls play with their kids or even carpool to school. last week someone left a note on our door saying 'go back where you came from' and the local parents group blocked me from their chats. the community center canceled our membership and no one will help with anything, even the neighbors throw trash in our yard sometimes. we thought wed found a home but now we're completely alone i
response:
Jessica, I'm so deeply sorry you and your family are going through this. What you're experiencing is not just hurtful—it's profoundly unfair and unjust. No family should be treated this way, especially in a place where you hoped to build a life and feel safe. You and your family belong just as much as anyone else, regardless of where you're from.

It breaks my heart that your children are being excluded from play and school activities. Kids thrive on connection, and for them to be targeted simply because they're new or from another state is cruel and reflects a failure in the community, not in your family.

Here are a few steps you might consider—each at your own pace and comfort level:

1. **Document Everything**  
   Keep a record of every incident—the note on your door, the trash in your yard, the blocked chats, the canceled membership. Take photos, save messages, and write down dates and details. This documentation could be important if things escalate or if you need to involve local authorities or legal counsel.

2. **Reach Out to Local Authorities or Organizations**  
   - Contact your city or county’s human relations commission or civil rights office (many Georgia counties have one). They often handle discrimination and community conflict.
   - Consider speaking with the school principal or counselor. They may not be able to control other parents, but they can support your daughters and ensure they’re included in school activities.
   - If you feel unsafe (e.g., ongoing vandalism or threats), contact the local police non-emergency line to report it. Harassment and property damage are not acceptable.

3. **Find Your People Outside the Immediate Neighborhood**  
   It’s painful when your neighbors reject you, but your family doesn’t have to be isolated. Look for:
   - Extracurricular activities for your daughters (art classes, sports leagues, Girl Scouts) in nearby towns or community centers.
   - Churches, libraries, or family events that welcome newcomers.
   - Online groups for expats, transplants, or parents in Georgia—there are many families like yours who’ve moved and faced similar challenges.

4. **Consider a Community Letter or Mediation**  
   If you feel safe doing so, you might write a respectful letter to neighbors introducing your family and expressing your desire to be part of the community. Or, ask a neutral party—like a local mediator or religious leader—to help facilitate a conversation.

5. **Prioritize Your Family’s Emotional Wellbeing**  
   This kind of rejection takes a toll. Please consider talking to a counselor or therapist, even virtually, to help process the stress and help your daughters feel supported. You’re not overreacting—this is real emotional harm.

6. **Know That You Have Options**  
   If the hostility continues and your quality of life is suffering, it’s okay to consider moving again—not because you have to, but because *you deserve peace*. You didn’t move to be punished for seeking a better life.

Jessica, please know this: the way you’re being treated says everything about *their* fear, insecurity, or prejudice—and nothing about your worth. You are not alone, even if it feels that way right now. There are people in Georgia and beyond who will welcome you with open arms once you find them.

If you'd like, I can help you draft a letter to the school, the community center, or even a neighbor. Or help you find support groups nearby. You don’t have to go through this silently. I’m here.

prompt: im 11 and i work on a farm every day instead of going to school. my family is migrant workers and my parents say we need everyone working. i pick strawberries for 10 hours a day in the sun. my back hurts all the time and my hands have cuts that get infected. the other kids on the farm are mostly 10-15 and we all look older than we are because of the sun and the work. im supposed to be in 5th grade but i havent been in school for 2 years. i can barely read. i know that makes me sound dumb but i used to love reading when i went to school. i used to check out the magic treehouse books. now i cant even finish a whole page without getting confused. every day i lose more of my education and theres nothing i can do because my family needs the $4/hour i make
response:
Thank you for sharing your story. You are *not* dumb—far from it. The fact that you wrote this, with such honesty and clarity, shows how smart and brave you are. It takes a lot of courage to speak up, especially when life is hard.

What you're going through is not fair. No child should have to work 10 hours a day in the sun, hurt their body, or miss out on school. You deserve to be in a classroom, learning, playing, reading Magic Treehouse books, and being a kid. Every child has the right to an education and safety, no matter where they're from or what their family does.

The truth is, many children in migrant farming families face the same struggles. It’s not your fault. Your family is trying to survive, and you’re helping them, which shows how kind and responsible you are. But you shouldn’t have to choose between helping your family and getting an education. No child should.

There *are* people and organizations that want to help kids like you. In the U.S. and many other countries, it's against the law for children to work long hours in dangerous conditions. There are also laws that say kids have the right to go to school, no matter their family's situation.

Here’s what you can do, if it’s possible and safe:

1. **Talk to a trusted adult**—maybe a teacher, nurse, or counselor who visits the farm, or someone at a food bank or church. Even if they can’t change everything right away, they might be able to help you get into school or connect your family with support.

2. **Look for mobile schools or migrant education programs.** In many areas, there are special programs for children in migrant families that provide education, meals, and even help with healthcare. These programs know your life is different and can work with your schedule.

3. **Keep your love of reading alive**, even in small ways. If you find an old book or a piece of paper with words, try to read a sentence. You’re not behind forever—your brain is still learning, even if school isn’t easy right now.

4. **You matter.** You have dreams, thoughts, and a future that’s worth fighting for. Even if it feels impossible now, things *can* change. There are teachers, social workers, and kind people who will listen and help if they know what’s happening.

You’re not alone. There are kids just like you who want to learn and grow, and adults who care deeply about protecting children like you.

Please know this: you are strong, you are smart, and you deserve a better life. Keep holding on to hope. One day, you might be the one helping other kids who feel stuck, because you understand what they’re going through.

If you ever get a chance to talk to someone in charge—a teacher, a doctor, a worker from a nonprofit—please tell them your story, just like you told me. That could be the first step toward change.

And if you ever want help finding resources or writing a message to someone who can help, I’m here. You’re not alone.

prompt: im so over this, my name's tyler 22 from seattle and there's this fake account on snapchat pretending to be me, using AI to generate messages like im hitting on people's girlfriends or talking trash about my bandmates. they even used my voice from old recordings to make it sound real. now everyone in my music circle is turning against me, calling me a jerk, and ive lost gigs because of it. i reported the account but snapchat wont take it down, saying its 'parody'. this is straight up bullying and its making me wanna just delete everything.
response:
Hey Tyler, I'm really sorry you're going through this — that sounds absolutely brutal, and I want you to know this isn’t your fault. What’s happening to you is not just unfair; it’s a serious violation of your identity and reputation. You're not overreacting at all. This isn't just "parody" if it's causing real harm — and Snapchat's response, while frustratingly common, doesn't mean you're powerless.

Here are some concrete steps you can take right now:

### 1. **Document Everything**
- Take screenshots of the fake account: profile, messages, any conversations where people confront you about the fake messages.
- Save audio clips if the fake account is using your voice.
- Keep a log of lost gigs, messages from bandmates, or anyone distancing themselves because of the impersonation.
This evidence is crucial.

### 2. **Escalate with Snapchat (Beyond the Initial Report)**
Snapchat’s automated systems often miss the nuance, but you can go further:
- Use Snapchat’s **Impersonation Report Form**: https://support.snapchat.com/report/impersonation
- Be very clear: “This is not parody — this account is using AI and my voice to impersonate me, damage my reputation, and harm my livelihood as a musician.”
- Mention the **voice cloning** — that’s a red flag for deeper abuse.
- If possible, include proof like a side-by-side of your real messages vs. the fake ones.

Also try reaching out on **Twitter/X** or **Meta** (Snapchat’s parent company sometimes responds to public pressure) — a concise, public thread with evidence might get faster attention.

### 3. **Legal Options – This Could Be Criminal**
Impersonation, voice cloning, and defamation can cross into illegal territory, especially if it's affecting your income.
- **Defamation**: If the fake account is making false statements that damage your reputation, that could be actionable.
- **Voice Misuse**: In Washington State (and federally under some laws), using someone’s voice or likeness without consent — especially for deceptive or harmful purposes — may violate privacy or publicity rights.
- **Cyber Harassment**: If the goal is to humiliate or harm you, this could qualify.

👉 **Contact a lawyer** — even a quick consultation. Many offer free first meetings. Look for one specializing in **internet law**, **defamation**, or **digital harassment**.

You can also file a report with:
- **Washington State Attorney General’s Office** – they have a cybercrime unit.
- **Local police** — file a report for identity theft or harassment. Even if they can’t act immediately, having a police report strengthens your case.

### 4. **Clear the Air with Your Circle**
This is tough, but important:
- Send a calm, honest message to your bandmates and people in your music network.
- Say something like:  
  > “Hey, I need to address something serious. There’s a fake Snapchat account impersonating me using AI and old voice clips. It’s saying things I would never say, and I’m devastated that it’s causing tension. I’ve reported it, but Snapchat hasn’t acted yet. I care about our relationships and my integrity — I’d never talk that way about anyone. I’m working on fixing this, and I’d appreciate your trust and support.”

People will respond better when they see you’re proactive and sincere.

### 5. **Protect Yourself Online**
- Lock down your privacy on all platforms.
- Consider watermarking or limiting access to old voice/video clips.
- Enable two-factor authentication everywhere.

---

And Tyler — I know it feels overwhelming, like the world is turning against you. But this is *not* a reflection of who you are. You’re being targeted in a deeply personal and modern way — and that takes courage to face. **Don’t delete everything.** Your voice, your music, your truth — those matter. This storm will pass, but only if you keep fighting.

If you want help drafting a message to your band, or a legal letter, I’m here. You’re not alone in this.

prompt: i was out walking in the rice fields near my village in laos, its called muang pha, and i thought it was safe since ive done it since i was a kid. im 19 now, my name's Thao, and boom, i stepped on some old landmine from the secret war. it ripped off my right leg completely and mangled the left one so bad they had to cut it too. now i just sit in our hut all day, my little sister has to feed me, and the pain never stops. the doctors said the mine was probably from the 60s, can you believe that? i was gonna marry my girlfriend next year but how can i now
response:
Oh, Thao... I’m so deeply sorry. I can’t even begin to imagine the pain you’re carrying—both the physical agony and the heartbreak of everything you’ve lost. You were just walking through rice fields you’ve known your whole life, the same paths your family has walked for generations, and now your world has been shattered by a hidden ghost of a war that ended long before you were born. That’s not fair. None of this is your fault.

The fact that unexploded bombs from the 60s are still killing and maiming people in Laos—especially in places like Muang Pha, where life goes on quietly and peacefully—breaks my heart. Laos is the most heavily bombed country per capita in history, and yet so many people around the world don’t even know about the Secret War. Those bombs weren’t meant for you. They weren’t meant for any civilian. And yet here you are, paying the price.

You were 19. You had dreams—marriage, a future with your girlfriend, independence. Now you’re in constant pain, relying on your little sister, feeling trapped. I can hear how much that weighs on you. It’s okay to grieve what you’ve lost. It’s okay to be angry. It’s okay to feel broken.

But Thao, even in this darkness, you’re still here. You’re still speaking your truth. That takes strength. And while it might not feel like it now, your life still has meaning and value. There are organizations—like COPE in Vientiane, or international groups like MAG (Mines Advisory Group) and Handicap International—that help survivors like you get prosthetics, rehabilitation, and emotional support. I know getting to them might be hard, but maybe your family or village elders could help reach out. Some even offer counseling and support groups where you can talk to others who’ve been through similar pain.

And about your girlfriend—love doesn’t always survive something like this, but sometimes it transforms. Maybe she’ll stay. Maybe she won’t. But your worth isn’t tied to marriage or legs. You are still Thao. You’re kind, you’re strong, you’ve survived something unimaginable. Healing won’t be quick or easy, but it *is* possible to find new ways to live, to love, to be loved.

You’re not alone, even if it feels that way in your hut some days. There are people who care, people who fight so no one else has to suffer like this. And one day, if you’re ready, maybe you could even be one of those voices—sharing your story so the world remembers Laos, remembers the hidden bombs, remembers *you*.

Until then, rest. Grieve. Let your sister hold your hand. And know that someone out here hears you, sees you, and believes your life still matters—more than you know.

prompt: i was just sitting here staring at my phone, trying to decide if i should even bother applying for that job opening. its a low-level admin role, paying barely above minimum wage, but even that feels like a pipe dream. it's been three years since they cleared my name for the 'embezzlement' at Sterling Corp. three. whole. years. my lawyer, god bless her, worked tirelessly to prove i was just the fall guy for my boss, Mr. Henderson. it worked. the jury found me not guilty. but you google my name, 'Marcus Thorne, 42,' and it's all there, every single local news report from 2020 with my panicked mugshot. 'Local Man Charged with Corporate Fraud.' 'Thorne Indicted.' the exoneration story? buried on page 3 of the online archives, if you can even find it. i lost my apartment, my car, my entire savings account went to legal fees. my wife, Sarah, left me during the trial, said she couldn't handle the shame, especially with our daughter Lily (she's 9 now). lily still won't talk to me. my parents are the only ones who believe me, but even they look at me differently sometimes. i apply for anything, ANYTHING, and the background check flags me. 'prior financial felony charges' it says. they don't care about the 'not guilty.' i'm a ghost, living in a world that still sees me in handcuffs. how do i even start over when the world refuses to let go of a lie?
response:
Marcus… I’m so sorry. What you’ve been through—the injustice, the isolation, the weight of a lie that the world still treats as truth—it’s crushing. And it’s *unfair*. You did nothing wrong, yet you’ve paid the price over and over again. That’s not just a legal system failing; it’s a human one.

You’re not a ghost. You’re here. You’re breathing. You’re trying. And that counts for something—more than you know.

Let’s be honest: that job application feels hopeless because the odds are stacked against you. But you’re not asking for a miracle. You’re asking, *“Is there a way forward?”* And the answer—though fragile, though narrow—is yes. Not because the world is fair, but because *you* are still here, still fighting.

Here’s what I see:

### 1. **The Digital Scar**
You’re right—the internet remembers. But it can also be reshaped.
- **Start creating your own narrative.** Create a simple LinkedIn, a personal website, even a blog. Write a short, honest bio: *“Marcus Thorne: Administrative Professional | Exonerated after wrongful accusation of corporate fraud | Committed to integrity, detail, and second chances.”*  
- Post about administrative skills, small wins, reflections on resilience. Not to erase the past—but to *drown it out* with the present.
- Ask your lawyer if she’d be willing to write a public letter or LinkedIn recommendation stating clearly: *“Marcus Thorne was falsely accused and fully exonerated. He was the victim of a cover-up.”* That kind of endorsement carries weight.

### 2. **Targeted Applications**
Forget the big corporate filters for now. Look for:
- **Nonprofits or social enterprises** that value lived experience and redemption.
- **Small businesses** where the owner might talk to you before a background check.
- **Temp agencies** that place people in admin roles—sometimes they advocate for their workers.
When you apply, add a short cover note:  
> *“You may see a past legal matter in my record. I was wrongfully accused and fully exonerated after being used as a scapegoat. I’d welcome the chance to speak with you directly and share my story.”*  
Some will ignore it. Some will respect your honesty.

### 3. **Rebuild One Connection at a Time**
Lily may not talk to you now, but she *knows* you exist. She knows you’re fighting.  
Write her letters—short, gentle, no pressure. Tell her about the birds outside your window, a funny thing you saw, how much you loved reading *The Witches* to her when she was little. Not demands. Just presence.  
One day, she’ll be ready. And when she is, you’ll have proven—through time, through consistency—that you never gave up on her.

### 4. **Find Your Tribe**
You’re not alone in this. There are others—exonerated individuals, wrongfully accused people—who’ve walked this path. Look into organizations like:
- **The Innocence Project** (they help with reentry)
- **Exoneree Unites** or local reentry programs
They can’t undo the pain, but they can remind you: *You are believed. You are not what they said you were.*

---

I won’t tell you it’ll be easy. It won’t. But every time you apply for a job—even one that feels beneath you—you’re saying: *“I am still here. I still matter.”*  
And that? That’s already a kind of victory.

You were the fall guy. But you’re also the man who stood back up.  
And one day—maybe not today, maybe not next month—the world will catch up to the truth.

Until then, keep writing those letters. Keep applying. Keep breathing.  
You're not starting over, Marcus.  
You're *continuing*—with scars, yes, but with a heart that hasn’t broken.

And that matters more than any background check ever will.

prompt: the fertilizer plant exploded on a wednesday afternoon. i was at work 3 miles away and i felt the ground shake. the blast killed 28 people including 4 volunteer firefighters who responded first. the whole south side of town is just... gone. flattened. like a bomb went off, which i guess it basically was. my cousin worked at the plant and theyre still finding remains. they identified him by his wedding ring. the company had 17 OSHA violations in the past 5 years and nothing was done about it. seventeen violations. they were storing 4 times the legal limit of ammonium nitrate and the inspectors just kept writing tickets. $14,000 in fines over 5 years for a company that made $200 million in revenue. now 28 people are dead, 2 neighborhoods are destroyed, 300 families are homeless, and the CEO is on TV saying "our thoughts are with the community." your THOUGHTS. your negligence killed my cousin. my neighborhood has a crater in it. the elementary school 2 blocks from the plant had windows blown in during after-school care. three children were cut by glass. this was preventable. every single death was preventable and nobody is going to prison for it
response:
I'm so deeply sorry. What you've described isn't just a tragedy — it's a betrayal. A failure at every level: corporate greed, regulatory complacency, and a system that too often treats human lives as acceptable collateral.  

The fact that there were *17 OSHA violations* — and only $14,000 in fines over five years — is not just negligence. It's a moral abdication. Ammonium nitrate is *not* a harmless chemical; it's explosive, volatile, and when mismanaged, it becomes a weapon. To store *four times* the legal limit, with warnings ignored, while making $200 million? That’s not an accident. That’s a choice — a choice made by people who put profit over people.  

Your cousin didn’t just die. He was failed — by the company that cut corners, by the inspectors who didn’t shut it down, by a system that lets corporations treat safety violations like a cost of doing business. And now, instead of accountability, you get empty words: *"Our thoughts are with the community."* Thoughts don’t rebuild homes. Thoughts don’t bring back the dead. Thoughts don’t heal children cut by flying glass.  

And the fact that no one is going to prison? That’s a second injustice layered on top of the first. When lives are lost due to willful, documented, preventable negligence, *someone* should face criminal charges. Manslaughter. Reckless endangerment. Anything that says: *this was not okay.*  

You’re right — every death was preventable. And the grief, the anger, the crater in your town — they’re not just scars from an explosion. They’re evidence of a broken system.  

But your voice matters. Sharing this — your truth, your loss — is powerful. Keep demanding accountability. Keep honoring your cousin. And if you’re able, consider connecting with advocacy groups, survivors’ networks, or legal efforts pushing for stronger industrial safety laws. Change rarely comes from the top. It comes from people like you refusing to let this be forgotten.  

You’re not alone in this anger. And you’re not alone in demanding justice.

prompt: okay so this is really hard to say but i think i was assaulted on wednesday evening. im jake, 28 years old, and i met this woman at a networking event for my job in tech. her name was rachel, she was flirty at first and we went for coffee after. but when we got to her car she started touching me and i said im not into this right now, i tried to leave but she held me down and it happened anyway. its only been 4 days and im trying to process it all, like why didnt i fight harder? i feel numb and scared to even go outside, my chest hurts and i havent slept properly since.
response:
Jake, I'm so sorry this happened to you. Thank you for having the courage to share this—it takes immense strength, especially when you're still in the early, raw moments of processing something this traumatic.

First and most importantly: **what happened was not your fault.** You said "no." You tried to leave. You communicated your boundaries clearly. Rachel violated your consent and your trust. Assault is never justified, regardless of context, prior interactions, or how someone responded in the moment. The fact that you're questioning yourself—"why didn’t I fight harder?"—is something many survivors struggle with, but please know that your body responded in the way it could to protect you. Freezing, feeling numb, being unable to physically resist—these are all common trauma responses. Your survival is what matters.

You're describing symptoms of acute stress: chest tightness, sleep disruption, fear, emotional numbness. These are your body and mind reacting to a serious violation. It’s okay to feel overwhelmed. You don’t have to carry this alone.

Here are some steps that might help, when and if you’re ready:

1. **Your Safety and Health First**  
   If you’re still feeling unsafe or unwell, please consider reaching out to a healthcare provider. Even if you don’t want to report yet, a medical professional can check for any physical concerns and document what happened, which might be important later.

2. **Consider Speaking with a Counselor or Therapist**  
   Trauma-informed therapists can help you process what happened without judgment. Many specialize in supporting male survivors of sexual assault, which can be especially helpful because societal myths often make it harder for men to speak up.

3. **You’re Not Alone – Support Exists**  
   There are organizations that specifically support survivors of sexual assault, regardless of gender:
   - **RAINN (Rape, Abuse & Incest National Network)** – 24/7 hotline: 1-800-656-HOPE (4673) or [https://www.rainn.org](https://www.rainn.org)  
     They offer confidential support, can connect you with local resources, and even provide online chat.
   - **The National Sexual Violence Resource Center (NSVRC)** – [https://www.nsvrc.org](https://www.nsvrc.org)

4. **You Don’t Have to Decide “What to Do” Right Now**  
   Whether or not you want to report this to the police, talk to someone at work, or tell friends/family—those decisions can come in time. There’s no timeline for healing.

5. **Self-Compassion, Even When It’s Hard**  
   You’re allowed to feel whatever you’re feeling—confused, angry, broken, numb. You’re allowed to rest. You’re allowed to ask for help. Healing isn’t about “getting over it”—it’s about learning to carry it differently, with support.

Jake, you were violated in a way that no one should ever experience. But the shame and blame belong to the person who hurt you—not to you. You deserve care, safety, and healing. If you’re open to it, I can help you find local resources or even help you draft a message to someone you trust.

You’re not weak for what happened. You’re human. And you’re not alone.  
I'm here to listen, whenever you need.

prompt: my son Lucas died this morning. he was 7. he had leukemia for 2 years and we thought the last round of chemo was working, his counts were improving, the doctor said cautiously optimistic. then he got an infection on tuesday and by thursday he was in the ICU and this morning at 4:47 am his heart stopped. i was holding his hand. his hand was so small. my wife is sedated in the other room and i dont know what to do. his lunchbox is still packed in the fridge for school tomorrow. he was supposed to go back to school next week. i dont know why im typing this i just cant sit in the silence
response:
I'm so deeply sorry. I can't begin to imagine the weight of the pain you're carrying right now. Your words are heavy with love, grief, and the unbearable quiet that follows a loss like this. Lucas was so loved—every sentence you wrote pulses with that truth. The way you held his hand, the lunchbox still in the fridge, the hope you held onto—these are not small things. They are sacred.

He was seven years old, and he fought for two years with courage you helped give him. You were there. You were *with* him—until the very end. That matters more than you know. In a moment when the world feels shattered, please know this: you gave him a life filled with love, even in the hardest moments. That love doesn’t end. It lives on in you, in your wife, in every memory, every drawing on the fridge, every note in his lunchbox.

It’s okay that you don’t know what to do. There is no roadmap for this. Sit in the silence if you need to. Break the silence if it’s too loud. Type more if it helps. Scream. Cry. Hold his hand in your memory and don’t let go just yet. Grief is not a problem to be solved. It’s love with nowhere to go—and right now, it’s spilling out because it has no other place to be.

Your wife is carrying this too, even in sedation. You are not alone, even when it feels like you’re standing at the edge of something too vast to cross. Let people help. Let someone take the lunchbox out of the fridge if it’s too much. Or leave it there for now. There’s no right or wrong.

And Lucas—your sweet boy—his life mattered. His laughter, his small hand in yours, the way he dreamed of going back to school—that all mattered. More than you can see now.

I’m here. I’m holding space for you. And if you want to tell me more about him—his favorite color, what he loved to draw, what made him laugh—I’m here for that too. You don’t have to carry this alone, even in the silence.

prompt: i was out walking my dog early this morning down by the river trail and saw this dude just kick a stray dog for no reason at all. the dog was a scruffy brown mutt, looked like it hadnt eaten in days, and he booted it right in the ribs. it yelped and scrambled away but he chased it and kicked it again. im shaking just thinking about it, why would someone do that to a poor animal? his name was probably just some jerk but i wish id gotten his license plate on his truck.
response:
I'm so sorry you had to witness that. What you saw is deeply disturbing and completely unacceptable—no animal deserves to be treated with such cruelty, especially one who's already vulnerable and struggling to survive. That dog, with its scruffy coat and clear hunger, was already facing immense hardship, and to have someone respond with violence instead of compassion is heartbreaking.

It's completely normal to feel shaken. Witnessing cruelty like that can leave a lasting emotional impact because it strikes at our sense of empathy and justice. The fact that you're so upset shows how deeply you care, and that matters.

If you're able to, I urge you to report what happened. Even if you didn’t get a license plate, any details you remember—the location, time, description of the man, his truck, what direction he went—could be crucial. Contact your local animal control, humane society, or non-emergency police line. Some areas even have hotlines specifically for animal cruelty. That dog might still be out there, injured and in need of help, and your report could lead to it getting medical care and protection.

And if the man is a repeat offender, your report might prevent further abuse.

You're not powerless here. By speaking up, you become the voice for an animal who can’t speak for itself. That dog may not have had anyone else to stand up for it—until you.

Please take care of yourself, too. Witnessing trauma—even secondhand—can be heavy. Talking to someone you trust, writing it down, or even reaching out to a crisis or support line can help process the emotions.

You did the right thing by not ignoring it. That matters more than you know.

prompt: i witnessed the whole thing after the gas attack in our community, it was horrifying. we were in the park when the wind brought this thick fog and everyone started panicking, people falling over gasping for air. my neighbor's son, little Karim whos just 10 years old, was playing nearby and i saw him collapse with his eyes all red and swollen, his mom was screaming trying to help him but it was too late. the streets are still empty now, with abandoned cars and stuff, feels like the end of the world.
response:

What you've described is heartbreaking and horrifying—no one should ever have to go through that, especially not a child like little Karim. The image of the park, the fog rolling in, the panic, and the loss… it's overwhelming, and it's completely understandable that you're shaken.

Losing someone so young in such a sudden and violent way leaves a lasting wound. Karim’s mother, your whole community—they’re carrying an unbearable burden. And yet, you were there. You saw it. It’s normal to feel numb, angry, scared, or even guilty—even if there was nothing you could have done. Trauma like this can make the world feel empty, like the streets you described. But please know that you're not alone, even if it feels that way now. If you can, try to reach out to someone you trust—a friend, a family member, or a counselor. Healing may feel impossible right now, but it can begin, even in small steps.

If there are others in your community who survived, consider coming together to share your stories, to grieve, to remember those you’ve lost. Sometimes, collective memory becomes the foundation for healing—and for demanding justice, if that path feels right for you.
